--- ROUTE FAILURE (THREE DEAD) The count was an argument before it was a number.

The bell came from Sector Nine, not as a roar—just a single strike repeated until someone answered. Residential stairwell. Upper-terrace split. Twelve families still inside, one route. Jhace was already marked from Sector Nine. Corven was worse. Senne was fresh enough to stand without shaking. Tiffani looked at the slate wall and made the decision she would later pretend was only math. Two operators at one‑eighty each meant coordination, handoffs, spotters, and failure points. One operator at two‑forty meant simplicity. A clean line. “Single deployment,” she said. “Senne. Duration cap: two‑forty. Terminate on my bell.” Senne went pale as the heat took her chest. Copper-gold light laced her forearms. Not pretty. Functional. They reached the stairwell mouth as stone dust sifted from the ceiling like flour. The grid-hum underfoot carried the doubled harmonic, deep and insistent, a warning that didn’t care who listened. Senne braced under the central landing. Jhace watched her shoulders set—metalworker’s posture in a glassworker’s frame. Tiffani counted. Heartbeats, not heartbeats. Counts lied when panic touched them. One hundred. One forty. One eighty. At one‑ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn’t walked because she’d trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding. The evacuation lane kinked. The next family stumbled. The stone didn’t collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket. Three people were on the wrong side when it did: two adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve. Tiffani saw them, and for a moment the slate in her hands felt obscene. “Hold,” she said, voice flat. “Keep holding.” Senne’s eyes didn’t move. She couldn’t spare the motion. Two‑ten. Two‑twenty. Two‑thirty. The pocket didn’t clear. The child’s coughing turned wet. At two‑forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway. Because protocols existed for a reason. Because if she didn’t, she would never stop. Senne released. The stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along. They dug until their arms went numb. They found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles. Back in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision. Deployment: Senne — 240 heartbeats Evacuated: 47 Casualties: 3 Cause: Inadequate route assessment. Secondary beam shear. She wrote “cause” as if precision could make it reversible. Her hand moved to write the next revision. The chalk touched slate. Nothing came out. She stared at the number. Three. Not a count. Three names she would never know. Three families who would ask why their— The chalk snapped in her grip. She set the broken pieces down carefully. Picked up a new stick. Wrote: Route spotters required. Three per deployment. No exceptions. Underlined it twice. Then she closed the slate and sat in the dark workshop, counting her own breaths because the numbers on the page wouldn't behave. Her fingers kept shaking anyway.

---




CHAPTER 6: WITNESS

Brother Corran didn’t wear Temple colors. The first thing Jhace noticed was the badge—bright, clean, pinned where it couldn't be missed. Next was how the man didn't look tired. Not even a little. Corran’s eyes went straight to Jhace’s hands. “For the record, I’m not here to stop you,” Corran said, before anyone asked. Bram hovered by the door like a lock that wanted to be useful. Tiffani set her slate down deliberately. “Then why are you here?” “Because you’re real,” Corran said. “And because real things attract predators.” Jhace’s mouth tightened. “Crown.” Corran’s gaze didn’t flinch. “Yes. Also Guild. Also Temple. Anyone who thinks they can turn you into a lever.” Bram’s voice came dry. “Ah yes. The holy trinity of getting fucked: prayer, production, paperwork.” Tiffani shot him a look. “Bram.” He raised his hands. “What? I’m helping.” Bram gestured toward the door. “Temple says you’re divine. Guild says you’re labor. Crown says you’re property. Everybody’s trying to fuck the same lever.” He pulled a folded packet from his coat and set it on Jhace’s bench. Paper. Old style. Archive weight. “Stage Three cases,” Corran said. “Documented. Not rumors.” Tiffani’s hands went still. “How many?” “Enough.” Corran’s voice was flat, and that flatness carried exhaustion. “Most die. A few don’t. The ones who don’t… wish they had.” Jhace’s tremor ticked up at the word die. He forced his hands flat on the bench. “Why give this to us?” Corran hesitated. That hesitation was the crack in the Crown. “Because someone above me wants a registry,” he said. “Names. Locations. Deployment authority. They’re calling it order. It’s control.” Tiffani’s eyes narrowed. “Who?” Corran looked toward the door as if the walls might report him. “Administrator Corlexi. Infrastructure Compliance.” Bram gave a low whistle. “Clean hands. Clean coat. Probably wipes his ass with forms.” Jhace stared at the packet. “So you’re here to warn us.” “I’m here to give you data,” Corran corrected. “If you want to fight control, you need numbers. Not speeches.” Tiffani opened the packet and read, eyes moving fast. Jhace watched her face tighten at certain lines—durations, costs, outcomes. She didn’t gasp. She didn’t dramatize. She just absorbed. “This case,” she said, tapping a page. “Stage Three survival. How?” Corran’s mouth went thin. “He didn’t terminate. He couldn’t. The hold locked. Autonomic failure started and the manifestation kept running anyway. The structure held until it didn’t matter.” Jhace felt cold crawl up his spine despite the workshop heat. “Locked hold.” Corran nodded. “It’s not a tool you put down when you’re done. Sometimes it’s a hook in your chest that drags you until something breaks.” Tiffani’s pen scratched. Stage Three: autonomic failure. Locked hold possible. Termination may fail. Bram shifted. “You said predators.” Corran’s eyes met Bram’s. “Tallec is filing complaints. Says you’re obstructing industry. Says your protocols slow production.” Jhace’s jaw clenched so hard it hurt. Corran leaned closer, voice low. “Here’s the fracture you wanted. I don’t like Corlexi’s registry. I don’t like Temple’s sermons. I don’t like Guild’s schedules. But I like fewer dead.” He straightened. “If you build methodology, build it fast. Because the Crown is already drafting theirs. And theirs won’t care what it costs you.” He turned to leave. Tiffani called after him. “Why risk telling us this?” Corran paused at the door. “Because Junction Seven wasn’t the first ignored report,” he said. “And I’m tired of pretending ignorance is the same as innocence.” Then he was gone. Jhace’s hands stayed flat on the workbench. “If this registry happens…” He swallowed. “I’m not doing it for paper.” Bram exhaled slowly. “And because guilt is cheaper than funerals. Not by much. But cheaper.” Tiffani climbed the workshop stairs slower than she had a Tide ago. Her right knee caught on the third step—not pain, just resistance, like the joint was learning to refuse. She stopped on the landing. Put her palm to the wall until the world steadied. The knee had locked twice this Anneal. Not during a hold. During stairs. During the parts where bodies were supposed to cooperate. She’d logged operator tremor. Operator vein-lines. Operator autonomic failure. She had not logged her own joint learning to refuse weight. Because if she logged it, she’d have to adjust. Delegate. Sit down. And if she sat down, someone else would coordinate from the deck. Someone else would make the calls. She took the next step anyway. She didn’t log it. Some costs weren’t manifestation. Some were just labor. Jhace’s hands shook on the bench like they were trying to answer him.

---




CHAPTER 7: MERCHANT HALL

Merchant Hall was a cathedral for trade: iron ribs, stone spine, glass skylights that made everything inside look clean even when it wasn’t. And it smelled like bodies and money and fear—the kind of fear that tries to wear perfume. It started failing at mid‑Tide. The bell sequence came sharp and frantic—no warning cadence, no time to wedge first. The Hall had been packed when the terrace shifted again. Four hundred inside, Bram said. Maybe more. Tiffani arrived before Jhace. When he pushed through the crowd, she was already on the steps, slate in hand, shouting like a foreman who’d learned math. “Operators on me. Two per span. Spotters assigned. No hero holds.” “Duration limit. One-eighty heartbeats maximum. You terminate on my signal. No argument.” Merra shoved through the dust with a slate clutched to her chest. Ash streaked her hair like she’d tried to comb through a fire. “We have a problem. Southern stairwell collapsed. Fifty people trapped on upper floors. Can’t reach main exits.” “Time estimate,” Tiffani said. “Low and high.” “Unknown. Debris blocking secondary stairs. Could be two hundred heartbeats. Could be six hundred.” “Then we don’t gamble.” Tiffani didn’t raise her voice. She didn’t have to. “Clear lanes. Count bodies out. You want prayer, pray while you walk.” She swung her slate toward the operators. “Corven—baseline check. When chest heat starts, report it. Don’t interpret. Report.” Corven swallowed. “If it hits, I tell you. If I taste copper, I tell you.” Jhace didn’t look at him. “Don’t spiral. Do the minimum.” “Minimum,” Corven echoed, like repetition could make it true. “Senne—status.” “Getting hollow,” Senne said. “Chest feels empty. Light’s harder to maintain.” “That’s Stage Two warning.” Tiffani’s eyes cut sharp. “You terminate on my signal or I terminate you myself.” Senne gave her a tired half-smile. “Sure. Die on schedule. Very professional.” “Kess—report.” “I am present and available for assignment.” “Available doesn’t mean useful. Report symptoms. Stop performing.” “Symptoms are within acceptable parameters for operational requirements.” Bram leaned in, voice too bright for the amount of people about to die. “Translation: Kess is bleeding and pretending it’s fashion.” “I am not bleeding.” Merra didn’t bother being gentle. “Kess—your nose is bleeding.” “I am aware of the nasal discharge. It is minor. Barely noteworthy. I’ve had worse nosebleeds from seasonal changes.” “Yeah,” Bram said. “And I’ve had worse ideas than this hold. Doesn’t make them good.” From the crowd, a Temple Observer screamed, “Let the worthy hold! Let the unworthy fall!” Bram snapped his head toward the sound. “Hey—shut up. If we’re picking who falls, you’re first. That’s democracy.” “Ignore them,” Tiffani said. “Move.” “Spotters, call markers.” “Jhace tremor increasing!” “Corven tremor increasing!” “Senne vein-line onset!” Bram barked at the evac line like a foreman with a broken sense of humor. “Keep moving! Nobody stops! You stop, you become part of the debris! Count them out!” Merra’s voice cut through the dust. “Alternate route open. Final evacuation commencing.” “Count them,” Tiffani said. “Call numbers.” Jhace’s breath came tight. “One hundred.” “State exit count,” Tiffani said, “not your suffering.” “One hundred. Out.” Daved’s voice came rough through the grit. “They’re not out.” Jhace’s head snapped. “No.” “They’re not out.” “Daved—state the variable,” Tiffani said. “How many still inside?” “Enough.” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Extend. Thirty heartbeats. No more.” “Tiff—” Jhace started. “I am not debating physics with the dead in the hallway,” she said. “Extend.” Thirty heartbeats passed like a slow knife. Merra shouted from the corridor mouth, “Final group emerging. Ten heartbeats!” “Ten heartbeats is still ten,” Tiffani said. “Move.” Daved’s hands tightened. “Hold.” “Daved!” Jhace lunged a step closer. “Hold.” Merra’s slate slapped stone. “Clear! All civilians safe!” “Terminate all operators,” Tiffani snapped. “Immediate coordinated shutdown.” Lights guttered and went out—one by one—except Daved’s. “Can’t,” Daved said. “Daved. Breathe,” Jhace said, too close, too desperate. “Can’t.” “Terminate,” Tiffani said. “That is not a negotiation.” “They’re not out.” “They are out. Merra confirmed. Terminate now.” Daved’s jaw worked like he was chewing copper. “Already crossed.” Bram shoved his way in with words because silence was making everyone honest. “Daved—buddy—love the commitment, hate the method acting.” “Bram, shut up,” Jhace hissed. “I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s a disorder.” Tiffani stepped in until Daved had to see her. “Look at me. If you extend past this, you cross Stage Three threshold.” Daved’s eyes stayed flat. “Already crossed.” Jhace’s throat tightened. “He locked.” “I see it,” Tiffani said. They hauled Daved back from the structure like pulling a hook out of meat. “Terminate!” Tiffani shouted. The light finally cut. Daved folded like a tool dropped from numb hands. Tiffani’s voice went small for one syllable. “I know.”

They carried Daved out on a door panel when the Hall finally stopped shifting enough to allow hands near him. His eyes were open. His body was too still. Tiffani walked beside the makeshift stretcher, writing with one hand, wiping dust from her face with the other. Tiffani sat alone in the documentation room after they carried Daved out. Her slate lay open to the cost taxonomy. Stage Three. Locked hold. Autonomic failure. She had written it perfectly. Every marker. Every heartbeat. Every outcome. Her hand moved to add the summary line. She stared at the slate. At the line that read: Casualties: 3. She tried to write the justification. The calculation. The reason extending duration had been correct under the circumstances. The chalk snapped. Her hands shook because she'd been awake too long and her calf cramped—no magic required. The chalk snapped anyway. She stared at her shaking fingers. She didn’t log her tremor. She didn’t log that she’d miscounted heartbeats during the hold because her vision had blurred. She didn’t log that she’d wanted to ring the termination bell at one-eighty but hadn’t, because Daved’s eyes had asked her not to. Some data points weren’t for the ledger. Some were just for carrying.

---

Jhace’s tremor made his jaw ache. He'd wanted method like a wrench—turn it, get the same result. Instead he got a mouthful of pennies and hands that wouldn't stop. Method had just asked for a price it didn’t have the right to demand.

---




CHAPTER 8: THREE TIDES

Daved woke on the third Tide. Three Tides. Long enough for sweat to dry into salt on his collar. Long enough for hope to feel stupid. Jhace stood by the cot in the infirmary room, hands shaking in front of him like they were arguing with air. Tiffani stood on the other side, slate ready, eyes red at the edges from lack of sleep she refused to name. Daved’s eyes opened slowly. His hands were strapped to the cot rails—not as restraint, but as prevention. His fingers still twitched with phantom hold. He tried to speak. His throat made a sound like stone rubbing stone. Tiffani leaned in. “Don’t force voice. Use slate.” Daved wrote: Did it work “Yes. Thirty-two evacuated. One operator Stage Three event. Locked hold. Autonomic failure onset.” He wrote: Cost “Severe tremor. Motor delay. Speech impairment. Autonomic instability. Probability of full recovery: low.” He wrote: Worth it “That’s not a question I can answer. Thirty-two lives. One body broken. You determine if that exchange was acceptable.” Jhace’s throat tightened. “They got thirty-two out.” He couldn’t decide what to do with his hands. Shaking, useless things. He folded them behind his back like that would fool anybody.

Daved wrote: One body is not a small thing “Correct,” Tiffani said. “It is not small. It is also not reversible.” Bram wandered in like a bad idea that knew your schedule. “This room smells like piss and heroism. Worst cologne.” “Leave,” Tiffani said, without looking up, “if you can’t behave.” “I’m behaving,” Bram said. “I’m just… shaped wrong.” Daved wrote: You okay “Define operational parameters for ‘okay.’” Not irrelevant “Compared to your autonomic failure, my current symptoms are statistically minor.” Still matters Tiffani’s pen stopped. “I can’t get the copper taste out of my mouth anymore,” she said quietly. “Food tastes wrong. Water tastes wrong. I think it’s in my teeth.” Jhace started, “Tiff—” “That’s not a Stage marker,” she cut in. “So it doesn’t get logged.” Daved wrote smaller this time: Don’t let them call this holy “I won’t.” “Nobody’s calling this holy,” Bram said. “They’re calling it ‘resource allocation.’ Much sexier.” “Bram.” “I know,” he said. “I’m disgusting. It’s my brand.”

Jhace didn’t know if that was a promise to Daved or a threat to the world.

---




CHAPTER 9: THE SHARD-BEARER (RUMOR)

They called him Shard‑Bearer because nobody had a better word for what they thought they’d seen. A man in the eastern districts with a bright line in his chest. A glow that didn’t fade after termination because there was no termination. A hold that didn’t shake. A body that didn’t pay. Rumors traveled faster than Crown notices and cleaner than Temple sermons. They came with dates and witness counts like proof. Tiffani pinned three reports to the workshop wall with nails. Report one: Torne Reach — Tide 0+3. One operator held a bridge span alone for “half an Anneal.” No tremor. No vein‑lines. Report two: Brinefall — Tide 0+5. A whole terrace stairwell stabilized by “one man’s hands.” Witnesses describe “glass light under the skin.” Tiffani tapped the pinned report with the end of her pen. “Report three: delayed secondary collapse. Building held by Shard-Bearer for six hundred heartbeats. Post-release: immediate structural failure. No tremor visible in operator. No warning signs. Casualties: seventeen.” Jhace stared at the number like it had teeth. “He didn’t save them. He killed them slower.” “Correct,” Tiffani said. “The cost didn’t vanish. It moved—into the structure, into the people around it. Somewhere we can’t ignore.” “Heroics.” The word came out like a curse. “That word implies exemption,” Tiffani said. “There is no exemption. Only deferral.” Bram leaned over the table, too casual for the subject. “So the Shard-Bearer is just… consequences with better marketing.” “Don’t,” Jhace said. “Sorry,” Bram said. “I forgot we’re being solemn about the murder wizard.” Tiffani didn’t look up. “The registry will want this rumor. It provides false relief. False hope that cost can be avoided.” “And people die for that,” Jhace said. “Then we make sure the registry doesn’t become a leash,” Tiffani said.

Jhace’s tremor pulsed like a warning. He didn’t know the Shard‑Bearer. He could already feel the change—in the way strangers leaned back from doorways, in the way foremen stopped pretending they weren’t watching. --- Jhace saw him in Sector Twelve. Not by choice. They were moving toward another assessment call when the crowd parted the wrong way—like people avoided a spill in the street. A grain store had folded inward. One beam still held the upper floor up at an angle that should have been impossible. A man stood under it with both hands on the timber. Alone. No spotters. No wedges. No shouting counts. Just a man holding timber like it weighed nothing and everyone else forgetting how to breathe. Just light—copper‑gold, dense, not filament‑thin. It didn’t thread into the structure like Jhace’s holds did. It sat inside the beam like a replacement spine, telling wood and stone what shape to keep. Tiffani stopped beside Jhace. Her pen was already moving. Shard‑Bearer. Visual confirmation. The man’s chest glowed through his shirt. Not a flicker. A steady line, as if someone had pressed a shard under skin and the body had agreed. “No tremor,” Tiffani murmured. “Not paying,” Jhace said. The words tasted wrong the moment he spoke them. “Incorrect,” she said. “Look at his eyes.” Jhace looked. The man’s eyes were copper‑gold and flat, putting off their own light like heated coins. Jhace couldn’t find a tremor in it. That scared him more than any shake. The beam settled another fraction. The store stopped complaining. The man released the timber and walked away without checking who escaped. No count. No confirmation. No termination signal. No glance back. Tiffani underlined her note twice. Cost unidentified. Method unreplicable. Do not engage. Jhace’s tremor ticked up. Not from fear. From wanting to know what it would feel like to hold without shaking.




CHAPTER 10: LICENSING

They arrived with paper first. The first page had a coffee ring on it. The seal ink was smudged where somebody’s thumb had dragged across it like the Crown was just another sweaty foreman.

A Crown clerk with clean boots unrolled forms on Jhace’s bench like a priest laying out offerings. Behind the clerk stood two guards, a Temple observer, and Tallec—smiling like he’d finally found a way to win without touching a pipe. And at the center, Bram Tallow in a different coat than usual: administrator’s cut, badge pinned. Still Bram, but now carrying Crown weight. “Don’t start,” Jhace said, seeing the coat. Bram’s eyes flicked down to Jhace’s shaking hands and then away. “I didn’t ask for this.” Jhace looked at the coat. The badge. The way Bram wore it like it didn’t fit his bones. “You took it anyway.” Bram’s grin tried to show up and died halfway. “Yeah. Because if I don’t, they send someone who actually likes forms. Someone who’ll file you under ‘infrastructure asset’ and sleep like a baby.” He touched the badge once. Like it burned. “I’m terrified,” he said, quiet enough to almost count as a confession. “Terrified they turn you into numbers. Terrified I help them do it. Terrified I get good at it.” The grin came back, wrong‑shaped. “So I’m coping by making dick jokes. Extremely healthy. Very professional.” Tiffani stepped forward. “What is it?” Bram nodded at the clerk. “Manifestor Registry. Compliance documentation. Deployment authority.” “By order of Infrastructure Compliance Office. Administrator Corlexi presiding.” “Oh good,” Bram said. “A sentence that already feels like a noose.” The clerk didn’t look up. “Name. District. Verified activation capability. Cost markers. Compliance agreement. You will not manifest outside authorized events.” “Define ‘deployment authority,’” Tiffani said. “Infrastructure Compliance Office.” “That is not a definition,” Tiffani said. “That is a label.” “It is the issuing body.” “Then who determines what qualifies as an ‘authorized event’?” “Infrastructure Compliance Office.” Jhace’s jaw worked. “What happens if we refuse?” “Then you are unlicensed. Unlicensed manifestation will be prosecuted as endangerment.” Tallec leaned in with a smile that didn’t reach his eyes. “Finally. Structure. No more renegade holds.” Bram stared at him like he’d stepped in something. “Tallec, you look like a man who’d schedule his own mother’s funeral for throughput.” “At least someone respects schedules,” Tallec said. “Don’t start,” Jhace warned. “I didn’t start,” Bram said. “He started. I’m just adding sex noises.” Corran stepped in like he didn’t believe doors applied to him. “Schedules don’t respect physics.” The clerk’s eyes flicked up, irritated. “Brother Corran. You are not assigned here.” “For the record,” Corran said, “I’m observing.” He looked at Jhace. “And if the Crown orders you to hold a vault instead of a residential stairwell?” Tallec’s voice went oily. “Assets protect assets.” Jhace’s hands went flat on the bench, because he didn’t trust them to do something irreversible. “Next time I hold, it’ll be because something is falling. Not because you want it to stay standing.” “Romantic,” Bram said. “I only break my body for strangers, not for paperwork.” Tiffani’s gaze stayed on the form. “If we don’t sign, we lose access to incidents. They will send licensed manifestors—or force untrained ones—to hold.” She set her pen down with a little click, the way you set down a blade when you're done being polite. “We sign,” she said. “But our standard rides with it.” The clerk’s mouth opened. “That is not—” “Duration limits,” Tiffani said. “Spotters. Termination criteria. Cost tracking. You will not turn people into disposable beams.” Bram leaned toward the clerk. “She means ‘stop treating people like disposable beams,’ but she’s saying it in math so you can understand.” “The registry does not negotiate terms with operators.” “Then the registry gets incomplete operators,” Tiffani said. “Your choice.” Tallec’s smile widened. “You become assets. Next time you hold, you’ll do it where the Crown tells you to hold. Not where you feel like playing hero.” “‘Playing hero,’” Bram echoed. “That’s rich coming from a man who thinks schedules are moral philosophy.” “Production keeps people alive,” Tallec said. “So does not crushing them,” Jhace said. “This conversation is over,” Tallec said, like he had the authority to end reality. “Was it a conversation?” Bram asked. “Felt more like a threat with paperwork attached.”

Jhace signed the registry with a hand that wouldn’t hold still. When it was done, the clerk rolled the papers and left as if the room wasn’t full of broken bodies and hard choices. Tallec lingered in the doorway. “Next time you hold,” he said to Jhace, “you’ll do it where the Crown tells you.” Jhace met his eyes. “Next time I hold, it’ll be because something is falling. Not because you want it to stay standing.” Tallec laughed and walked away. The grid‑hum under the floor kept its doubled harmonic. The doubled harmonic kept humming under the floor like a threat that didn’t need words. ---




CHAPTER 11: HUNGER

The hunger wasn’t metaphor. It lived behind Jhace’s sternum as a warmth that never fully left after the first hold. A phantom heat that flared whenever the grid‑hum dipped, whenever dust fell, whenever someone screamed the word hold like it was prayer. Tiffani called it activation pressure. Jhace called it wanting. He sat beside Daved’s cot in the infirmary again, watching the older man’s hands twitch in sleep. Daved’s eyes opened. He didn’t need a slate this time. His voice was still rough, but it existed. “Feels… good,” Daved rasped. Jhace frowned. “What does?” “The start,” Daved said. “Chest heat. When it locks in. For a heartbeat you feel… useful. Strong. Like the city can’t touch you.” Jhace’s tremor worsened, like his body agreed. “And then,” Daved continued, eyes flat, “it takes you apart.” Tiffani wrote without looking up. “Addictive response mixed with autonomic threat. Noted.” Jhace frowned. “What does.” “The reinforcement loop,” Tiffani said. “Chest heat, perceived control, reduced panic during manifestation. It becomes desire.” Daved’s eyes narrowed. “Liar.” Jhace’s head snapped. “What.” “You want it,” Daved said. “The hold. You want to feel it again.” “I don’t want to chase the rumor.” “You want it anyway.” Jhace didn’t answer. His tremor did. “That want will kill you faster than the hold,” Daved said. “He is correct,” Tiffani said. “The want is not evidence. It is impulse.” Jhace’s voice went raw. “I want to hold without paying. I want to save every time the bell rings and not come apart.” “Hungry hands hold until they break,” Daved said. “Then we do not feed it,” Tiffani said. “We constrain it with protocol.” “Protocol doesn’t stop bells,” Jhace said. “No,” Tiffani said. “It stops stupidity.” Daved’s mouth twitched. “Sometimes. Not always.” “Then we decide what we sacrifice,” Tiffani said. “The Crown can wait. The Temple can wait. The Guild can wait.” Jhace’s gaze stayed forward. “We decide.” “And when you decide wrong?” Daved asked. “We document it,” Tiffani said. “We learn. We don’t repeat.” “Some mistakes don’t give you time to learn,” Daved said.

Outside, a bell rang—different cadence. Not collapse. A drill signal. Crown‑mandated training. Jhace’s chest heat flared anyway, eager and stupid. He hated himself for that flare. He stood, hands shaking, and went to the training yard because refusal would make them criminals. Tiffani followed, slate in hand, limping slightly from too many stands on stone floors. Daved watched them go and muttered, almost too quiet to hear: “Hungry hands hold until they break.” Jhace pretended he didn’t hear. He did. ---




CHAPTER 12: METHOD

The alert came late. Like an insult.

It was a narrow failure—no mass collapse, no Hall, no warehouse full of people.

A residential stairwell in Sector Nine. A narrow stone throat that had been patched too many times since the Piercing. A single child trapped beneath a fallen landing slab, crying until her voice turned hoarse.

Tiffani arrived first. Her limp was worse now, but she moved like it didn’t matter. She scanned the structure in three heartbeats. Load paths. Weak seams. The slab’s weight distribution.

“Clear lane,” she ordered as the others reached the site. “Spotters assigned. Wedges first.”

She pointed left, then froze. “No—right. Right corridor first. Sorry.”

The apology tasted like rust. She didn’t repeat it.

Jhace’s hands shook so hard he had to jam them into his coat pockets to stop them from betraying him in front of the crowd.

He could feel the doubled harmonic through the stairwell stone. Deep. Constant. The grid had never recovered. It had just learned to fail slower.

Bram arrived with Crown guards and a face that said he’d tried, briefly, to be serious and failed on contact.

“Registry protocol,” he said. “Two licensed operators inbound.”

Tiffani didn’t look at him. “They’ll be too late.”

“Cool,” Bram said. “Love the part where the Crown schedules rescue like it’s a delivery window.”

She looked at the sagging landing slab, the trapped space beneath it, the child’s choking turning into wet silence. Her voice tightened into a blade.

“One operator. One-twenty heartbeats max. We brace, we lift, we extract. Terminate immediately after.”

“Then who?” Jhace said.

“Not you.”

“Then who.”

“The child is choking, Jhace.”

Jhace’s tremor rattled against his bones like a tool trying to escape. “I decide.”

Bram’s breath punched out. “Fuck—no, don’t—okay, you’re doing it. You’re doing the thing. Great. Fantastic. I hate this.”

Jhace stepped in. Copper flooded his mouth like blood.

For a heartbeat, nothing happened.

Then the hold locked.

The stairwell’s doubled harmonic snapped into alignment with his chest, like a collar seating in the wrong notch.

Tiffani’s voice cut clean through the noise. “Wedges—now. Lift on my count. Three heartbeats. Pull.”

Boots scraped. Wedges hammered. Stone complained.

“Three,” Tiffani said. “Two. One. Now—pull!”

Hands reached into the dark. The child came out gasping, eyes wide, spit and dust and life.

“Terminate,” Tiffani said.

Jhace didn’t move.

“Terminate,” she repeated, like command could override biology.

Silence.

Bram’s voice cracked into the gap. “He’s not moving.”

“Jhace.” Tiffani stepped in close. “Move.”

Bram hovered like he wanted to shake him and knew better. “Say something. Blink. Spit. Insult me. Anything.”

“Stop talking,” Tiffani snapped.

“I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s how I pray.”

Jhace could still see. Still hear. Still count.

He stared at his hands, waiting for tremor.

There was no tremor.

There was nothing.

He tried to will a finger. Nothing.

Panic hit—animal, immediate, nothing to do with heat or holding.

He tried to shout. Nothing left his throat.

He tried to jerk a shoulder. Nothing.

He tried to blink faster—signal—anything.

His lids moved on their own schedule. Slow. Unbothered.

The body was there. The controls were there. The link was gone.

The coal behind his sternum pulsed once. Steady. Patient. Still ready.

Ready to hold.

But he couldn’t lift a finger to tell it no.

Only the wanting stayed—locked in meat that wouldn’t answer.

Tiffani moved into his field of view and knelt like she was inspecting a fracture line. Ink and copper sat on her hands; she smelled like slate dust and too long without sleep.

“Blink once for yes,” she said. “Twice for no.”

He blinked once.

“Do you still feel the doubled harmonic through the stone?”

Once.

“Can you tell me where it dipped before Tallow Street?”

Once. He could see it like a chart behind his eyes—deep note, then the metallic taste, then the split.

Tiffani slid her slate into his line of sight. Two columns. LOCATION. HEARTBEATS. Her writing stayed precise even as her knuckles whitened.

“Can you predict where it fails next?”

Once. Not a comfort. A capability.

Tiffani’s mouth tightened. “Then you’re not finished.” She didn’t say it like hope. She said it like reassignment.

Jhace stared at his hands, waiting for tremor. Nothing.

It was worse than shaking. Silence in the muscles felt like a lie.

Tiffani dropped to her knees beside him, slate already out, chalk already moving.

“Stage Four,” she said, voice flat because panic was a luxury. “Complete motor loss. Locked hold with autonomic override. Cost: total paralysis below manifestation threshold.”

Bram swallowed. “You’re writing this down.”

“Yes.”

“Right now.”

“Yes.”

“While he’s—”

“Yes,” Tiffani said, and her hand didn’t shake. “Because if I don’t, this becomes a story someone else gets to tell. And they will tell it wrong.”

She swallowed once, hard, then kept writing.

When the crowd noise finally thinned and the guards stopped pretending they weren’t watching, Tiffani stood.

“Corlexi will get what I give him,” she said.

Bram blinked. “Which is what, exactly—an instruction manual with a hidden knife?”

“A method,” Tiffani said. “With constraints. With brakes. Without permission to repeat this.”

“So… a leash on the leash,” Bram said.

“Something like that.”

“You’re terrifying when you’re calm,” Bram said.

“I’m not calm,” Tiffani said.

“Right,” Bram said, voice softer than his jokes. “You’re just… controlled. Like a blade.”

---




CHAPTER 13: THE LEDGER

Tiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still. It wasn’t Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you. The ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear. She made three piles. Four, if she was honest. One stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership. One stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it. One stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated. The last pile wasn’t for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud. Bram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he’d stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him. “You look like a priest,” Tiffani said without looking up. Bram glanced down at the coat. “Don’t you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I’m doing it recreationally.” “You’re late.” “Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.” He leaned in, saw the piles. “Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment.” Tiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. “This goes to Corlexi.” Bram’s fingers hovered. “This the ‘please don’t leash us’ letter?” “This is the ‘you get what I give you’ packet,” she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. “Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no.” Bram squinted. “And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Not included.” Bram’s smile went sharp. “That’s either genius or suicidal.” “It’s survivable.” She turned the next page over and wrote a title line in the margin: DEPLOYMENT AUTHORITY. Then she crossed it out and rewrote: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.” Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.” “There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.” She pushed the next stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification. Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.” “This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.” Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.” “They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.” Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.” Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.” Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers. “Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.” Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.” Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?” “Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.” Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage. Chloen read the first line. YOU CAN SAY NO. She looked up. “That’s new.” “It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.” Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.” And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD. Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.” Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.” Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.” Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it. The door clicked. The room held its breath. Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen. “For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.” Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.” Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.” Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult. Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.” Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.” Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?” Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm. “Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.” Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.” “It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.” Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?” “Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.” Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.” He left. Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.” “He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.” Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?” Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.” She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred. “Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge. Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission. Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.” “I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said. Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing. Tiffani didn’t move. She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.

--- CODA: GLASS IN THE HAND Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them. He’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can't spit out. Stone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn't reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule. Someone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive. Jhace tried to sleep. Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him. He flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking. Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place. Jhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed. “I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest. Outside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short. Jhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.

--- CODA: THE SHARD Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them. Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job. A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling. Then the breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive. They didn't speak. They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for multiple Tides arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.

--- END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS ---

---




CODA

Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them. He’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can't spit out. Stone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn't reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule. Someone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive. Jhace tried to sleep. Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him. He flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking. Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place. Jhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed. “I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest. Outside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short. Jhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.

The guard’s breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive. They didn't speak. They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for multiple Tides arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.

*END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS*

[ASSISTANT]
Yes—I see what Chapters 1–2 are *doing* now, and I also see the exact spots where the reader can get lost or think you contradicted yourself.

## What the reader currently believes after Ch1–Ch2
- **Jhace’s belief (street-truth):** something *buried* is erupting out on the plain. A “rim-rise” that reads like a wall because dust hides the base and the near face is vertical enough at distance.  
- **Tiffani’s belief (instrument-truth):** something **passed through Te’Oga** in a straight line and the sky got torn where it left. The stars and her reference baseline don’t lie.

That’s not contradictory. It’s two **layers of truth** with different access to information.

The contradiction happens when the prose accidentally gives Jhace *too much cosmic certainty* (or gives Tiffani *too much visual omniscience*), instead of keeping their knowledge gates clean.

## Lock logic, but keep rhythm variable
You can “lock” Chapters 1–2 because they are doing stable, repeatable jobs:

### Chapter 1 job (Jhace)
- Alarm → movement → **first sight of Exit Face + Rift** → immediate local consequence → involuntary first hold → cost signal (shake begins).

### Chapter 2 job (Tiffani)
- Baseline ritual → deviation → **instrument proves reality changed** → she gets to street → she turns chaos into corridor math → she witnesses sustained hold + gives “Terminate.”

That structure is solid. Rhythm can vary inside it (long breaths at reveal beats, short bursts during danger beats) without changing the event order.

## The specific “fix list” you flagged (brainstorm, not drafting)
### 1) Jhace should think “buried thing,” not “pierced through”
Make Jhace’s internal question *local and plausible*:
- “What in the Underface could push the plain up like that?”
- “What chamber broke?”
- “What pressure throws stone into a ring?”

He can notice the **Rift** and **wind pull** and still not connect “straight-through line.” The Rift can read as “sky damage” caused by the Exit Face, not proof of a pass-through.

### 2) Tiffani uses stars, but now they’re misaligned because the axis changed
This is a powerful way to remove “mystery box” confusion fast—**not by exposition**, but by letting her baseline be violated in a way any reader understands:

- She had a stable map.  
- The map no longer matches the sky.  
- Therefore Te’Oga’s orientation changed.

Important: she doesn’t need to *see the entire spindle forever*. She only needs:
- A reference star pattern that used to land in known positions.
- A drift that is **not instrument error**.
- A single confirmed “line transit” event across her lens view.

That gives her enough to conclude: “the world got kicked.”

### 3) The “wall” read
Your current Chapter 1 already improved this with **bands/layers/curvature**. If you want it to feel less like a literal wall:
- Keep the first impression as “wall.”
- Immediately follow with one **disqualifying detail**: *it curves* and *it’s layered strata*, not masonry.

That’s it. One disqualifier is all you need to keep readers from thinking you’re trying to sell them a rectangular monolith.

### 4) Tremor timing (remove pre-first-use tremor)
Clean rule for Chapters 1–2:
- **No tremor before the first involuntary hold.**
- The shake starts **after release** (or in the breath right after the slab drop), as a cost marker.

If you want foreshadowing before the hold, use **non-tremor** tells:
- heat behind the sternum
- breath hitch
- chest pressure
- fingertip numbness
- teeth ache from the deeper vibration

### 5) Remove spit/swallow lines entirely
Easy: don’t “manage” the copper taste. Just let it sit as a symptom. The reader will track it.

### 6) Tiffani’s hum tic
Works—keep it **only in her POV**, and make it a processing reflex:
- starts when she’s classifying/deciding
- cuts off the instant she notices herself doing it
- never becomes “cute,” never becomes a song

## Direct “WTF happened” explanation (Te’Oga terms, simple-reader version)
If you need a clean, reader-proof understanding of the Piercing that still respects that **Jhace doesn’t fully grasp it yet**, here’s the simple model you’re building toward:

- A colossal, spindle-shaped mass strikes Te’Oga at an ocean entry point and **does not stop**.  
- It drives through stone like a drill through soft metal—straight path, no pause.  
- On the far side, the Exit Face forms: the ground doesn’t just crack—**it heaves upward into a ringed rise** as pressure and displaced strata buckle and pile around the opening.  
- When the spindle breaches the sky above the Exit Face, the sky **tears open into the Rift**—a clean wound where black space is visible in open Kindling-light.  
- The throat (Exit Face) becomes a vent: air pressure equalizes through it in pulses, so wind pulls toward it and sometimes punches outward hard enough to shove dust, cloth, and breath.  
- Nobody dies from “being struck by the spindle” because nobody is hit by it directly; people die (or almost die) from **what the city does after**: structures failing, slabs dropping, bad bracing, panic compression, secondary collapse.

That explanation can be *true* even while Chapter 1 stays street-confused and Chapter 2 stays instrument-certain.

## One more continuity constraint you mentioned
“Tsunami later”: perfect. Don’t pay it off in 1–2. Just seed:
- the throat “breathing”
- suspended water behaving wrong near the Exit Face
- the idea that flow paths are compromised

Then you cash the ocean consequence in a later chapter without bloating the opener.

## What I need from you to keep continuity tight (no drafting request, just input)
Answer these with one line each:
1) Axis shift: does Te’Oga go from “tilted” to “near straight” instantly on Tide 0, or does it settle over multiple pulses?  
2) Does Tiffani **conclude axis shift** in Chapter 2, or only log “star map invalid” and refuse to name cause yet?  
3) Particulate name preference vibe: practical (workers name) or clinical (Tiffani name)? Example options:
- practical: **coppergrit**, **gold-fines**, **sparkdust**
- clinical: **Cu-Au fines**, **conductive particulate**, **rift-fall**

If you answer those, Chapters 1–2 can be “locked” for event logic without you having to rewrite the whole damn spine later.

[TOOL]
# DOCX reading, creation, and review guidance

## Reading DOCXs
- Use `soffice -env:UserInstallation=file:///tmp/lo_profile_$$ --headless --convert-to pdf --outdir $OUTDIR $INPUT_DOCX` to convert DOCXs to PDFs.
  - The `-env:UserInstallation=file:///tmp/lo_profile_$$` flag is important. Otherwise, it will time out.
- Then Convert the PDF to page images so you can visually inspect the result:
  - `pdftoppm -png $OUTDIR/$BASENAME.pdf $OUTDIR/$BASENAME`
- Then open the PNGs and read the images.
- Only do python printing as a last resort because you will miss important details with text extraction (e.g. figures, tables, diagrams).

## Primary tooling for creating DOCXs
- Create and edit DOCX files with `python-docx`. Use it to control structure, styles, tables, and lists. Install it with `pip install python-docx` if it's not already installed.
- After **every meaningful batch of edits**—new sections, layout tweaks, styling changes—render the DOCX to PDF:
  - `soffice -env:UserInstallation=file:///tmp/lo_profile_$$ --headless --convert-to pdf --outdir $OUTDIR $INPUT_DOCX`
- Convert the PDF to page images so you can visually inspect the result:
  - `pdftoppm -png $OUTDIR/$BASENAME.pdf $OUTDIR/$BASENAME`
- **Inspect every PNG before moving on.** If you see any defect, fix the DOCX and repeat the render → inspect loop until all pages look perfect.


## Quality expectations
- Aim for a client-ready document: consistent typography, spacing, margins, and layout hierarchy. Heading levels should be obvious, lists aligned, and paragraphs easy to scan.
- Never ship obvious formatting defects such as clipped or overlapping text, default-template styling, broken tables, unreadable characters, or inconsistent bullet styling.
- Charts, tables, and visuals must be legible in the rendered PNGs—no pixelation, misalignment, missing labels, or mismatched colors.
- Never use the U+2011 non-breaking hyphen or other unicode dashes as they will not be rendered correctly. Use ASCII hyphens instead.
- Citations, references, and footnotes must be human-readable and professional. No tool-internal tokens (e.g., `[145036110387964†L158-L160]`), malformed URLs, or placeholder text should be present in the document.
- You must convert all citations into a human-readable format in the document with standard scholarly citation format. No `【【turn1541736113682297662view0†L11-L19】` notations are allowed in the document as the reader cannot interpret them (such citations will be severely penalized).
- Content should be concise, relevant, and free of boilerplate AI phrasing. Ensure each section adds value and flows logically.

## Final checks
- Re-run the DOCX → PDF → PNG loop after your final changes and inspect **every page** at 100% zoom. Look for subtle issues like inconsistent spacing, widows/orphans, or misaligned bullet levels.
- Correct every formatting defect you see in the PNGs, including but not limited to: overlapping text or shapes, clipped text or shapes that are cut off, black squares, broken tables, unreadable characters, etc.
- Only deliver the DOCX once the latest PNG review confirms the document is visually flawless and professionally styled.
- Keep intermediate files organized (or cleaned up) so reviewers can easily locate final outputs.

from docx import Document
from docx.text.paragraph import Paragraph
import re, os, copy, math, textwrap, json, datetime
path="/mnt/data/N01_GripThatHolds_Formatted.docx"
doc=Document(path)

[ASSISTANT]
forbidden_patterns = [
    r"\bEarth\b", r"\bday\b", r"\bdays\b", r"\bweek\b", r"\bweeks\b",
    r"\bmonth\b", r"\bmonths\b", r"\byear\b", r"\byears\b", r"\bhour\b", r"\bhours\b",
    r"\bminute\b", r"\bminutes\b", r"\bsecond\b", r"\bseconds\b"
]
# also "daylight" and "midday"?
extra_patterns=[r"\bdaylight\b", r"\bmidday\b", r"\bmidnight\b"]
pat=re.compile("|".join(forbidden_patterns+extra_patterns), re.IGNORECASE)

hits=[]
for i,p in enumerate(doc.paragraphs):
    if pat.search(p.text):
        hits.append((i,p.text))
len(hits), hits[:10]

[TOOL]
(1,
 [(149,
   "Tiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still. It wasn’t Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you. The ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear. She made three piles. Four, if she was honest. One stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership. One stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it. One stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated. The last pile wasn’t for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud. Bram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he’d stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him. “You look like a priest,” Tiffani said without looking up. Bram glanced down at the coat. “Don’t you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I’m doing it recreationally.” “You’re late.” “Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.” He leaned in, saw the piles. “Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment.” Tiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. “This goes to Corlexi.” Bram’s fingers hovered. “This the ‘please don’t leash us’ letter?” “This is the ‘you get what I give you’ packet,” she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. “Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no.” Bram squinted. “And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Not included.” Bram’s smile went sharp. “That’s either genius or suicidal.” “It’s survivable.” She turned the next page over and wrote a title line in the margin: DEPLOYMENT AUTHORITY. Then she crossed it out and rewrote: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.” Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.” “There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.” She pushed the next stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification. Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.” “This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.” Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.” “They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.” Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.” Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.” Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers. “Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.” Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.” Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?” “Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.” Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage. Chloen read the first line. YOU CAN SAY NO. She looked up. “That’s new.” “It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.” Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.” And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD. Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.” Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.” Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.” Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it. The door clicked. The room held its breath. Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen. “For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.” Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.” Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.” Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult. Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.” Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.” Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?” Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm. “Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.” Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.” “It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.” Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?” “Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.” Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.” He left. Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.” “He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.” Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?” Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.” She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred. “Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge. Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission. Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.” “I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said. Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing. Tiffani didn’t move. She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.")])

It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you. The ink pooled anyway. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it. One stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated. The last pile wasn’t for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. spit_hits=[]
for i,p in enumerate(doc.paragraphs):
    if re.search(r"\bspit|swallow|swallowed\b", p.text, re.IGNORECASE):
        spit_hits.append((i,p.text))
len(spit_hits), spit_hits[:20]

   "Brother Corran didn’t wear Temple colors. The first thing Jhace noticed was the badge—bright, clean, pinned where it couldn't be missed. Next was how the man didn't look tired. Not even a little. Corran’s eyes went straight to Jhace’s hands. “For the record, I’m not here to stop you,” Corran said, before anyone asked. Bram hovered by the door like a lock that wanted to be useful. Tiffani set her slate down deliberately. “Then why are you here?” “Because you’re real,” Corran said. “And because real things attract predators.” Jhace’s mouth tightened. “Crown.” Corran’s gaze didn’t flinch. “Yes. Also Guild. Also Temple. Anyone who thinks they can turn you into a lever.” Bram’s voice came dry. “Ah yes. The holy trinity of getting fucked: prayer, production, paperwork.” Tiffani shot him a look. “Bram.” He raised his hands. “What? I’m helping.” Bram gestured toward the door. “Temple says you’re divine. Guild says you’re labor. Crown says you’re property. Everybody’s trying to fuck the same lever.” He pulled a folded packet from his coat and set it on Jhace’s bench. Paper. Old style. Archive weight. “Stage Three cases,” Corran said. “Documented. Not rumors.” Tiffani’s hands went still. “How many?” “Enough.” Corran’s voice was flat, and that flatness carried exhaustion. “Most die. A few don’t. The ones who don’t… wish they had.” Jhace’s tremor ticked up at the word die. He forced his hands flat on the bench. “Why give this to us?” Corran hesitated. That hesitation was the crack in the Crown. “Because someone above me wants a registry,” he said. “Names. Locations. Deployment authority. They’re calling it order. It’s control.” Tiffani’s eyes narrowed. “Who?” Corran looked toward the door as if the walls might report him. “Administrator Corlexi. Infrastructure Compliance.” Bram gave a low whistle. “Clean hands. Clean coat. Probably wipes his ass with forms.” Jhace stared at the packet. “So you’re here to warn us.” “I’m here to give you data,” Corran corrected. “If you want to fight control, you need numbers. Not speeches.” Tiffani opened the packet and read, eyes moving fast. Jhace watched her face tighten at certain lines—durations, costs, outcomes. She didn’t gasp. She didn’t dramatize. She just absorbed. “This case,” she said, tapping a page. “Stage Three survival. How?” Corran’s mouth went thin. “He didn’t terminate. He couldn’t. The hold locked. Autonomic failure started and the manifestation kept running anyway. The structure held until it didn’t matter.” Jhace felt cold crawl up his spine despite the workshop heat. “Locked hold.” Corran nodded. “It’s not a tool you put down when you’re done. Sometimes it’s a hook in your chest that drags you until something breaks.” Tiffani’s pen scratched. Stage Three: autonomic failure. Locked hold possible. Termination may fail. Bram shifted. “You said predators.” Corran’s eyes met Bram’s. “Tallec is filing complaints. Says you’re obstructing industry. Says your protocols slow production.” Jhace’s jaw clenched so hard it hurt. Corran leaned closer, voice low. “Here’s the fracture you wanted. I don’t like Corlexi’s registry. I don’t like Temple’s sermons. I don’t like Guild’s schedules. But I like fewer dead.” He straightened. “If you build methodology, build it fast. Because the Crown is already drafting theirs. And theirs won’t care what it costs you.” He turned to leave. Tiffani called after him. “Why risk telling us this?” Corran paused at the door. “Because Junction Seven wasn’t the first ignored report,” he said. “And I’m tired of pretending ignorance is the same as innocence.” Then he was gone. Jhace’s hands stayed flat on the workbench. “If this registry happens…” He swallowed. “I’m not doing it for paper.” Bram exhaled slowly. “And because guilt is cheaper than funerals. Not by much. But cheaper.” Tiffani climbed the workshop stairs slower than she had a Tide ago. Her right knee caught on the third step—not pain, just resistance, like the joint was learning to refuse. She stopped on the landing. Put her palm to the wall until the world steadied. The knee had locked twice this Anneal. Not during a hold. During stairs. During the parts where bodies were supposed to cooperate. She’d logged operator tremor. Operator vein-lines. Operator autonomic failure. She had not logged her own joint learning to refuse weight. Because if she logged it, she’d have to adjust. Delegate. Sit down. And if she sat down, someone else would coordinate from the deck. Someone else would make the calls. She took the next step anyway. She didn’t log it. Some costs weren’t manifestation. Some were just labor. Jhace’s hands shook on the bench like they were trying to answer him."),
  (36,
   'Merchant Hall was a cathedral for trade: iron ribs, stone spine, glass skylights that made everything inside look clean even when it wasn’t. And it smelled like bodies and money and fear—the kind of fear that tries to wear perfume. It started failing at mid‑Tide. The bell sequence came sharp and frantic—no warning cadence, no time to wedge first. The Hall had been packed when the terrace shifted again. Four hundred inside, Bram said. Maybe more. Tiffani arrived before Jhace. When he pushed through the crowd, she was already on the steps, slate in hand, shouting like a foreman who’d learned math. “Operators on me. Two per span. Spotters assigned. No hero holds.” “Duration limit. One-eighty heartbeats maximum. You terminate on my signal. No argument.” Merra shoved through the dust with a slate clutched to her chest. Ash streaked her hair like she’d tried to comb through a fire. “We have a problem. Southern stairwell collapsed. Fifty people trapped on upper floors. Can’t reach main exits.” “Time estimate,” Tiffani said. “Low and high.” “Unknown. Debris blocking secondary stairs. Could be two hundred heartbeats. Could be six hundred.” “Then we don’t gamble.” Tiffani didn’t raise her voice. She didn’t have to. “Clear lanes. Count bodies out. You want prayer, pray while you walk.” She swung her slate toward the operators. “Corven—baseline check. When chest heat starts, report it. Don’t interpret. Report.” Corven swallowed. “If it hits, I tell you. If I taste copper, I tell you.” Jhace didn’t look at him. “Don’t spiral. Do the minimum.” “Minimum,” Corven echoed, like repetition could make it true. “Senne—status.” “Getting hollow,” Senne said. “Chest feels empty. Light’s harder to maintain.” “That’s Stage Two warning.” Tiffani’s eyes cut sharp. “You terminate on my signal or I terminate you myself.” Senne gave her a tired half-smile. “Sure. Die on schedule. Very professional.” “Kess—report.” “I am present and available for assignment.” “Available doesn’t mean useful. Report symptoms. Stop performing.” “Symptoms are within acceptable parameters for operational requirements.” Bram leaned in, voice too bright for the amount of people about to die. “Translation: Kess is bleeding and pretending it’s fashion.” “I am not bleeding.” Merra didn’t bother being gentle. “Kess—your nose is bleeding.” “I am aware of the nasal discharge. It is minor. Barely noteworthy. I’ve had worse nosebleeds from seasonal changes.” “Yeah,” Bram said. “And I’ve had worse ideas than this hold. Doesn’t make them good.” From the crowd, a Temple Observer screamed, “Let the worthy hold! Let the unworthy fall!” Bram snapped his head toward the sound. “Hey—shut up. If we’re picking who falls, you’re first. That’s democracy.” “Ignore them,” Tiffani said. “Move.” “Spotters, call markers.” “Jhace tremor increasing!” “Corven tremor increasing!” “Senne vein-line onset!” Bram barked at the evac line like a foreman with a broken sense of humor. “Keep moving! Nobody stops! You stop, you become part of the debris! Count them out!” Merra’s voice cut through the dust. “Alternate route open. Final evacuation commencing.” “Count them,” Tiffani said. “Call numbers.” Jhace’s breath came tight. “One hundred.” “State exit count,” Tiffani said, “not your suffering.” “One hundred. Out.” Daved’s voice came rough through the grit. “They’re not out.” Jhace’s head snapped. “No.” “They’re not out.” “Daved—state the variable,” Tiffani said. “How many still inside?” “Enough.” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Extend. Thirty heartbeats. No more.” “Tiff—” Jhace started. “I am not debating physics with the dead in the hallway,” she said. “Extend.” Thirty heartbeats passed like a slow knife. Merra shouted from the corridor mouth, “Final group emerging. Ten heartbeats!” “Ten heartbeats is still ten,” Tiffani said. “Move.” Daved’s hands tightened. “Hold.” “Daved!” Jhace lunged a step closer. “Hold.” Merra’s slate slapped stone. “Clear! All civilians safe!” “Terminate all operators,” Tiffani snapped. “Immediate coordinated shutdown.” Lights guttered and went out—one by one—except Daved’s. “Can’t,” Daved said. “Daved. Breathe,” Jhace said, too close, too desperate. “Can’t.” “Terminate,” Tiffani said. “That is not a negotiation.” “They’re not out.” “They are out. Merra confirmed. Terminate now.” Daved’s jaw worked like he was chewing copper. “Already crossed.” Bram shoved his way in with words because silence was making everyone honest. “Daved—buddy—love the commitment, hate the method acting.” “Bram, shut up,” Jhace hissed. “I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s a disorder.” Tiffani stepped in until Daved had to see her. “Look at me. If you extend past this, you cross Stage Three threshold.” Daved’s eyes stayed flat. “Already crossed.” Jhace’s throat tightened. “He locked.” “I see it,” Tiffani said. They hauled Daved back from the structure like pulling a hook out of meat. “Terminate!” Tiffani shouted. The light finally cut. Daved folded like a tool dropped from numb hands. Tiffani’s voice went small for one syllable. “I know.”'),
