  (150,
   "--- CODA: GLASS IN THE HAND Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them. He’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can't spit out. Stone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn't reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule. Someone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive. Jhace tried to sleep. Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him. He flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking. Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place. Jhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed. “I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest. Outside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short. Jhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back."),
  (151,
   "--- CODA: THE SHARD Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them. Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job. A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling. Then the breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive. They didn't speak. They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for multiple Tides arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky."),
  (156,
   "Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them. He’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can't spit out. Stone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him. He flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking. Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place. Jhace swallowed.    "The guard’s breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive. They didn't speak. They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. [ASSISTANT]
from docx.oxml import OxmlElement
from docx.oxml.ns import qn

def delete_paragraph(paragraph):
    p = paragraph._element
    p.getparent().remove(p)
    paragraph._p = paragraph._element = None

# find chapter boundaries
paras=doc.paragraphs
# indices of chapter headings
chapter_indices=[]
for i,p in enumerate(paras):
    if re.match(r"CHAPTER\s+\d+:", p.text.strip(), re.IGNORECASE):
        chapter_indices.append(i)
chapter_indices[:10], len(chapter_indices)

([2, 8, 14, 19, 24, 31, 35, 42, 48, 52], 13)

[ASSISTANT]
chapter1_text = """CHAPTER 1: THE EXIT FACE

The bell rang different. Wrong.

Jhace didn’t stop to listen for the pattern. Chalk down. Slate left open. Kit strap already in his hand. Moving first. Thinking after.

Pipe-thrum lived under every brace in the quarter—familiar, steady. This vibration ran deeper. Slow. Heavy. Not traveling through pipes so much as through the stone itself.

He hooked the kit over his shoulder and yanked the collar keys off the peg.

Another ring. Still wrong.

“Shit.”

Keys into his palm. Sorted by feel while he jogged. Ladder shaft taken two steps at a time.

The bell answered again.

Then another. Multiple streets.

Same urgency, different locations.

The ladder spit him into the service throat behind the terraces—tight stone on both sides, pipework overhead, air already thickening with dust that turned lamplight dull. People pushed past—some running, some walking like their joints had locked.

Jhace caught a forearm and steadied an older man before he went down.

“What?” he barked.

No answer. Just a point—arm shaking toward the tunnel mouth like the man couldn’t stop it.

Jhace let go and kept going.

The bells rang fast now. Uneven. Overlapping.

Tallow Street opened around him—shops half-shuttered, tools dropped where hands abandoned them, bodies clustered with heads tilted the same way.

No one looked at each other.

Everyone stared past the terraces.

Jhace followed their line.

Something had risen on the plain.

At first glance it read as a wall—too tall, too broad, too vertical from this distance. Dust and haze hid the base. The near face looked smooth in patches and jagged in others.

Then his eye caught the detail that didn’t fit “wall.”

Bands. Layers. Familiar strata shoved upward and turned wrong—light stone over dark, dark over light, seams exposed like the city had been cut and lifted. The mass didn’t stop left or right. It curved, disappearing into the dust as it wrapped away from sight.

A ring. A rim-rise. Not built. Forced.

Buried, then driven up. By what, he couldn’t name.

Wind tugged toward it. Not a normal breeze—steady pull that carried grit low across the street, as if the plain had become a drain.

Jhace’s ears popped once.

Then he saw the sky.

Kindling-light over Saint Merro—normal and bright—except for the Rift. A clean tear in the open blue where black space showed through. Stars sat inside it, sharp and wrong for Kindling.

The Rift’s edge shimmered.

The rim-rise kept climbing toward it.

The pull of wind shifted again—stronger, then uneven, as if it came in pulses.

Near the rim, water hung in the air. Too much of it. Too high. Not falling. Suspended in heavy sheets and beads that didn’t know what to do next.

A hard push of air hit the street.

Not a gust. A pressure change that shoved dust and cloth and breath in the same direction. The crowd made one sound—half scream, half inhale—and the street seemed to sway under it.

Then the suspended water dropped.

Not a clean pour. It broke apart on the way down—spray, mist, and heavy slaps that hit shutters, stone, shoulders. Salt and grit with it.

The impact rolled through the terraces hard enough to rattle iron and split tired mortar. Tiles skittered. Hinges chattered.

Copper hit Jhace’s mouth. Sharp. Immediate.

Stone answered again, closer.

Up on the terrace edge, a building shed façade. A slab tore loose and started down toward the street where bodies were still moving too slow.

Jhace’s arm came up without permission. Palm out. A load-shift reflex, useless and automatic.

His chest locked.

A pull snapped tight behind his sternum. Breath cut off. Something inside him hooked to the falling stone—line-tension without a line, anchored to mass and gravity.

The slab stopped.

Caught. Held in open air.

Jhace couldn’t move.

He tried to pull back. The pull tightened—exact and punishing—answering resistance with more force.

Dust hung. Pebbles drifted instead of dropping. Street noise broke into pieces—boots, coughing, someone calling a name and failing to finish it.

People ran under the suspended stone.

Jhace couldn’t turn his head to track them. He only felt the structure through whatever had latched on—micro-shifts, strain, the constant pressure of weight trying to win.

A small body stumbled out last. Child-small. Dust-white. Alive.

As soon as the child cleared the shadow, the tension inside Jhace slipped.

The slab dropped.

It hit the street with a crack that shook the quarter. The shock threw Jhace backward hard. He hit stone. Air gone. Vision bright at the edges.

Copper surged again.

He pushed up on one elbow.

His hands were steady for a single breath.

Then they started shaking.

Fine vibration under the skin. Small. Constant. Not enough to stop gross movement. Enough to ruin precision.

He clenched a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.

The shake stayed.

A woman stood a few steps away, dust clinging to her cloak. Hair tied back. Slate in hand. Chalk already moving while everyone else still stared at the plain and the Rift.

She didn’t look at the Rift.

She looked at Jhace’s hands.

Then at his chest.

Back to the slate.

And she started counting as if the count was the only safe thing in the street.

A bell rang again—closer. Same cadence, different throat.

Jhace looked down and realized he was gripping empty air.

He moved anyway—back toward the broken lintel, back into the dust—because the load was still there and the city hadn’t finished falling.
"""

chapter2_text = """CHAPTER 2: THE PATTERN

The theodolite refused to settle.

Tiffani reset it three times since Kindling. Lens wiped clean. Base leveled. Locks seated. Reference stars acquired.

Glass stayed clear. Edge stayed true.

Crosshair drifted anyway.

The star sat wrong in the frame—off by a fraction that kept increasing.

Not just drift. A baseline failure. The sky’s map had changed.

She pressed her palm to the primary lens frame.

Vibration.

Faint. Constant. A doubled harmonic riding under the normal hum—too precise for weather, too regular for fatigue. Molars ached. Fingertips buzzed.

She pulled away and wrote before her mind could build excuses.

Tide 0 — Kindling
Instrument: stable.
Reference stars: misaligned beyond tolerance.
Star Seven-Three: westward drift beyond baseline.
Frame vibration: doubled harmonic present.
Error source: external.

A low, tuneless hum slipped out of her—barely above a whisper. Not a melody. A narrowing. The moment she caught it, it stopped.

Copper sat on her tongue.

Baseline metallic tang noted across the last two Tides. Prior attributions: ventilation residue. Pipe dust. Hot glass.

This exceeded baseline.

Light at the lens port changed.

Not dimming. Not brightening.

Bending.

She looked up.

For one breath she didn’t move. One useless sentence flashed through her head: What the fuck is that.

Then the decision tree returned.

Unknown intrusion. Linear transit. High velocity. No thermal wake observed.

A line crossed the sky above Saint Merro—straight, dark, moving too fast to track at first. Her lens stack stole horizon and bent sight around the curve, pulling distance closer than it had any right to be. The line resolved into form.

A spindle.

Pointed ends. Wide center. Surface turning as it traveled. Vein-lines inside it lit in bursts—colors flashing through brief windows of transparency, then gone.

Her hand lifted halfway without permission, palm out, a reflex without value.

The spindle crossed her view in a single breath. No roar. No fire. Only displacement—light sliding aside.

Then gravity lurched.

One hard up-down stagger. Slate skated across stone. A clamp snapped. Glass sang once and went silent.

Tiffani caught the observation frame and forced her eyes back to the port.

The spindle was gone.

Through. Below. Somewhere into stone and sea and Underface. The exact path was not visible from here.

Pressure arrived a breath later.

A city-wide inhale that didn’t finish.

Dust fell from ceiling mortar in a slow drift.

Then copper-gold grit started falling outside.

Fine. Glittering. Catching lamplight. It coated the stone lip of the port. It clung to the iron frame in flecks that shifted when she moved.

She touched it with a fingertip.

Abrasive. It bit at skin where it met sweat.

She wrote fast.

Intrusion event: spindle-form transit.
Velocity: extreme.
Grit: copper-gold, immediate post-transit.
Behavior: adheres to metal. Abrasive. Possible conduction.

Chalk slipped on the last word. Smear.

She stared at the mark. Rewrote harder until the line obeyed.

A bell rang.

Distant. Down-terrace. Fast and uneven.

Then another. Different direction. Same cadence.

The city had flipped into alarm and it wasn’t flipping back.

Slate and coat in hand. Spiral stair taken three at a time.

Street level was choking gray. Dust thick enough to blunt lamplight. Bells overlapping across quarters. Wind tugging toward the plain in steady pulls that rose and fell.

Tallow Street.

Façade folded inward. Stone slabs riding down on beams that should have carried the load. People screaming from inside the crush.

Tiffani pushed through bodies, shoulder-first. No apologies.

A foreman saw the slate and grabbed at the first thing that looked like a plan.

“You—help! Brace it! We’ve got people in there!”

She followed his line of sight.

Under the broken lintel, a man with collar keys clipped to his belt had both hands on the main support column. Dust streaked his face. Jaw locked hard.

Copper-gold filaments ran from his palms into the stone seam.

The structure had stopped moving.

Paused. Still unstable. Still dangerous.

His breath came out in short, controlled pulls. He was reading the load through his fingertips—weight, tension, micro-slip. Heat sat in his chest and flared with every shift.

“Move,” he rasped. “Get them out.”

Boots pounded. Voices called locations. Someone coughed dust and kept crawling anyway.

Tiffani stepped closer. Slate already open. Chalk moving.

She didn’t look at him like a miracle.

She looked at him like a system that could fail.

“How long until it slips?” she demanded.

His jaw flexed. “I don’t know.”

“A few breaths or many.”

“I don’t know.”

“Then we measure.” She raised her voice without screaming. “Corridor A first. Count bodies out. Call the number when you clear. No guessing.”

People came out in bursts—three, then five, then a cluster carrying an older man whose leg bent wrong.

His breathing stayed controlled. His hands didn’t shake while the filaments were running. They couldn’t afford to.

Tiffani kept her eyes on the line and her chalk on the slate. Observe. Record.

One hundred.

One hundred twenty.

His shoulders began to tremble. Load, not fear.

At two hundred, strain climbed into his chest. At two-twenty, his vision narrowed at the edges, sparks flickering in the copper-gold threads.

“How many?” he rasped.

She didn’t look up. “Ten. Eleven. Twelve—”

A scream from inside. Child. High. Thin. Cut off.

The filaments brightened hard. His palms went white around the stone.

“No,” he hissed.

“Go,” she snapped at the breach. “Last sweep. Now.”

A rescuer crawled under the paused beam line and dragged a small body out by the shoulders. The child coughed dust and cried like she’d been holding it in for a Tide.

“Twelve out,” Tiffani said. “Corridor clear.”

The structure tried to move the instant she spoke. Like it had been waiting for the count.

“Terminate,” she said.

No softness. No cruelty. Procedure.

He let the filaments go.

Stone settled its last inch. Iron squealed. Dust dumped down in a sheet. The space where bodies had been was empty.

He staggered into open air.

Then his hands started shaking.

Fine vibration under the skin. Small. Constant.

He clenched a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.

The shake stayed.

Tiffani stepped in beside him. Chalk still moving.

“Name.”

“Jhace Torrins,” he said. Copper still sharp in his mouth.

She wrote it without looking. “Duration—” Chalk snapped. She swore under her breath and finished the line anyway.

“Don’t,” he snapped. Too hard. Too fast.

She met his eyes. Flat. Measuring. Certain.

“If we don’t measure it,” she said, “we kill you next time.”

Copper sat on her tongue too.

Another bell rang down-terrace.

Different street.

Same cadence.

Tiffani marked the direction on her slate and ran. Jhace followed, shaking hands and all.
"""

[ASSISTANT]
def replace_chapter(doc, chap_num, new_text):
    paras=doc.paragraphs
    # find start index
    start=None
    for i,p in enumerate(paras):
        if re.match(rf"CHAPTER\s+{chap_num}\s*:", p.text.strip(), re.IGNORECASE):
            start=i
            break
    if start is None:
        raise ValueError("chapter start not found")
    # find next chapter start
    end=len(paras)
    for j in range(start+1,len(paras)):
        if re.match(r"CHAPTER\s+\d+\s*:", paras[j].text.strip(), re.IGNORECASE):
            end=j
            break
    # delete range start..end-1
    # delete from end-1 down to start
    for k in range(end-1, start-1, -1):
        delete_paragraph(paras[k])
    # insert new paragraphs at position start (now paragraph count changed)
    # python-docx has no direct insert at index; we can add at end then move XML
    # We'll insert by creating paragraphs at end, then move them before element at 'start' position.
    # Determine reference element: the paragraph currently at index start (after deletion) or None if at end.
    paras2=doc.paragraphs
    ref = paras2[start]._element if start < len(paras2) else None
    
    # Create paragraphs at end
    new_lines=[ln.rstrip() for ln in new_text.strip().split("\n")]
    # We want blank lines as paragraph breaks
    # We'll parse into paragraphs: consecutive non-empty lines join with newline? In manuscript, single line per para.
    new_paras=[]
    buf=[]
    for ln in new_lines:
        if ln.strip()=="":
            if buf:
                new_paras.append("\n".join(buf))
                buf=[]
        else:
            buf.append(ln)
    if buf: new_paras.append("\n".join(buf))
    
    created=[]
    for txt in new_paras:
        p=doc.add_paragraph(txt)
        created.append(p)
    # move created paragraphs to correct location
    body = doc._body._element
    for p in created:
        body.remove(p._element)
    insert_pos = ref
    for p in created:
        if insert_pos is None:
            body.append(p._element)
        else:
            body.insert(body.index(insert_pos), p._element)
    return start, end

doc2=Document(path)
replace_chapter(doc2,1,chapter1_text)
replace_chapter(doc2,2,chapter2_text)
len(doc2.paragraphs), doc2.paragraphs[0].text

(328, 'N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS')

pat=re.compile("|".join(forbidden_patterns+extra_patterns), re.IGNORECASE)
hits=[]
for i,p in enumerate(doc2.paragraphs):
    m=pat.search(p.text)
    if m:
        hits.append((i,m.group(0),p.text))
len(hits), hits[:5]

   'daylight',
   "Tiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still. It wasn’t Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you. The ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear. She made three piles. Four, if she was honest. One stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership. One stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it. One stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated. The last pile wasn’t for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud. Bram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he’d stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him. “You look like a priest,” Tiffani said without looking up. Bram glanced down at the coat. “Don’t you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I’m doing it recreationally.” “You’re late.” “Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.” He leaned in, saw the piles. “Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment.” Tiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. “This goes to Corlexi.” Bram’s fingers hovered. “This the ‘please don’t leash us’ letter?” “This is the ‘you get what I give you’ packet,” she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. “Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no.” Bram squinted. “And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Not included.” Bram’s smile went sharp. “That’s either genius or suicidal.” “It’s survivable.” She turned the next page over and wrote a title line in the margin: DEPLOYMENT AUTHORITY. Then she crossed it out and rewrote: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.” Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.” “There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.” She pushed the next stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification. Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.” “This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.” Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.” “They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.” Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.” Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.” Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers. “Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.” Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.” Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?” “Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.” Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage. Chloen read the first line. YOU CAN SAY NO. She looked up. “That’s new.” “It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.” Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.” And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD. Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.” Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.” Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.” Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it. The door clicked. The room held its breath. Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen. “For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.” Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.” Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.” Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult. Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.” Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.” Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?” Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm. “Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.” Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.” “It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.” Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?” “Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.” Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.” He left. Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.” “He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.” Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?” Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.” She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred. “Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge. Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission. Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.” “I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said. Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing. Tiffani didn’t move. She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.")])

[ASSISTANT]
def find_occ(doc, pattern):
    out=[]
    for i,p in enumerate(doc.paragraphs):
        if re.search(pattern,p.text,re.IGNORECASE):
            out.append((i,p.text))
    return out
sw=find_occ(doc2, r"\bswallow|swallowed|spit\b")
len(sw), sw[:20]

   "--- CODA: GLASS IN THE HAND Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them. He’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can't spit out. Stone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn't reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule. Someone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive. Jhace tried to sleep. Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him. He flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking. Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place. Jhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed. “I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest. Outside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short. Jhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back."),
  (320,
   "--- CODA: THE SHARD Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them. Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job. A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling. Then the breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive. They didn't speak. They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Copper flooded Jhace's mouth so fast he gagged and couldn't move enough to spit. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for multiple Tides arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky."),

 [(318,
   "Tiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still. It wasn’t Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you. The ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear. She made three piles. Four, if she was honest. One stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership. One stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it. One stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated. The last pile wasn’t for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud. Bram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he’d stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him. “You look like a priest,” Tiffani said without looking up. Bram glanced down at the coat. “Don’t you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I’m doing it recreationally.” “You’re late.” “Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.” He leaned in, saw the piles. “Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment.” Tiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. “This goes to Corlexi.” Bram’s fingers hovered. “This the ‘please don’t leash us’ letter?” “This is the ‘you get what I give you’ packet,” she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. “Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no.” Bram squinted. “And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Not included.” Bram’s smile went sharp. “That’s either genius or suicidal.” “It’s survivable.” She turned the next page over and wrote a title line in the margin: DEPLOYMENT AUTHORITY. Then she crossed it out and rewrote: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.” Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.” “There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.” She pushed the next stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification. Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.” “This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.” Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.” “They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.” Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.” Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.” Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers. “Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.” Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.” Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?” “Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.” Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage. Chloen read the first line. YOU CAN SAY NO. She looked up. “That’s new.” “It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.” Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.” And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD. Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.” Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.” Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.” Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it. The door clicked. The room held its breath. Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen. “For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.” Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.” Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.” Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult. Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.” Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.” Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?” Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm. “Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.” Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.” “It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.” Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?” “Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.” Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.” He left. Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.” “He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.” Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?” Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.” She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred. “Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge. Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission. Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.” “I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said. Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing. Tiffani didn’t move. She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.")])

"Tiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still. It wasn’t Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you. The ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear. She made three piles. Four, if she was honest. One stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership. One stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it. One stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated. The last pile wasn’t for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud. Bram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he’d stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him. “You look like a priest,” Tiffani said without looking up. Bram glanced down at the coat. “Don’t you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I’m doing it recreationally.” “You’re late.” “Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.” He leaned in, saw the piles. “Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment.” Tiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. “This goes to Corlexi.” Bram’s fingers hovered. “This the ‘please don’t leash us’ letter?” “This is the ‘you get what I give you’ packet,” she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. “Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no.” Bram squinted. “And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Not included.” Bram’s smile went sharp. “That’s either genius or suicidal.” “It’s survivable.” She turned the next page over and wrote a title line in the margin: DEPLOYMENT AUTHORITY. Then she crossed it out and rewrote: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.” Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.” “There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.” She pushed the next stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification. Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.” “This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.” Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.” “They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.” Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.” Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.” Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers. “Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.” Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.” Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?” “Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.” Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage. Chloen read the first line. YOU CAN SAY NO. She looked up. “That’s new.” “It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.” Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.” And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD. Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.” Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.” Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.” Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it. The door clicked. The room held its breath. Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen. “For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.” Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.” Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.” Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult. Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.” Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.” Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?” Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm. “Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.” Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.” “It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.” Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?” “Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.” Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.” He left. Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.” “He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.” Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?” Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.” She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred. “Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge. Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission. Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.” “I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said. Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing. Tiffani didn’t move. She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway."

[ASSISTANT]
for i,p in enumerate(doc2.paragraphs):
    m=re.search(r"\bdaylight\b", p.text, re.IGNORECASE)
    if m:
        print(i,p.text)

[TOOL]
318 Tiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still. It wasn’t Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you. The ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear. She made three piles. Four, if she was honest. One stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership. One stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it. One stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated. The last pile wasn’t for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud. Bram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he’d stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him. “You look like a priest,” Tiffani said without looking up. Bram glanced down at the coat. “Don’t you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I’m doing it recreationally.” “You’re late.” “Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.” He leaned in, saw the piles. “Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment.” Tiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. “This goes to Corlexi.” Bram’s fingers hovered. “This the ‘please don’t leash us’ letter?” “This is the ‘you get what I give you’ packet,” she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. “Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no.” Bram squinted. “And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Not included.” Bram’s smile went sharp. “That’s either genius or suicidal.” “It’s survivable.” She turned the next page over and wrote a title line in the margin: DEPLOYMENT AUTHORITY. Then she crossed it out and rewrote: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.” Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.” “There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.” She pushed the next stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification. Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.” “This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.” Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.” “They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.” Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.” Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.” Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers. “Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.” Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.” Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?” “Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.” Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage. Chloen read the first line. YOU CAN SAY NO. She looked up. “That’s new.” “It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.” Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.” And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD. Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.” Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.” Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.” Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it. The door clicked. The room held its breath. Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen. “For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.” Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.” Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.” Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult. Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.” Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.” Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?” Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm. “Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.” Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.” “It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.” Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?” “Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.” Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.” He left. I’m deciding where it jams.” Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?” Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.” She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred. “Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge. Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission. Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.” “I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said. Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing. Tiffani didn’t move. She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.

[ASSISTANT]
def scan_words(doc, words):
    found=[]
    for w in words:
        for i,p in enumerate(doc.paragraphs):
            if re.search(rf"\b{re.escape(w)}\b", p.text, re.IGNORECASE):
                found.append((w,i,p.text))
                break
    return found
scan_words(doc2, ["Earth","day","days","week","weeks","month","months","year","years","hour","hours","minute","minutes","second","seconds","daylight"])

[TOOL]
(7287,
 "Tiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still. It wasn’t Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you. The ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear. She made three piles. Four, if she was honest. One stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership. One stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it. One stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated. The last pile wasn’t for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud. Bram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he’d stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him. “You look like a priest,” Tiffani said without looking up. Bram glanced down at the coat. “Don’t you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I’m doing it recreationally.” “You’re late.” “Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.” He leaned in, saw the piles. “Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment.” Tiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. “This goes to Corlexi.” Bram’s fingers hovered. “This the ‘please don’t leash us’ letter?” “This is the ‘you get what I give you’ packet,” she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. “Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no.” Bram squinted. “And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Not included.” Bram’s smile went sharp. “That’s either genius or suicidal.” “It’s survivable.” She turned the next page over and wrote a title line in the margin: DEPLOYMENT AUTHORITY. Then she crossed it out and rewrote: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.” Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.” “There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.” She pushed the next stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification. Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.” “This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.” Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.” “They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.” Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.” Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.” Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers. “Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.” Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.” Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?” “Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.” Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage. Chloen read the first line. YOU CAN SAY NO. She looked up. “That’s new.” “It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.” Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.” And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD. Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.” Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.” Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.” Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it. The door clicked. The room held its breath. Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen. “For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.” Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.” Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.” Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult. Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.” Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.” Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?” Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm. “Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.” Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.” “It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.” Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?” “Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.” Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.” He left. Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.” “He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.” Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?” Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.” She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred. “Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge. Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission. Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.” “I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said. Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing. Tiffani didn’t move. She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.")

[ASSISTANT]
def set_paragraph_text(paragraph, text):
    # remove all runs
    for r in paragraph.runs[::-1]:
        r._element.getparent().remove(r._element)
    paragraph.add_run(text)

# regex replacements list (pattern, repl)
repls = [
    (re.compile(r"\bdaylight\b", re.IGNORECASE), "Kindling-light"),
    (re.compile(r"\bEarth\b", re.IGNORECASE), "Te’Oga"),  # just in case
    (re.compile(r"Blink\.\s*Spit\.", re.IGNORECASE), "Blink. Cuss."),
    (re.compile(r"\bspit\b", re.IGNORECASE), "cough"),  # generic, maybe too broad
]

for w,i,t in sw:
    print(i, t[:120])

[ASSISTANT]
def apply_regex_replacements(doc, regex_repls):
    changed=0
    for p in doc.paragraphs:
        original=p.text
        new=original
        for pat, repl in regex_repls:
            new = pat.sub(repl, new)
        if new != original:
            set_paragraph_text(p, new)
            changed += 1
    return changed

regex_repls = [
    (re.compile(r"\bdaylight\b", re.IGNORECASE), "Kindling-light"),
    (re.compile(r"\bspit and dust and life\b", re.IGNORECASE), "coughing dust and alive"),
    (re.compile(r"Blink\.\s*Spit\.", re.IGNORECASE), "Blink. Cuss."),
    (re.compile(r"\bBram swallowed\.", re.IGNORECASE), "Bram hesitated."),
    (re.compile(r"\bShe swallowed once, hard, then kept writing\.", re.IGNORECASE), "She locked her jaw once, hard, then kept writing."),
]
changed=apply_regex_replacements(doc2, regex_repls)
changed

   'The tremor from Tallow Street hadn\'t stopped. It had spread from his hands into his forearms, up to his shoulders. A fine, relentless vibration that made every tool on his belt rattle. The heat in his chest was still there. Banked. Waiting. A foreman ran past him toward the breach, saw his Crown inspector marks, and grabbed his shoulder hard enough to bruise. "You held Tallow," the foreman said. "Do it again." Jhace pulled free. "I don\'t—" "There\'s forty people in there," the foreman cut in. "Maybe more. Wedges won\'t hold. Straps won\'t reach. You\'re what we\'ve got." The copper taste flooded Jhace\'s mouth again. His chest heat flared. The doubled harmonic he\'d felt in the grid-hum arrived inside him—two mis-timed pulses locking into the same rhythm. His heartbeat and the failing building\'s stress pattern aligned like they were the same system. He didn\'t want this. He didn\'t understand this. He moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn\'t ignore. "Clear the approach," Jhace said, voice rough. "Get spotters. I need someone counting." He stepped under the sagging upper floor. The heat detonated behind his sternum. He put both hands on the nearest support column. Filament-light threaded from his palms into the stone—brighter this time, thicker, like the copper-gold dust in the air was feeding it. The warehouse stopped settling. The groan of stressed iron went quiet. Jhace\'s breath shook. He could feel the load distribution through his fingertips: uneven, shifting, trying to collapse in three directions at once. from docx.text.paragraph import Paragraph

[ASSISTANT]
forbidden = ["Earth","day","days","week","weeks","month","months","year","years","hour","hours","minute","minutes","second","seconds"]
# regex whole word case-insensitive
patterns = {w: re.compile(rf"\b{re.escape(w)}\b", re.IGNORECASE) for w in forbidden}
hits=[]
for i,p in enumerate(doc.paragraphs):
    t=p.text
    for w,pat in patterns.items():
        if pat.search(t):
            hits.append((i,w,t))

[ASSISTANT]
import re
forbidden = ["Earth","day","days","week","weeks","month","months","year","years","hour","hours","minute","minutes","second","seconds"]
patterns = {w: re.compile(rf"\b{re.escape(w)}\b", re.IGNORECASE) for w in forbidden}
hits=[]
for i,p in enumerate(doc.paragraphs):
    t=p.text
    for w,pat in patterns.items():
        if pat.search(t):
            hits.append((i,w,t))
len(hits), hits[:15]

[TOOL]
(0, [])

[ASSISTANT]
sub_hits=[]
for i,p in enumerate(doc.paragraphs):
    t=p.text.lower()
    if "day" in t or "week" in t or "month" in t or "year" in t or "hour" in t or "minute" in t or "second" in t or "earth" in t:
        sub_hits.append((i,p.text))
len(sub_hits), sub_hits[:20]
