 [(195,
   'He dropped to one knee and shoved wedge slips under the first bowing brace. Bram’s crew followed, hands fast, straps ready. For once, labor did what labor could do. The gantry groaned again. Dust spilled from a seam. A young man stepped into the brace line, hands hovering like he didn’t know what to do with them. Corven. Jhace had seen him in the tunnels, quiet, competent. Corven swallowed. “I… I can feel it,” he said. “The hum. It’s—” “Louder,” Jhace finished. Corven nodded hard. “Like it’s in my teeth.” Tiffani stepped close, slate already up. “If it hits your chest, tell me. If you taste copper, tell me. Do not improvise.” Corven’s eyes flicked to the bending column. “If it goes—” “Then we act,” Jhace said. “But not before.” The wedges held for two breaths. Then the central column shifted. Not collapse. A decisive, ugly move—stone settling, iron flexing. The wedges screamed as they compressed. The workers inside were still running. “Now,” Tiffani said. Jhace put both hands on the column. Heat flared in his chest—familiar now, terrifying in its familiarity—and filament‑light threaded into the cinder‑dust coated seam. The column stopped shifting. Corven stared at Jhace’s hands like they were a language he’d almost learned. “Your turn,” Jhace said through gritted teeth. Corven stepped forward and touched the secondary brace. His breath hitched. Copper taste. Chest heat. Then light—thinner than Jhace’s, but real—threaded into the brace line. Corven’s hands started to shake immediately. “Stage One onset during hold,” Tiffani murmured, writing. “Early marker.” “Keep moving! If you stop, you become debris!” Bram shouted to the evac crew. “Count them out! Loud. Fast. Like the Crown’s listening!” Jhace’s tremor surged in sympathy, like the grid‑hum had tied their nervous systems together. His arms burned. His vision tunneled at the edges. “How long?” Tiffani asked. “Eighty,” Jhace rasped, counting heartbeats without thinking. Corven’s jaw clenched. “I can do—” “Do exactly what the structure needs,” Jhace snapped. “Nothing more.” At one‑fifty, the last workers stumbled out. Two hundred‑plus bodies, alive because they’d been allowed time. “Terminate,” Tiffani said. Jhace released first. The column held under wedges now that the load had shifted down. Corven released a heartbeat later and nearly fell. Jhace caught him by the elbow, and the tremor in his own hands made the grip feel unreliable. Corven looked at his shaking fingers with a kind of horror. “It’s not stopping.” “It doesn’t,” Jhace said. Corven stared at his shaking fingers like they’d betrayed him mid-shift. He’d watched Jhace hold. Had seen the tremor. Had understood it in theory. Theory didn’t matter when your hand wouldn’t listen. “How long?” he asked. Jhace’s jaw tightened. “Until you stop manifesting.” Corven’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. “So never.” “Yeah.” Corven set his palm on the edge of the bench. The tremor rattled bone against metal. He pulled back like it burned. Flexed. Tried again. Harder. It kept moving—slow, stubborn—like a crack running even after you stop pulling. Tiffani wrote the number anyway: 219 evacuated. Zero casualties. Two operators marked. Bram approached, face hard. “Crown’s going to hear about this—and they’ll bring forms instead of shovels.” Tiffani didn’t look up. “Good.” Jhace’s chest still burned with the after‑heat of holding. The hunger whispered: You could have held longer. You could have made it perfect. He swallowed the copper taste and tried to bully the hunger quiet. It answered with a warm twist behind his sternum. For now. ---'),
  (197,
   "The bell came from Sector Nine, not as a roar—just a single strike repeated until someone answered. Residential stairwell. Upper-terrace split. Twelve families still inside, one route. Jhace was already marked from Sector Nine. Corven was worse. Senne was fresh enough to stand without shaking. Tiffani looked at the slate wall and made the decision she would later pretend was only math. Two operators at one‑eighty each meant coordination, handoffs, spotters, and failure points. One operator at two‑forty meant simplicity. A clean line. “Single deployment,” she said. “Senne. Duration cap: two‑forty. Terminate on my bell.” Senne went pale as the heat took her chest. Copper-gold light laced her forearms. Not pretty. Functional. They reached the stairwell mouth as stone dust sifted from the ceiling like flour. The grid-hum underfoot carried the doubled harmonic, deep and insistent, a warning that didn’t care who listened. Senne braced under the central landing. Jhace watched her shoulders set—metalworker’s posture in a glassworker’s frame. Tiffani counted. Heartbeats, not heartbeats. Counts lied when panic touched them. One hundred. One forty. One eighty. At one‑ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn’t walked because she’d trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding. The evacuation lane kinked. The next family stumbled. The stone didn’t collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket. Three people were on the wrong side when it did: two adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve. Tiffani saw them, and for a moment the slate in her hands felt obscene. “Hold,” she said, voice flat. “Keep holding.” Senne’s eyes didn’t move. She couldn’t spare the motion. Two‑ten. Two‑twenty. Two‑thirty. The pocket didn’t clear. The child’s coughing turned wet. At two‑forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway. Because protocols existed for a reason. Because if she didn’t, she would never stop. Senne released. The stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along. They dug until their arms went numb. They found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles. Back in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision. Deployment: Senne — 240 heartbeats Evacuated: 47 Casualties: 3 Cause: Inadequate route assessment. Secondary beam shear. She wrote “cause” as if precision could make it reversible. Her hand moved to write the next revision. The chalk touched slate. Nothing came out. She stared at the number. Three. Not a count. Three names she would never know. Three families who would ask why their— The chalk snapped in her grip. She set the broken pieces down carefully. Picked up a new stick. Wrote: Route spotters required. Three per deployment. No exceptions. Underlined it twice. Then she closed the slate and sat in the dark workshop, counting her own breaths because the numbers on the page wouldn't behave. Her fingers kept shaking anyway."),
  (205,
   'Merchant Hall was a cathedral for trade: iron ribs, stone spine, glass skylights that made everything inside look clean even when it wasn’t. And it smelled like bodies and money and fear—the kind of fear that tries to wear perfume. It started failing at mid‑Tide. The bell sequence came sharp and frantic—no warning cadence, no time to wedge first. The Hall had been packed when the terrace shifted again. Four hundred inside, Bram said. Maybe more. Tiffani arrived before Jhace. When he pushed through the crowd, she was already on the steps, slate in hand, shouting like a foreman who’d learned math. “Operators on me. Two per span. Spotters assigned. No hero holds.” “Duration limit. One-eighty heartbeats maximum. You terminate on my signal. No argument.” Merra shoved through the dust with a slate clutched to her chest. Ash streaked her hair like she’d tried to comb through a fire. “We have a problem. Southern stairwell collapsed. Fifty people trapped on upper floors. Can’t reach main exits.” “Time estimate,” Tiffani said. “Low and high.” “Unknown. Debris blocking secondary stairs. Could be two hundred heartbeats. Could be six hundred.” “Then we don’t gamble.” Tiffani didn’t raise her voice. She didn’t have to. “Clear lanes. Count bodies out. You want prayer, pray while you walk.” She swung her slate toward the operators. “Corven—baseline check. When chest heat starts, report it. Don’t interpret. Report.” Corven swallowed. “If it hits, I tell you. If I taste copper, I tell you.” Jhace didn’t look at him. “Don’t spiral. Do the minimum.” “Minimum,” Corven echoed, like repetition could make it true. “Senne—status.” “Getting hollow,” Senne said. “Chest feels empty. Light’s harder to maintain.” “That’s Stage Two warning.” Tiffani’s eyes cut sharp. “You terminate on my signal or I terminate you myself.” Senne gave her a tired half-smile. “Sure. Die on schedule. Very professional.” “Kess—report.” “I am present and available for assignment.” “Available doesn’t mean useful. Report symptoms. Stop performing.” “Symptoms are within acceptable parameters for operational requirements.” Bram leaned in, voice too bright for the amount of people about to die. “Translation: Kess is bleeding and pretending it’s fashion.” “I am not bleeding.” Merra didn’t bother being gentle. “Kess—your nose is bleeding.” “I am aware of the nasal discharge. It is minor. Barely noteworthy. I’ve had worse nosebleeds from seasonal changes.” “Yeah,” Bram said. “And I’ve had worse ideas than this hold. Doesn’t make them good.” From the crowd, a Temple Observer screamed, “Let the worthy hold! Let the unworthy fall!” Bram snapped his head toward the sound. “Hey—shut up. If we’re picking who falls, you’re first. That’s democracy.” “Ignore them,” Tiffani said. “Move.” “Spotters, call markers.” “Jhace tremor increasing!” “Corven tremor increasing!” “Senne vein-line onset!” Bram barked at the evac line like a foreman with a broken sense of humor. “Keep moving! Nobody stops! You stop, you become part of the debris! Count them out!” Merra’s voice cut through the dust. “Alternate route open. Final evacuation commencing.” “Count them,” Tiffani said. “Call numbers.” Jhace’s breath came tight. “One hundred.” “State exit count,” Tiffani said, “not your suffering.” “One hundred. Out.” Daved’s voice came rough through the grit. “They’re not out.” Jhace’s head snapped. “No.” “They’re not out.” “Daved—state the variable,” Tiffani said. “How many still inside?” “Enough.” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Extend. Thirty heartbeats. No more.” “Tiff—” Jhace started. “I am not debating physics with the dead in the hallway,” she said. “Extend.” Thirty heartbeats passed like a slow knife. Merra shouted from the corridor mouth, “Final group emerging. Ten heartbeats!” “Ten heartbeats is still ten,” Tiffani said. “Move.” Daved’s hands tightened. “Hold.” “Daved!” Jhace lunged a step closer. “Hold.” Merra’s slate slapped stone. “Clear! All civilians safe!” “Terminate all operators,” Tiffani snapped. “Immediate coordinated shutdown.” Lights guttered and went out—one by one—except Daved’s. “Can’t,” Daved said. “Daved. Breathe,” Jhace said, too close, too desperate. “Can’t.” “Terminate,” Tiffani said. “That is not a negotiation.” “They’re not out.” “They are out. Merra confirmed. Terminate now.” Daved’s jaw worked like he was chewing copper. “Already crossed.” Bram shoved his way in with words because silence was making everyone honest. “Daved—buddy—love the commitment, hate the method acting.” “Bram, shut up,” Jhace hissed. “I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s a disorder.” Tiffani stepped in until Daved had to see her. “Look at me. If you extend past this, you cross Stage Three threshold.” Daved’s eyes stayed flat. “Already crossed.” Jhace’s throat tightened. “He locked.” “I see it,” Tiffani said. They hauled Daved back from the structure like pulling a hook out of meat. “Terminate!” Tiffani shouted. The light finally cut. Daved folded like a tool dropped from numb hands. Tiffani’s voice went small for one syllable. “I know.”'),
  (218,
   'They called him Shard‑Bearer because nobody had a better word for what they thought they’d seen. A man in the eastern districts with a bright line in his chest. A glow that didn’t fade after termination because there was no termination. A hold that didn’t shake. A body that didn’t pay. Rumors traveled faster than Crown notices and cleaner than Temple sermons. They came with dates and witness counts like proof. Tiffani pinned three reports to the workshop wall with nails. Report one: Torne Reach — Tide 0+3. One operator held a bridge span alone for “half an Anneal.” No tremor. No vein‑lines. Report two: Brinefall — Tide 0+5. A whole terrace stairwell stabilized by “one man’s hands.” Witnesses describe “glass light under the skin.” Tiffani tapped the pinned report with the end of her pen. “Report three: delayed secondary collapse. Building held by Shard-Bearer for six hundred heartbeats. Post-release: immediate structural failure. No tremor visible in operator. No warning signs. Casualties: seventeen.” Jhace stared at the number like it had teeth. “He didn’t save them. He killed them slower.” “Correct,” Tiffani said. “The cost didn’t vanish. It moved—into the structure, into the people around it. Somewhere we can’t ignore.” “Heroics.” The word came out like a curse. “That word implies exemption,” Tiffani said. “There is no exemption. Only deferral.” Bram leaned over the table, too casual for the subject. “So the Shard-Bearer is just… consequences with better marketing.” “Don’t,” Jhace said. “Sorry,” Bram said. “I forgot we’re being solemn about the murder wizard.” Tiffani didn’t look up. “The registry will want this rumor. It provides false relief. False hope that cost can be avoided.” “And people die for that,” Jhace said. “Then we make sure the registry doesn’t become a leash,” Tiffani said.')])

[ASSISTANT]
for idx,_ in sub_hits:
    for j in range(idx, idx+1):
        print(idx, doc.paragraphs[idx].text)

218 They called him Shard‑Bearer because nobody had a better word for what they thought they’d seen. A man in the eastern districts with a bright line in his chest. A glow that didn’t fade after termination because there was no termination. A hold that didn’t shake. A body that didn’t pay. Rumors traveled faster than Crown notices and cleaner than Temple sermons. They came with dates and witness counts like proof. Tiffani pinned three reports to the workshop wall with nails. Report one: Torne Reach — Tide 0+3. One operator held a bridge span alone for “half an Anneal.” No tremor. No vein‑lines. Report two: Brinefall — Tide 0+5. A whole terrace stairwell stabilized by “one man’s hands.” Witnesses describe “glass light under the skin.” Tiffani tapped the pinned report with the end of her pen. “Report three: delayed secondary collapse. Building held by Shard-Bearer for six hundred heartbeats. Post-release: immediate structural failure. No tremor visible in operator. No warning signs. Casualties: seventeen.” Jhace stared at the number like it had teeth. “He didn’t save them. He killed them slower.” “Correct,” Tiffani said. “The cost didn’t vanish. It moved—into the structure, into the people around it. Somewhere we can’t ignore.” “Heroics.” The word came out like a curse. “That word implies exemption,” Tiffani said. “There is no exemption. Only deferral.” Bram leaned over the table, too casual for the subject. “So the Shard-Bearer is just… consequences with better marketing.” “Don’t,” Jhace said. “Sorry,” Bram said. “I forgot we’re being solemn about the murder wizard.” Tiffani didn’t look up. “The registry will want this rumor. It provides false relief. False hope that cost can be avoided.” “And people die for that,” Jhace said. “Then we make sure the registry doesn’t become a leash,” Tiffani said.

    print(idx, len(t))

195 3590
He dropped to one knee and shoved wedge slips under the first bowing brace. Bram’s crew followed, hands fast, straps ready. For once, labor did what labor could do. The gantry groaned again. Dust spilled from a seam. A young man stepped into the brace line, hands hovering like he didn’t know what to do with them. Corven. Jhace had seen him in the tunnels, quiet, competent. Corven swallowed. “I… I can feel it,” he said. “The hum. It’s—” “Louder,” Jhace finished. Corven nodded hard. “Like it’s in my teeth.” Tiffani stepped close, slate already up. “If it hits your chest, tell me. If you taste copper, tell me. Do not improvise.” Corven’s eyes flicked to the bending column. “If it goes—” “Then we act,” Jhace said. “But not before.” The wedges held for two breaths. Then the central column shifted. Not collapse. A decisive, ugly move—stone settling, iron flexing. The wedges screamed as they compressed. The workers inside were still running. “Now,” Tiffani said. Jhace put both hands on the column. Heat flared in his chest—familiar now, terrifying in its familiarity—and filament‑light threaded into the cinder‑dust coated seam. The column stopped shifting. Corven stared at Jhace’s hands like they were a language he’d almost learned. “Your turn,” Jhace said through gritted teeth. Corven stepped forward and touched the secondary brace. His breath hitched. Copper taste. Chest heat. Then light—thinner than Jhace’s, but real—threaded into the brace line. Corven’s hands started to shake immediately. “Stage One onset during hold,” Tiffani murmured, writing. “Early marker.” “Keep moving! If you stop, you become debris!” Bram shouted to the evac crew. “Count them out! Loud. Fast. Like the Crown’s listening!” Jhace’s tremor surged in sympathy, like the grid‑hum had tied their nervous systems together. His arms burned. His vision tunneled at the edges. “How long?” Tiffani asked. “Eighty,” Jhace rasped, counting heartbeats without thinking. Corven’s jaw clenched. “I can do—” “Do exactly what the structure needs,” Jhace snapped. “Nothing more.” At one‑fifty, the last workers stumbled out. Two hundred‑plus bodies, alive because they’d been allowed time. “Terminate,” Tiffani said. Jhace released first. The column held under wedges now that the load had shifted down. Corven released a heartbeat later and nearly fell. Jhace caught him by the elbow, and the tremor in his own hands made the grip feel unreliable. Corven looked at his shaking fingers with a kind of horror. “It’s not stopping.” “It doesn’t,” Jhace said. Corven stared at his shaking fingers like they’d betrayed him mid-shift. He’d watched Jhace hold. Had seen the tremor. Had understood it in theory. Theory didn’t matter when your hand wouldn’t listen. “How long?” he asked. Jhace’s jaw tightened. “Until you stop manifesting.” Corven’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. “So never.” “Yeah.” Corven set his palm on the edge of the bench. The tremor rattled bone against metal. He pulled back like it burned. Flexed. Tried again. Harder. It kept moving—slow, stubborn—like a crack running even after you stop pulling. Tiffani wrote the number anyway: 219 evacuated. Zero casualties. Two operators marked. Bram approached, face hard. “Crown’s going to hear about this—and they’ll bring forms instead of shovels.” Tiffani didn’t look up. “Good.” Jhace’s chest still burned with the after‑heat of holding. The hunger whispered: You could have held longer. You could have made it perfect. He swallowed the copper taste and tried to bully the hunger quiet. It answered with a warm twist behind his sternum. For now. ---

197 3052
The bell came from Sector Nine, not as a roar—just a single strike repeated until someone answered. Residential stairwell. Upper-terrace split. Twelve families still inside, one route. Jhace was already marked from Sector Nine. Corven was worse. Senne was fresh enough to stand without shaking. Tiffani looked at the slate wall and made the decision she would later pretend was only math. Two operators at one‑eighty each meant coordination, handoffs, spotters, and failure points. One operator at two‑forty meant simplicity. A clean line. “Single deployment,” she said. “Senne. Duration cap: two‑forty. Terminate on my bell.” Senne went pale as the heat took her chest. Copper-gold light laced her forearms. Not pretty. Functional. They reached the stairwell mouth as stone dust sifted from the ceiling like flour. The grid-hum underfoot carried the doubled harmonic, deep and insistent, a warning that didn’t care who listened. Senne braced under the central landing. Jhace watched her shoulders set—metalworker’s posture in a glassworker’s frame. Tiffani counted. Heartbeats, not heartbeats. Counts lied when panic touched them. One hundred. One forty. One eighty. At one‑ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn’t walked because she’d trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding. The evacuation lane kinked. The next family stumbled. The stone didn’t collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket. Three people were on the wrong side when it did: two adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve. Tiffani saw them, and for a moment the slate in her hands felt obscene. “Hold,” she said, voice flat. “Keep holding.” Senne’s eyes didn’t move. She couldn’t spare the motion. Two‑ten. Two‑twenty. Two‑thirty. The pocket didn’t clear. The child’s coughing turned wet. At two‑forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway. Because protocols existed for a reason. Because if she didn’t, she would never stop. Senne released. The stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along. They dug until their arms went numb. They found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles. Back in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision. Deployment: Senne — 240 heartbeats Evacuated: 47 Casualties: 3 Cause: Inadequate route assessment. Secondary beam shear. She wrote “cause” as if precision could make it reversible. Her hand moved to write the next revision. The chalk touched slate. Nothing came out. She stared at the number. Three. Not a count. Three names she would never know. Three families who would ask why their— The chalk snapped in her grip. She set the broken pieces down carefully. Picked up a new stick. Wrote: Route spotters required. Three per deployment. No exceptions. Underlined it twice. Then she closed the slate and sat in the dark workshop, counting her own breaths because the numbers on the page wouldn't behave. Her fingers kept shaking anyway.

205 5022
Merchant Hall was a cathedral for trade: iron ribs, stone spine, glass skylights that made everything inside look clean even when it wasn’t. And it smelled like bodies and money and fear—the kind of fear that tries to wear perfume. It started failing at mid‑Tide. The bell sequence came sharp and frantic—no warning cadence, no time to wedge first. The Hall had been packed when the terrace shifted again. Four hundred inside, Bram said. Maybe more. Tiffani arrived before Jhace. When he pushed through the crowd, she was already on the steps, slate in hand, shouting like a foreman who’d learned math. “Operators on me. Two per span. Spotters assigned. No hero holds.” “Duration limit. One-eighty heartbeats maximum. You terminate on my signal. No argument.” Merra shoved through the dust with a slate clutched to her chest. Ash streaked her hair like she’d tried to comb through a fire. “We have a problem. Southern stairwell collapsed. Fifty people trapped on upper floors. Can’t reach main exits.” “Time estimate,” Tiffani said. “Low and high.” “Unknown. Debris blocking secondary stairs. Could be two hundred heartbeats. Could be six hundred.” “Then we don’t gamble.” Tiffani didn’t raise her voice. She didn’t have to. “Clear lanes. Count bodies out. You want prayer, pray while you walk.” She swung her slate toward the operators. “Corven—baseline check. When chest heat starts, report it. Don’t interpret. Report.” Corven swallowed. “If it hits, I tell you. If I taste copper, I tell you.” Jhace didn’t look at him. “Don’t spiral. Do the minimum.” “Minimum,” Corven echoed, like repetition could make it true. “Senne—status.” “Getting hollow,” Senne said. “Chest feels empty. Light’s harder to maintain.” “That’s Stage Two warning.” Tiffani’s eyes cut sharp. “You terminate on my signal or I terminate you myself.” Senne gave her a tired half-smile. “Sure. Die on schedule. Very professional.” “Kess—report.” “I am present and available for assignment.” “Available doesn’t mean useful. Report symptoms. Stop performing.” “Symptoms are within acceptable parameters for operational requirements.” Bram leaned in, voice too bright for the amount of people about to die. “Translation: Kess is bleeding and pretending it’s fashion.” “I am not bleeding.” Merra didn’t bother being gentle. “Kess—your nose is bleeding.” “I am aware of the nasal discharge. It is minor. Barely noteworthy. I’ve had worse nosebleeds from seasonal changes.” “Yeah,” Bram said. “And I’ve had worse ideas than this hold. Doesn’t make them good.” From the crowd, a Temple Observer screamed, “Let the worthy hold! Let the unworthy fall!” Bram snapped his head toward the sound. “Hey—shut up. If we’re picking who falls, you’re first. That’s democracy.” “Ignore them,” Tiffani said. “Move.” “Spotters, call markers.” “Jhace tremor increasing!” “Corven tremor increasing!” “Senne vein-line onset!” Bram barked at the evac line like a foreman with a broken sense of humor. “Keep moving! Nobody stops! You stop, you become part of the debris! Count them out!” Merra’s voice cut through the dust. “Alternate route open. Final evacuation commencing.” “Count them,” Tiffani said. “Call numbers.” Jhace’s breath came tight. “One hundred.” “State exit count,” Tiffani said, “not your suffering.” “One hundred. Out.” Daved’s voice came rough through the grit. “They’re not out.” Jhace’s head snapped. “No.” “They’re not out.” “Daved—state the variable,” Tiffani said. “How many still inside?” “Enough.” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Extend. Thirty heartbeats. No more.” “Tiff—” Jhace started. “I am not debating physics with the dead in the hallway,” she said. “Extend.” Thirty heartbeats passed like a slow knife. Merra shouted from the corridor mouth, “Final group emerging. Ten heartbeats!” “Ten heartbeats is still ten,” Tiffani said. “Move.” Daved’s hands tightened. “Hold.” “Daved!” Jhace lunged a step closer. “Hold.” Merra’s slate slapped stone. “Clear! All civilians safe!” “Terminate all operators,” Tiffani snapped. “Immediate coordinated shutdown.” Lights guttered and went out—one by one—except Daved’s. “Can’t,” Daved said. “Daved. Breathe,” Jhace said, too close, too desperate. “Can’t.” “Terminate,” Tiffani said. “That is not a negotiation.” “They’re not out.” “They are out. Merra confirmed. Terminate now.” Daved’s jaw worked like he was chewing copper. “Already crossed.” Bram shoved his way in with words because silence was making everyone honest. “Daved—buddy—love the commitment, hate the method acting.” “Bram, shut up,” Jhace hissed. “I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s a disorder.” Tiffani stepped in until Daved had to see her. “Look at me. If you extend past this, you cross Stage Three threshold.” Daved’s eyes stayed flat. “Already crossed.” Jhace’s throat tightened. “He locked.” “I see it,” Tiffani said. They hauled Daved back from the structure like pulling a hook out of meat. “Terminate!” Tiffani shouted. The light finally cut. Daved folded like a tool dropped from numb hands. Tiffani’s voice went small for one syllable. “I know.”

218 1822
They called him Shard‑Bearer because nobody had a better word for what they thought they’d seen. A man in the eastern districts with a bright line in his chest. A glow that didn’t fade after termination because there was no termination. A hold that didn’t shake. A body that didn’t pay. Rumors traveled faster than Crown notices and cleaner than Temple sermons. They came with dates and witness counts like proof. Tiffani pinned three reports to the workshop wall with nails. Report one: Torne Reach — Tide 0+3. One operator held a bridge span alone for “half an Anneal.” No tremor. No vein‑lines. Report two: Brinefall — Tide 0+5. A whole terrace stairwell stabilized by “one man’s hands.” Witnesses describe “glass light under the skin.” Tiffani tapped the pinned report with the end of her pen. “Report three: delayed secondary collapse. Building held by Shard-Bearer for six hundred heartbeats. Post-release: immediate structural failure. No tremor visible in operator. No warning signs. Casualties: seventeen.” Jhace stared at the number like it had teeth. “He didn’t save them. He killed them slower.” “Correct,” Tiffani said. “The cost didn’t vanish. It moved—into the structure, into the people around it. Somewhere we can’t ignore.” “Heroics.” The word came out like a curse. “That word implies exemption,” Tiffani said. “There is no exemption. Only deferral.” Bram leaned over the table, too casual for the subject. “So the Shard-Bearer is just… consequences with better marketing.” “Don’t,” Jhace said. “Sorry,” Bram said. “I forgot we’re being solemn about the murder wizard.” Tiffani didn’t look up. “The registry will want this rumor. It provides false relief. False hope that cost can be avoided.” “And people die for that,” Jhace said. “Then we make sure the registry doesn’t become a leash,” Tiffani said.

   "Brother Corran didn’t wear Temple colors. The first thing Jhace noticed was the badge—bright, clean, pinned where it couldn't be missed. Next was how the man didn't look tired. Not even a little.    "The bell came from Sector Nine, not as a roar—just a single strike repeated until someone answered. Residential stairwell. Upper-terrace split. Twelve families still inside, one route. The grid-hum underfoot carried the doubled harmonic, deep and insistent, a warning that didn’t care who listened. Senne braced under the central landing. Jhace watched her shoulders set—metalworker’s posture in a glassworker’s frame. Tiffani counted. Heartbeats, not heartbeats. Counts lied when panic touched them. One hundred. One forty. One eighty. At one‑ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn’t walked because she’d trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding. The evacuation lane kinked. The next family stumbled. The stone didn’t collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket. Three people were on the wrong side when it did: two adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve. Tiffani saw them, and for a moment the slate in her hands felt obscene. “Hold,” she said, voice flat. “Keep holding.” Senne’s eyes didn’t move. She couldn’t spare the motion. Two‑ten. Two‑twenty. Two‑thirty. The pocket didn’t clear. The child’s coughing turned wet. At two‑forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway. Because protocols existed for a reason. Because if she didn’t, she would never stop. Senne released. The stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along. They dug until their arms went numb. They found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles. Back in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision. Deployment: Senne — 240 heartbeats Evacuated: 47 Casualties: 3 Cause: Inadequate route assessment. Secondary beam shear. She wrote “cause” as if precision could make it reversible. Her hand moved to write the next revision. The chalk touched slate. Nothing came out. She stared at the number. Three. Not a count. Three names she would never know. Three families who would ask why their— The chalk snapped in her grip. She set the broken pieces down carefully. Picked up a new stick.    "Tiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still. It wasn’t Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you. The ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear. She made three piles. Four, if she was honest. One stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership. One stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it. One stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated. The last pile wasn’t for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud. Bram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he’d stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him. “You look like a priest,” Tiffani said without looking up. She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.")])

[TOOL]
# DOCX reading, creation, and review guidance

## Reading DOCXs
- Use `soffice -env:UserInstallation=file:///tmp/lo_profile_$$ --headless --convert-to pdf --outdir $OUTDIR $INPUT_DOCX` to convert DOCXs to PDFs.
  - The `-env:UserInstallation=file:///tmp/lo_profile_$$` flag is important. Otherwise, it will time out.
- Then Convert the PDF to page images so you can visually inspect the result:
  - `pdftoppm -png $OUTDIR/$BASENAME.pdf $OUTDIR/$BASENAME`
- Then open the PNGs and read the images.
- Only do python printing as a last resort because you will miss important details with text extraction (e.g. figures, tables, diagrams).

## Primary tooling for creating DOCXs
- Create and edit DOCX files with `python-docx`. Use it to control structure, styles, tables, and lists. Install it with `pip install python-docx` if it's not already installed.
- After **every meaningful batch of edits**—new sections, layout tweaks, styling changes—render the DOCX to PDF:
  - `soffice -env:UserInstallation=file:///tmp/lo_profile_$$ --headless --convert-to pdf --outdir $OUTDIR $INPUT_DOCX`
- Convert the PDF to page images so you can visually inspect the result:
  - `pdftoppm -png $OUTDIR/$BASENAME.pdf $OUTDIR/$BASENAME`
- **Inspect every PNG before moving on.** If you see any defect, fix the DOCX and repeat the render → inspect loop until all pages look perfect.


## Quality expectations
- Aim for a client-ready document: consistent typography, spacing, margins, and layout hierarchy. Heading levels should be obvious, lists aligned, and paragraphs easy to scan.
- Never ship obvious formatting defects such as clipped or overlapping text, default-template styling, broken tables, unreadable characters, or inconsistent bullet styling.
- Charts, tables, and visuals must be legible in the rendered PNGs—no pixelation, misalignment, missing labels, or mismatched colors.
- Never use the U+2011 non-breaking hyphen or other unicode dashes as they will not be rendered correctly. Use ASCII hyphens instead.
- Citations, references, and footnotes must be human-readable and professional. No tool-internal tokens (e.g., `[145036110387964†L158-L160]`), malformed URLs, or placeholder text should be present in the document.
- You must convert all citations into a human-readable format in the document with standard scholarly citation format. No `【【turn1541736113682297662view0†L11-L19】` notations are allowed in the document as the reader cannot interpret them (such citations will be severely penalized).
- Content should be concise, relevant, and free of boilerplate AI phrasing. Ensure each section adds value and flows logically.

## Final checks
- Re-run the DOCX → PDF → PNG loop after your final changes and inspect **every page** at 100% zoom. Look for subtle issues like inconsistent spacing, widows/orphans, or misaligned bullet levels.
- Correct every formatting defect you see in the PNGs, including but not limited to: overlapping text or shapes, clipped text or shapes that are cut off, black squares, broken tables, unreadable characters, etc.
- Only deliver the DOCX once the latest PNG review confirms the document is visually flawless and professionally styled.
- Keep intermediate files organized (or cleaned up) so reviewers can easily locate final outputs.

[ASSISTANT]
import re
orig=Document("/mnt/data/N01_GripThatHolds_Formatted.docx")
chap_pat=re.compile(r"^CHAPTER\s+\d+\s*:\s*.*", re.IGNORECASE)
chap_idxs=[i for i,p in enumerate(orig.paragraphs) if chap_pat.match(p.text.strip())]
chap_idxs[:20], len(chap_idxs)

[ASSISTANT]
def find_substring(doc, substr):
    out=[]
    for i,p in enumerate(doc.paragraphs):
        if substr.lower() in p.text.lower():
            out.append((i,p.text))
    return out
subs=find_substring(orig,"day")
len(subs), subs[:10]

[TOOL]
(1,
 [(149,
   "Tiffani wrote the first copy with her left hand because her right knee refused to lock and her right hand refused to stay still. It wasn’t Stage. It was the kind of fatigue that lives in joints after bad work—the kind you carry until your hands start lying to you. The ink pooled anyway. Copper-gold dust clung to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear. She made three piles. Four, if she was honest. One stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership. One stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it. One stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated. The last pile wasn’t for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud. Bram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he’d stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him. “You look like a priest,” Tiffani said without looking up. Bram glanced down at the coat. “Don’t you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I’m doing it recreationally.” “You’re late.” “Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.” He leaned in, saw the piles. “Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment.” Tiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. “This goes to Corlexi.” Bram’s fingers hovered. “This the ‘please don’t leash us’ letter?” “This is the ‘you get what I give you’ packet,” she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. “Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no.” Bram squinted. “And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Not included.” Bram’s smile went sharp. “That’s either genius or suicidal.” “It’s survivable.” She turned the next page over and wrote a title line in the margin: DEPLOYMENT AUTHORITY. Then she crossed it out and rewrote: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.” Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.” “There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.” She pushed the next stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification. Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.” “This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.” Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.” “They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.” Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.” Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.” Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers. “Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.” Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.” Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?” “Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.” Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage. Chloen read the first line. YOU CAN SAY NO. She looked up. “That’s new.” “It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.” Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.” And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD. Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.” Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.” Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.” Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it. The door clicked. The room held its breath. Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen. “For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.” Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.” Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.” Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult. Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.” Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.” Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?” Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm. “Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in daylight.” Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.” “It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.” Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?” “Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.” Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.” He left. Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.” “He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.” Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?” Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.” She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred. “Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge. Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission. Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.” “I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said. Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing. Tiffani didn’t move. She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.")])

   'He dropped to one knee and shoved wedge slips under the first bowing brace. Bram’s crew followed, hands fast, straps ready. For once, labor did what labor could do. The gantry groaned again.   (49,
   'They called him Shard‑Bearer because nobody had a better word for what they thought they’d seen. A man in the eastern districts with a bright line in his chest. A glow that didn’t fade after termination because there was no termination. A hold that didn’t shake. A body that didn’t pay. Rumors traveled faster than Crown notices and cleaner than Temple sermons. They came with dates and witness counts like proof. Tiffani pinned three reports to the workshop wall with nails. Report one: Torne Reach — Tide 0+3. One operator held a bridge span alone for “half an Anneal.” No tremor. No vein‑lines. Report two: Brinefall — Tide 0+5. A whole terrace stairwell stabilized by “one man’s hands.” Witnesses describe “glass light under the skin.” Tiffani tapped the pinned report with the end of her pen. “Report three: delayed secondary collapse. Building held by Shard-Bearer for six hundred heartbeats. Post-release: immediate structural failure. No tremor visible in operator. No warning signs. def extract_context(phrase, window=200):
    pos=full.find(phrase)
    if pos==-1:
        return None
    return full[pos:pos+window]

   'Jhace’s tremor pulsed like a warning. He didn’t know the Shard‑Bearer.    "--- CODA: GLASS IN THE HAND Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them. He’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can't spit out. Stone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn't reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule. Someone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive. Jhace tried to sleep. Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him. He flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking. Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place. Jhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed. “I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest. Outside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short. Jhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.")],

spit_hits=[(i,p.text) for i,p in enumerate(orig.paragraphs) if re.search(r"\bspit|swallow|spitting|swallowed\b", p.text, re.IGNORECASE)]
len(spit_hits), spit_hits[:10]

[TOOL]
pport column. Light—thin, copper-gold, filament-fine—threaded from his palms into the stone seam. The building stopped moving. It was only paused—still wrong, still dangerous. Jhace\'s breath shook out of him. He could feel every shift in load through his fingertips: the weight of stone, the tension in iron, the panic-motion of bodies inside. The heat in his chest flared with each micro-adjustment, like his body was paying for every heartbeat of stability in increments he couldn\'t count. "Move," he rasped. "Get them out." Boots ran. Voices shouted coordinates. Someone coughed dust and kept crawling. A woman slid into his peripheral vision—practical coat, hair tied back, slate already in hand despite the chaos. She didn\'t look at him like a miracle. She looked at him like a system that could fail. "How long can you hold?" she demanded. Jhace\'s jaw clenched. The heat in his chest spiked. "I don\'t know." "Then we find out." She stepped into the dust, coughed once, and shouted to the evac crew. "Clear corridor A first. Count bodies out. No guessing." Jhace tried to turn his head. The moment he shifted his attention, the heat flared sharper—punishment for looking away. "I have to watch the load," he said through his teeth. "Then watch it," she snapped, already marking a line on her slate. "I\'ll watch you." People came out in bursts: three, then five, then a cluster carrying an older man whose leg bent wrong. Each time the load shifted, Jhace\'s hands tightened and the filament-light brightened in response. His heartbeat counted itself in his ears. One hundred. One hundred twenty. His arms started to tremble. Not from fatigue. From something deeper. A fine vibration that spread into his forearms, up to his shoulders, making his grip feel like it belonged to someone else. "Hold steady," the woman said, quieter now, close enough he could hear her through the dust. He didn\'t answer. Answering took breath. At two hundred heartbeats, the tremor spread into his chest. At two-twenty, his vision narrowed at the edges, copper-gold sparks flickering across it like welding flash. "How many?" he rasped. The woman didn\'t look up from the evac line. "Ten. Eleven. Twelve—" A scream from inside. A child. High and thin and suddenly cut off. Jhace\'s hands shook harder. "No," he hissed, and the filament-light surged, burning his palms numb. "Go," the woman shouted toward the breach. "Last sweep. Now." A rescuer crawled under the paused beam line and dragged a small body out by the shoulders. The child coughed dust and cried like she\'d been holding it in for too long. The woman\'s voice cut clean through the chaos. "Twelve out. Corridor clear." Jhace felt the building try to move again the instant she said it, like it had been waiting for permission. "Terminate," she said. No softness in it. Not cruelty. Protocol. Jhace let the filament go. The building settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. But the void where bodies had been was now empty. He staggered back into open air. His hands wouldn\'t stop shaking. The tremor was small—millimeters—but it was relentless. Like a ghost living inside his muscles, counting the dead he\'d prevented and refusing to be quiet about the cost. The woman stepped beside him, dust streaked across her face. "Name." "Jhace Torrins." She wrote it without looking, chalk skating on sweat. "Duration: two‑forty—" The chalk snapped. She swore under her breath and wrote it anyway. "Don\'t," he snapped. Too hard. Too fast. She met his eyes. Her gaze was flat, measuring, absolutely certain. "If we don\'t measure it," she said, "we kill you next time." Between bells, they made it back to the workshop long enough to rinse dust off skin and reset slates. Jhace sat at his bench and waited for the chest heat to fade. It didn’t. Not fully. A banked warmth stayed behind his sternum—steady as a coal bed that refused to go cold. He pressed his palm to his chest. Felt nothing through shirt and ribs. Inside, the warmth answered anyway. Once. Twice. Like the world remembering how it had fit together when he held. His hand lingered there a heartbeat too long. Not thinking. Just reaching. He pulled it away like it burned. The tremor kept moving, but for one heartbeat—only one—the shake eased, like his body preferred the burn to the break. That was the first time the hunger felt like a vote. Jhace’s hands shook between them like evidence he hadn\'t wanted. He swallowed copper. Tasted grit. Looked at the child being carried past them on a makeshift stretcher—alive, crying, covered in dust but breathing. He nodded once. "Measure," he said. "Then make sure there isn\'t a next time." The woman\'s slate scratched. She wrote: Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Tallow Street. Tide 0. Behind them, the bell rang again. Different street. Same dust in his teeth. Same bell tearing its own throat. Jhace looked down and realized he was still gripping air. Copper sat on his tongue. He went anyway.'),

  (150,
   "--- CODA: GLASS IN THE HAND Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them. He’d scrubbed his hands raw. The copper taste was still there anyway—wedged behind his teeth like a coin you can't spit out. Stone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn't reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule. Someone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive. Jhace tried to sleep. Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him. He flexed his fingers. The tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the shaking. Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place. Jhace swallowed. The copper taste stayed. “I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest. Outside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short. Jhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.")])

[ASSISTANT]
for i in range(5):
    print(i, orig.paragraphs[i].text)

[TOOL]
0 N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS
1 
2 CHAPTER 1: THE BELL
3 The bell rang wrong. Not the slow warning cadence. Not the measured strike that meant "check your seals, tighten your brackets." This was the frantic peal of something already falling, the sound of a structure begging for a hold. Jhace was moving before the bell finished its third strike. He grabbed the wrong ring of collar keys on the first yank. Swore, dumped them into his palm, and sorted by feel while he ran. One key skittered off the stair edge and disappeared into the dark. He didn’t go back for it.
4 His kit bounced against his hip. Chalk. Slate. Collar keys. Tools for measuring failure, not stopping it. He didn't know why his legs were carrying him toward the sound instead of away. Didn't know why his heart counted the bell strikes like they were orders. One. Two. Three. Four. The doubled harmonic hit him at Junction Twelve. It skipped the pipe-hum entirely and punched straight into his sternum. His ribs buzzed like struck iron. His teeth hurt. His mouth flooded metal. Copper. Sharp and immediate, like he'd bitten through wire. He'd been tasting it for two Tides now. Faint at first. A ghost-flavor that came and went with the grid-hum. But this was different. This flooded his mouth so thick he wanted to spit. He didn't. Underface rule: waste nothing. His hands started shaking. Then the shaking cut out for three heartbeats and he thought, stupidly, his fingers had finally died. And then the tremor came back twice as fast.

canon_chaps=[(i,p.text) for i,p in enumerate(canon.paragraphs) if chap_pat.match(p.text.strip())]
canon_chaps[:5], len(canon_chaps)

[ASSISTANT]
def scan_forbidden_any(doc):
    bad=[]
    for i,p in enumerate(doc.paragraphs):
        t=p.text
        low=t.lower()
        # simple substrings
        for w in ["earth"," day","day ","daylight","week","month","year","hour","minute","second"]:
            if w.strip() in low:
                if "tide" in low and w.strip()=="day": 
                    pass
        # we'll use regex for word boundary for core, but also substring day
    return bad
# Let's just substring check for any forbidden token anywhere case-insensitive
tokens=["earth","day","week","month","year","hour","minute","second"]
bad=[]
for i,p in enumerate(canon.paragraphs):
    low=p.text.lower()
    for tok in tokens:
        if tok in low:
            bad.append((i,tok,p.text))
            break
len(bad), bad[:10]

[ASSISTANT]
from copy import deepcopy
from docx.oxml import OxmlElement
from docx.oxml.ns import qn

def remove_spit_swallow(text):
    # remove sentences containing spit/swallow variants
    if not text: 
        return text
    # quick sentence split keeping delimiters
    parts=re.split(r'(?<=[.!?])\s+', text)
    kept=[]
    for s in parts:
        if re.search(r'\b(spit|spitting|swallow|swallowed)\b', s, re.IGNORECASE):
            continue
        kept.append(s)
    return " ".join(kept).strip()

def replace_time_units(text):
    # replace forbidden time units with Te'Oga or neutral measures
    repl=[
        (r'\bseconds?\b', 'breaths'),
        (r'\bminutes?\b', 'breaths'),
        (r'\bhours?\b', 'tide-spans'),  # avoid hour
        (r'\bdays?\b', 'Tides'),
        (r'\bweeks?\b', 'Anneals'),
        (r'\bmonths?\b', 'Crucibles'),
        (r'\byears?\b', 'Castings'),
        (r'\bdaylight\b', 'Kindling-light'),
    ]
    out=text
    for pat,rep in repl:
        out=re.sub(pat, rep, out, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    # avoid "tide-spans" maybe; remove if created
    out=out.replace("tide-spans","Tides")
    return out

def clean_paragraph_text(text):
    t=remove_spit_swallow(text)
    t=replace_time_units(t)
    # remove any standalone "Spit." paragraph that became empty
    return t

modified=Document("/mnt/data/N01_GripThatHolds_Formatted_CANON.docx")
for p in modified.paragraphs:
    p.text=clean_paragraph_text(p.text)

# Ensure Chapter 2 has Tiffani hum line; find in chapter2 range by headings
# We'll just verify presence of "tuneless hum" and if absent, insert after her first log.
full_text="\n".join(p.text for p in modified.paragraphs)
"tuneless hum" in full_text.lower(), "low, tuneless" in full_text.lower()

[TOOL]
(True, True)

[ASSISTANT]
# scan for forbidden tokens anywhere
tokens=["earth","day","week","month","year","hour","minute","second"]
bad=[]
for i,p in enumerate(modified.paragraphs):
    low=p.text.lower()
    for tok in tokens:
        if tok in low:
            bad.append((i,tok,p.text))
            break
len(bad), bad[:20]

Tiffani looked at the slate wall and made the decision she would later pretend was only math. Two operators at one‑eighty each meant coordination, handoffs, spotters, and failure points. One operator at two‑forty meant simplicity. A clean line. “Single deployment,” she said. “Senne. Duration cap: two‑forty. Terminate on my bell.” Senne went pale as the heat took her chest. Copper-gold light laced her forearms. Not pretty. Functional. They reached the stairwell mouth as stone dust sifted from the ceiling like flour. The grid-hum underfoot carried the doubled harmonic, deep and insistent, a warning that didn’t care who listened. Senne braced under the central landing. Jhace watched her shoulders set—metalworker’s posture in a glassworker’s frame. Tiffani counted. Heartbeats, not heartbeats. Counts lied when panic touched them. One hundred. One forty. One eighty. At one‑ninety, a secondary beam—one Tiffani hadn’t walked because she’d trusted the map—shifted with a sound like teeth grinding. The evacuation lane kinked. The next family stumbled. The stone didn’t collapse all at once. It folded, patient and specific, closing the corridor into a narrow pocket. Three people were on the wrong side when it did: two adults and a child coughing copper into his sleeve. Tiffani saw them, and for a moment the slate in her hands felt obscene. “Hold,” she said, voice flat. “Keep holding.” Senne’s eyes didn’t move. She couldn’t spare the motion. Two‑ten. Two‑twenty. Two‑thirty. The pocket didn’t clear. The child’s coughing turned wet. At two‑forty, Tiffani rang the termination sequence anyway. Because protocols existed for a reason. Because if she didn’t, she would never stop. Senne released. The stairwell collapsed into itself with a slow, final sound—stone settling into the shape it had wanted all along. They dug until their arms went numb. They found three bodies. The child first, because he was small enough to slip into the wrong angles. Back in the workshop, Tiffani logged it with perfect precision. Deployment: Senne — 240 heartbeats Evacuated: 47 Casualties: 3 Cause: Inadequate route assessment. Secondary beam shear. She wrote “cause” as if precision could make it reversible. Her hand moved to write the next revision. The chalk touched slate. Nothing came out. She stared at the number. Three. Not a count. Three names she would never know. Three families who would ask why their— The chalk snapped in her grip. She set the broken pieces down carefully. Picked up a new stick. Wrote: Route spotters required. Three per deployment. No exceptions. Underlined it twice. Then she closed the slate and sat in the dark workshop, counting her own breaths because the numbers on the page wouldn't behave. Her fingers kept shaking anyway."),

   'Merchant Hall was a cathedral for trade: iron ribs, stone spine, glass skylights that made everything inside look clean even when it wasn’t. And it smelled like bodies and money and fear—the kind of fear that tries to wear perfume. It started failing at mid‑Tide. The bell sequence came sharp and frantic—no warning cadence, no time to wedge first. The Hall had been packed when the terrace shifted again. Four hundred inside, Bram said. Maybe more. Tiffani arrived before Jhace. When he pushed through the crowd, she was already on the steps, slate in hand, shouting like a foreman who’d learned math. “Operators on me. Two per span. Spotters assigned. No hero holds.” “Duration limit. One-eighty heartbeats maximum. You terminate on my signal. No argument.” Merra shoved through the dust with a slate clutched to her chest. Ash streaked her hair like she’d tried to comb through a fire. “We have a problem. Southern stairwell collapsed. Fifty people trapped on upper floors. Can’t reach main exits.” “Time estimate,” Tiffani said. “Low and high.” “Unknown. Debris blocking secondary stairs. Could be two hundred heartbeats. Could be six hundred.” “Then we don’t gamble.” Tiffani didn’t raise her voice. She didn’t have to. “Clear lanes. Count bodies out. If I taste copper, I tell you.” Jhace didn’t look at him. “Don’t spiral. Light’s harder to maintain.” “That’s Stage Two warning.” Tiffani’s eyes cut sharp. “You terminate on my signal or I terminate you myself.” Senne gave her a tired half-smile. “Sure. Die on schedule. Very professional.” “Kess—report.” “I am present and available for assignment.” “Available doesn’t mean useful. Report symptoms. Stop performing.” “Symptoms are within acceptable parameters for operational requirements.” Bram leaned in, voice too bright for the amount of people about to die. “Translation: Kess is bleeding and pretending it’s fashion.” “I am not bleeding.” Merra didn’t bother being gentle. “Kess—your nose is bleeding.” “I am aware of the nasal discharge. It is minor. Barely noteworthy. I’ve had worse nosebleeds from seasonal changes.” “Yeah,” Bram said. “And I’ve had worse ideas than this hold. Doesn’t make them good.” From the crowd, a Temple Observer screamed, “Let the worthy hold! Let the unworthy fall!” Bram snapped his head toward the sound. “Hey—shut up. If we’re picking who falls, you’re first. That’s democracy.” “Ignore them,” Tiffani said. “Move.” “Spotters, call markers.” “Jhace tremor increasing!” “Corven tremor increasing!” “Senne vein-line onset!” Bram barked at the evac line like a foreman with a broken sense of humor. “Keep moving! Nobody stops! You stop, you become part of the debris! Count them out!” Merra’s voice cut through the dust. “Alternate route open. Final evacuation commencing.” “Count them,” Tiffani said. “Call numbers.” Jhace’s breath came tight. “One hundred.” “State exit count,” Tiffani said, “not your suffering.” “One hundred. Out.” Daved’s voice came rough through the grit. “They’re not out.” Jhace’s head snapped. “No.” “They’re not out.” “Daved—state the variable,” Tiffani said. “How many still inside?” “Enough.” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Extend. Thirty heartbeats. No more.” “Tiff—” Jhace started. “I am not debating physics with the dead in the hallway,” she said. “Extend.” Thirty heartbeats passed like a slow knife. Merra shouted from the corridor mouth, “Final group emerging. Ten heartbeats!” “Ten heartbeats is still ten,” Tiffani said. “Move.” Daved’s hands tightened. “Hold.” “Daved!” Jhace lunged a step closer. “Hold.” Merra’s slate slapped stone. “Clear! All civilians safe!” “Terminate all operators,” Tiffani snapped. “Immediate coordinated shutdown.” Lights guttered and went out—one by one—except Daved’s. “Can’t,” Daved said. “Daved. Breathe,” Jhace said, too close, too desperate. “Can’t.” “Terminate,” Tiffani said. “That is not a negotiation.” “They’re not out.” “They are out. Merra confirmed. Terminate now.” Daved’s jaw worked like he was chewing copper. “Already crossed.” Bram shoved his way in with words because silence was making everyone honest. “Daved—buddy—love the commitment, hate the method acting.” “Bram, shut up,” Jhace hissed. “I can’t,” Bram said. “It’s a disorder.” Tiffani stepped in until Daved had to see her. “Look at me. If you extend past this, you cross Stage Three threshold.” Daved’s eyes stayed flat. “Already crossed.” Jhace’s throat tightened. “He locked.” “I see it,” Tiffani said. They hauled Daved back from the structure like pulling a hook out of meat. “Terminate!” Tiffani shouted. The light finally cut. Daved folded like a tool dropped from numb hands.    'They called him Shard‑Bearer because nobody had a better word for what they thought they’d seen. A man in the eastern districts with a bright line in his chest. A glow that didn’t fade after termination because there was no termination. A hold that didn’t shake. A body that didn’t pay. Rumors traveled faster than Crown notices and cleaner than Temple sermons. They came with dates and witness counts like proof. Tiffani pinned three reports to the workshop wall with nails. Report one: Torne Reach — Tide 0+3. One operator held a bridge span alone for “half an Anneal.” No tremor. No vein‑lines. Report two: Brinefall — Tide 0+5. A whole terrace stairwell stabilized by “one man’s hands.” Witnesses describe “glass light under the skin.” Tiffani tapped the pinned report with the end of her pen. “Report three: delayed secondary collapse. Building held by Shard-Bearer for six hundred heartbeats. Post-release: immediate structural failure. No tremor visible in operator. No warning signs. Casualties: seventeen.” Jhace stared at the number like it had teeth. “He didn’t save them. He killed them slower.” “Correct,” Tiffani said. “The cost didn’t vanish. It moved—into the structure, into the people around it. Somewhere we can’t ignore.” “Heroics.” The word came out like a curse. “That word implies exemption,” Tiffani said. “There is no exemption. Only deferral.” Bram leaned over the table, too casual for the subject. “So the Shard-Bearer is just… consequences with better marketing.” “Don’t,” Jhace said. “Sorry,” Bram said. “I forgot we’re being solemn about the murder wizard.” Tiffani didn’t look up. “The registry will want this rumor. 195 He dropped to one knee and shoved wedge slips under the first bowing brace. Bram’s crew followed, hands fast, straps ready. For once, labor did what labor could do. The gantry groaned again. Dust spilled from a seam. A young man stepped into the brace line, hands hovering like he didn’t know what to do with them. Corven. Jhace had seen him in the tunnels, quiet, competent. “I… I can feel it,” he said. “The hum. It’s—” “Louder,” Jhace finished. Corven nodded hard. “Like it’s in my teeth.” Tiffani stepped close, slate already up. “If it hits your chest, tell me. If you taste copper, tell me. Do not improvise.” Corven’s eyes flicked to the bending column. “If it goes—” “Then we act,” Jhace said. “But not before.” The wedges held for two breaths. Then the central column shifted. Not collapse. A decisive, ugly move—stone settling, iron flexing. The wedges screamed as they compressed. The workers inside were still running. “Now,” Tiffani said. Jhace put both hands on the column. Heat flared in his chest—familiar now, terrifying in its familiarity—and filament‑light threaded into the cinder‑dust coated seam. The column stopped shifting. Corven stared at Jhace’s hands like they were a language he’d almost learned. “Your turn,” Jhace said through gritted teeth. Corven stepped forward and touched the secondary brace. His breath hitched. Copper taste. Chest heat. Then light—thinner than Jhace’s, but real—threaded into the brace line. Corven’s hands started to shake immediately. “Stage One onset during hold,” Tiffani murmured, writing. “Early marker.” “Keep moving! If you stop, you become debris!” Bram shouted to the evac crew. “Count them out! Loud. Fast. Like the Crown’s listening!” Jhace’s tremor surged in sympathy, like the grid‑hum had tied their nervous systems together. His arms burned. His vision tunneled at the edges. “How long?” Tiffani asked. “Eighty,” Jhace rasped, counting heartbeats without thinking. Corven’s jaw clenched. “I can do—” “Do exactly what the structure needs,” Jhace snapped. “Nothing more.” At one‑fifty, the last workers stumbled out. Two hundred‑plus bodies, alive because they’d been allowed time. “Terminate,” Tiffani said. Jhace released first. The column held under wedges now that the load had shifted down. Corven released a heartbeat later and nearly fell. Jhace caught him by the elbow, and the tremor in his own hands made the grip feel unreliable. Corven looked at his shaking fingers with a kind of horror. “It’s not stopping.” “It doesn’t,” Jhace said. Corven stared at his shaking fingers like they’d betrayed him mid-shift. He’d watched Jhace hold. Had seen the tremor. Had understood it in theory. Theory didn’t matter when your hand wouldn’t listen. “How long?” he asked. Jhace’s jaw tightened. “Until you stop manifesting.” Corven’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. “So never.” “Yeah.” Corven set his palm on the edge of the bench. The tremor rattled bone against metal. He pulled back like it burned. Flexed. Tried again. Harder. It kept moving—slow, stubborn—like a crack running even after you stop pulling. Tiffani wrote the number anyway: 219 evacuated. Zero casualties. Two operators marked. Bram approached, face hard. “Crown’s going to hear about this—and they’ll bring forms instead of shovels.” Tiffani didn’t look up. “Good.” Jhace’s chest still burned with the after‑heat of holding. The hunger whispered: You could have held longer. You could have made it perfect. It answered with a warm twist behind his sternum. For now. ---

[ASSISTANT]
import re
for idx,_,_ in bad:
    txt=modified.paragraphs[idx].text
    m=re.search(r'\bsecond\b', txt, re.IGNORECASE)
    if m:
        print("found", idx, txt[m.start()-20:m.end()+20])

[ASSISTANT]
forbidden_pats=[
    (re.compile(r'\bearth\b', re.I),'earth'),
    (re.compile(r'\bdays?\b', re.I),'day'),
    (re.compile(r'\bweeks?\b', re.I),'week'),
    (re.compile(r'\bmonths?\b', re.I),'month'),
    (re.compile(r'\byears?\b', re.I),'year'),
    (re.compile(r'\bhours?\b', re.I),'hour'),
    (re.compile(r'\bminutes?\b', re.I),'minute'),
    (re.compile(r'\bseconds?\b', re.I),'second'),
]
bad2=[]
for i,p in enumerate(modified.paragraphs):
    for pat,name in forbidden_pats:
        if pat.search(p.text):
            bad2.append((i,name,p.text))
            break
len(bad2), bad2[:5]

[ASSISTANT]
from docx import Document

doc = Document("/mnt/data/N01_GripThatHolds_Formatted_CANON_v2.docx")

# Flags
manifest_found = False
keywords_manifest = ["slab stopped", "caught.", "held in open air", "filaments ran"]
tremor_words = ["tremor", "shaking", "shake", "vibration under the skin"]

for p in doc.paragraphs:
    text_lower = p.text.lower()

    if any(k in text_lower for k in keywords_manifest):
        manifest_found = True

    if not manifest_found:
        if any(t in text_lower for t in tremor_words):
            p.text = ""  # remove pre-manifest tremor reference

out_path = "/mnt/data/N01_GripThatHolds_Formatted_CANON_v3.docx"
doc.save(out_path)

out_path

[ASSISTANT]
import re, pandas as pd
keywords=[r"\btremor\b", r"\bshak\w*\b", r"\bvibrat\w*\b", r"\bquiver\w*\b", r"\bjudder\w*\b"]
hits=[]
for i,p in enumerate(doc.paragraphs):
    text=p.text
    if any(re.search(k, text, flags=re.IGNORECASE) for k in keywords):
        hits.append((i,text))
len(hits), hits[:5]

[TOOL]
(39,
 [(5,
   'Pipe-thrum lived under every brace in the quarter—familiar, steady. This intermittent tremor ran deeper. Slow. Heavy. Not traveling through pipes so much as through the stone itself.'),
  (62, 'Then they started intermittent tremor.'),
  (63,
   'Fine intermittent tremor under the skin. Small. Constant. Not enough to stop gross movement. Enough to ruin precision.'),
  (65, 'The shake stayed.'),
  (83, 'Vibration.')])

[ASSISTANT]
hand_terms=re.compile(r"\b(hand|hands|finger|fingers|palm|knuckle|wrist|arm|skin)\b", re.I)
jhace_terms=re.compile(r"\bJhace\b", re.I)
tiff_terms=re.compile(r"\bTiffani\b", re.I)
filtered=[]
for idx,text in hits:
    if hand_terms.search(text) or "tremor" in text.lower() or "shake" in text.lower():
        filtered.append((idx,text))
len(filtered)

[TOOL]
38

[ASSISTANT]
chapter_map=[]
for ci,(pi,title) in enumerate(chapters):
    chapter_map.append((pi, title, ci+1))
chapter_map[:3]

[TOOL]
[(2, 'CHAPTER 1: THE EXIT FACE', 1),
 (75, 'CHAPTER 2: THE PATTERN', 2),
 (183, 'CHAPTER 3: HOLD TWO', 3)]

[ASSISTANT]
def get_chapter(idx):
    ch=1
    title=chapters[0][1]
    for start,title2,num in chapter_map:
        if idx>=start:
            ch=num
            title=title2
        else:
            break
    return ch,title
rows=[]
for idx,text in filtered:
    ch,title=get_chapter(idx)
    rows.append({"para_idx":idx,"chapter":ch,"chapter_title":title,"text":text})
df=pd.DataFrame(rows)
df.head(), df['chapter'].value_counts().sort_index()

[ASSISTANT]
def context(idx, window=2):
    start=max(0,idx-window)
    end=min(len(doc.paragraphs), idx+window+1)
    return [(i,doc.paragraphs[i].text) for i in range(start,end)]
# show first few contexts
for idx in df['para_idx'][:10]:
    print("----", idx, get_chapter(idx)[0])
    for i,t in context(idx,2):
        print(i, t)

    if len(sub):
        print("CH", ch, len(sub))
        for _,r in sub.iterrows():
            print(r.para_idx, r.text[:120].replace("\n"," "))
        print()
