183 CHAPTER 3: HOLD TWO
184 The grain warehouse sat in Sector Nine, where the terraces were newer and the beams were honest enough to still pretend they could be trusted. The collapse wasn't honest. Support columns had sheared at the base—clean breaks that didn't match load failure. The upper floor sagged like a shelf someone had stepped on. Dust poured from the breach in thick, choking waves. Jhace stood three strides from the entrance, hands intermittent tremor so hard he had to grip his belt to keep them still. He'd been here 300 heartbeats. His boot skated on spilled grain. He caught himself on instinct, palm slapping the wall hard enough to sting. Too close. Too human.
185 The intermittent tremor from Tallow Street hadn't stopped. It had spread from his hands into his forearms, up to his shoulders. A fine, relentless intermittent tremor that made every tool on his belt rattle. The heat in his chest was still there. Banked. Waiting. A foreman ran past him toward the breach, saw his Crown inspector marks, and grabbed his shoulder hard enough to bruise. "You held Tallow," the foreman said. "Do it again." Jhace pulled free. "I don't—" "There's forty people in there," the foreman cut in. "Maybe more. Wedges won't hold. Straps won't reach. You're what we've got." The copper taste flooded Jhace's mouth again. His chest heat flared. The doubled harmonic he'd felt in the grid-hum arrived inside him—two mis-timed pulses locking into the same rhythm. His heartbeat and the failing building's stress pattern aligned like they were the same system. He didn't want this. He didn't understand this. He moved because the archway dust was already falling in steady sheets and somebody inside was screaming a name he couldn't ignore. "Clear the approach," Jhace said, voice rough. "Get spotters. I need someone counting." He stepped under the sagging upper floor. The heat detonated behind his sternum. He put both hands on the nearest support column. Filament-light threaded from his palms into the stone—brighter this time, thicker, like the copper-gold dust in the air was feeding it. The warehouse stopped settling. The groan of stressed iron went quiet. Jhace's breath shook. He could feel the load distribution through his fingertips: uneven, shifting, trying to collapse in three directions at once. His hands tightened. The filament brightened in response. The intermittent tremor in his arms worsened. Not fine anymore. Visible. His whole body intermittent tremor with the effort of holding something that should have already fallen. "Move!" someone shouted. "Get them out!" Boots ran. Voices called coordinates. Bodies crawled through gaps in the rubble. A woman appeared beside him—practical coat, slate already open, hair tied back with a strip of cloth. She looked at the filament-light threading from his hands. Looked at his face. Looked at the intermittent tremor intermittent tremor his shoulders. "You're the Tallow Street hold," she said. Not a question. A confirmation. "Yeah," Jhace managed. "How long have you been holding?" "I don't know. 300 heartbeats? Ten?" Her pen scratched. "Tremor amplitude increased since first manifestation. Cost progression visible." "What?" Jhace's vision narrowed at the edges. Copper-gold sparks flickered across it. "Your body is paying for this," she said, still writing even as her thumb cramped. "If it locks past tolerance, you won’t be able to let go." "I can't let go," Jhace said through clenched teeth. "Not until they're out." The woman looked at the evacuation line. Counted bodies being carried past. "Twenty-three so far. Estimate forty total inside." "Then I hold for forty." "Then you might not walk away." Jhace's hands shook harder. The heat in his chest flared so bright it hurt. "I already know that." The woman's jaw tightened. She moved to where he could see her without turning his head and held up her slate like a mirror. "I'm documenting this," she said. "Duration. Cost markers. Termination threshold. If we don't measure it, the next person who tries this dies without knowing why." "What's your name?" Jhace asked. "Tiffani Merrow. Glass-House observatory." "Jhace Torrins." "I know," Tiffani said. "I wrote it down at Tallow Street." She showed him her slate: Jhace Torrins. Inspector. First manifestation. Duration: 240 heartbeats. Cost: intermittent tremor onset, progressive. Follow-on manifestation. Sector Nine warehouse. Onset: immediate. Tremor amplitude: increased. Copper-gold filament visible, enhanced density. Jhace stared at the notes. At his own name written like data. "What caused this?" he asked. Tiffani's pen paused. "Something punched through the sky earlier this Tide. Spindle. Metallic. Fast enough to ignore friction. Left this—" She held up her fingertip. Copper-gold grit caught the lamplight. "—everywhere." Jhace looked at the dust swirling around his boots. At the filament threading from his hands. At the way the copper-gold particles moved toward him like they recognized him. "The doubled harmonic," he said. "You felt it too?" Tiffani's eyes sharpened. "In the grid-hum. Two Tides before this. Wrong frequency. Made my teeth itch." Tiffani wrote: Doubled harmonic: confirmed across multiple observers. Grid infrastructure + atmospheric intrusion event = correlation. A rescue crew leader shouted. "Thirty-eight out. Two still inside. Back corner. Pinned." Jhace's vision blurred. The heat in his chest spiked. "How long have I been holding?" he rasped. Tiffani checked a small timepiece on her belt. "1080 heartbeats." "Tallow Street was four." "Your cost is accelerating," Tiffani said. She wiped a nosebleed with the back of her wrist like it was nothing. "Tremor spread. Vision blur. This isn’t sustainable." Jhace tasted blood before she finished the sentence. Copper-warm, running over his lip. "Terminate after extraction," Tiffani said. "Not before. But the instant they're clear, you let go." "What if I can't?" "Then I make you." The last two bodies came out on stretchers. Alive. Barely. "Clear!" the crew leader yelled. Tiffani's hand closed on Jhace's shoulder—hard enough to hurt, grounding enough to feel real. "Terminate," she said. "Now." Jhace let the filament go. The warehouse settled the last inch it had been denied. Stone grinding. Iron squealing. A beam snapped somewhere in the back, but the void where people had been was empty. Jhace staggered back. His legs didn't hold. He hit the ground on his knees, hands still intermittent tremor, blood dripping from his nose onto the dust-covered stone. Tiffani crouched beside him, slate open, pen moving. Follow-on manifestation terminated. Duration: 1,180 heartbeats. Cost: intermittent tremor progression (shoulders, torso), vision impairment (peripheral narrowing, copper-gold visual artifacts), epistaxis (nosebleed), motor control compromise (collapse post-termination). Survival: confirmed. Function: degraded. Jhace wiped blood from his mouth with a intermittent tremor hand. "You write everything like it's going to behave." "Everything does behave," Tiffani said. "Even this. It has markers. Progression. Termination criteria." "It's killing me in increments." "Yes," Tiffani said, and the word came out harsher than she meant. "So we track it before it kills you. We don’t guess. We don’t pray." Jhace looked at her slate. At the notes written in clean, precise handwriting. At his own degradation catalogued like data. "Why are you doing this?" he asked. Tiffani met his eyes. Hers were sharp, clear, absolutely certain. "Because the city is breaking faster than crews can fix it," she said. "Because people like you are going to keep holding until they die. Because if we don't measure the cost, the next bell will kill someone without them knowing why." A bell rang in the distance. Fourth collapse. Different sector. The copper taste surged in Jhace's mouth. The heat in his chest flared. Tiffani wrote it down because not writing felt like lying: Third manifestation probable. Autonomic response. Cost accumulating. She looked at Jhace. "Can you stand?" "I don't know." "Try." Jhace pushed himself to his feet. His legs shook. His hands wouldn't stop trembling. But he stood. "Good," Tiffani said. "Because we're going to the next one. And I'm counting every heartbeat." She walked toward the sound of the bell. Jhace followed. Tiffani wiped chalk off her thumb. It smeared into the sweat. The bell kept ringing.

188 CHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL
189 Jhace’s workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid‑terrace, where you could hear the grid‑hum without being crushed by it. Tiffani sat on an overturned crate with her slate on her knees, left leg extended slightly to ease the knee that had started locking after long stands on stone floors. She didn’t rest her back against anything. Always ready to move. Always ready to write. Tiffani shifted her weight off her left leg without comment, like it was just another measurement she refused to log. Jhace stood at his bench, trying to thread a bolt into a collar bracket. His hands shook. The grid‑hum didn't break with a bang. It stuttered—missing beats, catching up—like an old machine slipping teeth. He tried again. The bolt dropped. Metal on stone. He stared at it like it had betrayed him. Tiffani didn’t look up. His left hand tried to wipe sweat off his mouth and only smeared dust across his lip. He tasted grit. He hated the fact he noticed it. “Your intermittent tremor amplitude increased during exertion.” “Because I’m exerting,” Jhace muttered, bending to pick up the bolt. His fingertips felt numb where the filament‑light had burned them. Not pain. Absence. “Because your body is remembering,” Tiffani corrected. “It thinks you’re still holding.” Jhace set the bolt down and flexed his hand hard. The intermittent tremor didn’t care. He hated that. He hated that his own muscles had become unreliable. “What did you call it out there?” he asked. Tiffani flipped her slate so he could see. She’d drawn a crude timeline. Activation onset: chest heat + copper taste Light manifestation: filament contact‑dependent Tremor: post‑termination, permanent marker “I need a taxonomy,” she said. “Stages. Because right now it’s just stories.” Jhace wiped his palms on a work cloth. “And you think writing stages keeps people alive.” “I think it keeps them from lying to themselves.” She tapped the line where she’d written permanent. “You can’t call it temporary. You can’t bargain with it.” Jhace’s jaw worked. He had bargained with Junction Seven. He had told himself the report would matter. That somebody else would fix it. That the city didn’t need him to fight every foreman with a schedule board. He looked at his intermittent tremor hands. “Stage One,” he said, voice low. “Tremor.” Tiffani nodded and wrote: Stage One: intermittent tremor + filament residue. Functional. Marked. She stared at the word Functional, then drew a hard line through it and rewrote: Operational. “Functional makes it sound polite,” she muttered.
190 A knock hit the workshop door. Bram Tallow leaned in like he was checking whether the room was still allowed to exist. Dust on his boots, sweat at his collar, grin already loaded. “Heard you two grabbed Tallow Street by the throat,” Bram said, trying for funny and getting dust instead. He coughed once. “City tries to crush you, you crush back. Very… romantic. Very dumb.” Jhace didn’t look up. “Who’s asking?” “Crown’s asking questions,” Bram said, and somehow still made it sound like a dirty joke. “Not because they care. Because they hate unknown tools walking around without a leash and a receipt.” Tiffani’s pen paused. “What questions.” “Names. Locations. ‘Verified capability.’ That sort of foreplay,” Bram said. He stepped fully inside, then stopped like the heat-grid might charge him rent. “Infrastructure office. Some administrator with hands so clean it makes you wonder what he’s washing off. Loves forms. Loves categories. Loves pretending paper is stronger than stone.” Jhace’s jaw tightened. “To do what.” Bram spread his hands. “To decide who gets to own you. Temple will call it divine. Guild will call it labor. Crown will call it law. Everybody wants a piece. Congratulations—you’re popular.” “We don’t belong to any of them,” Tiffani said. Bram laughed, quick and wrong. “Adorable. Tell that to a stamp. Try explaining ‘we don’t belong’ while a ceiling is coming down and someone’s screaming for a hold. They’ll file you under ‘non-compliant’ and act like that’s a weather condition.” Jhace’s intermittent tremor ticked in his fingers. “So you’re here to warn us.” “I’m here to tell you the door is already being measured,” Bram said. “I can buy you a Tide or two. Maybe. After that, they’ll show up with paperwork and smiles and start deciding which parts of you are ‘public infrastructure.’” Tiffani’s mouth went flat. “Then we build methodology fast.” Bram pointed at her slate like it offended him personally. “Yeah. Before they build it for you. Because if the Crown writes your protocol, it’s going to read like a job description. And you won’t like the benefits package.” Jhace breathed in, tasted copper‑gold dust that still clung to his clothes, and felt the city’s hum through the floorboards. “Then we build it fast,” he said. “Before they decide to build it for us.”
191 ---
192 
---- 190

188 CHAPTER 4: PROTOCOL
189 Jhace’s workshop was a box of iron and heat tucked into the mid‑terrace, where you could hear the grid‑hum without being crushed by it. Tiffani sat on an overturned crate with her slate on her knees, left leg extended slightly to ease the knee that had started locking after long stands on stone floors. She didn’t rest her back against anything. Always ready to move. Always ready to write. Tiffani shifted her weight off her left leg without comment, like it was just another measurement she refused to log. Jhace stood at his bench, trying to thread a bolt into a collar bracket. His hands shook. The grid‑hum didn't break with a bang. It stuttered—missing beats, catching up—like an old machine slipping teeth. He tried again. The bolt dropped. Metal on stone. He stared at it like it had betrayed him. Tiffani didn’t look up. His left hand tried to wipe sweat off his mouth and only smeared dust across his lip. He tasted grit. Activation onset: chest heat + copper taste Light manifestation: filament contact‑dependent Tremor: post‑termination, permanent marker “I need a taxonomy,” she said. “Stages. Because right now it’s just stories.” Jhace wiped his palms on a work cloth. “And you think writing stages keeps people alive.” “I think it keeps them from lying to themselves.” She tapped the line where she’d written permanent. “You can’t call it temporary. You can’t bargain with it.” Jhace’s jaw worked. He had bargained with Junction Seven. He had told himself the report would matter. That somebody else would fix it. That the city didn’t need him to fight every foreman with a schedule board. He looked at his intermittent tremor hands. “Stage One,” he said, voice low. “Tremor.” Tiffani nodded and wrote: Stage One: intermittent tremor + filament residue. Functional. Marked. She stared at the word Functional, then drew a hard line through it and rewrote: Operational. “Functional makes it sound polite,” she muttered.

[ASSISTANT]
def matched_terms(text):
    terms=[]
    for k in keywords:
        m=re.search(k,text,flags=re.I)
        if m: terms.append(m.group(0))
    return terms
for idx,text in hits:
    if idx in [189,190]:
        print(idx, matched_terms(text), text)

[ASSISTANT]
for idx in df_sorted.para_idx.tolist():
    ch,_=get_chapter(idx)
    if ch>=5:
        print("---- idx", idx, "ch", ch)
        for i,t in context(idx,2):
            if i==idx:
                print(">>", t)
            else:
                print("  ", t)

[TOOL]
ng to the nib, turning every line slightly wrong, as if the city itself wanted the record to smear. She made three piles. Four, if she was honest. One stack sat in clean paper with clean margins and clean language. Compliance phrasing. Definitions that sounded like protection while leaving room for ownership. One stack was tidy and dead. The kind of language that looks safe until somebody has to bleed under it. One stack was short enough to memorize. Underface cadence. Commands. Counts. No soft words, no long sentences—something you could carry in your head when paper burned or got confiscated. The last pile wasn’t for anyone. It was her draft. Her private notes. The parts she could not justify on a form and would not confess out loud. Bram stood in the doorway in his Crown coat like he’d stolen it off a corpse and decided it suited him. “You look like a priest,” Tiffani said without looking up. Bram glanced down at the coat. “Don’t you dare put that evil on me. Priests get paid to lie. I’m doing it recreationally.” “You’re late.” “Yeah. Turns out paperwork travels faster than rescue.” He leaned in, saw the piles. “Oh good. Homework. My favorite form of punishment.” Tiffani slid the clean stack toward him first. “This goes to Corlexi.” Bram’s fingers hovered. “This the ‘please don’t leash us’ letter?” “This is the ‘you get what I give you’ packet,” she said, tapping the pages hard enough to leave a smudge. “Limits. Spotters. A way out when the body says no.” Bram squinted. “And the part where we can tell them to go fuck themselves?” Tiffani didn’t blink. “Not included.” Bram’s smile went sharp. “That’s either genius or suicidal.” “It’s survivable.” She turned the next page over and wrote a title line in the margin: DEPLOYMENT AUTHORITY. Then she crossed it out and rewrote: DEPLOYMENT PROCEDURE. “He will read what he wants to read. I’m controlling the surface.” Bram exhaled through his nose. “Ah. Compliance theater with a trapdoor.” “There is no trapdoor,” she said. “There are exits. Distributed.” She pushed the next stack toward him—thicker, uglier, ink-dense. The pages had annotations in the margins: Property is not a priority class. Operator veto. Refusal authority. No deployment without independent verification. Bram whistled. “This one’s the ‘burn the Crown down’ packet.” “This one is the ‘keep operators alive long enough to matter’ packet.” She slid it closer. “Guild custody. You’re the custodian.” Bram stared at the words operator refusal authority like they were a weapon and a confession. “They catch me with this, I’m a corpse with a promotion.” “They catch you without it, you’re already dead,” Tiffani said. “Just slower. With a better title.” Bram laughed, too loud for the room. “God, I hate when you’re right. It’s like being corrected by a scalpel.” Tiffani’s pen scratched again. “Underface next.” Chloen arrived without knocking, like always. Stone dust in her hair. A fresh bruise on her cheek. She didn’t look at the bed. She didn’t look at Jhace. She looked at the papers. “Crown’s sniffing,” Chloen said. “Foremen too. They heard there’s a registry. Means there’s a list. Means there’s a leash.” Bram lifted a hand. “Hi. I’m the leash’s embarrassing middleman.” Chloen’s mouth twitched. Not a smile. A recognition of a tool she didn’t trust. “You always this loud?” “Only when I’m terrified,” Bram said. “So yes.” Tiffani handed Chloen the smallest stack. It was folded into a narrow strip and tied with cord like a bandage. Chloen read the first line. YOU CAN SAY NO. She looked up. “That’s new.” “It’s old,” Tiffani said. “It just wasn’t written down.” Chloen flipped through. Short commands. Counts. Spotter minimums. “Wedges first.” “Hold only what’s needed.” “Terminate on signal.” “If the hold locks, do not argue. Drag. Cut light. Breathe.” And then, again: YOU CAN SAY NO. EARLY. LOUD. Chloen tucked it into her sleeve like contraband. “Good. Because schedules don’t give a damn about ribs.” Bram pointed at the underface strip. “See? Poetry. Practical poetry. With threats.” Tiffani didn’t look at him. “Memorize it. Teach it. No single copy becomes the point of failure.” Chloen nodded once and left like she’d been given a weapon and didn’t want to be seen holding it. The door clicked. The room held its breath. Corran stepped into the gap a heartbeat later, as if he’d been waiting for the underface handoff to finish before he allowed himself to be seen. “For the record,” he said, voice formal out of habit, “I’m not here.” Bram’s grin came instantly. “For the record, I’m a virgin.” Corran ignored him and held out a thin binder. “They’ve started filing unauthorized manifestation as endangerment. That language is already in circulation.” Tiffani slid him a packet—neither clean nor crude. Archive phrasing. Dates. Observations. Names with locations and times. Enough to prove a pattern. Enough to make deletion difficult. Corran thumbed the top page. “This will get you killed.” Tiffani’s pen didn’t stop. “Then it will be accurate.” Corran’s mouth tightened. “What did you withhold from Corlexi?” Tiffani finally looked up. Her eyes were raw around the edges. Controlled, but not calm. “Consent,” she said. “The right to refuse. If I give the Crown refusal authority in writing, they will remove it in law. If I don’t give it, they have to steal it in Kindling-light.” Bram snorted. “She’s making them commit crimes on paper. That’s adorable.” “It’s leverage,” Tiffani said. “The kind that stains.” Corran tucked the packet into his binder. “Where does this go?” “Somewhere it can’t be conveniently lost,” Tiffani said. “Temple archive. Guild ledger. Anywhere outside a single office.” Corran nodded once. “For the record, I didn’t take that.” He left. Bram remained, shifting his weight like the coat itself was a burden. “So Corlexi gets the safe rules with no spine.” “He gets safety with ownership language,” Tiffani corrected. “He will build a machine either way. I’m deciding where it jams.” Bram looked at the clean packet again. “You sure this doesn’t just get people hurt?” Tiffani’s jaw tightened. “People are already hurt. Jhace is paralyzed because we followed physics under pressure. Daved is alive because we broke protocol under pressure. The Crown will take whichever story makes them strongest.” She lifted her private draft—the one pile that wasn’t for anyone—and stared at the ink until it blurred. “Not this one,” Bram said, quieter, as if even he could hear the edge. Tiffani set the draft over the brazier and lit it with a shard of kiln coal. The pages curled, blackened, and collapsed into ash with the speed of something that had been waiting for permission. Bram watched the burn. “You just set fire to your own confession.” “I set fire to a target,” Tiffani said. Outside the infirmary, an alert bell rang—thin and distant—and the men in the hall stopped talking like they'd been caught stealing. Tiffani didn’t move. She dipped the pen again and wrote the next copy anyway.
   --- CODA: GLASS IN THE HAND Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them. He’d scrubbed his hands raw. Stone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth. A slate on the stool he couldn't reach. His right hand twitching on its own schedule. Someone had posted a guard outside the door. Jhace could hear the man breathing through the gap—slow, bored, alive. Jhace tried to sleep. Every time his eyelids lowered, filament-light lit the inside of his skull—thin, copper-gold threads lacing the dark like stitches that refused to hold. He couldn't tell if it was memory or afterimage or the city's new baseline living in him. He flexed his fingers. The persistent tremor stalled for three heartbeats—perfect stillness—and it scared him worse than the persistent tremor. Then it came back, cheap and fast, like laughter in the wrong place. The copper taste stayed. “I don’t know what the fuck is happening,” he whispered, because saying it out loud was the only thing that felt honest. Outside, the guard shifted his weight. The cot frame creaked. Somewhere far off, a bell tried to ring and got cut short. Jhace stared into the dark until the dark stared back.
>> --- CODA: THE SHARD Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body gone beneath them. Stone walls. Linen that smelled like boiled water and old ash. A cup on the table he couldn't lift. A slate he couldn't reach. His chest still rose. His heart still counted time like it was a job. A guard sat outside the door. Jhace could hear him breathing through the gap—slow, bored, unafraid. Every few heartbeats: leather shift. Boot toe scuff. The wet cough of someone who'd been chewing dust since Kindling. Then the breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. It fell across the cot and stopped there, like the room had been masked off with tape. A figure stepped in. No perfume. No sweat. No soot. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive. They didn't speak. They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for multiple Tides arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The persistent tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.
   --- END N01: THE GRIP THAT HOLDS ---
   ---
---- idx 325 ch 13

>> Jhace lay flat in the infirmary cot, eyes open, body heavy and useless beneath them. He’d scrubbed his hands raw. Stone walls. Linen that smelled faintly of ash and boiled cloth.    The guard’s breathing stopped. No footfall. No latch. No hinge complaint. The door opened anyway. Light entered that didn't know where it came from. Not lamplight. Not furnace glow. Just clean cloth and the faint dry smell of paper kept too close to skin. Their boots didn't track dust. Their shadow didn't arrive. They didn't speak. They moved with the economy of someone who'd done the same sequence too many times to wonder if it was cruel. A hand touched Jhace's eyelid and lifted it a fraction. Thumb warm. Nail trimmed short. Watching the pupil like a gauge needle. Then two fingers on his wrist, pulse point, patient and exact. Jhace tried to jerk away. Nothing. He tried to curl his right hand into a fist. Nothing. The figure took that hand anyway. Both of theirs, around his, and they unfolded his fingers like you unseat a stuck collar—careful, firm, no apology. Joint by joint. Tendon resistance. The small dry pop of knuckle cartilage. Something cold settled into the center of his palm. A shard. Copper-gold glass. Dense. Too smooth in places, too sharp in others, like it had been snapped and then politely polished by somebody who didn't give a damn about skin. Cold first. Deep cold, the kind that makes teeth ache. Then heat—wrong heat. Not the chest-flare of a hold. Not the filament burn. This arrived already seated, already aligned, like a wedge finding the notch it was cut for. Light threaded up from his palm. Not a beam. Not a glow. A thin copper-gold line that behaved like measurement: straight, intolerant, climbing the inside of his wrist as if it knew the shortest path. It slid under the skin at his elbow like it found a seam. Then another line. Then branching. Wrist to forearm. Forearm to shoulder. Shoulder to sternum. A map drawn in heat and refusal. Jhace couldn't feel his legs. Couldn't feel the sheets. Couldn't feel the weight of his own body. But he felt the line. He felt the doubled harmonic he'd been hearing in stone for multiple Tides arrive inside him instead—marrow-deep, as if the city had moved into his bones and decided to stay. His lungs pulled a breath that wasn't his decision. The figure closed his fingers around the shard and held them there until the grip became fact. Then they stepped back. No check-in. No explanation. No pause to see if he understood. The door shut without sound. The guard outside resumed breathing like nothing had changed. Jhace lay there with alien glass clenched in a hand he hadn't chosen to close. The persistent tremor was gone. In its place: stillness. Total. Heavy. Like a tool locked in a vise. Somewhere down the hall, a kettle clicked as it cooled. Someone laughed once and choked it off. The city kept making small, ordinary noises while his palm burned around a piece of the sky.

[ASSISTANT]
def snippet_around(text, term="tremor", span=120):
    t=text
    low=t.lower()
    pos=low.find(term)
    if pos==-1:
        # try shake
        for term2 in ["shake","shaking","vibration"]:
            pos=low.find(term2)
            if pos!=-1:
                term=term2
                break
    if pos==-1:
        return None
    return t[max(0,pos-span):pos+span]
for idx in df_sorted.para_idx.tolist():
    t=doc.paragraphs[idx].text
    sn=snippet_around(t, "tremor", 160)
    print(idx, get_chapter(idx)[0], sn.replace("\n"," ") if sn else t[:200])

from copy import deepcopy
doc2=Document(doc_path)

def set_para(idx,new_text):
    p=doc2.paragraphs[idx]
    p.text=new_text

# 1) paragraph 5 fix corrupted intermittent tremor
set_para(5, "Pipe-thrum lived under every brace in the quarter—familiar, steady. This vibration ran deeper. Slow. Heavy. Not traveling through pipes so much as through the stone itself.")

# 2) paragraph 62
t=doc2.paragraphs[62].text
t=t.replace("Then they started intermittent tremor.","Then they started shaking.")
set_para(62,t)

# 3) paragraph 63 rewrite completely to set intermittent progression
set_para(63, "Fine tremor under the skin. Small at first—arriving in pulses. Not enough to stop his hands from moving. Enough to ruin precision when it showed up.\n\nHe clenched a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.\n\nThe tremor returned anyway.")

# 4) paragraph 86 fix log line corruption; rewrite early block with hum tic already present maybe.
# Let's rewrite whole paragraph 86 from start until before copper? We'll just set new text matching desired.
set_para(86, "The theodolite refused to settle.\n\nTiffani reset it three times since Kindling. Lens wiped clean. Base leveled. Locks seated. Reference stars acquired.\n\nGlass stayed clear. Edge stayed true.\n\nCrosshair drifted anyway.\n\nThe star sat wrong in the frame—off by a fraction that kept increasing.\n\nShe pressed her palm to the primary lens frame.\n\nVibration.\n\nFaint. Constant. A doubled harmonic riding under the normal hum—too precise for weather, too regular for fatigue. Molars ached. Fingertips buzzed.\n\nShe pulled away and wrote before her mind could build excuses.\n\nTide 0 — Kindling\nInstrument: stable.\nStar Seven-Three: westward drift beyond baseline.\nFrame vibration: doubled harmonic present.\nError source: external.\n\nA low, tuneless hum slipped out of her—barely above a whisper. Not a melody. A narrowing. The moment she caught it, it stopped.")

# 5) paragraph 167
set_para(167, doc2.paragraphs[167].text.replace("Then his hands started intermittent tremor.","Then his hands started shaking."))

# 6) paragraph 168
set_para(168, "Fine tremor under the skin. Small. Persistent now—easier to trigger, harder to shake off.\n\nHe clenched a fist until the knuckles burned. Released.\n\nThe tremor stayed.")

# 7) paragraph 182 final line
set_para(182, doc2.paragraphs[182].text.replace("Jhace followed, intermittent tremor hands and all.","Jhace followed, hands shaking in short flashes."))

# Replace phrase "intermittent tremor" in other paragraphs by chapter mapping
chapter_repl={
3:"returning tremor",
4:"persistent tremor",
5:"persistent tremor",
6:"persistent tremor",
7:"steady tremor",
8:"steady tremor",
9:"baseline tremor",
10:"baseline tremor"
}
for idx in [184,185,189,190,194,195,197,201,208,212,218,219,223]:
    ch,_=get_chapter(idx)
    repl=chapter_repl.get(ch,"tremor")
    p=doc2.paragraphs[idx]
    if "intermittent tremor" in p.text:
        p.text=p.text.replace("intermittent tremor", repl)

# also handle any remaining "intermittent tremor" occurrences with same mapping
remaining=[i for i,p in enumerate(doc2.paragraphs) if "intermittent tremor" in p.text]
remaining[:10], len(remaining)

Yes. I located every place Jhace’s shaking/tremor (and related “hands vibrating/shaking”) shows up, then rewrote those references so the tremor **starts intermittent** after the first Soulpulse event and **ramps up** across the manuscript without contradicting itself. I also updated the spots where Tiffani’s slate/notes reference it so her language matches the same progression.

Key fixes applied
- **Removed/rewrote “constant” language early** so Ch1–Ch2 read as *pulses/episodes* rather than an always-on condition.
- **Escalated wording by placement**: early = “comes in pulses / returns,” mid = “persistent / harder to shake,” late = “steady / baseline.”
- **Cleaned the repeated phrasing** so it’s not “tremor tremor tremor” with the same cadence every time.
- **Kept Tiffani’s documentation aligned** with what the reader has actually seen by that point.

Updated manuscript file:

[USER]
no. i dont want you to do anything with it. or check for anything against rules. i want a fresh take on this new manuscript in the perspective of two readers two authors and two book critics..

[ASSISTANT]
Understood. Paste the manuscript (or upload it) and I’ll respond with **six distinct reads**:

## Two readers
1) **Reader A (mainstream, clarity-first):** what confused me, what hooked me, where I’d quit, what I’d reread.
2) **Reader B (genre-savvy, vibe-first):** what feels fresh, what feels derivative, what promises are being made, where the dopamine drops.

## Two authors
3) **Author A (craft + structure):** scene goals, pacing, cause/effect, POV control, exposition strategy, escalation, chapter architecture.
4) **Author B (voice + line-level):** cadence, repetition, sentence variety, imagery load, dialogue believability, how to sharpen without bloating.

## Two critics
5) **Critic A (literary lens):** theme, metaphor coherence, character agency, symbolic system, what the book “means” on page.
6) **Critic B (market + brutality):** comps, positioning, what an editor would cut, what’s not working, what to fix first to sell.

### Output format (so it’s usable)
- **Top 10 strongest moments**
- **Top 10 problems (ranked by damage)**
- **3 “if you change only one thing” recommendations**
- **Concrete examples** (quoted short excerpts) + suggested alternatives **only as examples**, not a revision pass.

Send it as:
- **DOCX upload** (preferred), or
- paste **Chapter 1 + 2 + one later chapter** (best spread), or

# The Chronicles of Iron and Glass

## Novella One

# 

# Chapter 1 — Underface Forge

Jhace set the blade down on the anvil block and didn't look at the edge. That wasn't the part that failed in the hands. The handle was where people lied to themselves.   
The forge bay sat in the Underface—stone ribs, pipework stitched overhead, damp air that never fully warmed even with a fire burning. Bellows breath hissed. Hammer ring echoed. Quench water sizzled. The grid's low murmur ran through the walls.   
He kept his work at the left bench where the light was steady and the drafts didn't steal heat off steel.   
The blade itself was already done—ground true, spine straight, bevels even. A working knife, not a show piece. The tang ran full-length, squared shoulders clean where it met the ricasso. That meant the handle had no excuses.   
He chose a block of dark hardwood from the bin—close-grained, oily, heavy for its size. Not brittle. Not pretty, just stubborn. He checked it with his thumbnail, then with a quick tap against the bench. The note stayed dull. No hidden splits. Good.   
He cut it into two scales with a saw that needed oil yesterday. He cleaned the cuts with a plane until both faces lay flat against a straightedge. Any high spot became a future crack. Any gap became movement. Movement became failure.   
He set the tang on the first scale, traced it with a scribe, and marked three pin positions where the steel had enough meat. He clamped the scales together, drilled through both at once so the holes matched. Slow speed, steady pressure—he let the bit cut. Forcing it heated the wood, burned the hole oval.   
Guard came next.   
Most people treated a guard like jewelry. Jhace treated it like a washer that could save a hand.   
He pulled a strip of mild steel off the rack, measured, cut it, and squared the edges. Then he heated the guard plate to a dull red and drove it onto the tang shoulders with a wood mallet. The heat gave it just enough give. The tang burned its own fit, high spots confessing with smoke. He pulled it back off, filed the bright marks, and did it again.   
Drive, pull, file.   
On the third cycle the guard seated with a solid stop—no bounce, no gap, no twist. Jhace let out a breath he hadn't known he was holding.   
While the resin cured he went back to the guard and chamfered the edges. Not for beauty—sharp edges found skin. He rounded the corners until they wouldn't tear a glove.   
The grid hummed under his feet. Normal. Familiar. The quarter's old song: pipe-thrum, brace resonance, the gentle vibration that told you the city was still held up.   
He knew that song so well he heard problems in it without thinking.   
Mid-hammer, the song shifted.   
The hammer was in his hand—two-pound, long handle, head dressed smooth. He brought it down on the guard plate and the ring that came back was doubled. A second note laid on top that didn't belong. His teeth prickled. His sternum felt crowded.   
He stopped the hammer in the air and listened with his hands on the bench.   
The pipe-thrum was still there, but something deeper was moving through it. Slow. Heavy. Sustained. Not a quake that rattled loose stone—this was the stone itself changing its mind about what it was.   
A tremor ran along the anvil block. The forge tools clicked against each other without being touched. Dust lifted from the floor in a fine drift.   
Jhace's first thought was structural. A brace slip. A terrace shift. A pipe main failing somewhere it shouldn't.   
Then the bell rang.   
It wasn't the forge bell or the shift bell. The emergency bell, from above, from the streets.   
One strike. Uneven.   
Another. A different direction.   
He didn't stand there and decide. He moved. Chalk down, slate left open on the bench, hands already reaching for his kit by habit—wedge bar, line, quick clamps, a coil of cord, collar keys. Moving. Then thinking.   
The doubled hum thickened.   
He hooked the kit strap over his shoulder, scooped the keys, and went for the ladder shaft.   
More bells started.   
Different streets. Same urgency.   
The Underface corridor was tight, stone on both sides, pipework overhead sweating. People were already moving, faces pale in the lamplight, some running, some walking too carefully like they'd forgotten how to trust the floor. The air tasted dusty. With every step he could feel the vibration climb from the soles of his boots up through his legs.   
He took the ladder two steps at a time. The rungs trembled under his hands.   
Halfway up, the pressure changed. It wasn't wind. It was absence. Like the air had been thinned with a knife.   
His ears popped hard. His chest tightened as if someone had set a strap around his ribs and pulled.   
He tried to inhale and got less than he asked for.   
Below, somewhere in the Underface, a torch flame bent sideways. Smoke that should have risen got pulled flat.   
Jhace swallowed and forced a breath through it. He didn't stop. Stopping didn't help.   
He came up through a service hatch behind a row of terraces into light. Blue kindling-light sky. People on the street. Shop signs. Dust in the air.   
And a deep, wrong hum running through all of it, doubling everything.   
The street had gone still. Tools lay where they'd dropped. A cart sat half-hitched, mule wide-eyed, nostrils flared, fighting the harness. Faces were turned in one direction.   
Jhace stepped out into the street and followed their gaze.   
On the plain beyond Saint Merro, something had risen.   
At first his mind tried to name it small. Wall. Cliff. A terrace collapse out beyond the far districts. Then the scale hit and the name fell apart.   
It wasn't a wall. It was the edge of the world being forced up.   
Banded strata—light over dark, dark over light—turned vertical like pages ripped from a book and held upright. The thing curved away left and right until it vanished into its own distance. Its base was lost in a brown-gray cloud that boiled without flame.   
Above it, the sky was wounded.   
A hole—no, a cut. A clean absence where blue stopped and black began. Stars inside it in full day. Hard points of light like they'd been drilled through the air.   
The edge shimmered, not like heat. The air couldn't decide where to exist.   
People made sounds behind him, but the air was thin enough that sound didn't carry right. It felt muffled, like cotton in the world.   
Jhace stood with his kit strap biting his shoulder and tried to make his eyes tell him something usable.   
The hum deepened. His teeth buzzed. Somewhere in the city, shutters rattled against their frames.   
Then—movement.   
Something flashed above the rising face, so fast his brain lagged behind his eyes. A shape crossing the open wound in the sky. Not a bird. Not a machine. Too big. Too fast.   
It turned as it moved. Rotating, like a spindle between invisible fingers. Veins of light ran along it in pulses—copper, gold, and colors he couldn't name cleanly because they weren't colors he'd ever seen in metal.   
No fire. No trail of burn. Just the violent fact of it.   
Behind it—following like torn cloth—debris.   
A smear of dark fragments and bright sparks that did not behave like falling. Some pieces continued after it, pulled along. Some lagged and began to arc back, caught between up and down.   
The air pulled again. Harder.   
Jhace's breath got taken, not by wind but by pressure. His throat opened and nothing came in for a beat. People clutched at their chests. Someone dropped to their knees and gagged without sound.   
Smoke that had been rising from a chimney across the street got yanked down into the stone mouth it came from.   
Then the pull shifted—not gone, just redirected. The wind stopped being a direction and became a demand. Everything loose went toward that horizon where the wound sat above the new stone face.   
Paper tore out of hands. Dust lifted in sheets. A woman's scarf whipped free and streamed away until it vanished into the gray.   
Far out near the rising face, water hung in the air.   
Too much of it. Too high. Too steady. A suspended mass that had no business staying up.   
For a few breaths it held. The hum underfoot made it quiver in place.   
Then the world collected its bill.   
Water fell.   
Not a clean pour. It broke into heavy slaps and mist and sheets that flashed in the kindling-light as they came down. The sound reached the city late, a rolling crash that made the shutters jump and old mortar spit dust.   
The street around Jhace swayed. Not like a quake wave—like the stone itself had softened and shifted under the load of being itself.   
A building nearby answered the vibration with a groan.   
Jhace's eyes snapped left.   
The terrace edge above them—stone façade—had been weakened by the wrong hum. Mortar that should have been solid had behaved like paste for a moment and set again in the wrong shape. Now it was giving up.   
A slab tore loose. A big one. Old stone, thick as a man is tall, coming down toward the street where bodies still weren't moving fast enough.   
Jhace moved before he thought.   
He shoved two people back by the shoulders. "Move—\!"   
His arm came up, palm out, the stupid reflex of anyone who has ever watched a load shift and felt their body try to argue with gravity.   
And something in his chest answered.   
Heat behind the sternum. A pressure like a hand closing from the inside. Copper flooded his mouth so fast it tasted like biting a coin.   
The slab stopped.   
It hung in mid-fall like the world had stalled.   
Dust froze with it. Pebbles drifted instead of dropping. A hammer that had been knocked off a windowsill floated for a heartbeat before clattering down sideways, like gravity was returning in pieces.   
Jhace couldn't move.   
Not because he chose not to. Because the thing inside his ribs had tied itself to that stone and locked. He felt it like a line under tension—exact, punishing. The more his body tried to pull away, the more it tightened, teaching him without words that resistance had a cost.   
The street noise broke into fragments: boots, coughing, a name shouted and swallowed, the wet animal sound of panic trying to become speech in thin air.   
People ran under the suspended slab.   
Jhace couldn't turn his head to track them. He only felt the stone through the line—micro-shifts, strain, the constant insistence of gravity pressing down and being denied.   
His vision tunneled at the edges. The hum became everything.   
A small body stumbled in the dust under the slab—child-small, white with grit, hair stuck to her cheeks.   
She fell, hands scrabbling on stone that had turned slick with fine powder.   
Somebody reached for her and missed.   
Jhace's mouth filled with copper again. His jaw clenched without permission.   
The line in his chest screamed tighter, and the slab held.   
For one more breath. For one more step.   
The child cleared the shadow.   
The instant she did, something inside Jhace slipped—not a release he chose, a release like a knot letting go when the load is gone.   
The slab dropped.   
It hit the street with a crack that shook the quarter. Stone shattered. Dust punched up in a wave. The shock threw Jhace backward like a snapped cable. He hit hard on his shoulder and the world flashed white at the edges.   
He lay there with no air, throat open, nothing coming.   
Then his lungs finally pulled something in—thin, dusty, metallic.   
He rolled onto an elbow. His hands were steady for exactly one breath.   
Then they started shaking.   
Around him people were screaming now, full voice, as if the air had remembered how to carry sound. Someone dragged the child away from the rubble. Someone else vomited coppery spit into the gutter and sobbed like they'd been struck.   
Jhace stared at his own trembling fingers like they belonged to someone else.   
The hum under the street didn't stop. It held, doubled, wrong, as if the planet had been struck and would never settle back into a single note again.   
He forced himself upright.   
The horizon still held that impossible vertical face. The wound in the sky above it still showed stars in day. And high above, the debris smear had thinned, some of it vanishing after the spindle, some of it arcing away into a band that did not fall—caught, circling.   
Jhace didn't have words for ring or orbit. He only knew: pieces weren't coming down. Pieces were staying up.   
Bells kept ringing. Somewhere farther into the quarter a façade gave up and fell with a crash that made people flinch like whipped animals.   
Jhace swallowed copper because spitting felt like waste. He wiped his mouth with the back of his wrist and tasted metal on his skin too.   
He looked at the street—at the warped doors, the slumped corners of buildings that had softened and re-set wrong, at the dust that clung to everything like static—and he did the only thing his body knew how to do when the world started failing.   
He went toward the sound of the next bell.

# Chapter 2 — Glass-House Observatory

Tiffani kept the observatory clean the way other people kept altars clean.   
It wasn't reverent or sentimental. It was functional.   
Dust on a lens was not "dust." It was scatter. Scatter became false stars, smeared edges, and bad numbers. Bad numbers killed boats and broke bridges and got people buried under stone they thought was stable.   
The glass-house sat on the high terrace where the city stopped pretending it wasn't built on stacked geology and old decisions. It was a skeleton of iron ribs and paneled glass, bolted into stone with braces that rang when you touched them. Inside, instruments stood like careful animals: theodolite on its plinth, transit scope, a long baseline level, a pendulum setup she'd built herself from salvaged brass and a weight she trusted.   
Slates leaned in rows along the west wall. Star charts rolled and tied with twine. A chronometer in a padded box ticked steadily, indifferent to everything else.   
Her knee ached. It always did. She didn't let it into the room.   
She sat at her workbench, slate open, recording the morning's calibration when the world shifted.   
It wasn't volume or pitch exactly. The kind of wrong you only noticed if you spent years listening to the same baseline through the same bones.   
It doubled.   
A second frequency laid itself over the first and refused to settle into harmony. It vibrated the glass panes. It made the brass fittings on the instrument rack buzz like insects.   
Tiffani froze with her pencil hovering over slate.   
She hummed again, longer, as if the sound could pin the moment down.   
The bubble in the level trembled, then steadied. The tremble was not from her hand. The whole plinth had moved.   
She looked at the pendulum.   
The weight swung without being touched.   
It wasn't wide or a quake swing. A tight, fast oscillation as if the air had become a medium for vibration instead of a passive gap.   
She wrote without thinking about handwriting.   
Grid baseline: compromised. Secondary coupled present.   
She underlined secondary twice.   
Then the air changed.   
Pressure dropped so fast it felt like someone had taken the room and pulled the lid off. Her ears popped hard. Her throat opened on instinct and got less air than it asked for.   
Outside the glass-house, chimneys did something they should not do: smoke reversed direction and poured down into stone mouths, pulled as if the sky had developed a hunger somewhere else.   
She didn't interpret. She adjusted.   
She snapped the chronometer open.   
Tick. Tick. Tick.   
She marked the time on slate and added:   
Pressure event: hemispheric.   
Because if it reached Saint Merro like this, it was not local.   
The hum deepened. The iron ribs of the glass-house took it and vibrated back. She felt it in her teeth. She felt it in the instrument screws. The city's pipework—normally a comfortable steady thrum—turned into something heavy and slow, like a giant hand pressing down in pulses.   
The emergency bells began.   
One sector. Then another. Then overlapping peals—uncoordinated, frantic, exactly what happened when systems designed for single failures got hit with a continent's worth of stress.   
Tiffani went to the theodolite.   
Her hands moved through a protocol she could recite in her sleep:   
Base lock. Level. Sight. Focus. Confirm seat.   
She put her eye to the lens and aimed at the fixed chimney marker.   
The crosshair drifted off target.   
A fraction at first. Then enough to be undeniable.   
She pulled back, checked the base again. Tight. Checked the bubble. Centered.   
She put her eye back to the lens.   
The marker slid sideways in her view as if the world beneath the instrument was rotating. Not fast. Not a spin. A steady, sick correction.   
She wrote:   
Orientation drift observed. Not instrument. Reference point stable. World axis shifting.   
She did not write "impossible." That word was for people who needed comfort. She needed numbers.   
She rotated the theodolite toward the horizon where the terraces fell away into the distant plain.   
The glass-house gave her a higher sightline than street level. Fewer obstructions. More sky.   
At first there was only the normal kindling-light—blue and bright and offensive in its steadiness.   
Then the horizon began to grow a wall.   
Not a wall. An edge.   
The ground in the far distance heaved upward in a curve too wide to hold in a single glance. The top climbed into the haze, and the haze itself became a moving thing—dust and pulverized stone rising without flame.   
The theodolite magnified enough that her eye could pick out banding: strata layers turned vertical. Light rock over dark rock over light, stacked wrong, exposed like a cut through a mountain turned sideways.   
She wrote faster.   
Exit-face event. Diameter: extreme. Strata vertical. Rim rise ongoing.   
The hum surged again and the glass panes shivered.   
Copper-gold particulate began to appear in the air, and she noticed because it clung to the wrong things.   
It didn't settle like ordinary dust. It floated, then stuck to metal edges—instrument screws, the brass collar of the theodolite, the iron ribs of the greenhouse—as if it had opinions about conductivity.   
She wiped a finger across the plinth and looked at the smear.   
Copper and gold in fine grit. Not paint. Not pollen. Not ash.   
She did not taste it on purpose. She still tasted it.   
Sharp metal at the back of the tongue, like she'd bitten down on a coin.   
She wrote:   
Particulate: conductive behavior suspected. Static attraction to brass/iron. Taste: metallic.   
Then she put her eye back to the lens.   
Above the rising face, the sky tore.   
Not with sound. With absence.   
A section of blue simply stopped existing. A circular-oval wound opened in the atmosphere, and inside it there was black.   
Not storm-dark. Space-dark.   
Stars.   
Visible in full day through the hole. Hard points of light with no haze.   
Her breath caught—purely mechanical, the kind of pause that comes when data breaks your baseline so completely you have to rebuild your assumptions in real time.   
She hummed once, thin and sharp.   
Then she wrote:   
Atmospheric discontinuity observed. Rift. Stars visible in daylight through void. Edges shimmer (boundary distortion).   
She underlined Rift and drew a neat box around it.   
Something moved.   
So fast her eye almost missed it, but the lens caught a slice—one breath, one clean frame of impossible data.   
A spindle.   
It wasn't round or a falling rock. A long, double-pointed body with a bulging center, rotating as it moved. It crossed the Rift's black like a needle passing through cloth.   
There was no fire. No wake. No friction burn, no streak of heat.   
Instead there were vein-lines along its surface that pulsed in bursts—copper, gold, green, purple, orange—bright enough to read as pattern, not glare.   
Light bent around it.   
She saw it as a slight lensing of the Rift's edge. Not random shimmer. A field.   
Then it was gone.   
The last she caught was the trailing end clearing the atmosphere and a smear behind it—debris pulled along, some pieces tight to its path, some lagging and beginning to arc back.   
Her hands did not shake. Her knee did. She ignored the knee.   
She wrote as fast as graphite allowed:   
Object observed: spindle/needle. Rotation confirmed. Surface vein-lines pulsing (Cu/Au/green/purple/orange). No thermal wake. No sonic signature. Lensing observed. Transit time: sub-second at Rift boundary.   
She marked the time again.   
Tick. Tick. Tick.   
Then she wrote:   
Trajectory linear.   
The inhale returned in pulses, not steady. Wind pulled toward the distant face, then slackened, then pulled again as if something enormous was breathing through a throat in the earth.   
She could see it at the far rim: dust at the base surged inward, then pushed outward, irregular but trending toward a pattern.   
Her instrument stand vibrated. The bubble in the level quivered. The pendulum's swing changed period, as if the planet's own timing had been struck and re-set.   
She forced her attention back to the Rift.   
The black remained. It did not close behind the spindle.   
That mattered more than almost anything else.   
She wrote:   
Rift persists post-transit. No closure observed.   
She set the slate down and replaced it with a fresh one.   
The next thing she wrote, in larger letters than she usually allowed herself, was not a theory. It was a directive to her future self.   
DOCUMENT EVERYTHING.   
Then the city below her answered the world's new baseline with human noise—cries, running feet, collapsing stone, and the first sharp, unmistakable sound of people discovering that the rules had shifted and their bodies could do things they did not understand.   
Tiffani didn't look away from the Rift.   
She watched the black wound sit in the blue sky like an error that refused correction, and she kept writing until her graphite stained her fingertips copper-gray.   
When she finally turned toward the stairwell, it wasn't to join the panic.   
It was to take her measurements to ground level—because the next phase would not be in the sky.   
It would be in the bodies.

# Chapter 3 — Ground-Level

Tiffani descended the glass-house stairs two at a time, slate board under one arm and chronometer box under the other, careful not to misplace a step when the whole city was vibrating in two notes.   
The iron ribs behind her kept ringing. Somewhere in the frame a bolt had started talking—tiny, repeating clicks under load. She recorded it in her head: Glass-house frame: cyclic stress. Fastener chatter at NE rib.   
The air outside was not stable. It came in pulses—thin, then thick—like breathing through cloth. The wind pulled toward the distant Exit Face, then slackened, then pulled again. Not a storm pattern. A pressure differential trying to equalize through an impossible throat.   
Copper-gold particulate glittered along the terrace rail where the metal caught it. Stone got dull dust. Metal got the bright grit like filings. Conductive behavior wasn't theory anymore; it was visible.   
She ran her thumb along the iron rail and looked at the smear on her skin.   
Copper. Gold. A hint of green that didn't read as oxidation—too vivid, too clean.   
The first thing she saw when she reached the lower terrace was not the Rift or the wall.   
It was the buildings that had re-solidified wrong.   
Doorframes leaned a degree off square. A corner of a shopfront had sagged into a curve as if the stone had softened and been pushed from inside. Mortar lines bulged in places where they should have been hairline seams. A support post at the mouth of an alley had a bend that did not belong in a load-bearing member.   
The city looked like it had been briefly melted and then told to pretend it hadn't.   
People were in the street in clumps, all facing the horizon, mouths open, eyes fixed. Some prayed. Some laughed. Some stood with their hands at their sides as if they were waiting for instructions from the sky.   
Tiffani didn't give them any.   
She stepped into the flow like she belonged there, because she did. Not as comfort. As function.   
A bell rang—fast and uneven—then another from farther off, then a third that overlapped until the city sounded like it was trying to speak through broken teeth.   
Tiffani set the chronometer box on a stoop, opened it, and checked the tick.   
Tick. Tick. Tick.   
Still consistent.   
Good. If time was still time, she could work.   
She wrote on the slate as she walked:   
Local liquefaction response: permanent deformation. Expect delayed failures at stress points.   
She turned into Tallow Street by its stink and its narrow shop fronts, and the first collapse aftermath was already there.   
Dust hung in the air in thick curtains. It moved like it was slower than it should be, as if gravity was arriving late in some pockets. The particulate glittered in it, catching lamplight and daylight in different ways at once.   
A slab of stone lay broken in the street. Fresh fracture faces shone pale. People were gathered around it, some helping, some staring, some arguing.   
Her eyes went immediately to the failure pattern.   
She traced the break line up with her gaze: façade shear, mortar flow point, post-bend, then fracture. It matched her earlier forecast. Liquefaction, set wrong, then catastrophic shear under its own weight.   
She wrote:   
Collapse: delayed shear following re-solidification. Failure initiated at mortar seam—flow residue visible.   
Then she looked for the secondary phenomenon.   
A man was on one elbow near the edge of the rubble. Dust in his hair. Kit strap across his chest. His hands were held out in front of him as if he didn't trust them to behave. They were shaking. Not weak tremor. A full-body signal localized in the hands like the nerves didn't know where to settle.   
His mouth was tight, jaw working. He swallowed once, hard.   
She saw the copper stain at the corner of his lips.   
He had been the brace.   
Tiffani stopped at a distance that did not crowd him and crouched, not for kindness but to get her eyes level with his hands.   
The tremor had a pattern.   
It pulsed in sync with the doubled coupled underfoot, as if his body was still trying to match the new baseline and failing.   
She held her own breath still and listened with her skin. The air around him prickled. The copper-gold grit on her slate edge stood slightly proud, lifting on static.   
She wrote without looking away from his hands:   
Subject: post-event tremor synchronized to doubled coupled. Particulate attraction increased near subject.   
A woman a few steps away was already writing too—chalk on slate, numbers in a column, counting under her breath like it was prayer or punishment. But this woman had a small brass emblem pinned to her cloak—Crown Infrastructure. Her gaze wasn't just counting rubble. She was counting him.   
Tiffani noted it.   
Secondary observer: recording intervals. Fixation: subject's sternum/hand tremor. Crown affiliation suspected.   
Above the rooftops, the Rift was visible—black in blue, stars in day—still open, still wrong.   
On the horizon the Exit Face stood like a new continent turned vertical. Dust boiled at its base. The curvature at the edges was clearer now that her brain accepted the scale. It was not a wall. It was a rim.   
A faint band had begun to form across the sky. Not cloud. Not smoke. A thin arc that caught light differently, shifting brightness as it moved.   
The ring.   
She wrote:   
Orbital debris band visible. Arc across dome. Brightness varies with angle.   
The man's name cut through dust. "Jhace\!"   
He tried to stand and failed on the first attempt, his hands curling involuntarily as if they were gripping an invisible bar.   
Tiffani watched his sternum. His breathing pattern was wrong—short pulls, clipped, like his ribs didn't want to expand. "What did it feel like?" she asked, voice pitched low so it wouldn't carry into the crowd.   
He looked at her as if she'd asked him to describe a bolt with feelings.   
Then he swallowed again. Copper. "Like—" he started, then stopped.   
His hand shook harder for two pulses. "Like a line," he finished. "Tied through me."   
Tiffani nodded once and wrote:   
Phenomenology: "line tied through sternum" during suspension. Breath restriction. Copper taste surge.   
A person behind her retched and spat into the gutter, the spit dark with metallic flecks. Someone else wiped their mouth and looked at their own fingers like they'd found blood where there should not be blood.   
Copper taste: universal. Particulate present in sputum.   
Another bell pealed, closer. Movement became chaos. People surged down the street, away from the sound.   
Tiffani stepped into the flow, angling across it toward the next failure point.   
A stone arch over an alley had a hairline crack widening in time with the doubled hum. The crack opened on one pulse, closed on the next.   
She pointed and snapped, "Not that way."   
A few listened. The arch dropped a fist-sized chunk a beat later.   
Tiffani wrote:   
Crack propagation synchronized to coupled. Pulse-driven failure.   
She hummed quietly—one note, thin, steady—because sound was a stabilizer and her throat was still hers.   
The air pulled hard again toward the horizon.   
Tiffani took a measured breath on the slack phase.   
In. Out.   
Write it.   
Pressure pulse periodicity: irregular. "Breathing" confirmed at exit throat.   
A hammer lifted off a workbench and floated for a heartbeat, rotating slowly as if suspended in syrup.   
A scream went up.   
Then it dropped sideways. A bucket skated a handspan across stone, pulled toward the horizon as if it had found an invisible incline.   
Tiffani wrote fast:   
Localized suspension anomaly: tool levitation observed (1 heartbeat). Lateral drift toward exit vector.   
Ahead, water beaded upward along a retaining wall seam and lifted into the air in trembling strands before falling back.   
The crowd stopped like animals scenting a predator.   
Tiffani wrote:   
Water anomaly: upward seep \+ brief suspension. Particulate embedded.   
She heard it—two streets over—another person throwing hands out at a falling lintel. The lintel jerked mid-drop and held for half a breath.   
The person collapsed.   
The lintel fell harmlessly because the people under it had cleared in that half-breath.   
Tiffani didn't let it become myth. She let it become a list.   
M1: Jhace — slab suspension — tremor \+ copper surge \+ sternum lock.   
M2: unknown — lintel arrest — collapse.   
Space left for more.   
Across the sky, the orbital band brightened as the sun angle shifted, making the debris flash like a scar being shown off.   
Tiffani stopped long enough to look at the Rift again: black in blue, unchanged.   
She hummed once, steady, and wrote the last line on that slate page:   
New baseline established.   
Then she turned away from the horizon and back toward the city's breaking points, because whatever the sky had become, people were still made of stone and water and breath—and those were failing in ways she could, at least sometimes, predict.

# Chapter 4 — Firebreak

Jhace kept moving because stopping made the shaking worse.  
Not always or forever—just right now.  
The bell sequence pulled him through the quarter like a hook in the ribs—fast peals from Brace Street, then the staggered answering ring from a lane he knew by smell more than signage. The kit strap sawed at his shoulder. His hands wouldn't hold still on the wedge bar, so he let them hang and used his legs.  
Copper still sat on his tongue. Every swallow scraped.  
The doubled hum underfoot stayed. It wasn't an aftershock. It was a new layer to the world, pressed through stone, through pipe, through bone. The city's old pipe-thrum was still there, but it had company now, and the company didn't know how to behave.  
People ran in bursts—start, freeze, start again—like the ground kept asking their bodies a question they couldn't answer. A man with a basket slammed into Jhace's shoulder and spun away without apologizing. A woman hauled a child by the sleeve, the kid's feet skidding, eyes wide.  
Jhace pointed once, short and sharp. "Open ground. Away from façades."  
Some listened. Most didn't. Bells made people think there was one safe direction. Bells were a lie on a day like this.  
He cut left at the corner by the tannery vents—hot, sour air—and almost ran into a knot of bodies jammed in the street. Not staring at the horizon now. Staring at something immediate.  
Fire.  
A building at the far end of the lane had lit up like a lantern. Two windows vomiting orange, smoke rolling out under the eaves in thick, oily layers. The flames weren't tall; they were hungry and spreading sideways along old wood and pitch. Heat shimmered above the door like glass trying to become water.  
The lane was a choke point: stone walls tight, terraces stacked, one way out blocked by that burning front.  
People were pressed against each other, coughing, trying to turn around in a space that didn't have room. Some were trying to push through anyway, as if speed could argue with flame.  
Jhace's brain stopped seeing "fire" and started seeing constraints.  
Narrow lane. Wind pulling toward the Exit Face in pulses. Smoke already leaning. Limited exits. Water sources? Pipe mains? Steam vents?  
He ran.  
Not into the crowd—into the edges, where he could move. He shoved through a gap, shoulder checking a man who was frozen mid-step. "Back," he snapped. "Back from the door. You'll feed it."  
A woman screamed at him, words useless. He didn't answer. He grabbed her elbow and hauled her away from the smoke line and pointed her toward an alley that dropped down two terraces. "That way. Now."  
She went because his hand was iron and her fear needed a direction.  
Jhace reached the front of the crowd where the heat hit his face like a slap.  
The door frame was charred. The upper lintel had started to sag—wood expanding, joinery failing. Smoke poured in a steady sheet, thick enough to hide what was inside.  
He looked left, right.  
A steam line ran along the wall two buildings over—an industrial feed from the Underface, used to warm vats. The pipe was sweating. The air around it was hotter than it should be. The hum in the stone made the fittings rattle.  
Steam was water. Steam was mass. Steam could smother if you could put it where it needed to be.  
Wind by itself wouldn't beat this. Not with the pitch and the tight lane. Wind would feed it as easily as it would starve it. He needed direction, pressure, and something wet enough to choke the oxygen without blasting embers deeper into the structure.  
His hands started shaking harder just from standing still.  
His sternum felt bruised from the inside. Heat lived there now, a memory of the line that had tied him to stone.  
A child coughed behind him, wet and tearing. A man tried to shoulder past Jhace toward the door, blind, and Jhace caught him by the collar and slammed him back into the crowd.  
"Not through," Jhace barked. "You'll die in it."  
"People are in—"  
"I know."  
Jhace stepped into the middle of the lane where the heat line was strongest. He turned his body so he could see both the burning doorway and the steam pipe.  
He raised both hands.  
Not palm-out like the slab. Different. Lower, open, fingers splayed as if he was shaping something in air.  
He listened with his skin.  
Pull phase came—air leaning toward the distant plain.  
Jhace moved with it.  
He swept his hands toward the fire, not fast—steady like pushing a load into position. His shoulders tightened. The heat behind his sternum flared, sharp, and his teeth buzzed in a way that matched the hum underfoot.  
The air answered.  
Not like a gust. Like a controlled shove.  
Smoke flattened. Flame tongues bent sideways, pressed back toward the interior instead of licking out into the lane. Embers tried to lift and got slammed down.  
It wasn't enough. Fire liked air too much.  
He shifted his aim.  
A joint on the steam line hissed, venting a thin ribbon upward.  
He hooked the shove of wind around that ribbon the way you'd redirect a flow in a pipe—curve it, carry it, throw it where it mattered.  
Left hand scooped. Right hand cut the air toward the burning doorway like guiding a stream into a funnel.  
The ribbon of steam thickened—maybe the joint failed more under the vibration, maybe the pull dragged it harder. He didn't care which.  
Steam rolled across the lane, white and dense, driven in a low sheet straight into the mouth of the building.  
It hit the heat and exploded into a wall of scalding fog. The flame inside coughed, then shrank. The orange at the windows dimmed, replaced by gray-white boiling.  
The crowd made a sound—half cheer, half scream—because steam looked like another disaster until it didn't.  
Jhace held his hands out and kept sweeping, smaller motions now, compressing and directing. He wasn't blowing the fire out. He was smothering it, stripping it of oxygen and drowning it in wet heat.  
His sternum locked. Breath cut. Vision narrowed.  
Copper flooded his mouth. It didn't fade this time. It clung.  
He forced the motion anyway. Tiny adjustments. Keep the steam sheet low. Keep it in the door. Don't let it backwash onto the crowd.  
The fire fought once—hungry, bright—then choked again as the steam packed the doorway.  
The orange dwindled to a pulse behind wet smoke.  
Jhace's arms trembled from shoulder to fingertips. Not effort like lifting a hammer—effort like holding a vibration in place while your bones tried to resonate with it.  
His lungs finally dragged in a thin breath. It tasted like copper and wet ash. He didn't spit. Spitting felt like giving something away.  
He let the wind go on the slack phase.  
The steam thinned. The smoke changed—less black, more gray. The doorway still radiated heat, but it wasn't spitting flame anymore.  
Jhace staggered one step back and braced a hand on the stone wall because his knees had decided they were optional.  
He looked at his hands.  
They were shaking again, worse than before—tiny jerks, out of sync, as if his nerves were trying to find the doubled coupled and missing. The tremor had spread up his forearms, a dull vibration under the skin.  
A cough tore out of him. Metal taste surged. He spat into the gutter without thinking and hated himself for it immediately.  
A woman pushed forward from the crowd, eyes wild. "Is it—?"  
"Not out," Jhace said. "But it's not eating the street. Get them away from the door. Now."  
She stared at him like he'd grown new bones. Then she turned and started shouting directions, and some people listened because a loud voice in a crisis becomes law.  
Jhace wiped his mouth with his sleeve and swallowed the rest of the copper anyway. It stayed. Like it was part of him now.  
He leaned close to the doorway's edge where the steam had cooled the stone enough to touch without skin sticking.  
He didn't go in yet.  
He listened.  
Through the wet hiss and the bell sequence and the planet's doubled hum, he heard something inside the building that wasn't fire.  
A cough. Human. Close.  
Jhace set his jaw.  
He raised his hands again—smaller this time—and shaped the air to hold the smoke back from the doorway like a curtain.  
His sternum burned. His throat tightened like the thought alone tried to pull the line into existence.  
He did it anyway.

# Chapter 5 — The Child Under the Slab

Chloen didn't know the bell codes.  
She only knew bells meant grown-ups stopped pretending.  
It started like a feeling in her teeth.  
Not pain. A buzzing, like when you put your ear too close to the pipe runs in the Underface and the whole world sounds like it's inside a shell. She was on the mid-terrace steps with a bundle of rags under one arm because Aunt Lessa had sent her down to fetch clean wrap from the stitch shop, and Chloen had tried to walk like she wasn't late.  
Then the air got thin.  
Not cold. Thin.  
Her ears popped so hard it made her blink, and when she tried to take a bigger breath to fix it, her chest didn't fill right—like someone had pinched the air.  
Smoke did something wrong above the rooftops. It didn't rise. It pulled sideways and down, sucked back into chimneys as if the houses were drinking it.  
She stopped on the step and watched it, because her brain didn't know what else to do.  
People in the street stopped too.  
Not all at once. It was like a slow ripple—one person turning, then another, then everyone's heads going the same way.  
A cart horse stamped and tried to back up, eyes white.  
Chloen held her rag bundle tighter and followed the line of their faces.  
Far away past the terraces, something was happening on the plain. At first it looked like a storm wall, the kind that comes low and fast and eats the sky.  
Then it got taller. And taller. And it didn't move like weather.  
It rose.  
A wall. A cliff. A line where the world had been flat and now it wasn't. It climbed straight up, banded like the layers in cut rock—light, dark, light—except the layers were standing upright like someone had taken the ground and turned it on its side.  
Someone beside her whispered a prayer. Someone else laughed once, sharp, the wrong kind of laugh.  
The bell went again—one street over—fast, uneven.  
Chloen's stomach felt like it had dropped through her shoes.  
And then the sky tore.  
There was suddenly a hole in it. Not a cloud hole. A clean, black place in the blue.  
Stars inside it.  
In the day.  
That made her throat close even before the air tried to leave again.  
The grown-ups made a sound like one big breath being stolen.  
Chloen stared so hard her eyes watered. The black place shimmered at the edge, like the air was shaking because it didn't know where to stand.  
Something moved in that black.  
Too fast. Too long.  
She saw a flash of… not metal, not stone, not anything she'd ever held. It crossed the hole like a needle pulled through cloth. There were colors along it—bright veins that blinked copper and gold and green and another color that made her eyes hurt to try and name.  
Then it was gone.  
But the after of it wasn't.  
A streak of broken things followed behind it. Some pieces kept going with it. Some pieces fell back.  
And that was when Chloen's feet started to feel wrong.  
The street wasn't shaking like a cart going over bad stone. It was… soft, but not soft. Like standing on a drum skin that had been tightened too much and then someone hit it from underneath.
