The houses creaked.  
Mortar dust sifted out of seams like flour.  
People started moving all at once then. Not neat. Not ordered. They bumped and shoved and grabbed each other by sleeves.  
Chloen got pushed off the step and nearly fell. Someone's elbow hit her and she yelped, but the sound came out small and thin because the air was still wrong.  
Chloen tried to back up toward the wall of the nearest building because walls were safe.  
A crack sounded above her head.  
Stone was coming loose.  
A whole piece of building face—big and flat and heavy—tearing away from the terrace edge above and starting to fall.  
The street turned into screaming.  
Chloen ran.  
Her legs didn't feel like hers. The ground made her stumble like it was shifting under every step. Dust got in her mouth. It tasted like pennies.  
A man grabbed her arm—too hard—and yanked her sideways. "Keep moving\!" he barked, and shoved her forward.  
Chloen tried. But the crowd was thick and confused and the air kept pulling toward the plain like the sky had a mouth open somewhere.  
The slab shadow hit her.  
All light went wrong under it—dim and gray with dust swirling like smoke.  
Chloen's foot caught on something and she went down hard on her hands and knees. Her palms stung. The rags slid out of her grip.  
She looked up and saw the underside of the falling stone fill her whole world.  
She tried to scream and it stuck.  
She tried to get up and her arms shook like they'd forgotten how.  
Boots pounded past. Someone's heel clipped her shoulder and she fell sideways.  
The stone was too close. She could see the grain in it, old tool marks, dark seams where water had lived.  
She thought, stupidly, I don't want to die under a wall.  
Then the stone stopped.  
Not slowed. Stopped.  
It hung above her like a ceiling that had decided to hover.  
Dust froze in the air—enough that Chloen noticed. Copper-gold flecks glittered and stuck to her eyelashes.  
The street noise thinned into fragments. Boots. Coughing. A name being called.  
Chloen pushed up onto her knees.  
Someone's hand appeared—an adult hand, dusty, knuckles scraped—and pulled her elbow.  
Chloen tried to stand and her legs buckled. The hand yanked again and she got her feet under her.  
She looked up.  
The slab hovered above, held in mid-air. People ran underneath it, ducking even though it wasn't moving.  
Chloen's eyes tracked past the stone and found the man out in front of the crowd where nobody else stood.  
His arm was out, palm toward the slab.  
His face was tight. Concentrated like he was lifting something heavy and trying not to show it.  
His throat worked like breathing hurt.  
His eyes were on the stone. On the line of fall.  
On her.  
That made her move.  
Chloen stumbled forward out from under the slab.  
Her feet slid in dust. Her lungs pulled in air that tasted like metal. Her eyes watered so much she could barely see.  
She made it to the edge of the shadow.  
Light changed.  
One step, and she was out.  
She turned her head without meaning to, to look back.  
The man's arm was still out.  
For a heartbeat, nothing changed.  
Then his face flickered like something inside him slipped.  
The slab dropped.  
It slammed the street with a crack that punched up through Chloen's legs and into her stomach. Stone shattered. Dust exploded outward.  
The shock threw her forward. She hit the ground again, hard.  
Sound came back all at once.  
Screams. Shouting. Crying. The wet cough of dust in lungs.  
Chloen scrambled to her feet, half-blind, choking. Her tongue tasted copper like she'd been sucking on a nail.  
She looked back again.  
The man had gone down too—thrown backward like the stone had snapped a rope tied to him. He was on one elbow now, trying to sit up, and his hands were shaking so hard it looked like he couldn't hold them still even if he wanted to.  
A woman nearby—dust in her hair, slate in hand—was staring at him like she'd found a crack in the world and was trying to measure it.  
Chloen didn't know any of that. She just saw: he'd done something impossible, and it had hurt him.  
The bell rang again from somewhere deeper in the quarter.  
Chloen turned and ran toward the nearest doorway because doors meant shelter, even when shelter was a lie.  
She could still taste copper.  
She could still feel the hum in her bones—two notes fighting.  
And when she closed her eyes, she saw the man's outstretched palm holding up a slab that should have killed her.

# Chapter 6 — Parapet / Tsunami Turn

Jhace kept his hands low where he could see them.  
Not because he thought they'd betray him again. Because they already had.  
Steam still clung to his sleeves. His kit strap bit his shoulder in a line that matched the sternum pressure like they'd agreed on a shape. The bell sequence had dragged them up terrace after terrace until the streets thinned and the city's edges stopped pretending they were edges and admitted they were a face—stone cut away into a drop so steep it made your stomach go light.  
They reached a break in the upper terrace wall where the parapet had cracked and slumped. Someone had already cleared rubble back from the lip. People stood in a row, shoulder to shoulder, staring down.  
Tiffani came up beside him without asking permission, slate tucked tight to her ribs. The copper grit on its edge lifted and settled in the wind pulses like filings on a magnet. Her knee hitched once. She ignored it and leaned forward.  
Chloen arrived a breath later, small in the gap between bodies, hair dust-matted, eyes wide in a way that wasn't just fear anymore. She didn't push. She slipped into the space that opened when people shifted away from the edge.  
Below them, the mountain face dropped into a basin of terraces and distant roofs. A city lay out there—larger than Saint Merro by a margin you could feel in the grid lines, in the breadth of its streets, in the density of its stacked stone. Smoke ribbons rose from chimneys. From this height it looked intact. From this height everything looked survivable.  
The sea did not.  
It came as a moving wall—water climbing out of itself, the surface torn into foam and debris. Not a wave you could swim in. A mass. A slab with no seams. It carried dark things inside it—wood, roof beams, something long and pale that might have been a mast or might have been a spine. It advanced with a certainty that made the bells feel pointless.  
Jhace watched the lip of the water where it met air. He watched the leading edge chew the ground ahead of it into mist. He watched the way the whole body of it leaned forward like it was being pulled by a hand you couldn't see.  
His teeth prickled on the doubled hum even from up here. The stone under the parapet vibrated in two layers. One old. One new.  
Chloen made a thin sound behind her teeth and swallowed it.  
Tiffani didn't speak. Her pencil moved.  
Jhace didn't look at her slate. He looked down at the city and counted what would fail first.  
Outer terraces. Lower supports. Any bridge span with a crooked seat from liquefaction. Any wall that had set wrong and was waiting for load.  
The wave kept coming.  
It should have hit the city like an axe.  
It didn't.  
Right before contact, the water changed direction.  
It wasn't slowly or like a tide choosing a channel.  
It turned.  
The leading edge of the wave kinked sideways as if a blade had cut a groove through it. Foam that had been rushing straight at the city snapped into a new vector, sliding along an invisible plane. The whole mass followed—tons of water re-routing as one body, redirected away from the densest district and forced into the open basin to the left where there was stone and scrub and nothing stacked for people to die in.  
Spray erupted where the redirected water scraped over ground. A new roar rolled up the face, late and heavy, vibrating through the parapet and into Jhace's sternum like a hand pressing to see if he'd learned.  
The city below still took damage—outer edges flooded, streets swallowed—but the central core, the thick-built heart, did not get hit straight on. The wave passed it like a fist missing a jaw by inches.  
A few people at the parapet cried out. Not words. Just noise. Relief and disbelief braided together.  
Jhace didn't make a sound.  
He watched the water. He watched the moment it obeyed something other than gravity.  
He felt his mouth fill with copper in anticipation, not after.  
That was the part that scared him.  
His hands twitched once, a small jerk toward the parapet, like the body remembered the line and wanted to throw it again. He clenched his fingers into his palm and held them there until the tremor settled back into its baseline pattern.  
Someone down there had done it.  
Someone had put a hand on a moving ocean and told it no.  
Jhace's stomach tightened with a hunger that wasn't food and wasn't heroism. It was the clean, brutal urge to be capable at that scale—the same itch that made him re-seat a guard until the bright marks stopped lying, only now the guard was the world and the mallet was whatever lived behind his sternum.  
He hated how much he wanted it.  
He glanced sideways without turning his head all the way.  
Tiffani's pencil was moving fast but controlled. Her slate was angled toward the horizon, not toward him. Her eyes were on the redirected flow, tracking, measuring. The copper grit on the slate edge lifted in tiny waves and settled again.  
Good.  
He was glad—suddenly, sharply glad—that she couldn't write down what he was thinking. Glad there wasn't an instrument that could read hunger and file it under M3.  
Because if she wrote it, it would become real in a way he didn't get to deny.  
Tiffani murmured, not to him, not to the crowd. To the slate. "Vector shift."  
She wrote it like it was enough.  
Then, quieter: "Force applied."  
Jhace swallowed copper. It scratched.  
Chloen leaned forward, gripping the broken parapet stone with both hands like she could keep herself from falling into the sight of it. Her knuckles were white under dust.  
"Did… did the water—" she started.  
"It turned," Jhace said. Flat. Confirming. No comfort.  
Chloen blinked hard and looked between the redirected basin and the city that had just not died.  
"How?"  
Jhace didn't answer because he didn't have one that wasn't a lie.  
Tiffani did not look up from her slate. "Not wind," she said, as if correcting herself before anyone else could. "Not terrain. Too late. Too clean."  
Jhace's jaw worked. He tasted metal again.  
He imagined his own hands doing it.  
His sternum tightened like the thought alone tried to pull the line into existence. Heat climbed under his ribs.  
He forced his breath into a measured pull on the slack phase. In. Out.  
He did not move his hands.  
A guard captain nearby was marking cracks with chalk. His slate-dust assistant watched Jhace with sharp eyes, making notes in a small book. Crown emblem on his sash. Jhace looked away, but the itch in his palms didn't stop.  
Down in the basin, the diverted wave slammed into rock and scrub and the underside of lower terraces. The impact threw up a fan of mist that glittered with copper-gold flecks even at this distance, catching the kindling-light in flashes that made the air look full of filings.  
Jhace watched the mist drift and settle and watched, for one heartbeat, a section of spray hang wrong—suspended, slow—before gravity reclaimed it.  
Field residue.  
A use.  
A cost.  
Somewhere down there, someone was paying.  
Jhace turned his head enough to look at Tiffani's slate at last—not to read it, but to see where she was looking.  
Her pencil stopped for a single beat.  
She wrote one new line, crisp as a stamped mark:  
M3: Mass-water vector deviation. Subject unknown.  
Then she added, smaller:  
Applied at last-second proximity.  
Jhace stared at the words and felt his hunger sharpen into direction.  
Somebody near the waterline. Somebody close enough to get drowned if they missed. Somebody who had touched the ocean and lived.  
He looked back down the face, scanning for a human scale point in the chaos. Too far. Too much spray. Too many moving pieces.  
Chloen's voice was small. "Is it… like what you did?"  
Jhace's hands flexed once, involuntary. The tremor tried to rise.  
He kept his eyes on the basin. "Bigger," he said.  
Tiffani's pencil ticked against the slate once—one controlled tap. A signal she used instead of swearing.  
"The same," she corrected, still not looking at him. "Only more."  
Jhace let that sit between his ribs like a weight.  
Below, the city's bells—distant, faint—began to ring, delayed by distance and wind. A new sequence, thin from far away, layered over Saint Merro's closer peals.  
Two cities ringing. One planet humming in two notes.  
Jhace took one step back from the parapet, not because he was done looking, but because if he stayed there he would start reaching.  
His sternum was already too awake.  
He wiped his mouth with his sleeve and tasted steam, ash, copper.  
He was glad Tiffani couldn't write what he was thinking.  
He wasn't glad he was thinking it.  
Somewhere down there, someone had moved an ocean.  
And now he knew it could be done.  
A small metal hook clattered on stone near his foot—something from a brace timber that had fallen loose. Jhace's hands flexed before he could stop them. For a fraction of a second, the hook lifted off the pavement, just an inch, then dropped again as he released the thought.  
No one saw it.  
But he felt it.  
And the copper taste surged again, sharp and insistent, like his body was trying to warn him about what he was becoming.

# Chapter 7 — Upper Terrace Sorting / Meeting

Tiffani kept her slate closed.   
Not because there was nothing to write. Because there was too much, and the act of recording would change what people thought they were doing. If she raised graphite now, they'd start performing their panic for the page. They'd turn grief into testimony.   
She wasn't ready to be that kind of authority.   
The upper terrace had become a sorting place.   
Not formally or by decree—by physics.   
Rubble moved downhill when it could. People moved uphill when they could breathe. Anyone who could still walk drifted toward the wider stone flats where the terraces broadened and the parapets were intact enough to lean on. The broken streets below funneled survivors toward this ledge the way water found low points.   
Volunteers had formed lines. Hands passed stone from hand to hand. Smaller pieces went into baskets. Larger slabs were propped with scavenged timber and wedge bars. Someone had tied a length of rope across a crack in the paving to keep the crowd from stepping onto a section that shifted on the doubled pulse.   
A woman with a carpenter's hammer had marked doorframes with wax. A boy with a long pole tapped stones and listened for the hollow note of delamination. Someone—older, with a scarf over their mouth—had turned a smashed cart into a supply table and was rationing water in cups like it mattered, because it did.   
Tiffani moved through the worklines without stepping into anyone's path. Her eyes tracked loads and fractures the way her hands tracked stars. She counted by seeing.   
One: a retaining wall seam opened a hair each pulse.   
Two: a lintel with a new shear line, chalked in red.   
Three: a brace post that had set crooked after liquefaction—compensating now, but only by being loud about it.   
Chloen sat on the edge of a low wall with two other children, legs tucked up, hair still dust-matted. An older woman was wiping her face with a damp cloth. The girl's eyes tracked movement constantly, like she expected the stone to start falling again without warning.   
Tiffani watched her for three breaths, then looked away. She would not become a watcher who offered nothing.   
Jhace was farther off, near a collapsed arch, using a wedge bar and a section of pipe as leverage to lift a beam enough for two men to drag a body out. Alive. Coughing. Covered in soot and dust, but moving under their own command.   
Jhace's hands shook when he wasn't working.   
When he worked, they steadied.   
The tremor wasn't "fear." It was a signal problem. A resonance mismatch.   
Tiffani did not approach him.   
Not yet.   
The first reunions happened near the supply table. A woman with a split lip found her mother by accident and collapsed into her arms with a sound that was half laugh and half sob. A man held a child to his chest and repeated the child's name like the repetition could rewind time.   
Tiffani didn't write any of that down.   
It would be theft.   
The meeting assembled without announcement.   
A semicircle of people collected near the parapet where the ground was stable and the view kept everyone honest. No one wanted to look at the Rift constantly, but it sat above the Exit Face like a pressure in the corner of the eye, refusing to be ignored.   
A city factor arrived first—cloak dusted, rings on both hands, trying to stand like authority still meant anything. Two brace captains came with him, both with cracked knuckles and the practiced gaze of people who spent their lives keeping stone from deciding otherwise.   
A priest came too, not in full ceremonial robes—practical wool, a throat wrap, a small iron emblem at the collar. He wasn't smiling. He wasn't preaching. He looked tired in the way that meant he'd already done triage with words and found words insufficient.   
Two engineers from the pipe quarter took positions at the edge of the semicircle. One carried a ledger. The other carried nothing but kept rubbing his fingers together as if grit had gotten under his skin.   
And then there was Tiffani, standing slightly apart with her slate still closed, because the moment she began recording, the meeting would change shape around her.   
They spoke in fragments at first. "Entry?" one engineer asked, voice rough. "If the wound is an exit, where's the other end?"   
"Not here," the brace captain said. "We felt it before we saw it. The pull came from elsewhere."   
The factor turned his head, looking down the mountain face toward the far city and the basin where the water had redirected. "That wave should have—"   
"It didn't," someone cut in. A woman with dust in her hair and a wrist wrapped in cloth. She was not official. She was simply loud enough to earn space. "It turned."   
A murmur ran through the semicircle. Agreement. Disbelief. The kind of sound people make when reality refuses to behave.   
The priest lifted a hand slightly, not to silence them but to gather them. "We need to name what we can name," he said. "And leave the rest for later."   
Good instinct.   
Naming stabilized panic. But naming incorrectly was worse than leaving blank space.   
Tiffani opened her slate.   
Not to write everything. To anchor what mattered.   
She wrote one line at the top and let the people see the shape of it without reading it aloud:   
Observed: Rift persists. Exit Face stable. Pressure pulses. Doubled coupled baseline.   
She did not write M1, M2, M3. Not yet.   
The brace captain's eyes flicked to her slate. He swallowed once. "Doubled," he repeated, as if tasting the concept. "So it isn't settling. It's… permanent?"   
Tiffani didn't answer with certainty because certainty was a claim. She answered with measurement.   
"It hasn't decayed," she said. "Not in the time I've been listening."   
"How long?" the factor demanded.   
Tiffani glanced at the chronometer in her mind, not in her hand. She didn't need to open the box to know the intervals. "Since first pressure drop," she said. "It continues."   
One of the pipe engineers spoke without lifting his gaze from the ground. "The mains are singing wrong," he said. "Valves chatter. Pressure reverses in cycles. The whole grid—" His voice broke, then steadied again. "It's like it's being pulled."   
Tiffani nodded once. "Toward the throat," she said, and watched their faces register the word.   
No one liked calling it a throat. It made it sound alive.   
The brace captain shifted his weight. "What about the floating?" he asked. "The slab that—" His eyes flicked briefly in the direction of the street where it had happened. He didn't say "miracle." He didn't say "magic." He said the thing itself. "That hold."   
The semicircle tightened a fraction. Every survival meeting had a moment where the topic turned from stone to people, because the temptation was always to believe bodies could fix what buildings could not.   
Tiffani's knee pulsed. She ignored it and kept her voice level.   
"It happened," she said. "More than once."   
The factor's rings flashed as he clenched his hand. "Who?"   
Tiffani looked at the brace captain, then the engineer, then the priest, and decided something that felt like a line being drawn.   
Not all truths belonged to a crowd. "Untrained," she said instead. "Stress-triggered. Short duration. Cost immediate."   
"What cost?" the priest asked softly.   
Tiffani thought of Jhace's hands shaking. Thought of copper taste in every mouth. Thought of the tool that had levitated for one heartbeat and the way the grit had lifted along her slate edge when certain bodies were nearby.   
She wrote a second line where they could see the words form.   
Symptoms observed: tremor, copper surge, sternum pressure, breath restriction.   
The pipe engineer swallowed hard. "Copper," he repeated, and looked at his own fingertips as if they were stained.   
A woman in the back spat into the gutter and wiped her mouth with shaking hands.   
Tiffani didn't comment. She let the observation stand.   
The brace captain asked the question nobody wanted to voice. "Is it a gift?"   
The priest's jaw tightened. The factor looked relieved at the possibility. The engineers looked afraid of it.   
Tiffani chose the only honest answer. "It is a change," she said. "With a cost."   
Jhace stepped into the edge of the semicircle without ceremony. Dust on his sleeves. Kit strap still across his chest. His hands weren't steady, but his eyes were.   
He didn't speak at first. He looked past them, down the mountain face, to the basin where the redirected water had torn new channels through scrub and stone.   
Tiffani watched his throat work once.   
He said, "Someone moved the ocean."   
The semicircle went still. Even the doubled hum seemed louder against the sudden silence.   
The factor's mouth opened, then closed, as if words had failed to queue.   
The brace captain exhaled through his nose. "That's not possible."   
Jhace didn't look at him. "It happened."   
Tiffani opened her slate wider. Not for the crowd. For herself.   
She wrote, small and crisp:   
M3 confirmed by multiple witnesses: mass-water vector deviation.   
Then she closed the slate again.   
Because this meeting was not for making a taxonomy. It was for keeping bodies alive through the next hour.   
Tiffani lifted her eyes to the semicircle and spoke in the same tone she used when instructing an apprentice to tighten a base lock. "We have three immediate problems," she said. "Structure. Air. People."   
She pointed with two fingers, not dramatic, just directional.   
"Structure: mark cracks. Keep crowds off edges. Prop what can be propped. Do not stand under façades."   
"Air: when the pull comes, breathe shallow. When it slacks, fill. Teach it. Make it habit."   
"People: reunite who you can. Move the injured to stable flats. Keep water rationed. Keep fires from spreading."   
The factor stared at her as if he resented taking orders from someone without a title.   
Then a brace captain nodded once, sharp. "We can do that."   
The priest said, quieter, "And the rest?"   
Tiffani looked at the Rift without turning her head fully, feeling it as a pressure in the world rather than a sight.   
"The rest," she said, "we measure."   
Then she took a breath on the slack phase, listened to her knee register load and not fail, and moved back into the worklines where stone was passed hand to hand and names were called until families stitched themselves back together out of dust and luck.

# Chapter 8 — Names

Jhace waited until the crowd noise thinned enough that words didn't get swallowed by it.   
It happened in a pocket between tasks—after the meeting broke into work assignments, after the factor's people started pretending they were in charge again, after the brace captains peeled off to mark cracks and post rope lines.   
Tiffani stood near the parapet with her slate tucked under her arm, not writing, just watching the way the pressure pulses pushed dust into slow drifts and then pulled it back. Chloen hovered a step behind her like she'd decided proximity was safer than distance.   
Jhace came up on their left, wedge bar still in his hand because putting it down felt like tempting fate.   
He didn't soften his voice. There wasn't time to dress it.   
"Who are you?" he asked.   
Chloen flinched like he'd snapped at her. Tiffani didn't. She shifted her weight to spare her knee without letting it look like a concession.   
"Tiffani," she said. No preamble. No apology. Like she was labeling a specimen jar.   
Jhace nodded once, filed it away. "You live up here?"   
"Above the high terrace," she said. "Glass-house."   
Observatory, then. That explained the slate, the chronometer in her head, the way she looked at the Rift like it was a problem that could be measured into submission.   
Jhace looked down at the girl.   
Chloen's chin lifted a fraction, stubborn reflex. Her hands were still dirty. She'd stopped wiping them like it mattered.   
He didn't ask gently.   
"And you."   
She blinked, then said, "Chloen."   
The name sat wrong in his mouth for a beat—too soft for the day it had been forced into.   
He nodded anyway. "Chloen."   
He let the wedge bar rest against his thigh. The tremor in his fingers tried to start up and he ignored it by giving his hands something to do.   
"Good," he said, and meant: good you're alive.   
He didn't add the rest. He didn't know how to say it without making it sound like ownership.   
Tiffani's eyes were still on the horizon. "You're Jhace," she said.   
He looked at her.   
"I heard it," she added, because she knew how that sounded.   
Jhace's jaw worked. "Yeah."   
A pressure pulse rolled through the parapet stone. Dust lifted. Copper-gold grit along the slate edge rose a hair and settled again.   
Jhace watched it happen and took the metallic hit at the back of his tongue like a reminder. He swallowed. Stupid copper.   
He forced his attention away from his own body and back onto the practical.   
"There's a woman," he said. "Slate. Chalk. Counting."   
Tiffani's pencil hand twitched once at the word counting. Not interest—recognition.   
"I saw her twice," Jhace went on. "Once after the slab. Once again before the fire line. She wasn't looking at the Rift. She was looking at my hands."   
Chloen's gaze flicked between them. She'd seen that kind of watching too. Kids notice hunters.   
Tiffani finally looked at Jhace. "Describe her."   
"Dust cloak," he said. "Hair tied back. Writes fast. Counts like it's keeping her upright."   
Tiffani held that for a beat, then nodded once. "Not mine," she said. "Not from the glass-house."   
"Do you know who she is?" Jhace asked.   
"I know what she is," Tiffani said, and her voice went flatter. "A recorder. Maybe a registrar. Maybe just someone building a frame so she doesn't break."   
Jhace stared past her shoulder at the worklines forming—hands passing stone, names being called, rope being tied. He let the implication settle where it wanted.   
Someone else was already trying to make a system out of what had happened.   
He didn't like that. He also understood it.   
"That's three of us," Jhace said, more to himself than them. "One who holds. One who measures. One who survives."   
Chloen's mouth tightened. "I didn't—"   
"I know," Jhace cut in, not unkind. "You did."   
She stopped. Swallowed. That taste was getting old, even for him, and he hadn't been the one under the slab.   
Tiffani's gaze went back to the horizon. "The others will start asking," she said.   
"Let them," Jhace said. His hands flexed once, then stilled on the wedge bar. "But if that slate woman shows up again—"   
Tiffani's pencil tapped her slate once. Controlled. A marker.   
"—you tell me," Jhace finished.   
Tiffani didn't answer with words. She didn't need to.   
Chloen edged closer to the space between them, and Jhace didn't move away.   
The bells kept ringing in uneven waves. The doubled hum kept sitting under everything like a second heartbeat.   
And for the first time since the sky tore, Jhace had names attached to the bodies next to him—names he could shout the next time stone decided to fall.

# Chapter 9 — The Gap

Chloen didn't like looking up.   
Looking up was how you saw what wasn't there anymore.   
She stood on the wide stone flat where people kept passing rubble, where voices had turned into lists and directions, where the air pulsed thin-thick like someone breathing through a cloth. She stood with her arms wrapped around herself because her sleeves were still damp in places and her skin didn't feel like it fit right.   
Up the mountain face, Saint Merro stacked into itself—terrace on terrace, doors above doors, stairs that pretended they were streets. The city always looked like it had been built by someone who hated level ground.   
Now it looked like it had been bitten.   
Corners that used to be straight leaned. A whole section of parapet was missing. There were new scars where stone had slid and then stopped as if it had remembered rules mid-fall. Smoke rose from three places. One column was darker than the others.   
Chloen found the line where her street had been.   
Not by a sign. Signs were gone or turned sideways.   
By the bend in the terrace wall and the shape of a roofline that used to look like a crooked tooth.   
Her home had been tucked under that bend. Two rooms. One small window that never opened right. Aunt Lessa's pot hooks by the stove. A shelf with the chipped bowl they kept even though it was stupid to keep a chipped bowl.   
Chloen stared at the bend.   
The roofline tooth was missing.   
There was a gap where stone had dropped and filled itself in with dust.   
Her mouth did the metallic thing again—like someone had pressed a coin to the back of her tongue.   
She swallowed it because there was nowhere to put it.   
That taste was getting old. "Okay," she told herself, but quiet, because saying it loud would make it a lie people could hear. "Okay."   
She didn't cry.   
Not because she was brave. Because her body hadn't decided what kind of sound it wanted to make yet, and she didn't want to start and not be able to stop.   
She pressed her thumb hard into the heel of her other hand until it hurt. Pain was simple.   
A man passed carrying a basket of broken brick. He said, "Watch your feet, kid," without looking at her.   
Chloen watched her feet anyway.   
The stone underfoot wasn't steady in the way it used to be. It was steady like a tired person holding their face still so no one asked questions. Every few breaths the doubled hum ran up through her shoes, and the grit on the ground did a tiny lift-settle like it was answering something she couldn't hear.   
She imagined the city as a giant animal trying not to shiver.   
She didn't like that either.   
She turned her head so she didn't have to keep staring at the gap where her home had been.   
People talked around her like she wasn't there. Grown-ups did that when they didn't want to be careful.   
"—said the whole quarter folded," a woman whispered, voice tight. "Just… sank and set wrong."   
"Not folded," someone argued back. "Rang. Like a bell. I felt it in my teeth."   
"Entry was the ocean," a man said, too loud. "My cousin's out on the coast. If he's alive, he'll tell you—"   
"You don't know it was the ocean," another snapped. "You don't know anything."   
Chloen listened anyway, because listening was how you survived grown-ups.   
"—the sky's still open," someone murmured, and everyone who heard that line went quiet for a beat like they'd all looked up without wanting to.   
"Stars in the day," an old man said, and spat into the dust. "End-times."   
"Not end-times," a brace captain barked nearby. "Not if you keep your asses off the cracks I marked."   
A child cried. A baby, too little to understand the difference between hunger and fear.   
Chloen wanted to go find the older woman who'd wiped her face earlier and ask for the cloth again. The cloth felt like a promise that skin could be clean.   
She didn't move.   
If she moved, she might drift toward that gap. If she drifted toward the gap, someone might ask where her people were.   
Where's your aunt, Chloen?   
And if she answered, she would have to say: probably under a roof that doesn't exist anymore.   
So she stood still and practiced being invisible.   
She counted instead.   
One. A man with blood on his sleeve walking like he didn't want anyone to see it.   
Two. A cart turned into a table with cups lined up in a neat row.   
Three. Rope across a crack, tied tight enough it bit into stone.   
Four. A pile of slate fragments, chalk-smudged, like someone had been writing when the world decided not to.   
The wind pulled on the inhale phase and the whole crowd leaned without noticing. Chloen leaned too because the air told her to.   
When it slacked, she filled her lungs like she'd been taught and hated that she'd been taught anything today.   
She heard Tiffani's voice before she saw her.   
It wasn't loud or comforting. Just clear. "Keep crowds off edges. Prop what can be propped."   
Chloen shifted her eyes sideways.   
Tiffani was a few paces away in the semicircle that had broken into work groups. Slate tucked under her arm again. She stood like the ground was an instrument and she didn't trust it.   
Jhace was near her, wedge bar still in hand, looking at the city like he was trying to see through stone. "Someone moved the ocean," Jhace said, and even though Chloen had already watched it happen, hearing him say it made her stomach go cold again.   
Someone else answered—one of the important men with rings, maybe, or one of the brace captains. Chloen couldn't see who. She could hear the shape of the words.   
"Not possible."   
"It happened," Jhace said.   
Chloen kept her eyes on the ground. If she looked at them too long, they might look back and then they might see her face doing the thing it did when it wanted to break.   
Tiffani said, "We measure," and Chloen didn't know what that meant except it sounded like something you did when you couldn't fix.   
Jhace's voice lowered. "And if the slate woman shows up again—"   
Slate woman.   
Chloen's skin prickled. The woman with chalk. The counting. The eyes on Jhace's hands.   
Chloen didn't like her. She didn't know her. She didn't like the way the woman's attention had felt like fingers.   
Tiffani's pencil tapped once. A small sound. A decision sound.   
Chloen hugged herself tighter. She could still feel the weight of the slab above her in her bones, even though it had fallen and broken and was done.   
She looked up again, fast, like touching a bruise.   
The bend in the terrace wall. The missing tooth roofline. The gap.   
Her throat tightened.   
She kept it to herself.   
Aunt Lessa most likely dead.   
No one needed to know that right now. It wouldn't bring her back. It would only make Chloen real to the wrong people, and today was already full of wrong.   
Chloen stared at the skyline until her eyes watered, then blinked hard and looked away before the water could turn into something else. "Okay," she told herself again, quieter.   
Then, because she didn't know what else to do with the ache, she whispered a second thing—one she didn't believe, but needed anyway. "Still here."   
And she stayed on the stone flat where names were being called and found and lost again, listening to grown-ups argue about sky wounds and ocean turns, while she practiced the only skill she had left that felt dependable.   
Not crying.   
Not speaking.   
Not letting anyone see what the gap had taken.

# Chapter 10 — The Shard

Mobel didn't sit in the center of the room.  
Old habit. Old math.  
Center meant eyes. Eyes meant judgment. Judgment meant cages.  
He took the edge—back against a stone rib where the mortar line ran clean and straight. The kind of straight that made his teeth unclench. Purity liked lines. So did prisons. He did not pretend that contradiction did not matter. It mattered more than most prayers.  
His assistant—Kerrin, if you wanted the name said out loud—set a dented cup on the floor between them and did not ask him if he wanted it. Kerrin had learned the difference between offering help and offering pressure.  
The cup held water that tasted like grit.  
Everything tasted like grit now.  
Kerrin crouched, not kneeling. Close enough to be heard. Far enough to not be cornering him. Familiar choreography.  
Mobel watched his own hands for a moment. Not shaking. That was new. That was suspicious.  
"How do you feel?" Kerrin asked.  
Mobel let the question sit. He did not like questions that tried to turn his insides into language. In prison you learned that language could be used against you faster than fists.  
He swallowed once. Metallic again. Not copper, not coin—just that clean hit at the back of the throat like the air itself had teeth.  
"Alive," he said.  
Kerrin nodded like that was a complete medical assessment, because with Mobel it was.  
Outside, the terrace worklines kept moving. Stone scraping. Rope creak. Somewhere a bell rang once and then stopped, like even the bells were tired.  
Kerrin's eyes flicked briefly to Mobel's chest, then away. He did not stare. He never stared at the thing that made Mobel's skin crawl.  
The puncture was not visible anymore. It had not been visible for long. But Mobel still felt where it had been—the exact point under the sternum, slightly left, as if someone had tapped a nail through him and then pulled it out without leaving a hole.  
Except it did not feel removed.  
It felt installed.  
Mobel shifted his shoulders against the wall. The doubled hum ran up the stone and into his back like a second pulse trying to teach his bones a new pattern.  
Kerrin asked, "Was it… you? The water?"  
Mobel exhaled through his nose. A laugh wanted to come out and did not. He was not amused. He was still trying to decide if he should be afraid of himself.  
"I don't know what it was," Mobel said. "I know what happened."  
"That's what I'm asking."  
Mobel stared at the cup on the floor. Water sat still. Innocent. Like it had never been a moving wall that could erase cities.  
He said, "I was down there. Near the line where the basin drops. I heard it before I saw it. You don't hear water like that in the holy books. It isn't 'rushing.' It's a weight. It's a whole world deciding to move."  
Kerrin did not interrupt. That was another learned thing.  
Mobel continued anyway, because once he started, stopping felt worse.  
"I saw the wave coming. It wasn't a wave. It was a verdict." He rubbed his thumb along the edge of his palm, feeling callus. Familiar proof. "I did not think. I did not pray. There was not time to make myself clean enough."  
Kerrin's mouth tightened a fraction at that. Purity people talked about cleanliness like it was a weapon. Mobel knew better. Cleanliness was a hunger. It could save you. It could also make you cruel.  
Mobel said, "My body moved first. Hand up. Like I was going to push it."  
"You can't push water," Kerrin said quietly.  
"I know." Mobel's voice sharpened for a beat—old prison heat. Then he let it go. "That's the point. I did not know what I was doing until it was already in motion."  
He closed his eyes and saw it again: foam, debris, the leading edge chewing ground into mist—and then the turn. The impossible kink. The whole mass obeying a direction that was not terrain and was not wind.  
Mobel opened his eyes.  
"When it caught," he said, "it felt like the world grabbed my ribs from the inside. Like a line tied through me. Except—" he searched for the right words and hated that he did not have them "—except it was not pain first. It was access."  
Kerrin's eyebrows lifted. "Access."  
Mobel nodded once. "Like a door that had always been there and somebody finally stopped pretending it was a wall."  
He swallowed. Stupid metallic. He did not spit. Spitting felt like giving something away. "And then it hit," Mobel said. "Not the water. The possibility."  
Kerrin leaned in slightly despite himself. Then corrected his posture back to neutral. He had been with Mobel through prison conversion, through vows, through relapses into anger. He knew when Mobel was close to something dangerous.  
Mobel did not soften.  
"I could feel the water like it was part of me," he said. "Every strand. Every weight. I could have stopped it. I could have taken all the world's water and… put it somewhere else."  
"That's—"  
"I'm not done." Mobel's tone cut, then steadied again. "It was not a thought. It was a fact in my body for a breath."  
Kerrin went quiet.  
Mobel stared at the water in the cup. He imagined it lifting. Shaping. Becoming. A ridiculous show—animals made of water, a whole forged scene rendered in steam because part of him still thought in metals and heat even after he had sworn vows.  
He could have done it. That was the worst part.  
He could have done anything, and the only thing that stopped him was not virtue. It was ignorance.  
"So I redirected it," Mobel said. "Because I did not know what else I was capable of. Because if I tried to do anything fancy—" he almost said pretty and bit it off "—if I tried to control it too much, I might have failed and drowned everyone anyway."  
Kerrin's voice came careful. "That sounds like restraint."  
Mobel looked at him. "That sounds like fear."  
Kerrin held his gaze.  
Mobel broke it first. Another old habit. In prison you did not hold eyes too long unless you wanted a fight.  
His fingers drifted to his sternum without him deciding.  
Kerrin noticed. Did not comment.  
Mobel pressed lightly, as if checking for a bruise. Nothing on the surface. Underneath: heat. Pressure. A foreign solidity that was not flesh.  
"The shard," Mobel said.  
Kerrin's head tilted. "The—"  
"The thing." Mobel refused to name it like naming made it holy. "When it went through Te'Oga… something broke off. That's the best way I can explain it. A piece. A sliver. It hit me here."  
He tapped the spot, once.  
"I felt it. Like being punched with cold iron. Then it dissolved."  
Kerrin's voice dropped. "Dissolved."  
Mobel nodded. "No wound. No bleeding. Just… gone. Except not gone." He swallowed again, jaw tight. "It's like it's inside now. Not lodged. Not stuck. Integrated. Like it punctured the part of me that was sealed."  
Kerrin sat back on his heels, processing. "And you think that's why."  
Mobel did not answer immediately.  
Across the terrace, someone shouted a name and someone answered. A family stitched back together by sound alone. Mobel listened to it and felt something twist in him that had nothing to do with the doubled hum.  
"I think it's why I could touch the wave," Mobel said. "I think it's why it felt like I could touch everything."  
Kerrin waited, then asked, "Do you think you're—changed?"  
Mobel's mouth pulled tight. "We're all changed."  
Kerrin nodded. "By what principle?"  
Mobel's laugh came out sharp this time, a single breath. "You want the doctrine."  
"I want the map," Kerrin said. "So you don't drown in it."  
Mobel stared at the cup again.  
Form. Purity. Wholeness. Entropy.  
Four arguments that never ended. The universe held together by disagreement, the old priests said. Contradictions in balance. An engine that did not stall because it could not agree on the direction.  
Mobel had believed in Entropy when he was a criminal. Ruin was honest. Ruin paid you back quickly. Ruin did not pretend to forgive.  
Purity had taken him in prison because Purity offered something ruin never did: a line you could walk and not hate yourself every step.  
He did not pretend Purity made him good. Purity made him intentional.  
"I don't know," Mobel said. "Not yet."  
Kerrin's gaze stayed steady. "You moved a wall of water."  
Mobel's throat worked. "I redirected it."  
"Still."  
Mobel closed his eyes for half a breath and felt the phantom of it again—the door, the access, the terrifying scope.  
He opened his eyes.  
"Purity," he said, and the word tasted like both prayer and chain. "If I'm anything, I'm that. Because what I felt was not destruction. It was not shaping. It was not harmony. It was… cleansing force. A current. A pressure. A straightening."  
Kerrin nodded, slow. "Purity aligns."  
Mobel's jaw tightened. "Purity also burns."  
Kerrin did not disagree.  
Mobel's fingers curled once on his knee, a small involuntary grip like he was holding a handle that was not there.  
He pictured the ocean again, obeying. He pictured taking it and setting it down gentle as cloth. He pictured failing and drowning the city anyway.  
He tasted metallic again, and this time the annoyance rose hot.  
Stupid metal.  
He forced a breath on the slack phase.  
In. Out.  
Then he looked at Kerrin.  
"If it happens again," Mobel said, "don't let me get pretty with it."  
Kerrin's expression did not shift. "Pretty."  
"Don't let me make a show," Mobel said. "Don't let me forget what I am."  
Kerrin's answer was immediate. Familiar. A promise given before, in different forms.  
"I won't," he said.  
Outside, the terrace worklines kept moving. Stone passed hand to hand. Names called. Rope tightened. The world held itself together with labor and contradiction.  
Mobel sat at the edge of the room and listened to the doubled hum inside the wall, and tried to decide whether feeling alive was a blessing or just a different kind of sentence.  
A runner passed the open doorway outside, shouting over the terrace noise. "Assembly at the Basin Stadium. Names and skills. Bring your household."  
Kerrin's head turned toward the sound. "They're building lanes," he said. "Registrars. Wax tabs."  
Mobel felt the shard under his sternum answer, a quiet heat like a warning.  
Kerrin added, lower, as if the words themselves might summon attention: "They're asking about the ones who held things. The ones who turned water."

# Chapter 11 — The Sequence

The bells shifted sequence.  
Three short strikes. A pause. One long strike. Two short.  
Stone carried it clean. Pipe ribs carried it wrong, vibrating the pattern into extra notes. The doubled hum laid under the metal like a second hand trying to write over the first.  
People stopped moving. Heads turned toward the nearest station tower.  
Tiffani stopped too. Slate under her arm. Pencil in her fingers. She counted the spacing, not the meaning.  
Three. Pause. Long. Two.  
Station notice.  
It was not collapse warning or fire. Not brace call.  
The long strike meant: listen.  
A runner climbed the tower steps and pinned a sheet to the board below the bell yoke. Another runner held the corners when the wind pulled.  
Tiffani watched the paper flap, then watched the hands that pinned it. Copper-gold grit lifted along the slate edge and settled again.  
Jhace was two paces to her right. Wedge bar on his shoulder like a yoke. His hands stayed low. Visible.  
Chloen stayed close, small shadow tucked into the gap between their bodies and the crowd.  
The bell tower runner shouted.  
"By Crown order. Assembly at the Basin Stadium. Live address. Corlexi of Infrastructure. Two hours from first notice. All able bodies. Bring injured if transportable. Bring names for allocation."  
A second runner repeated it down the street, voice already hoarse.  
Tiffani did not write it. She copied the timing in her head.  
Corlexi.  
She had heard the name once in the glass-house, spoken with irritation by an older observer who disliked administrators. She had never seen him.  
The crowd broke into motion in fragments.  
People argued. People complied. People dragged carts. People carried children. Rope lines formed across cracks as if the street itself had become a ledger.  
Tiffani stepped to the slack phase and walked.  
Jhace moved with her without asking.  
Chloen moved because the bodies around her moved.  
At the next station, the same sequence rang. The same sheet hung. The same order.  
The Crown could not speak across the grid anymore, but it could still ring metal.  
Tiffani tracked the failures that made that true.  
Long-range line: dead. The doubled coupled rode the pipes and ate signal. Brass joints chattered under the pulse. Copper runs vibrated into noise.  
Local bell: usable. Barely.  
Messenger: human bandwidth.  
She kept those columns in her head the way she kept star positions.  
They reached a wider street where the terraces flattened and the stone was less fractured. A guard post had been set at the corner with a temporary marker: a pole, a hanging lantern, a scrap of white cloth tied in a knot.  
A young guard stood beside it. Crown colors on his sash, but the sash was too new, the stitching too bright. Helmet under one arm. Eyes tired anyway.  
He lifted a hand to stop the flow, then thought better of it and stepped aside to guide instead.  
"Stadium route," he called. "Keep to the marked flats. Avoid the red chalk. If you see rope, you do not step over it."  
His voice tried for authority and came out as practice.  
Tiffani watched his posture. Not trained yet. Assigned anyway.  
He spotted Jhace's wedge bar and grinned like he'd found something that made sense.  
"Good," the guard said. "Tools. Love a man who brings his own teeth to the problem."  
Jhace's mouth tightened. Not a smile. Not not.  
The guard's grin widened as if that was permission.  
"Name's Bram. Interning with the Crown. Which is a polite way of saying they put me where the stone falls and hope I learn fast."  
He paused, then added, "I did not choose the phrase 'interning with the Crown.' The phrase chose me."  
Tiffani felt her knee register load and adjusted her stride. Bram's voice was noise in the measurement.  
Jhace looked at Bram's sash. "Intern," he repeated.  
"Yes." Bram nodded too hard. "Like a nail that isn't sure if it's headed for wood or thumb."  
He started to wave them forward, then stopped mid-gesture like he could hear his own metaphor.  
"That one was bad," Bram said. "Forget I said thumb. The Crown would prefer no thumbs were mentioned."  
Jhace's eyes narrowed. "Finish it," he said.  
Bram blinked. "What?"  
"The joke," Jhace said. "You started it. You finish it."  
Bram's mouth opened, closed, opened again. "It's not a joke. It's a warning. A poorly hammered warning."  
"Still," Jhace said.  
Tiffani looked away from both of them. The doubled hum under the street held steady. The air pulsed thin-thick. This mattered more than whatever Bram was doing with words.  
Bram exhaled. "Fine. Intern is like a nail. If I go in straight, I hold. If I go in crooked, I split the work and everyone blames the hammer."  
He made a face like he regretted all of it.  
Jhace nodded once, satisfied in a way that made no sense.  
Chloen made a small sound. Not quite a laugh. It still existed in her chest and surprised her when it came out.  
Tiffani's pencil hand twitched. She did not write "Bram: humor coping mechanism" even though the line wanted to exist.  
Bram waved them into the moving column. "You three with me. Small group. Easier to keep alive."  
"You're escorting," Tiffani said.  
"That's the word." Bram glanced down at his sash like it might correct him. "Escort. Guide. Herd. Herd is wrong. People are not goats." He paused. "Most people are not goats."  
Jhace made a sound through his nose.  
Tiffani kept walking.  
They moved through a corridor of terraces toward the basin flats. The stadium sat below, a broad bowl carved into the stone, its seating rings cut for sound carry. The Crown used it for proclamations and festival trials. Today it was a loud throat for logistics.  
On the way, they passed a notice board with fresh chalk marks under the posted sheet: arrows, times, assembly colors.  
A second bell station rang in the same sequence. The pattern held.  
Bram kept talking like silence was a fault.  
"If anyone feels like they're going to pass out on the inhale, you wait for the slack phase. Shallow breaths. Stay upright. Don't sprint your lungs just because the air is misbehaving."  
Tiffani tracked the accuracy: coarse, but usable.  
Jhace watched the street edges. Load paths. Crack marks. Rope lines. He moved like he was always half a step away from catching something.  
Chloen watched Bram instead. Not the words. The hands. He kept them open. Visible.  
They reached the last ramp down to the stadium bowl. The crowd density increased. Rope lines became lanes. Guard posts multiplied. Someone had hung glass horns on a scaffold to carry a speaker's voice.  
Bram slowed and pointed with two fingers.  
"Stay together," he said. Then, as if he could not stop himself, "Unless you want to try the thrilling sport of getting lost in a disaster crowd. It's like hide-and-seek except the seeker is bureaucracy."  
Jhace's eyes cut toward Chloen.  
"Where'd you come from anyway?" Bram asked, still in that half-joking tone. "Where's your mum? Your guardian?"  
Chloen went still.  
Tiffani felt the change before she saw it. The air around the girl tightened. The grit on her slate edge lifted and did not settle on the first pulse.  
Bram's grin faltered as the question landed and failed to be a joke.

# Chapter 12 — The Question

Chloen could still feel the word guardian like a thumb pressed into a bruise.  
It was not the first time someone had asked.  
Usually it was a neighbor. A shopkeeper. A teacher who wanted a signature.  
Today it was a guard with a joke face.  
She did not look at Bram. Looking at people made them real, and real people asked follow-ups.  
She looked at his boots instead. Leather cracked at the toe. Ash in the seams. A smear of blue paint on the left heel.  
She tried to answer in the smallest way.  
"Lost," she said.  
The word came out flat. Not crying. Not lying. Not telling.  
Bram blinked. The grin fell off him like it had been hooked on wrong.  
"Ah," he said, and the joke engine in him revved and stalled. "Right. Sorry. That was... I stepped in it. Like, literally. Like a horse in a market. Except there are no horses. You get it." He stopped. "You don't have to get it."  
Jhace made a sound through his nose that might have been a laugh if it had been any other day.  
"Finish it," Jhace said.  
Bram stared at him. "What?"  
"The horse thing," Jhace said. "If you start a stupid metaphor you don't get to leave it bleeding in the street."  
Tiffani's jaw tightened. She looked past all of them toward the rope lanes and the scaffold horns.  
Bram opened his mouth, realized the rescue attempt would only make it worse, and tried anyway.  
"Fine," he said. "I stepped in it like a horse in a market, except the market is a disaster and the horse is me and the..." He waved a hand. "The point is: I'm sorry."  
Chloen's mouth twitched. She hated that it did. She kept her face still. But a small breath escaped her nose.  
Bram saw it and looked relieved like he'd managed to not make things worse by accident. He had. Barely.  
"Lost," Jhace repeated, softer than his voice had been in the forge. Not gentle. Just acknowledging.  
Chloen nodded once. She did not add aunt. She did not add gap. She did not add the missing tooth roofline.  
The bell sequence rang again, closer now. A station inside the stadium bowl. The sound bounced off stone and came back in layers.  
Tiffani spoke without looking at Bram. "Your assignment is lanes. Keep them moving."  
"Yes, ma'am," Bram said automatically, then reconsidered the word ma'am like it tasted wrong. "Yes. Right."  
They merged into the rope lane.  
Chloen watched the way the ropes were tied: double wrap, hitch, wedged under a stone cleat. Someone competent had done it. That was comforting in a small, stupid way.  
She walked between Jhace and Tiffani because that gap felt safer than the open.  
Bram walked ahead, turning his head every few steps to check the lane behind him. He kept making small hand signals to other guards. Not fancy. Just functional.  
Chloen kept her eyes on his hands.  
A woman in the lane beside them whispered, "They say the Crown's bringing in registrars." Another answered, "They already are." Another voice: "My cousin saw one counting. Chalk on slate."  
Chloen swallowed. The metallic taste showed up again, uninvited, like someone tapping her teeth with a coin.  
She thought: stupid taste.  
The lane curved down. The stadium opened.  
Stone seating tiers, cracked in places. Scaffolds built fast with pipe and strap. Glass horns mounted on frames. A platform at the center with a speaking rail.  
Crowds filled sections by color tabs tied to wrists: red cloth, blue cloth, raw twine for those without anything.  
Bram stopped at a checkpoint and lifted the rope.  
"Three together," he told the guard on duty. "One adult, one adult, one..." His eyes flicked to Chloen and he did not say kid. He said, "one minor."  
The guard nodded. No questions.  
Chloen felt her lungs unclench a fraction.  
Bram guided them to a section where the stone was chalk-marked stable. "Sit if you can," he said. "If you can't, stand where you won't tip someone into the next row. You'd be amazed how many adults forget gravity when they're anxious." He started to say something else, then stopped.  
Chloen slid into a seat at the end of a row. She kept her hands in her lap.  
Jhace stood, not sitting. Tiffani sat only after testing the stone with her heel and timing the slack phase.  
Bram backed out of the row and lifted the rope again so the next group could pass.  
Chloen watched him move away.  
People became bodies again. Not faces. Not questions.  
She kept her eyes on Bram until the crowd swallowed him.  
Bram had made jokes in front of priests, in front of brace captains, in front of a factor with rings on both hands.  
That question had still been the dumbest thing he'd said all day.  
He walked the rope lanes like they were a maze he had to solve without stepping on anyone. He kept his hands visible. He kept his mouth shut for a whole count of ten, which for him was an achievement and a sign of failure.  
He replayed it anyway.  
Where'd you come from. Where's your mum.  
Idiot.  
He'd meant: are you safe. Do you have someone. He'd delivered it like a joke because jokes were his way of making sharp things less sharp.  
It had landed sharp anyway.  
He adjusted a rope knot. Tightened it. Retied it because the first tie had been sloppy. He did not like sloppy.  
A guard captain in a crown tab passed and nodded at him once. "Intern. Keep them moving."  
"Yes, sir," Bram said.  
The captain moved on.  
Bram glanced back toward the section he'd placed them in.  
He found them by shape.  
Jhace: standing, wedge bar still in hand like a limb.  
Tiffani: sitting, slate closed on her knees, eyes forward.  
Chloen: small in the seat, shoulders tight, eyes too big for her face.  
Bram felt something in his chest that was not doubled hum and was not prayer. Just weight.  
He wanted to go back and say: sorry. He wanted to say: you don't have to answer me. He wanted to say: if you need water, I can get water.  
He did not.  
Because guards did not fix losses. Guards moved bodies.  
He kept walking the lanes.  
A bell struck the notice sequence again. Three short. Pause. One long. Two short.  
People quieted in waves.  
Bram stopped at the edge of a rope lane and watched the crowd settle into something like attention.  
He watched Chloen stand when the people around her stood, then sit when they sat, matching the tide of bodies with the same careful invisibility she'd carried all day.  
He watched her look once over her shoulder, not at him, just back at the path they'd walked as if checking whether the question had followed.  
He felt guilty anyway.  
He told himself he'd do better.  
He told himself he'd ask fewer questions.  
He told himself he'd keep his jokes aimed at the sky, not at kids.  
Then he saw the platform at the center of the stadium and the glass horns angled toward the stands, and he felt the larger truth settle in.  
Today was not going to stop handing him chances to fail.

# Chapter 13 — The Bowl

Bell stations at four corners of the stadium bowl rang the notice sequence in sequence.  
Guards raised ropes to open lanes. Guards lowered ropes to close lanes.  
People moved like water finding its level—older bodies toward the back where stone was stable, families with children toward the center where help could reach them, workers toward the edges where exits stayed clear. Rope lines formed not just from guard direction but from instinct, like bodies knew where the weak points would be before the stone showed them.  
A woman near the front tier pressed her palm flat against the seating stone every few breaths. Knuckles white. Jaw working. Copper grit clung to her lower lip. She swallowed it without spitting—spitting felt like giving something away.  
A forge-guild man stood with arms crossed, fingers tapping against his bicep—right hand on the first pulse, left on the second. His body remembered how to be whole in a world that had split.  
Children sat on parents' laps, small hands gripping sleeves, eyes wide. The strange comfort of seeing adults finally admit they didn't know what came next.  
The stadium had been built for contests—foot races, strength trials, endurance holds. Stone seats curved to carry sound from the center platform up to the highest tier. Now it held a different kind of contest: who could stay upright longest while the ground learned a new rhythm.  
Copper-gold grit dusted every surface, catching light when the sun hit it just right. It stuck to sweaty palms, clung to damp foreheads, glittered along the edges of slates and tools. People wiped it off without thinking, then stopped when they realized wiping did nothing. The grit was part of them now.  
A brace captain moved along the aisle lines, placing his boot on unstable stones to test them. His knee popped with every step—a familiar companion that had learned to sing in time with the doubled hum. He didn't grimace. He pressed his heel down harder and made a small chalk mark that looked like a bird in flight.  
The glass horns on the scaffold shivered on their mounts, vibrating with the undertone that never quite settled. An engineer tightened a strap joint, not because it was loose but because the ritual of adjustment made his hands stop shaking.  
A mother passed a clay cup hand to hand down her row. Not water. Weak tea made from stores they'd thought would last longer. Each sip was measured. Each swallow deliberate. When the cup reached the end of the row, it came back with a finger's worth left. The mother drank it without hesitation. Her tongue tasted metal anyway.  
The air pulled thin. Everyone's breath hitched. Then it slacked, and a collective inhale ran through the tiers like a wave moving uphill.  
Nobody spoke.  
Nobody needed to.  
They were all breathing the same wrong air, feeling the same split vibration in their bones, licking coin. For the first time since the sky tore open, the city wasn't a collection of strangers. It was a single body learning how to survive with a new heart.

# Chapter 14 — The Briefing

The stadium didn't just hold bodies—it held breath.  
Tiffani felt it in her knee first. The doubled hum had settled into a pattern, but her joint remembered the old rhythm and kept trying to match it. Every few pulses, the mismatch sent a sharp twinge up her spine—a reminder that her body was still fighting the new baseline.  
She found a position where she could see both the platform and the nearest aisle line. Her slate stayed closed across her lap. Not because there was nothing to write. Because some things couldn't be measured without changing them.  
The horn-glass amplifiers glinted in the afternoon light, their copper wire bracing shivering like spiderwebs in a breeze. The doubled hum lived in those wires now—a second heartbeat thrumming under the world's skin.  
Corlexi stepped onto the platform without fanfare. Clean coat. Straight seams. An iron emblem—a square frame with a single diagonal bar—pinned at his collar. Form. His hands rested on the podium rail, steady, but tendons in his forearms tightened and released. He was bracing, not for the crowd's reaction, but for the next pulse.  
She opened her slate just enough to write the time. Her graphite moved in the slack phase only, a discipline learned from years of measuring unstable instruments.  
Corlexi's voice carried through the glass horns, fractured in places where the hum interfered, clear where the cloth wraps held. He spoke in numbered sections, clinical, precise. The grid failure. The pressure shifts. The structural compromises. He used words like "re-set" and "baseline" and "hazard control."  
Tiffani wrote: GRID 98% FAIL (REPORT). CARRIER BEHAVIOR. HAZARD CONTROL.  
The words felt thin on the page. Inadequate. Like describing a wound by its shape while ignoring the blood.  
When he mentioned "anomalous load-bearing incidents," the stadium shifted. Not a wave. A single breath held. Jhace stood one tier down, wedge bar tucked under his arm like a promise he wouldn't keep. His hands were visible. Low. The tremor in his fingers had returned, tiny jerks that matched the second pulse of the doubled hum.  
A registrar approached their row, wax tabs in hand. "If you witnessed stabilization—load arrest, suspension, redirected flow—you step to assessment," he said, voice polished smooth. "This mark routes you."  
He reached for Jhace's wrist.  
Jhace didn't pull away. He locked his eyes on the registrar's hand like it was a tool he couldn't quite trust. "No," he said.  
Bram stepped in quickly. "It's not punishment," he repeated, the words rehearsed. "They're trying to keep you from getting mobbed. It's hazard control."  
Jhace's gaze didn't waver. "I said no."  
The registrar's smile stayed fixed, but his eyes tightened at the corners. "Refusal is noted," he said, and pressed a wax tab into Bram's palm instead. "If he changes his mind, bring him to the left table."  
Bram stared at the tab like it had bitten him.  
Corlexi continued speaking—population control, resource allocation, census nodes—but Tiffani's attention split. She watched the registrar's retreating back, the way his shoulders stiffened when he thought no one was looking. She watched Bram's hand tighten around the wax tab until his knuckles whitened. She watched Jhace's hands flex once, then still.  
Then the world stuttered.  
It wasn't a quake. It was a skip. Like the planet had missed a step in its new dance.  
Tiffani's knee caught on the expected interval and found nothing to land on. The slack phase didn't arrive on schedule. Air thinned. Metal flooded her mouth—not copper this time, but something sharper, cleaner, like biting into raw wire.  
A brace timber in the lower aisle line groaned under the unexpected load. A worker carrying a crate stumbled as the stone beneath him shifted. The crate broke. Glass shards scattered. His forearm struck a jagged edge.  
Blood hit stone.  
Tiffani's pencil moved without permission.  
BLOOD. LOCATION: AISLE 3\. CAUSE: PHASE NULL / LOAD SHIFT.  
She wrote around the red as if it were a stain on the page. Her letters narrowed. Her breath caught on the wrong pulse.  
Guards moved. Brace captains moved. Two medics pushed through with a cloth roll and a splint. Bram's face went pale, then locked into a mask of competence he hadn't earned yet. He held the rope post steady while bodies passed, but his knuckles were white on the wood.  
Corlexi remained on the platform. He didn't return to the podium. He didn't speak. His clean coat didn't change. But Tiffani saw his hand tighten on the rail, saw the pulse jump in his throat. He had felt the skip too.  
A guard's gaze snapped to the graphite smear on Tiffani's thumb. He stepped close enough that she could smell oil on his coat and the faint metallic tang on his breath—copper, like everyone else.  
"No notes in the tiers," he said—quiet, like a courtesy. His hand hovered near her slate as if it already belonged to the table.  
Tiffani lifted her empty palm in compliance. Closed meant closed. She let him see that much, and no more.  
The guard's eyes flicked to her knee, then away. He'd seen the hitch. He knew what it cost to stand here.  
"Your mother taught you to read, didn't she?" Tiffani asked, voice low.  
The question startled him. His hand dropped from his spear. "Yes," he admitted, then caught himself. "Irrelevant."  
"Nothing is irrelevant," Tiffani said. "Especially not the people who taught us how to see."  
She thought of her own mother's hands—ink-stained, steady—guiding her first pencil across slate. Measure twice, daughter. The world lies once.  
"I won't write in the tiers," Tiffani promised. "But I'll remember. And memory is the only thing they can't take from us twice."  
The guard's throat worked. He stepped back, giving her space. Not because she'd won. Because he'd remembered his own mother's hands.  
Tiffani moved toward the aisle where the blood still stained the stone. She could taste copper on her tongue—a souvenir from the stutter.  
She'd write later. In private.  
For now, she'd watch.  
And remember.  
The stadium had held its breath. Now it was learning how to exhale.

# Chapter 15 — The Omission

The bells rang the Assembly sequence—three low, one high, pause, repeat—but the vibration bled into the bronze, stretching the spacing until the pattern felt trained rather than instinctive.  
Tiffani stood where she could see the whole of it without being seen herself. Her slate was a weight against her ribs. The grit along its edge lifted and settled with each pulse, a living thing responding to the world's new heartbeat.  
Corlexi stood already at the center of the platform when the last echo died. No procession. No flourish. Crown gray—clean lines, no ornament. The way he held himself said he had practiced stillness until it read as calm instead of fear.  
He began.  
"Citizens of Saint Merro and the upper terraces," he said, voice carried clean through the glass horns. "We are convened under conditions without precedent."  
External. Sudden. Unpreventable.  
Tiffani underlined the words in her mind and waited for the evidence that would follow. It never came.  
He spoke of logistics—assembly points, rationing, repair priorities. Competent. Insufficient. He named the glass-houses. Acknowledged the Underface. Promised assessment.  
He never said permanent.  
He never said through.  
He never said elsewhere.  
He never said this will happen again.  
Around her, reactions registered in micro-movements. Relief where people heard "external" and translated it to not our fault. Anxiety where "assessment ongoing" sounded like no answers yet.  
Bram stood two rows down, assigned to the aisle. He leaned on his spear like it was a prop, jaw tight, eyes bright with the effort of paying attention. He nodded at the right places. When Corlexi spoke about "citizen cooperation," Bram's shoulders eased, just a touch.  
Jhace did not nod. He stood back from the risers, weight on his heels, arms folded like he was bracing a wall. His gaze never left Corlexi's hands. Not his face. His hands.  
Chloen sat on the stone step between two adults who weren't hers. Her feet didn't touch the ground. She swung them in time with the slack phase, then stopped when she noticed herself doing it, hugged her elbows tight.  
Corlexi moved to containment.  
"There have been reports," he said, carefully, "of anomalous load-bearing responses during the event."  
A ripple went through the crowd. Not noise. Attention.  
"These responses are under review," Corlexi continued. "They appear to be temporary, situational, and costly to the individuals involved."  
Costly. True. Still incomplete.  
"They are not weapons," he said. "They are not signs. They are not gifts."  
Tiffani's pencil hovered.  
He didn't say what they were.  
She watched the factor's ringed hand tighten on the rail. Watched a pipe engineer glance down at his own fingers like he expected them to glow. Watched Bram straighten, relief clear on his face at the word not weapons.  
Jhace's jaw set.  
Corlexi spoke for twelve minutes. Tiffani counted.  
Twelve minutes of answers that stabilized, and one omission that grew heavier with every breath.  
When he concluded, he thanked them for their restraint. Their patience. Their trust.  
Trust, Tiffani thought, was a measurement you only noticed after it failed.  
Corlexi stepped back from the glass horns. Applause started—tentative, scattered, then gathering momentum as people followed the first claps like a safety rail.  
Bram clapped. Once. Then again.  
Tiffani didn't.  
Corlexi inclined his head, turned, and walked off the platform along the side ramp. As he did, he glanced—not toward the crowd, not toward the glass-houses—but toward the far edge of the dais.  
Toward a woman already standing.  
Eries did not wait for permission.  
She stepped forward with a limp hidden by the sway of her wool mantle—a souvenir from Te'Oga, where the first tremors had swallowed her daughter whole. Her left hand stayed tucked in her pocket, wrapped around the copper-stained handkerchief she'd pressed to her mouth after every speech since the Event. Control costs blood, her daughter had whispered once, watching Eries drill doctrine into acolytes. Even yours.  
"And I would like to thank you," Eries said, voice clear and calm, "for your silence."  
Tiffani felt the room change shape. It wasn't louder or quieter. Aligned.  
Eries smiled—not kindly, not cruelly. Precisely. Like someone who knew the cost of the truth and intended to spend it anyway.  
"You've been told this was external," Eries said. "Unpreventable. Finished. I am here to tell you that this is not over—and pretending otherwise is not stability. It's delay."  
Around her, grown-ups shifted. Some nodded. Some crossed their arms like that might hold something in.  
"You have felt the cost," Eries continued. "In your mouths. In your joints. In your breath. You have seen stone obey hands. You have seen water turn. That frightens you. It should."  
Tiffani's pencil touched the slate. Just touched. Didn't move.  
"But fear," Eries said, "is not the enemy. Confusion is."  
Chloen made a soft sound—a snort caught and swallowed. Tiffani saw her fingers curl into her sleeves.  
"You are not broken," Eries said. "And this is not punishment. This is entropy made visible. Ruin does not mean evil. It means change without permission."  
Tiffani watched Eries' hand move to her pocket, the slight tremor as her fingers closed around the handkerchief. She saw the stain on the fabric when it shifted in the light. Copper-brown. Old.  
"And change," Eries said, spreading her hands, "can be guided."  
The word slid into the air like oil on water.  
"You don't need to understand it," Eries continued. "You don't need to measure it. You need to stop fighting what is already happening."  
Jhace shook his head once, sharp, like knocking something loose.  
"There are those among you," Eries said, "who have already proven capable. Not through training. Not through doctrine. Through instinct."  
Chloen's stomach dipped. Tiffani saw it in the girl's hunched shoulders.  
"You don't need registries," Eries went on smoothly. "You don't need lists. You don't need to be afraid of being counted. You need support. Structure. Permission."  
Permission was something adults gave you so they didn't have to watch.  
"This isn't about power," Eries said. "It's about responsibility."  
Tiffani's pencil moved finally. One line. Only one.  
Deployment begun. Effect: immediate compliance. Unknown cost.  
Beneath the words, she drew a small, sharp symbol—a notation only she would understand. The first note of her mother's lullaby.

# Chapter 16 — The Easy Answer

Chloen saw it before the words changed.  
Tiffani's hand moved the way it always did right before something mattered. Not fast. Not careful. Just ready. Like the slate had weight and she was deciding whether to lift it or let it stay where it was.  
Chloen watched the edge of it. The grit there lifted a little, then settled. Lifted again.  
She felt it in her teeth. That metallic taste was gone now—washed away or buried deeper, she wasn't sure which was worse.  
The lady on the platform—Eries, somebody whispered nearby, like names were currency now—hadn't stepped into the loud-glass, but her voice got everywhere anyway. It slid into the space between people and sat there like it belonged.  
"And now," the lady said, "I would like to speak about what we have not yet named."  
People leaned without noticing. Chloen leaned too, then stopped when she realized she was doing it and hugged her elbows tighter. She didn't want to move unless she meant it.  
Eries looked like she'd always been standing there. Dark dress. Bright pin at the collar, shaped like a broken loop. She smiled like she was sorry for everyone, but not enough to stop.  
"You've been told this was external," Eries said. "Unpreventable. Finished. I am here to tell you that this is not over—and pretending otherwise is not stability. It's delay."  
Chloen felt a small, sharp click inside her chest.  
Delay was a word you used when something bad was coming back.  
Around her, grown-ups shifted. Some nodded. Some crossed their arms like that might hold something in.  
Eries didn't raise her voice.  
"You have felt the cost," she said. "In your mouths. In your joints. In your breath. You have seen stone obey hands. You have seen water turn."  
Chloen thought of the wave. The way it bent like it had listened. Her fingers curled into her sleeves. She could still feel the weight of the slab above her in her bones, even though it had fallen and broken and was done.  
"That frightens you," Eries said. "It should."  
Chloen frowned.  
That wasn't how you talked if you were trying to help.  
"But fear," Eries went on, "is not the enemy. Confusion is."  
Tiffani's pencil hovered. Not writing. Waiting.  
Chloen wanted to tap her. To say don't. She didn't know why. She just knew the slate felt heavier than it should.  
"You are not broken," Eries said. "And this is not punishment. This is entropy made visible. Ruin does not mean evil. It means change without permission."  
Chloen didn't like the way that sounded tidy. Aunt Lessa had said once that tidy lies were the most dangerous kind because they fit so easily in your pockets.
