[USER]
Chapter 9 — The Gap
Chloen didn't go back to the street where the slab had fallen. She didn't need to. The dust still clung to her hair, her clothes, the grit under her nails. She could taste it every time she swallowed.

Instead, she followed Tiffani through the sorting lines and the rope-marked zones, stepping where the older woman stepped, keeping her eyes on the slate’s edge where coppergilt lifted and settled with every pressure pulse. It was safer to watch the grit than the rubble.

Jhace walked a pace behind them with his wedge bar balanced on his shoulder. His hands were steady now that they had work to do. Chloen had seen them shake earlier—fine tremors in the fingers when they weren't gripping anything. She'd seen men shake like that after fights. After fear. But Jhace’s shaking didn’t stop when the fear passed. It sat under his skin like a second pulse.

Tiffani stopped at a break in the terrace wall where the stone dropped away into a canyon of rubble. Not a collapse from today. This was older. Clean lines where beams had been cut. Square gaps where homes had been removed.

Chloen’s breath locked. The gap wasn’t just empty. It was measured. Marked with chalk lines at the edges like someone had taken a knife to the terrace and carved out a piece of it. The lines were fresh. The dust hadn’t settled over them yet.

Tiffani didn’t look at her. Her eyes tracked the chalk marks, the way they formed a rectangle in the stone. "Infrastructure clearance," she said. "They’re making lanes before the next pull cycle."

Chloen’s throat tightened. She knew that rectangle. She’d slept in the corner where the sun came through the gap in the shutter. She’d counted cracks in the ceiling before sleep. She’d pressed her ear to the wall to hear the neighbors arguing about bread prices.

Gone. Cut out. Like it had never been there.

A brace captain crossed their path, chalk in hand, marking a new line on the opposite wall. He didn’t see Chloen. Or if he did, he didn’t register her. She was small, dust-covered, standing in Tiffani’s shadow.

Jhace shifted his weight. His kit strap pulled tight across his chest. "That’s a whole row," he said.

Tiffani’s pencil ticked against her slate once. "They’re consolidating. Making defensible zones." She didn’t say it like approval. She said it like an instrument reading air pressure.

Chloen stepped forward before she thought. Her boot toe touched the chalk line at the edge of the gap. The stone ended sharp there. No debris. No broken beams. Just clean-cut absence.

She looked down. The drop was deep. Deeper than she remembered. Farther than her room had been from the ground.

Tiffani’s hand closed over Chloen’s shoulder—not hard, just present. A stop signal. Chloen flinched under it. She hadn’t realized she’d been moving.

"Chalk lines are boundaries," Tiffani said, low, her voice pitched for Chloen’s ears only. "Step over, and you’re unmarked. Unmarked means no rations. No shelter assignments. No path back."

Chloen stared at the rectangle of missing stone. Her chest felt tight. Like something was pressing in from all sides. She swallowed. Metal taste surged. She spat into the dust, then wiped her mouth with her sleeve.

Jhace moved up beside them. He didn’t look at the gap. He looked at the brace captain’s back. "They cut this before or after the pull cycles started?" he asked Tiffani.

"After," Tiffani said. "Timing confirms premeditation. Not emergency protocol."

Jhace’s jaw worked. He didn’t say anything else. He just stood there, wedge bar in hand, watching the chalk-dust man move down the line, drawing new boundaries on the world.

A pressure pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted in slow sheets off the rubble piles. Coppergilt rose off Tiffani’s slate in tiny arcs, drawn toward Jhace’s hands.

Chloen felt her ears pop. Her breath caught. The world went muffled. She gripped the edge of Tiffani’s cloak with one hand and didn’t let go.

When the slack came, air rushed back in. Sound returned with it. Distant bells. Near voices. The brace captain’s chalk scraping stone.

Tiffani’s hand was still on Chloen’s shoulder. It hadn’t moved. Chloen realized she was leaning into it. She pulled away. Stood straight. Wiped her face with both hands.

Jhace was looking at her. Not pity. Assessment. Like he was measuring the load she could carry.

"The street where the slab fell," he said suddenly. "You lived near there?"

Chloen’s throat closed. She nodded once.

"Underface or low terrace?" Tiffani asked, her voice flat.

"Underface," Chloen whispered. "Near the forge quarter."

Tiffani’s pencil moved on her slate. Not writing words. Just drawing the shape of the clearance zone, the chalk lines, the gap where Chloen’s home had been. Lines within lines. Boundaries within boundaries.

Jhace hefted his wedge bar. "No one’s pulling Underface yet," he said. "Pipe mains are still holding. If it goes, it’ll go fast. But it hasn’t gone yet."

Chloen didn’t know what to do with that. She looked at the gap again. At the clean-cut edges. At the dust settling on the chalk lines that said: this space is no longer yours.

A child’s voice carried from the sorting area behind them. Calling a name. A mother answered. Reunion. Temporary.

Chloen’s hands curled into fists at her sides. Her nails bit into her palms. The grit there stung.

"Where do I—" she started. Then stopped. The question was stupid. Everyone knew where unmarked people went. The edges. The gaps. The places between the chalk lines.

Tiffani’s hand moved from Chloen’s shoulder to her slate. She flipped it open. Wrote one line. Tore the page free. Handed it to Chloen.

Not words. Numbers. A sequence. 3-7-2. Then coordinates. Terrace levels. Lane markers.

"If they ask for papers," Tiffani said, "show them this. Say you’re assigned to water rationing detail. Shift three. It won’t hold forever. But it’ll get you food tonight."

Chloen took the paper. Her fingers were shaking. She didn’t know why. The tremor wasn’t hers. It was Jhace’s. Or the world’s. Or the stupid copper that kept flooding her mouth.

Jhace watched the brace captain mark another line farther down the terrace. "The woman with the slate," he said. "The one counting hands. She was here."

Tiffani’s eyes went sharp. "When?"

"After the cut. Before the pull cycle. She was writing in a book smaller than yours. Crown emblem on the sash."

Tiffani’s pencil tapped her slate. Once. A signal.

Chloen looked down at the paper in her hand. At the numbers. At the chalk line at her feet. At the gap where her room had been.

She took a breath. Then another. The air still tasted like metal. But she kept breathing.

She folded the paper carefully. Tucked it into her sleeve. Stepped back from the chalk line.

Still here, she thought. Not erased. Not yet.

The bells kept ringing. The stutter under the old thrum kept vibrating through the stone. And Chloen stood between the gap and the living, small and dust-covered, learning how to be counted again.

Chloen didn't go back to the street where the slab had fallen. She didn't need to. The dust still clung to her hair, her clothes, the grit under her nails. She could taste it every time she swallowed.

Instead, she followed Tiffani through the sorting lines and the rope-marked zones, stepping where the older woman stepped, keeping her eyes on the slate’s edge where coppergilt lifted and settled with every pressure pull. It was safer to watch the grit than the rubble.

Jhace walked a pace behind them with his wedge bar balanced on his shoulder. His hands were steady now that they had work to do. Chloen had seen them shake earlier—fine tremors in the fingers when they weren't gripping anything. She'd seen men shake like that after fights. After fear. But Jhace’s shaking didn’t stop when the fear passed. It sat under his skin like a second pulse.

Tiffani stopped at a break in the terrace wall where the stone dropped away into a canyon of rubble. Not a collapse from this crisis. This was older. Clean lines where beams had been cut. Square gaps where homes had been removed.

Chloen’s breath locked. The gap wasn’t just empty. It was measured. Marked with chalk lines at the edges like someone had taken a knife to the terrace and carved out a piece of it. The lines were fresh. The dust hadn’t settled over them yet.

Tiffani didn’t look at her. Her eyes tracked the chalk marks, the way they formed a rectangle in the stone. "Infrastructure clearance," she said. "They’re making lanes before the next pull."

Chloen’s throat tightened. She knew that rectangle. She’d slept in the corner where the sun came through the shutter gap. She’d counted cracks in the ceiling before sleep. She’d pressed her ear to the wall to hear the neighbors arguing about bread.

Gone. Cut out. Like it had never been there.

A brace captain crossed their path, chalk in hand, marking a new line on the opposite wall. He didn’t see Chloen. Or if he did, he didn’t register her. She was small, dust-covered, standing in Tiffani’s shadow.

Jhace shifted his weight. His kit strap pulled tight across his chest. "That’s a whole row," he said.

Tiffani’s pencil ticked against her slate once. "They’re consolidating. Making defensible zones." She didn’t say it like approval. She said it like an instrument reading pressure.

Chloen stepped forward before she thought. Her boot toe touched the chalk line at the edge of the gap. The stone ended sharp there. No debris. No broken beams. Just clean-cut absence.

She looked down. The drop was deep. Deeper than she remembered. Farther than her room had been from the ground.

Tiffani’s hand closed over Chloen’s shoulder—not hard, just present. A stop signal. Chloen flinched under it. She hadn’t realized she’d been moving.

"Chalk lines are boundaries," Tiffani said, low, her voice pitched for Chloen’s ears only. "Step over, and you’re unmarked. Unmarked means no rations. No shelter assignments. No path back."

Chloen stared at the rectangle of missing stone. Her chest felt tight. Like something was pressing in from all sides. She swallowed. Metal taste surged. She spat into the dust, then wiped her mouth with her sleeve.

Jhace moved up beside them. He didn’t look at the gap. He looked at the brace captain’s back. "They cut this before or after the pull cycles started?" he asked Tiffani.

"After," Tiffani said. "Timing confirms premeditation. Not emergency protocol."

Jhace’s jaw worked. He didn’t say anything else. He just stood there, wedge bar in hand, watching the chalk-dust man move down the line, drawing new boundaries on the world.

A pressure pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted in slow sheets off the rubble piles. Coppergilt rose off Tiffani’s slate in tiny arcs, drawn toward Jhace’s hands.

Chloen felt her ears pop. Her breath caught. The world went muffled. She gripped the edge of Tiffani’s cloak with one hand and didn’t let go.

When the slack came, air rushed back in. Sound returned with it. Distant bells. Near voices. The brace captain’s chalk scraping stone.

Tiffani’s hand was still on Chloen’s shoulder. It hadn’t moved. Chloen realized she was leaning into it. She pulled away. Stood straight. Wiped her face with both hands.

Jhace was looking at her. Not pity. Assessment. Like he was measuring the load she could carry.

"The street where the slab fell," he said suddenly. "You lived near there?"

Chloen’s throat closed. She nodded once.

"Underface or low terrace?" Tiffani asked, her voice flat.

"Underface," Chloen whispered. "Near the forge quarter."

Tiffani’s pencil moved on her slate. Not writing words. Just drawing the shape of the clearance zone, the chalk lines, the gap where Chloen’s room had been. Lines within lines. Boundaries within boundaries.

Jhace hefted his wedge bar. "No one’s pulling Underface yet," he said. "Pipe mains are still holding. If it goes, it’ll go fast. But it hasn’t gone yet."

Chloen didn’t know what to do with that. She looked at the gap again. At the clean-cut edges. At the dust settling on the chalk lines that said: this space is no longer yours.

A child’s voice carried from the sorting area behind them. Calling a name. A mother answered. Reunion. Temporary.

Chloen’s hands curled into fists at her sides. Her nails bit into her palms. The grit there stung.

"Where do I—" she started. Then stopped. The question was stupid. Everyone knew where unmarked people went. The edges. The gaps. The places between the chalk lines.

Tiffani’s hand moved from Chloen’s shoulder to her slate. She flipped it open. Wrote one line. Tore the page free. Handed it to Chloen.

Not words. Numbers. A sequence. 3-7-2. Then terrace marks. Lane markers.

"If they ask for papers," Tiffani said, "show them this. Say you’re assigned to water rationing detail. Mark three. It won’t hold forever. But it’ll get you food on the next ration pass."

Chloen took the paper. Her fingers were shaking. Fear did that. Thin air did that. The stone under her boots did that. She swallowed and tasted copper again.

Jhace watched the brace captain mark another line farther down the terrace. "The woman with the slate," he said. "The one counting hands. She was here."

Tiffani’s eyes went sharp. "When?"

"After the cut. Before the pull. She was writing in a book smaller than yours. Crown emblem on the sash."

Tiffani’s pencil tapped her slate. Once. A signal.

Chloen looked down at the paper in her hand. At the numbers. At the chalk line at her feet. At the gap where her room had been.

She took a breath. Then another. The air still tasted like metal. But she kept breathing.

She folded the paper carefully. Tucked it into her sleeve. Stepped back from the chalk line.

Still here, she thought. Not erased. Not yet.

The bells kept ringing. The stutter under the old thrum kept vibrating through the stone. And Chloen stood between the gap and the living, small and dust-covered, learning how to be counted again.

[ASSISTANT]
Edits applied to **Chapter 9** (lock-respecting, minimal disturbance):

- Removed/softened time phrasing drift: **“Shift three / tonight” → “Mark three / next ration pass”**
- Clarified the shaking attribution so it stays **Chloen-body / fear / air / ground**, not a muddled “maybe Jhace’s tremor”
- Kept the chalk-line governance beat sharp without adding new glossary terms

### Chapter 9 — The Gap (LOCKFIX v1)

Chloen didn't go back to the street where the slab had fallen. She didn't need to. The dust still clung to her hair, her clothes, the grit under her nails. She could taste it every time she swallowed.

Instead, she followed Tiffani through the sorting lines and the rope-marked zones, stepping where the older woman stepped, keeping her eyes on the slate’s edge where coppergilt lifted and settled with every pressure pull. It was safer to watch the grit than the rubble.

Jhace walked a pace behind them with his wedge bar balanced on his shoulder. His hands were steady now that they had work to do. Chloen had seen them shake earlier—fine tremors in the fingers when they weren't gripping anything. She'd seen men shake like that after fights. After fear. But Jhace’s shaking didn’t stop when the fear passed. It sat under his skin like a second pulse.

Tiffani stopped at a break in the terrace wall where the stone dropped away into a canyon of rubble. Not a collapse from this crisis. This was older. Clean lines where beams had been cut. Square gaps where homes had been removed.

Chloen’s breath locked. The gap wasn’t just empty. It was measured. Marked with chalk lines at the edges like someone had taken a knife to the terrace and carved out a piece of it. The lines were fresh. The dust hadn’t settled over them yet.

Tiffani didn’t look at her. Her eyes tracked the chalk marks, the way they formed a rectangle in the stone. "Infrastructure clearance," she said. "They’re making lanes before the next pull."

Chloen’s throat tightened. She knew that rectangle. She’d slept in the corner where the sun came through the shutter gap. She’d counted cracks in the ceiling before sleep. She’d pressed her ear to the wall to hear the neighbors arguing about bread.

Gone. Cut out. Like it had never been there.

A brace captain crossed their path, chalk in hand, marking a new line on the opposite wall. He didn’t see Chloen. Or if he did, he didn’t register her. She was small, dust-covered, standing in Tiffani’s shadow.

Jhace shifted his weight. His kit strap pulled tight across his chest. "That’s a whole row," he said.

Tiffani’s pencil ticked against her slate once. "They’re consolidating. Making defensible zones." She didn’t say it like approval. She said it like an instrument reading pressure.

Chloen stepped forward before she thought. Her boot toe touched the chalk line at the edge of the gap. The stone ended sharp there. No debris. No broken beams. Just clean-cut absence.

She looked down. The drop was deep. Deeper than she remembered. Farther than her room had been from the ground.

Tiffani’s hand closed over Chloen’s shoulder—not hard, just present. A stop signal. Chloen flinched under it. She hadn’t realized she’d been moving.

"Chalk lines are boundaries," Tiffani said, low, her voice pitched for Chloen’s ears only. "Step over, and you’re unmarked. Unmarked means no rations. No shelter assignments. No path back."

Chloen stared at the rectangle of missing stone. Her chest felt tight. Like something was pressing in from all sides. She swallowed. Metal taste surged. She spat into the dust, then wiped her mouth with her sleeve.

Jhace moved up beside them. He didn’t look at the gap. He looked at the brace captain’s back. "They cut this before or after the pull cycles started?" he asked Tiffani.

"After," Tiffani said. "Timing confirms premeditation. Not emergency protocol."

Jhace’s jaw worked. He didn’t say anything else. He just stood there, wedge bar in hand, watching the chalk-dust man move down the line, drawing new boundaries on the world.

A pressure pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted in slow sheets off the rubble piles. Coppergilt rose off Tiffani’s slate in tiny arcs, drawn toward Jhace’s hands.

Chloen felt her ears pop. Her breath caught. The world went muffled. She gripped the edge of Tiffani’s cloak with one hand and didn’t let go.

When the slack came, air rushed back in. Sound returned with it. Distant bells. Near voices. The brace captain’s chalk scraping stone.

Tiffani’s hand was still on Chloen’s shoulder. It hadn’t moved. Chloen realized she was leaning into it. She pulled away. Stood straight. Wiped her face with both hands.

Jhace was looking at her. Not pity. Assessment. Like he was measuring the load she could carry.

"The street where the slab fell," he said suddenly. "You lived near there?"

Chloen’s throat closed. She nodded once.

"Underface or low terrace?" Tiffani asked, her voice flat.

"Underface," Chloen whispered. "Near the forge quarter."

Tiffani’s pencil moved on her slate. Not writing words. Just drawing the shape of the clearance zone, the chalk lines, the gap where Chloen’s room had been. Lines within lines. Boundaries within boundaries.

Jhace hefted his wedge bar. "No one’s pulling Underface yet," he said. "Pipe mains are still holding. If it goes, it’ll go fast. But it hasn’t gone yet."

Chloen didn’t know what to do with that. She looked at the gap again. At the clean-cut edges. At the dust settling on the chalk lines that said: this space is no longer yours.

A child’s voice carried from the sorting area behind them. Calling a name. A mother answered. Reunion. Temporary.

Chloen’s hands curled into fists at her sides. Her nails bit into her palms. The grit there stung.

"Where do I—" she started. Then stopped. The question was stupid. Everyone knew where unmarked people went. The edges. The gaps. The places between the chalk lines.

Tiffani’s hand moved from Chloen’s shoulder to her slate. She flipped it open. Wrote one line. Tore the page free. Handed it to Chloen.

Not words. Numbers. A sequence. 3-7-2. Then terrace marks. Lane markers.

"If they ask for papers," Tiffani said, "show them this. Say you’re assigned to water rationing detail. Mark three. It won’t hold forever. But it’ll get you food on the next ration pass."

Chloen took the paper. Her fingers were shaking. Fear did that. Thin air did that. The stone under her boots did that. She swallowed and tasted copper again.

Jhace watched the brace captain mark another line farther down the terrace. "The woman with the slate," he said. "The one counting hands. She was here."

Tiffani’s eyes went sharp. "When?"

"After the cut. Before the pull. She was writing in a book smaller than yours. Crown emblem on the sash."

Tiffani’s pencil tapped her slate. Once. A signal.

Chloen looked down at the paper in her hand. At the numbers. At the chalk line at her feet. At the gap where her room had been.

She took a breath. Then another. The air still tasted like metal. But she kept breathing.

She folded the paper carefully. Tucked it into her sleeve. Stepped back from the chalk line.

Still here, she thought. Not erased. Not yet.

The bells kept ringing. The stutter under the old thrum kept vibrating through the stone. And Chloen stood between the gap and the living, small and dust-covered, learning how to be counted again.

about chapter 3
**You're right to push back. Let me think about this honestly.**

The passage **does** humanize Tiffani - it shows she seeks physical release from the pressure of being "instrument-brain" all the time. That's legitimate character work.

**But here's why it's still not working:**

1. **It's the wrong moment.** Chapter 3 is "Ground-Level Continuation" - Tiffani descending into chaos to document. The sexual memory interrupts that momentum in the second paragraph. If you want to humanize her through physical needs/desires, it needs to land in a moment where that revelation does narrative work, not as a random flashback while she's rushing down stairs during a catastrophe.

2. **The prose style shifts.** "Breath in her ear, heat, weight, the blunt certainty of it" - this is a different register than the rest of her POV. Not wrong inherently, but jarring. Tiffani thinking about sex would likely be more transactional/physical than sensory-romantic. Something like: "Her knee reminded her why anonymous encounters had costs beyond the immediate. She'd needed the reset. Now she needed to ignore the bill."

3. **It doesn't connect to anything else.** If this memory matters - if Tiffani's relationship to her body, to physical need, to being something other than pure measurement - then it should thread through the story. One isolated passage feels like it was dropped in rather than woven through.

**Here's my actual advice:**

If you want to show Tiffani has a human side that seeks physical connection/release, **keep the idea but move it and reshape it.** 

- Put it in a later chapter where it serves character development (maybe Ch13 "The Bowl" interlude, or a quiet moment in Act II)
- Make the prose match her voice: clinical observation of her own needs, not lyrical memory
- Connect it to the larger story: her body is becoming unreliable (knee pain, pressure pulls, copper taste) - maybe she's realizing the one thing she used to control (sexual encounters on her terms) is also being taken from her

**Or:** Cut it for now, and if you find the right moment later where Tiffani's physical needs/vulnerability matter to the plot, bring it back in a form that works.

You're the writer. If this matters to you for the character, fight for it - but fight for it in the right place with the right execution.

What's your actual goal with this passage? What does it need to do for Tiffani as a character?

[ASSISTANT]
You’re not wrong. That sex-memory beat in **Ch3** was doing the *right* character job in the *wrong* place, and it came in wearing the wrong clothes.

### What that passage should *do* for Tiffani (so it isn’t just “random horny flashback”)
Pick one core function and make every later echo serve it:

1) **Control/Release Loop:** she uses anonymous physical release as a hard reset when her brain won’t shut up. Not romance. Not intimacy. A lever.  
2) **Body-as-Instrument Conflict:** her body is becoming unreliable (knee, breath, pulls). The one “reset” she used to command on her own terms starts getting taxed, threatened, or turned into evidence.  
3) **Obsession Seed:** attraction isn’t “cute.” It’s compulsive, stress-linked, and it makes her reckless with boundaries later.

If it’s not doing at least one of those, cut it.

### What I changed in Chapter 3 (so it works)
I kept the *seed* but made it a **single, clinical “receipt” line** tied to the knee—no lyrical register, no momentum-kill, no explicit detail. The reader clocks “oh—she uses sex as a reset,” and we keep moving.

Also folded in the **other fixes** you flagged earlier:
- Chronometer continuity (she doesn’t just abandon it).
- Removed the near-copy “the city answered…” line.

**Updated file:**  
[Download Chapter 3 (LOCKFIX v4)](sandbox:/mnt/data/N01_Chapter_3_Ground-Level_Continuation_LOCKFIX_v4.md)

**Updated bundle (handover v2 + Ch2–Ch9, with Ch3 v4):**  
[Download ZIP](sandbox:/mnt/data/N01_handover_plus_ch2_to_ch9_lockfix_v2.zip)

### How to make it pay off later (so it’s woven, not dropped)
Do **two** more touches, max, and both in moments where it *matters*:
- **Later quiet pocket (post-crisis):** she considers using that lever again and hates that she wants it. Keep it transactional/clinical on-page.
- **When authority pressure tightens:** she realizes the “reset” habit is also a vulnerability—something that can be used, tracked, or turned into a hook.

If you want, I’ll write the **next seed** when we hit a chapter that actually has room for it (a low-noise interlude, not mid-sprint).

[USER]
Chapter 10 — The Shard
Chloen followed the rope lanes through the terrace streets, keeping her eyes on the knots where they were tied to stone cleats. The ropes moved in the wind pulls, tightening on the inhale phase, going slack on the exhale. Like breathing made visible.

She'd stayed with Tiffani and Jhace as long as she could, but when the worklines broke into smaller groups and the Crown runners started collecting names, she'd slipped away. Not running. Just moving. Like water finding cracks.

Her pocket held the slip of slate with the numbers Tiffani had given her—3-7-2, terrace marks, lane markers. She touched it every few steps, felt the graphite smear against her thumb. Paper wouldn't have survived the slab-fall day. Slate would.

She passed a brace team propping a leaning wall with scavenged timber. A man with cracked knuckles drove wedges while his partner called out pressure shifts. Their rhythm was precise: pull, drive, check. Pull, drive, check. The doubled hum underfoot seemed to guide them as much as hinder.

Ahead, where three rope lanes converged, a new post had been set up. Not wood. Not stone. A metal pole driven deep, strung with lanterns and bells. A Crown emblem hung from the crossbar.

Crowds thickened there. People stood in rows, not talking much. Some with wax tabs on their wrists. Some without. The ones without stood farther back, shoulders tight.

Chloen slowed. Watched.

A woman with a small, sharp face and a brass emblem on her cloak moved down the line, checking wrists, making marks on a slate. She didn't speak. Just pointed. Tabbed or untabbed. The untabbed got a nod toward the back rows.

Chloen's fingers tightened on her pocket. The numbers felt like a lifeline and a lie at once.

Then she saw Mobel.

He wasn't in line. He stood beside the post, talking low to the Crown woman. Not arguing. Not pleading. Just talking. His hands were clean. His clothes were travel-stained but not torn. He carried a small pack, worn smooth at the straps.

The Crown woman shook her head once. Final. She pointed to the back rows.

Mobel didn't move. He just stood there, weight on his heels, waiting. His face didn't change. But his shoulders did—subtle shift, like a brace settling under load.

Chloen edged closer, keeping behind a man with a broken arm. She didn't want to be seen watching. She just wanted to see what happened when someone refused to break.

A runner approached the post, breathless. He said something to the Crown woman, low and urgent. She glanced at Mobel, then back at the runner. Her mouth tightened.

Mobel didn't smile. But something in his posture relaxed. Like a weight lifted that nobody else could see.

The runner turned to the crowd. "Corlexi of Infrastructure requests Mobel of Te'Oga. Assembly at Basin Stadium. Live briefing. Now."

The Crown woman's head snapped toward Mobel. Her eyes narrowed. "You didn't say—" she started.

"I didn't need to," Mobel said. His voice was quiet but clear. "The request stands."

The woman made a sharp gesture toward the front rows. "Clear path," she barked at her assistants. "This one moves through."

People shifted. Made space. Mobel walked through the gap without looking at anyone. He passed within three steps of Chloen.

She saw the pack on his shoulder. She saw the way his left hand stayed tucked near his chest, fingers curled like they were holding something invisible. She saw the Crown woman watching him go, her face stiff with something that wasn't anger but wasn't respect either.

Chloen followed.

Not closely. Just enough to see.

Mobel walked with a slight limp. Not old injury. New. As if his body had learned a different rhythm and hadn't quite settled into it. His steps matched the slack phase of the pulls. In. Out. In. Out.

They reached a wider terrace where the stone was marked with fresh chalk lines. A runner waited there, holding a horse. Not a cart horse. A riding horse, lean and trembling, its eyes white at the edges.

Mobel stopped. Looked at the horse. Looked at the runner.

"The Basin Stadium," the runner said. "They're waiting."

Mobel didn't take the reins. "I don't ride," he said.

The runner's face tightened. "The request was clear."

"So is the ground," Mobel replied. "I'll walk."

The runner opened his mouth, closed it, then nodded sharply. "Two spans. No more. Or I report delay."

Mobel inclined his head once. "Two spans."

The runner mounted and rode ahead, glancing back every few breaths.

Chloen stayed in the shadow of a half-collapsed arch. Watched Mobel walk.

His limp was worse on the pull phase. His left hand stayed tucked near his chest. Sometimes it pressed flat against his sternum, just for a moment, like he was checking for something beneath his ribs.

A pressure pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted. Coppergilt glittered along the stone edges where metal met rock.

Chloen's ears popped. Her breath caught.

Mobel stopped walking. Stood perfectly still. His hand pressed flat against his chest. His eyes closed for three breaths. When they opened, he looked different. Calmer. Sharper.

He resumed walking. Faster now. Matching the slack phase.

Chloen followed at a distance, watching his hand, watching his limp, watching the way the coppergilt seemed to gather where he'd stood.

She didn't understand what she was seeing. But she understood this: Mobel carried something no one else had. Something that made the Crown woman step back. Something that made a runner wait. Something that lived under his ribs and answered to the doubled hum.

She touched the numbers in her pocket again. 3-7-2.

The Basin Stadium loomed ahead, a stone bowl carved into the mountain face. Voices rose from it—organized, orderly. Not panic. Procedure.

Mobel reached the stadium steps and paused. His hand pressed against his chest again. This time longer. When he lowered it, his fingers left smears on his shirt. Not dust. Not sweat.

Copper.

He climbed the steps without looking back.

Chloen stopped at the edge of the crowd forming at the base. She didn't have a tab. She didn't have a number that mattered here.

A guard approached her. Young. Nervous. His sash was too bright for the dust. "Name and guardian," he said. His voice cracked on "guardian."

Chloen's throat tightened. She reached for her pocket. For the numbers.

The guard's eyes followed her hand. Saw the slate slip. Saw the graphite smear. He hesitated.

Before he could speak, a woman's voice cut through the pull-thin air. "That one's with me," Tiffani said, stepping up beside Chloen. Her slate was closed. Her posture was straight. "Water rationing detail. Mark three."

The guard looked at Tiffani's face. At her hands. At the crown emblem on her own sash. He nodded once, sharp. "Mark three," he repeated, like memorizing a lesson.

Tiffani didn't smile. She just guided Chloen past the checkpoint with a hand on her shoulder. Light. Professional. Not gentle.

Chloen looked back once at the stadium steps. Mobel was gone. Only the copper smears remained on the stone where he'd stood.

Tiffani's hand stayed on her shoulder. "You watch the wrong things," she said, low. "Watch the ropes. Watch the cracks. Watch who gets marked."

Chloen didn't answer. She watched the copper smears glint in the Kindling-light. Watched them lift slightly on the next pull phase, like filings answering a hidden magnet.

She kept the numbers in her pocket. But now she knew there were other numbers. Other marks. Other things that lived under ribs and answered to the world's new heartbeat.

The stadium bells began to ring. Three short. One long. Two short.

The rope lanes tightened. The crowd moved.

Chloen moved with them. But her eyes stayed on the stairs where Mobel had stood, where the copper smears caught the light and didn't let go.

chapter10_text = """Chapter 10 — The Shard

Mobel kept to the rope lanes because ropes meant somebody had decided where the ground was allowed to be trusted.

The lines tightened on the pull phase and went slack on the release, a simple truth made visible. He timed his steps to it. When the air thinned and the world leaned toward the distant Exit Face, he did not fight it. He shortened his breath. He kept his weight centered. He kept his left hand close to his sternum where it could brace what the doubled baseline kept trying to wake.

He did not look up at the Rift again.
He already had enough impossible.

His pack rode high on his right shoulder. The strap had been repaired twice, and he could feel every stitch bite when the pressure changed. Under the flap, wrapped in oilcloth and wool, the shard sat like a bone you could not remove. He had padded it until it could not clink, but the world did not need sound to remind him it was there. It reminded him through his teeth. Through the thin copper taste that never fully washed away.

Ahead, where three rope lanes converged, the Crown had set a post into the paving: a metal pole driven deep, lanterns hung from a crossbar, bells tied under them, an emblem stamped and bright against dust.

People had formed rows without being told. Some wore wax tabs on their wrists. Some did not. The ones without stood farther back, shoulders tight, eyes scanning for someone to blame.

Mobel stepped into the line and kept his face neutral.

A woman with a sharp jaw and a brass emblem on her cloak moved along the front, checking wrists, making marks on a slate. She did not waste speech. Point. Wave through. Point. Send back.

When she reached him, she did not touch him. She looked at his wrist—bare—and at the pack strap cutting his shoulder. Her eyes lingered there a beat too long.

“Back rows,” she said.

“No,” Mobel replied.

Her pencil stopped. A fraction of a pause, just enough to prove she was used to compliance. “You’re unmarked.”

“I’m requested.”

“That’s a claim,” she said, and her eyes went to his left hand, the way it hovered near his chest as if it belonged there. “Back rows.”

The pull phase hit while she watched.
Air thinned. Bells seemed farther away. Dust lifted in slow sheets off the stone.

Mobel did not let his breath hitch where she could see it. He let it shorten. He pressed his palm flat against his sternum for one beat and held the pressure there, internal and quiet. The doubled baseline tugged at him anyway—at ribs, at teeth, at the shard in his pack—as if it wanted every hidden thing to announce itself.

The woman’s gaze sharpened.

Then a runner arrived, breathless, boots skidding on dust. He leaned in close and spoke into her ear. Whatever he said made her jaw tighten.

He turned to the crowd and raised his voice. “Corlexi of Infrastructure requests Mobel of Saint Merro. Assembly at Basin Stadium. Live briefing. Now.”

The woman’s head snapped back to Mobel. “You didn’t—”

“I didn’t need to,” Mobel said.

It was not satisfaction. It was procedure. He had delivered the only lever that mattered to the Crown: a directive with a name above theirs.

“Clear path,” she barked at her assistants. “This one moves through.”

Bodies shifted. A corridor opened. Mobel walked it without looking at faces, because faces were noise and noise made mistakes.

He passed within a few steps of a child standing near the rope knot—small, dust-matted, eyes too alert for her size. Her hand hovered near her sleeve like she was guarding something. She watched him, then watched the Crown woman, then watched the gaps between tabbed and untabbed people like she was learning a map.

He did not stop.
Stopping made attention stick.

A riding horse waited farther up the lane with another runner holding the reins. The animal’s eyes were white at the edges. Its flanks jumped at every pressure pull, as if the air itself kept biting it.

“The Basin Stadium,” the runner said. “You ride.”

Mobel looked at the horse’s feet. Looked at the stone beneath them, still breathing in pulses. “I don’t ride.”

“You walk and you lose time.”

“I walk and I arrive alive,” Mobel said.

The runner’s mouth opened, closed, then tightened into a nod. “Two spans. No more. Or I report delay.”

“Two spans,” Mobel agreed, and started up the terrace.

He walked on the release. He rested on the pull.
In. Out.
Thin breath. Full breath.
He kept his pace small and steady so his limp would not become spectacle. The limp was new. The world had taught his body a different rhythm and his joints had not accepted it yet.

Halfway up, the pull hit harder than it had below.

Air thinned to a demand.
Dust lifted.
A fine metallic grit gathered along metal edges and strap buckles as if drawn there by insistence.

Mobel stopped in a shadowed notch between buildings where fewer eyes could count him. He pressed his left hand to his sternum again, longer this time, and felt the internal heat answer—tight, contained, not a flare. He did not let it climb into his throat. He did not let it reach his hands.

Under his palm, the pressure shifted—like a line tightening without a visible anchor.

He opened his eyes and kept them on the stone ahead.

In his pack, the shard pulsed once, not with light but with presence. He could feel it through cloth and padding and denial. A sharp insistence that wanted connection.

Not yet, he told it, as if telling himself.

When the release came, he moved again.

The Basin Stadium rose ahead, a stone bowl carved into the mountain face. Voices came up from inside it—organized, layered, not the raw noise of panic. Procedure was being built in real time. That was what Infrastructure did: name, sort, restrict.

At the foot of the steps, the crowd thickened. A checkpoint had been thrown together from rope and two guards with sashes too clean for the dust. One of them held a slate and looked terrified of the authority it represented.

The child was there again, caught at the edge of the line.

“Name and guardian,” the young guard said, voice cracking on the second word.

The child’s hand went toward her sleeve.

A woman stepped in beside her before the child could fumble the motion. Older. Upright. Slate held closed against her ribs like it was an extension of her spine. Her posture was not Crown, but it was practiced enough that the guard treated it as if it were.

“That one’s with me,” the woman said. “Water rationing detail. Mark three.”

The guard’s eyes flicked to the slate, to the woman’s hands, to the way she did not ask for permission to exist in the lane. He swallowed, then nodded as if accepting a lesson. “Mark three,” he repeated, and waved them through.

Mobel took note without changing his pace.
A measurer. A survivor. A child the system would erase if she stood still.

He climbed the stadium steps.

Halfway up, the pull phase bit again. He stopped, hand to sternum, and held himself through it. When he lowered his hand, his fingertips left a dark metallic smear on his shirt—fine grit mixed with sweat, stubborn against cloth.

He did not wipe it away.
Evidence was sometimes a tool.

At the stadium threshold, he shifted his pack forward just enough to feel the shard through the padding. It sat heavy. Unmoving. Patient.

He stepped inside.

Behind him, the bells kept ringing in uneven waves. The doubled baseline kept working its way into every body that could still stand.

Mobel did not look back.
He had brought the shard to the place where names became orders.
Now he would find out what it demanded in return.
"""

[ASSISTANT]
def latest_version(files, key_prefix):
    # returns file with max version vN if exists; else first
    candidates=[f for f in files if f.startswith(key_prefix)]
    if not candidates:
        return None
    def vnum(fn):
        m=re.search(r'_v(\d+)\.md$', fn)
        return int(m.group(1)) if m else 0
    return max(candidates, key=vnum)

selected=[]
selected.append("N01_COMPLETE_HANDOVER.md")
selected.append(latest_version(lockfix_files, "N01_Chapter_2_Glass-House_Observatory_LOCKFIX"))
selected.append(latest_version(lockfix_files, "N01_Chapter_3_Ground-Level_Continuation_LOCKFIX"))
for ch in range(4,11):
    # build prefix by chapter number; file names vary; find candidates starting with N01_Chapter_{ch}_
    prefix=f"N01_Chapter_{ch}_"
    cand=[f for f in lockfix_files if f.startswith(prefix)]
    # select latest by v if multiple
    def vnum(fn):
        m=re.search(r'_v(\d+)\.md$', fn)
        return int(m.group(1)) if m else 0
    if cand:
        selected.append(max(cand, key=vnum))
selected

## Chapter 3 (the sex-seed)

That critique is correct.

If the point is **“Tiffani uses sex as a controlled pressure-release / compulsion and pays for it later”**, Chapter 3 is the wrong slot to spend that attention. It yanks focus off the ground-level cataloging job and it reads like a dropped-in “character spice,” not an integrated trait.

### What to do instead (recommended)
**Pull it out of Chapter 3 entirely** and re-seed it in a **quiet seam** where it *adds* tension instead of interrupting motion—e.g. after Chapter 7’s meeting when she’s alone with the slate/chronometer, or an interlude like Chapter 13.

### If you still want a *hint* in Chapter 3
Keep it **one clinical line** tied to the knee and her cost-accounting, not a sensory flashback. Example replacement line (fits her voice, doesn’t stall the scene):

> Her knee hitched. She logged it as damage and moved on—one more private cost she didn’t have time to pay attention to.

Then later, in a calmer chapter, you can cash it out with the “worth the reset / now she’s paying the bill” framing.

## Chapter 10 draft compliance vs handover (your version)

### Hard fail
- **POV mismatch:** Chapter 10 is locked as **Mobel’s chapter** (“The Shard”). Your draft is **Chloen POV** almost start-to-finish.

### Fix-required continuity issues
- **Tiffani wearing a Crown emblem / sash:** she shouldn’t suddenly read as Crown unless you show when/how she got that credential.
- **“Copper.” as a bodily smear:** your bible has **coppergilt** as particulate behavior; a literal “Copper.” smear reads like new biology/blood logic unless you explicitly define it as dust + sweat residue (or rename it).

### Everything else (good / keep)
- Rope-lane breathing logic works.
- Wax tabs / marking gate is strong and matches the emerging control apparatus.
- Corlexi request as leverage is a clean entry to the stadium thread.
- Mobel’s “sternum hand” tell is a good physical flag for his condition.

Chapter 11 — The Sequence
Tiffani watched the tremor in Jhace’s hands like she would watch a bridge seam widen under load.  
Not with pity. With measurement.  

He stood in the rope lane where the wind pulled hardest, directing a brace team through a collapsed archway. His wedge bar moved in steady arcs—drive, pull, seat—but every time his hand came off the handle to adjust his grip, it shook. Fine, rapid tremors that started at the fingertips and climbed the forearm like a signal losing its way.  

Tiffani didn’t write it down yet. She waited. Counted breaths. Counted pull cycles. Counted when the shaking worsened and when it eased.  

When the pull phase hit, his hands steadied.  
When the slack came, they shook harder.  

Correlation, not causation. Not yet.  

She moved closer, keeping her distance, letting the crowd flow around her like water around a stone. Her slate stayed closed. Her pencil stayed still. She recorded in her head:  
*Subject J. T4 baseline. Exacerbates on slack phase. Compensates during pull.*  

A child’s voice cut through the wind. “Jhace!”  
Chloen emerged from behind a broken wall, face smudged, eyes wide. She held up a canteen. “Water’s here.”  

Jhace’s hand jerked once as he took it—just a flicker before he forced stillness. He drank without spilling. Gave the canteen back. “Thanks.” His voice was flat. Controlled.  

Chloen nodded and stepped back, but her eyes stayed on his hands. She’d seen them shake longer than Tiffani had.  

Tiffani noted it: *Observer C. Recognizes pattern. Does not name it.*  

A pressure pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted in slow sheets. Coppergilt gathered along Tiffani’s slate edge, lifting in tiny arcs like filings answering a hidden magnet.  

Jhace’s hands steadied.  

The slack came. Air rushed back in. Sound returned with it—bells, voices, the scrape of stone on stone.  

Jhace’s tremor spiked. His fingers curled into fists at his sides. His jaw tightened like something was trying to climb out of his throat.  

Tiffani moved forward then, not to him, but to a nearby brace captain marking cracks with chalk. She opened her slate at the edge of his sightline without looking at him.  

*Pattern:*  
*— Slack phase = tremor increase*  
*— Pull phase = tremor decrease*  
*— Observation = tremor increase*  
*— Physical task = tremor decrease*  

The captain’s chalk stopped mid-stroke. “What’s that mean?” he asked, voice low.  

Tiffani didn’t answer. She closed the slate. Walked away.  

Jhace watched her go. His hands were still shaking.  

***  

The upper terrace had become a counting place.  
Not by choice. By physics.  
The rope lanes converged there, funneled by the mountain’s shape and the city’s broken grid. People moved through the lanes like water finding channels—some with wax tabs on their wrists, some without. The tabbed moved faster. The untabbed hesitated at every checkpoint.  

Tiffani stood at a high point where the lanes met, slate open, pencil ready. Not counting people. Counting patterns.  

She watched where shoulders tensed when the pull hit. Where hands went to throats when breath failed. Where coppergilt gathered on metal buckles and slate edges. Where the doubled hum made stone vibrate like a plucked string.  

She wrote:  
*Breath failure correlates to pull phase intensity.*  
*Coppergilt adhesion strongest on brass/iron during slack.*  
*Tremor patterns cluster where observation increases.*  

A Crown recorder stood nearby, brass emblem bright on her cloak, slate held tight to her ribs. She counted people. Tiffani counted symptoms.  

The recorder glanced at Tiffani’s slate. “What’s that?”  
“Measurements,” Tiffani said.  
“What kind?”  
“Baseline shifts.”  
The recorder’s eyes narrowed. “For who?”  
Tiffani did not answer. She closed her slate.  

***  

Jhace found her at the edge of the sorting area, away from the crowd, where the rope lanes thinned into single-file paths. He carried his wedge bar like it was part of his arm. His hands were steady on the handle.  

“You were watching my hands,” he said. No accusation. Statement.  

Tiffani didn’t look at him. She watched the dust lift on the pull phase. “I watch everything.”  

“Not everything,” he said. “Just the shaking.”  

She met his eyes then. “It’s data.”  

“Whose data?”  
“Yours. Mine. The planet’s. It doesn’t belong to anyone.”  

Jhace’s grip tightened on the wedge bar. His knuckles whitened. The tremor started again at his fingertips. “It’s in my hands,” he said. “It belongs to me.”  

Tiffani’s knee ached. She shifted her weight. Ignored it. “When you work, it lessens,” she said. “When people watch, it worsens. When the slack comes, it spikes.”  

Jhace stared at her. “You counted that.”  
“Yes.”  

“Why?”  
“Because it’s the shape of the cost,” she said. “If we don’t know the cost, we can’t measure the hold.”  

Jhace’s jaw worked. “You think I don’t know the cost?”  
“I think you feel it,” Tiffani said. “I think I can measure it.”  

A pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted. Coppergilt rose off Tiffani’s slate in tiny arcs and settled again on the slack.  

Jhace’s hands steadied on the wedge bar.  

Tiffani wrote one line on her slate, small and crisp:  
*Sequence confirmed: tremor ↔ phase ↔ attention.*  

She closed the slate.  
Jhace didn’t move. “What does that mean?”  
“It means,” Tiffani said, “that the next time you hold something, I’ll know how much it costs before you do.”  

She walked away before he could answer.  
His hands started shaking again as soon as she turned her back.  
She didn’t look.  
She didn’t need to.  
She already knew the sequence.  
The bells kept ringing. The doubled hum kept vibrating underfoot.  
And Tiffani moved through the lanes, slate under her arm, counting the cost of being alive in a world that had learned to break.

def snippet(pos, width=400):
    start=max(0,pos-width)
    end=min(len(text),pos+width)
    return text[start:end]

### Chapter 3 sex-seed: I’d move it (and I’d undo it in Ch3)

Your critique is correct: **Ch3 is the wrong delivery system** for that reveal. It interrupts the “get to ground / start cataloging” momentum and it shifts register hard.

**Fix:** remove the explicit flashback from Ch3, keep only the knee signal, and plant the first “sex-as-reset / compulsion seed” in **Ch13 (The Bowl interlude)** where it can actually *do work*.

**Ch3 replacement (keep it tight, in-voice):**  
Drop the explicit memory. Replace it with something like this where the knee is already being referenced / can naturally appear:

> Her knee hitched on the last steps. She logged it as load and kept moving. Pain was just another instrument reading, and this wasn’t the moment to argue with it.

That keeps the body present without hijacking the catastrophe beat.

**Ch13 seed (first real tell, transactional, not lyrical):**  
Put this in the interlude when she has a sliver of stillness and her body can “collect the bill”:

> Her knee kept time with the pressure cycle—flare on pull, dull on slack—an old injury made newly loud by a world that wouldn’t settle.  
>   
> She could still feel the bruise pattern from the inn mattress. Dock-worker weight from behind. Not romance. Not softness. A reset she’d learned to buy when her head got too full of numbers and nothing else would quiet it.  
>   
> It had been worth it last night. The ache proved it. The satisfaction had been clean: a choice made on her terms, a body obeying a protocol for once.  
>   
> Now the bill sat in her joint while the sky stayed torn, and she hated how quickly her mind tried to price the next time.

That’s your first seed: **she uses sex as control/relief**, and she’s starting to realize even that may get taken from her.

### Chapter 11: compliance + the fixes I’d make

**Passes (big stuff):**
- Tiffani treats Jhace’s tremor as **measurable cost** (matches Ch11 intent in the handover).
- You keep it **phase-linked** (pull/slack), and you tie it to **attention** (proto-protocol logic).
- Crown recorder stays distinct from Tiffani (good separation).

**Issues to fix (real ones):**

1) **Musical metaphor leak (hard ban):**  
> “vibrate like a plucked string”  
Cut it. Replace with structural language.

**Replace:**  
- “like a plucked string” → “like a tensioned cable under sudden load”  
(or “like a brace member ringing under stress” if you want it more in-world)

2) **Coppergilt phase inconsistency inside the chapter:**  
You show coppergilt **lifting on pull** and **settling on slack**, then you write “adhesion strongest… during slack.” That can work if you phrase it as **mobilize vs settle**.

**Fix the line to be unambiguous:**  
- “Coppergilt adhesion strongest on brass/iron during slack.” →  
  “Coppergilt mobilizes on pull; it *settles and clings* on slack—strongest on brass/iron.”

3) **The exposed “Pattern:” block is a little too lab-report on the page.**  
Tiffani can think this way, but the *presentation* is getting close to scaffolding. Keep the content, compress the format.

**Instead of:**
- *Pattern: — Slack phase… — Pull phase… — Observation… — Physical task…*

**Do:**
> Slack made the tremor spike. Pull damped it. Work steadied it. Eyes on him made it worse.

Same information, less “clipboard.”

4) **“plucked string” + “hidden magnet” stack risks turning the chapter into metaphors about fields.**  
One is fine; both in close proximity makes it feel like the narration is showing off.

If you keep the filings behavior, let it stand **without the magnet comparison** once, because coppergilt already *acts* like it.

### Chapter 11 — The Sequence (LOCKFIX pass)

Tiffani watched the tremor in Jhace’s hands like she would watch a bridge seam widen under load.  
Not with pity. With measurement.  

He stood in the rope lane where the wind pulled hardest, directing a brace team through a collapsed archway. His wedge bar moved in steady arcs—drive, pull, seat—but every time his hand came off the handle to adjust his grip, it shook. Fine, rapid tremors that started at the fingertips and climbed the forearm like a signal losing its way.  

Tiffani didn’t write it down yet. She waited. Counted breaths. Counted pull cycles. Counted when the shaking worsened and when it eased.  

When the pull phase hit, his hands steadied.  
When the slack came, they shook harder.  

Correlation, not causation. Not yet.  

She moved closer, keeping her distance, letting the crowd flow around her like water around a stone. Her slate stayed closed. Her pencil stayed still. She recorded in her head:  
*Subject J. T4 baseline. Exacerbates on slack phase. Compensates during pull.*  

A child’s voice cut through the wind. “Jhace!”  
Chloen emerged from behind a broken wall, face smudged, eyes wide. She held up a canteen. “Water’s here.”  

Jhace’s hand jerked once as he took it—just a flicker before he forced stillness. He drank without spilling. Gave the canteen back. “Thanks.” His voice was flat. Controlled.  

Chloen nodded and stepped back, but her eyes stayed on his hands. She’d seen them shake longer than Tiffani had.  

Tiffani noted it: *Observer C. Recognizes pattern. Does not name it.*  

A pressure pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted in slow sheets. Coppergilt gathered along Tiffani’s slate edge, lifting in tiny arcs toward Jhace’s hands before settling again.  

Jhace’s hands steadied.  

The slack came. Air rushed back in. Sound returned with it—bells, voices, the scrape of stone on stone.  

Jhace’s tremor spiked. His fingers curled into fists at his sides. His jaw tightened like something was trying to climb out of his throat.  

Tiffani moved forward then, not to him, but to a nearby brace captain marking cracks with chalk. She opened her slate at the edge of his sightline without looking at him.  

*Slack spikes tremor. Pull dampens. Work steadies. Attention worsens.*  

The captain’s chalk stopped mid-stroke. “What’s that mean?” he asked, voice low.  

Tiffani didn’t answer. She closed the slate. Walked away.  

Jhace watched her go. His hands were still shaking.  

***  

The upper terrace had become a counting place.  
Not by choice. By physics.  
The rope lanes converged there, funneled by the mountain’s shape and the city’s broken grid. People moved through the lanes like water finding channels—some with wax tabs on their wrists, some without. The tabbed moved faster. The untabbed hesitated at every checkpoint.  

Tiffani stood at a high point where the lanes met, slate open, pencil ready. Not counting people. Counting patterns.  

She watched where shoulders tensed when the pull hit. Where hands went to throats when breath failed. Where coppergilt gathered on metal buckles and slate edges. Where the doubled hum made stone vibrate like a tensioned cable taking sudden load.  

She wrote:  
*Breath failure correlates to pull phase intensity.*  
*Coppergilt mobilizes on pull; settles and clings on slack—strongest on brass/iron.*  
*Tremor patterns cluster where attention increases.*  

A Crown recorder stood nearby, brass emblem bright on her cloak, slate held tight to her ribs. She counted people. Tiffani counted symptoms.  

The recorder glanced at Tiffani’s slate. “What’s that?”  
“Measurements,” Tiffani said.  
“What kind?”  
“Baseline shifts.”  
The recorder’s eyes narrowed. “For who?”  
Tiffani did not answer. She closed her slate.  

***  

Jhace found her at the edge of the sorting area, away from the crowd, where the rope lanes thinned into single-file paths. He carried his wedge bar like it was part of his arm. His hands were steady on the handle.  

“You were watching my hands,” he said. No accusation. Statement.  

Tiffani didn’t look at him. She watched the dust lift on the pull phase. “I watch everything.”  

“Not everything,” he said. “Just the shaking.”  

She met his eyes then. “It’s data.”  

“Whose data?”  
“Yours. Mine. The planet’s. It doesn’t belong to anyone.”  

Jhace’s grip tightened on the wedge bar. His knuckles whitened. The tremor started again at his fingertips. “It’s in my hands,” he said. “It belongs to me.”  

Tiffani’s knee ached. She shifted her weight. Ignored it. “When you work, it lessens,” she said. “When people watch, it worsens. When the slack comes, it spikes.”  

Jhace stared at her. “You counted that.”  
“Yes.”  

“Why?”  
“Because it’s the shape of the cost,” she said. “If we don’t know the cost, we can’t measure the hold.”  

Jhace’s jaw worked. “You think I don’t know the cost?”  
“I think you feel it,” Tiffani said. “I think I can measure it.”  

A pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted. Coppergilt rose off Tiffani’s slate in tiny arcs and settled again on the slack.  

Jhace’s hands steadied on the wedge bar.  

Tiffani wrote one line on her slate, small and crisp:  
*Sequence confirmed: tremor ↔ phase ↔ attention.*  

She closed the slate.  
Jhace didn’t move. “What does that mean?”  
“It means,” Tiffani said, “that the next time you hold something, I’ll know how much it costs before you do.”  

She walked away before he could answer.  
His hands started shaking again as soon as she turned her back.  
She didn’t look.  
She didn’t need to.  
She already knew the sequence.  
The bells kept ringing. The doubled hum kept vibrating underfoot.  
And Tiffani moved through the lanes, slate under her arm, counting the cost of being alive in a world that had learned to break.

Chapter 12 — The Question
Chloen stayed in the rope lane shadows where the dust was thickest.
Not hiding. Just waiting for the counting to end.

The upper terrace had become a sorting ground.
Wax-tabbed moved first. Untabbed waited.
Chloen’s wrist was bare. Her sleeve covered the graphite smear where Tiffani’s numbers lived. She touched it every few breaths to make sure it was still there.

A brace team worked at the edge of the sorting area, propping a leaning wall with scavenged timber. Jhace directed them, his wedge bar moving in steady arcs. His hands were steady on the handle. Off the handle, they shook—fine tremors running up his forearms like water finding cracks in stone.

Chloen watched his knuckles whiten when a tabbed runner approached him with a slate. She watched how the runner’s eyes flicked to Jhace’s shaking fingers before asking his name.

T4, Chloen thought. That’s what Tiffani would call it.

She didn’t have words for the cost. She only had the memory of his palm outstretched under the slab, holding the world still while she scrambled out from under it.

A hand touched her shoulder. Light. Professional. Not gentle.

“Child,” the voice said. “You’re in the wrong lane.”

Chloen turned.
Bram stood there. Older. Dust on his sleeves. A sash with muted brass threads across his chest—not Crown gold, not Infrastructure silver. Something in between. His eyes were tired but sharp. They didn’t look at her face. They looked at her boots, her hands, the way she held her shoulders. Like he was measuring load capacity.

“I’m waiting for the water ration,” Chloen said. She kept her voice flat. Not small. Not loud. Just there.

Bram’s gaze flicked to her sleeve. To the graphite smear showing at the cuff. “Mark three?” he asked.

Chloen didn’t answer. She didn’t need to. The runner had already seen the smear.

Bram shifted his weight. “Water ration’s moved. Two terraces down. Rope lane three.” He didn’t move. Didn’t step aside. Just waited.

Above them, the Rift hung in the Kindling-light. Black in blue. Stars where there shouldn’t be stars. The doubled hum ran through the stone under their feet.

“You saw the man at the stadium stairs,” Bram said. Not a question. A fact.

Chloen’s throat tightened. She didn’t look at him. “I saw people.”

“Not just people.” Bram’s voice stayed low, even. “One man. Walking slow. Stopping on the pull phase. Hand to his chest.”

Chloen’s fingers curled into her palms. Grit stung where it caught in the cracks.

“He left copper smears on the stone,” Bram said. “Like your hands do after a hard breath.”

Chloen didn’t move. Didn’t speak.

A pressure pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted in slow sheets.
Coppergilt rose off Bram’s sash threads in tiny arcs before settling again.

Bram’s hand went to his own sternum for one breath. Just one. Then dropped back to his side.

“I know what it tastes like,” he said. “Not every mouth is flooded. But some are.”

Chloen’s jaw tightened. “I didn’t do anything.”

“No,” Bram agreed. “You just watched. That’s the only thing they can’t take from you yet. The watching.”

The slack came. Air rushed back in. Bells rang in uneven waves.

Bram stepped aside. “Rope lane three,” he said. “Two terraces down. Tell the runner Bram sent you.”

Chloen didn’t move. “Why?”

Bram’s eyes went to Jhace, still working the brace team. His hands shook harder now that the runner’s attention had moved away. “Because the ones who hold break first. The ones who count break next. The ones who watch...” He paused. “They’re the last to break. Or the first to remember what breaking looks like.”

A child’s cry rose from the sorting area. Not fear. Frustration. A boy with a broken wrist had been told to wait in the back rows. His mother argued with a brace captain who kept pointing at chalk lines on the ground.

Chloen watched the mother’s shoulders tighten. Watched the captain’s hands gesture at the lines like they were laws carved in stone.

“The question isn’t whether you saw him,” Bram said softly. “The question is what you do with what you saw.”

Chloen looked at her own hands. At the graphite smear. At the coppergilt catching in the creases of her knuckles.

Jhace’s voice carried across the terrace. Sharp. “Not that angle. Reset the wedge.”

His hands were steady on the bar. Off the bar, they shook like leaves in wind.

“Do you break things, little watcher?” Bram asked. “Or do you mend them?”

Chloen didn’t answer. She put her hand back in her pocket. Touched the numbers again. 3-7-2.

Bram didn’t wait for her reply. He turned and walked toward the arguing mother and captain. His steps were even. His shoulders didn’t tense on the pull phase like others did.

At the edge of the sorting area, he stopped. Didn’t speak to the captain. Didn’t speak to the mother. He just knelt and redrew the chalk line with his boot heel, making a small path around the boy’s broken wrist.

“Path’s for people,” he said to no one in particular. “Not just stones.”

The mother pulled her son through the gap without thanking him. The captain didn’t protest.

Bram stood up. Brushed dust from his knees. Looked back at Chloen. “Rope lane three,” he repeated. “Before they change the marks again.”

Chloen moved. Not toward rope lane three. Toward Jhace and the brace team.

Bram didn’t stop her.

At the work site, Jhace was leaning on his wedge bar, breathing shallow. His hands were shaking badly. His teeth buzzed. Someone handed him a canteen. He spilled half before getting it to his mouth.

Chloen stopped three steps away.

Jhace saw her. His eyes flicked to her sleeve. To the graphite smear. “Bram sent you?” he asked, voice rough.

Chloen nodded.

“He knows about the numbers,” she said.

Jhace’s grip tightened on the wedge bar. The shaking lessened. “Bram knows about most things that matter,” he said. “He just doesn’t talk about the ones that don’t.”

Chloen watched his hands. Watched the tremor climb his forearms when he wasn’t working. “He asked me a question,” she said.

Jhace’s jaw worked. “What question?”

“What I do with what I see.”

Jhace stared at her for three breaths. Then he handed her the canteen. “Hold this,” he said. “Steady.”

His hands were still shaking. But with the canteen in hers, the tremor in his fingers lessened, just a fraction.

Above them, the Rift stayed open. The doubled hum stayed underfoot.
And Chloen held the canteen steady while Jhace’s hands learned how to be still again.

### Chapter 12 — The Question (revised)

Chloen stayed in the rope-lane shadows where the dust was thickest.  
Not hiding. Waiting for the counting to finish with her.

The upper terrace had become a sorting ground.  
Wax-tabbed moved first. Untabbed waited.

Chloen’s wrist was bare. Her sleeve covered the graphite smear where Tiffani’s numbers lived. She touched it every few breaths to make sure it was still there—like the mark could evaporate if she stopped believing in it.

A brace team worked the edge of the sorting area, propping a leaning wall with scavenged timber. Jhace directed them, wedge bar moving in steady arcs. His hands were steady on the handle. Off the handle, they shook—fine tremors running up his forearms like water finding cracks.

A tabbed runner approached with a slate.

Chloen watched the runner’s eyes flick to Jhace’s fingers before he asked his name.

*T4,* Chloen thought. That’s what Tiffani would call it.  
Not fear. Not weakness. A pattern. A cost with a schedule.

The runner moved on. Jhace’s shaking got worse the moment attention left him, like his nerves had been waiting for permission to fail.

A hand touched Chloen’s shoulder. Light. Professional. Not gentle.

“Child,” a voice said. “You’re in the wrong lane.”

Chloen turned.

Bram stood there. Older. Dust on his sleeves. A sash with muted brass threads across his chest—not Crown gold, not Infrastructure silver. Something in between. His eyes were tired but sharp, and they didn’t settle on her face first. They checked boots, hands, shoulders. Load capacity, not sympathy.

“I’m waiting for the water ration,” Chloen said. Flat. Not small.

Bram’s gaze flicked to her cuff. To the graphite smear showing. “Mark three?” he asked.

Chloen didn’t answer. She didn’t need to. He’d already seen it.

Bram made a sound like he was about to say something helpful, then his mouth got ahead of his brain. “Wrong lane. Unless you’re—” he paused, eyes narrowing like he was rewinding himself— “Unless you’re trying to win an award for *Most Patient Bare-Wrist in a Disaster.*” He blinked once. “Bad award. No prize. Don’t recommend.”

Chloen stared at him.

Bram exhaled through his nose. “Water ration moved. Two terraces down. Rope lane three.” He didn’t step aside yet. Just held the space.

Above them, the Rift hung in the Kindling-light—black in blue, stars where there shouldn’t be stars. The doubled hum ran through the stone.

“You saw the man at the stadium stairs,” Bram said.

Chloen’s throat tightened. She kept her eyes on his sash threads instead of his face. “I saw people.”

“Not just people.” Bram’s voice stayed low, even. “One man. Walking slow. Stopping on the pull. Hand to his chest.”

Chloen’s fingers curled into her palms. Grit stung where it found cracks.

“He left copper smears on the stone,” Bram went on. “Like the world’s been handling him without gloves.”

Chloen didn’t move. Didn’t speak.

A pull phase hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted in slow sheets.

Bram’s eyes tracked nothing obvious—just the way bodies tightened when breath failed. His hand went to his own sternum for one breath. Just one. Then dropped.

“I know what it tastes like,” he said. “Not every mouth floods. But some do.”

Chloen swallowed hard. Metal taste surged anyway, like the world enjoyed proving people wrong.

Bram’s mouth twitched, like a joke tried to escape and he hated himself for wanting to let it. “Breathe shallow on pull. Full on slack.” He lifted two fingers, then caught himself. “That’s the whole sermon. Don’t make me repeat it. I charge per repetition.” His eyes flicked to her, then to the lanes. “Kidding. Mostly.”

The slack came. Air rushed back in. Sound returned with it—bells, voices, chalk scraping stone.

Bram stepped aside. “Rope lane three,” he said again. “Tell the runner Bram sent you.”

Chloen didn’t move. “Why?”

Bram’s gaze went to Jhace, still directing the brace team. Jhace’s hands shook harder now that he wasn’t actively driving the wedge. “Because the ones who hold break first,” Bram said. “The ones who count break next.” He paused, and his brain did that stutter-hop again—almost a joke, almost a shrug, almost a truth. “The ones who watch…” He swallowed. “They’re last to break. Or first to remember what breaking looks like.”

A runner shoved past carrying a water yoke. Behind him, a dock worker—broad shoulders, forearms roped with strain—dragged a crate across the stone like he was trying to out-muscle the planet.

Chloen saw Tiffani across the lane for half a breath, giving a sharp hand signal to redirect a crowd away from a crack. Her posture was straight. Her slate stayed closed. Her knee hitched once and she pretended it didn’t.

Then her gaze snagged—just a fraction—on the dock worker’s hands.

Not admiration. Not softness. Something hungry and inconvenient that looked like anger until you watched closely.

Tiffani blinked, cut her eyes away, and went back to directing bodies like nothing had happened.

Chloen didn’t understand it. She only understood the shape: Tiffani’s control wasn’t absence of wanting. It was wanting that got strangled quiet.

Bram noticed too. His eyes flicked once in her direction, then back to Chloen like he’d caught himself thinking something that wasn’t useful. “Anyway,” he said, too fast, as if speed could erase the detour, “you’re not unmarked *yet.* Keep it that way.”

A child’s cry rose from the sorting area—frustration, not fear. A boy with a broken wrist had been told to wait in the back rows. His mother argued with a brace captain pointing at chalk lines on the ground like they were laws.

Chloen watched the mother’s shoulders tighten. Watched the captain’s hands gesture at the lines like the stone itself had spoken.

“The question isn’t whether you saw him,” Bram said softly. “The question is what you do with what you saw.”

Chloen looked at her hands. At the graphite smear hidden under her sleeve. At the coppergilt catching in the creases like it wanted ownership.

Jhace’s voice cut across the terrace. Sharp. “Not that angle. Reset the wedge.”

His hands were steady on the bar. Off the bar, they shook like leaves.

“Do you break things, little watcher?” Bram asked. His mouth did the stutter again—almost a joke, almost not. “Or do you… I don’t know—help keep them from breaking worse?”

Chloen didn’t answer. She put her hand in her pocket and touched the numbers again. **3-7-2.**

Bram didn’t wait. He moved toward the argument.

He didn’t speak to the captain first. Didn’t speak to the mother. He just knelt, boot heel scraping chalk, and redrew the boundary into a narrow path around the boy’s broken wrist.

“Path’s for people,” Bram said to no one in particular. Then, like his brain couldn’t stop itself: “Not just stones. Stones don’t complain enough. If stones started yelling, we’d be out of work.” He blinked. “That’s also a bad joke. Ignore it.”

The mother pulled her son through the gap without thanking him. The captain didn’t protest. Bram stood, brushed dust from his knees, and looked back at Chloen.

“Rope lane three,” he repeated. “Before they change the marks again.”

Chloen moved.

Not toward rope lane three.

Toward Jhace.

Bram didn’t stop her. He just watched her choose and didn’t flinch.

At the work site, Jhace leaned on his wedge bar, breathing shallow. His hands shook badly. Teeth buzzing. Someone handed him a canteen; he spilled half before getting it to his mouth.

Chloen stopped three steps away.

Jhace saw her. His eyes flicked to her sleeve. To the graphite smear. “Bram send you?” he asked, voice rough.

Chloen nodded. “He knows about the numbers.”

Jhace’s grip tightened on the wedge bar. The shaking eased a fraction—like the body heard a threat and decided to behave for one more breath.

“Bram knows about most things that matter,” Jhace said. “He just doesn’t talk about the ones that don’t.”

Chloen watched his hands. Watched the tremor climb when he wasn’t working. “He asked me a question,” she said.

Jhace’s jaw worked. “What question?”

“What I do with what I see.”

Jhace stared at her for three breaths—pull phase building, slack incoming, his throat tightening like it knew the schedule.

Then he handed her the canteen. “Hold this,” he said. “Steady.”

Chloen wrapped both hands around it. Held.

Jhace’s fingers stopped shaking for one thin slice of time, like the tremor got confused by having nothing to perform with.

Above them, the Rift stayed open. The doubled hum stayed underfoot.

And Chloen held the canteen steady while Jhace’s hands learned—briefly—how to be still again.
