[TOOL]
Light bent around it. 
She saw it as a slight lensing of the Rift's edge. Not random shimmer. A field. 
Then it was gone. 
The last she caught was the trailing end clearing the atmosphere and a smear behind it—debris pulled along, some pieces tight to its path, some lagging and beginning to arc back. 
Her hands did not shake. Her knee did. She ignored the knee. 
She wrote as fast as graphite allowed: 
Object observed: spindle/needle. Rotation confirmed. Surface vein-lines pulsing (Cu/Au + unlabeled). No thermal wake. No sonic signature. Lensing observed. Transit time: within a blink at Rift boundary. 
She logged the tick-count again. 
Tick. Tick. Tick. 
Then she wrote: 
Trajectory linear. 
The inhale returned in pulses, not steady. Wind pulled toward the distant face, then slackened, then pulled again as if something enormous was breathing through a throat in the earth. 
She could see it at the far rim: dust at the base surged inward, then pushed outward, irregular but trending toward a pattern. 
Her instrument stand vibrated. The bubble in the level quivered. The pendulum's swing changed period, as if the planet's own timing had been struck and re-set. 
She forced her attention back to the Rift. 
The black remained. It did not close behind the spindle. 
That mattered more than almost anything else. 
She wrote: 
Rift persists post-transit. No closure observed. 
She set the slate down and replaced it with a fresh one. 
The next thing she wrote, in larger letters than she usually allowed herself, was not a theory. It was a directive to her future self. 
DOCUMENT EVERYTHING. 
Pressure pulls: log tick-count on each pull. Breathing: shallow on pull, full on slack. Coppergilt taste in mouth. Metallic. Persistent. Next phase won't be atmospheric. It will be biological. 
Then the city below her answered the world's new baseline with human noise—cries, running feet, collapsing stone, and the first sharp, unmistakable sound of people discovering that the rules had shifted. 
Tiffani didn't look away from the Rift. 
She watched the black wound sit in the blue sky like an error that refused correction, and she kept writing until her graphite stained her fingertips; coppergilt clung in the creases. 
The chronometer ticked. Steady. Indifferent. 
When she finally turned toward the stairwell, it wasn't to join the panic. 
It was to take her measurements to ground level—because the next phase would not be in the sky. 
It would be in the bodies.

[TOOL]
Pressure pulls: irregular. Directional draw toward Exit-face. “Breathing” pattern persists.

A hammer lifted off a workbench and floated for a heartbeat, rotating slowly as if the world had turned viscous.

A scream went up.

Then it dropped sideways. A bucket skated a handspan across stone, drawn toward the horizon as if it had found an invisible incline.

She wrote fast:

Localized suspension anomaly: tool levitation observed (1 heartbeat). Lateral drift toward Exit vector.

Ahead, water beaded upward along a retaining wall seam and lifted into the air in trembling strands before falling back. The grit clung to the wet edge and did not wash away clean.

She wrote:

Water anomaly: upward seep + brief suspension. Particulate adheres at wet boundary.

Somewhere farther down the quarter, a shout rose—sharp, panicked—the sound of someone throwing their body into the path of a drop. Tiffani did not chase it. She did not turn it into a story.

She opened a new list on the lower edge of her slate and kept it cold:

R1: Witness claim—load pause under fall (this site). Unverified.
R2: Reports of brief “holds” elsewhere during collapses. Treat as rumor until observed.

Above the rooftops, the debris band brightened as the sun angle shifted, making the arc flash like a scar being displayed.

Tiffani stopped long enough to check the Rift again: black in blue, unchanged.

She hummed once, steady—the moment between observe and classify—then cut it off when she saw the Crown woman’s head tilt, listening.

She wrote the last line on that slate page:

New baseline established.

The city answered with human noise—cries, running feet, collapsing stone, and the first sharp, unmistakable sound of people discovering the rules had shifted.

Tiffani didn’t look away from the broken street.

She watched coppergilt cling to shattered edges and knew: whatever the sky had become, people were still made of stone and water and breath—and those were failing in ways she could, at least sometimes, predict.

bash -lc cat > /mnt/data/N01_Chapter_10_The_Shard_LOCKFIX_v4.md <<'EOF'
Chapter 10 — The Shard
Chloen followed the rope lanes through the terrace streets, keeping her eyes on the knots where they were tied to stone cleats. The ropes moved in the wind pulls, tightening on the inhale phase, going slack on the exhale. Like breathing made visible.

She'd stayed with Tiffani and Jhace as long as she could, but when the worklines broke into smaller groups and the Crown runners started collecting names, she'd slipped away. Not running. Just moving. Like water finding cracks.

Her pocket held the slip of slate with the numbers Tiffani had given her—3-7-2, terrace marks, lane markers. She touched it every few steps, felt the graphite smear against her thumb. Paper wouldn't have survived the slab-fall day. Slate would.

She passed a brace team propping a leaning wall with scavenged timber. A man with cracked knuckles drove wedges while his partner called out pressure shifts. Their rhythm was precise: pull, drive, check. Pull, drive, check. The doubled hum underfoot seemed to guide them as much as hinder.

Ahead, where three rope lanes converged, a new post had been set up. Not wood. Not stone. A metal pole driven deep, strung with lanterns and bells. A Crown emblem hung from the crossbar.

Crowds thickened there. People stood in rows, not talking much. Some with wax tabs on their wrists. Some without. The ones without stood farther back, shoulders tight.

Chloen slowed. Watched.

A woman with a small, sharp face and a brass emblem on her cloak moved down the line, checking wrists, making marks on a slate. She didn't speak. Just pointed. Tabbed or untabbed. The untabbed got a nod toward the back rows.

Chloen's fingers tightened on her pocket. The numbers felt like a lifeline and a lie at once.

Then she saw Mobel.

He wasn't in line. He stood beside the post, talking low to the Crown woman. Not arguing. Not pleading. Just talking. His hands were clean. His clothes were travel-stained but not torn. He carried a small pack, worn smooth at the straps.

The Crown woman shook her head once. Final. She pointed to the back rows.

Mobel didn't move. He just stood there, weight on his heels, waiting. His face didn't change. But his shoulders did—subtle shift, like a brace settling under load.

Chloen edged closer, keeping behind a man with a broken arm. She didn't want to be seen watching. She just wanted to see what happened when someone refused to break.

A runner approached the post, breathless. He said something to the Crown woman, low and urgent. She glanced at Mobel, then back at the runner. Her mouth tightened.

Mobel didn't smile. But something in his posture relaxed. Like a weight lifted that nobody else could see.

The runner turned to the crowd. "Corlexi of Infrastructure requests Mobel of Te'Oga. Assembly at Basin Stadium. Live briefing. Now."

The Crown woman's head snapped toward Mobel. Her eyes narrowed. "You didn't say—" she started.

"I didn't need to," Mobel said. His voice was quiet but clear. "The request stands."

The woman made a sharp gesture toward the front rows. "Clear path," she barked at her assistants. "This one moves through."

People shifted. Made space. Mobel walked through the gap without looking at anyone. He passed within three steps of Chloen.

She saw the pack on his shoulder. She saw the way his left hand stayed tucked near his chest, fingers curled like they were holding something invisible. She saw the Crown woman watching him go, her face stiff with something that wasn't anger but wasn't respect either.

Chloen followed.

Not closely. Just enough to see.

Mobel walked with a slight limp. Not old injury. New. As if his body had learned a different rhythm and hadn't quite settled into it. His steps matched the slack phase of the pulls. In. Out. In. Out.

They reached a wider terrace where the stone was marked with fresh chalk lines. A runner waited there, holding a horse. Not a cart horse. A riding horse, lean and trembling, its eyes white at the edges.

Mobel stopped. Looked at the horse. Looked at the runner.

"The Basin Stadium," the runner said. "They're waiting."

Mobel didn't take the reins. "I don't ride," he said.

The runner's face tightened. "The request was clear."

"So is the ground," Mobel replied. "I'll walk."

The runner opened his mouth, closed it, then nodded sharply. "Two spans. No more. Or I report delay."

Mobel inclined his head once. "Two spans."

The runner mounted and rode ahead, glancing back every few breaths.

Chloen stayed in the shadow of a half-collapsed arch. Watched Mobel walk.

His limp was worse on the pull phase. His left hand stayed tucked near his chest. Sometimes it pressed flat against his sternum, just for a moment, like he was checking for something beneath his ribs.

A pressure pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted. Coppergilt glittered along the stone edges where metal met rock.

Chloen's ears popped. Her breath caught.

Mobel stopped walking. Stood perfectly still. His hand pressed flat against his chest. His eyes closed for three breaths. When they opened, he looked different. Calmer. Sharper.

He resumed walking. Faster now. Matching the slack phase.

Chloen followed at a distance, watching his hand, watching his limp, watching the way the coppergilt seemed to gather where he'd stood.

She didn't understand what she was seeing. But she understood this: Mobel carried something no one else had. Something that made the Crown woman step back. Something that made a runner wait. Something that lived under his ribs and answered to the doubled hum.

She touched the numbers in her pocket again. 3-7-2.

The Basin Stadium loomed ahead, a stone bowl carved into the mountain face. Voices rose from it—organized, orderly. Not panic. Procedure.

Mobel reached the stadium steps and paused. His hand pressed against his chest again. This time longer. When he lowered it, his fingers left smears on his shirt. Not dust. Not sweat.

Copper.

He climbed the steps without looking back.

Chloen stopped at the edge of the crowd forming at the base. She didn't have a tab. She didn't have a number that mattered here.

A guard approached her. Young. Nervous. His sash was too bright for the dust. "Name and guardian," he said. His voice cracked on guardian.

Chloen's throat tightened. She reached for her pocket. For the numbers.

The guard's eyes followed her hand. Saw the slate slip. Saw the graphite smear. He hesitated.

Before he could speak, a woman's voice cut through the pull-thin air. "That one's with me," Tiffani said, stepping up beside Chloen. Her slate was closed. Her posture was straight. "Water rationing detail. Mark three."

The guard looked at Tiffani's face. At her hands. He nodded once, sharp. "Mark three," he repeated, like memorizing a lesson.

Tiffani didn't smile. She just guided Chloen past the checkpoint with a hand on her shoulder. Light. Professional. Not gentle.

Chloen looked back once at the stadium steps. Mobel was gone. Only the copper smears remained on the stone where he'd stood.

Tiffani's hand stayed on her shoulder. "You watch the wrong things," she said, low. "Watch the ropes. Watch the cracks. Watch who gets marked."

Chloen didn't answer. She watched the copper smears glint in the Kindling-light. Watched them lift slightly on the next pull phase, like filings answering a hidden magnet.

She kept the numbers in her pocket. But now she knew there were other numbers. Other marks. Other things that lived under ribs and answered to the world's new heartbeat.

The stadium bells began to ring. Three short. One long. Two short.

The rope lanes tightened. The crowd moved.

Chloen moved with them. But her eyes stayed on the stairs where Mobel had stood, where the copper smears caught the light and didn't let go.
EOF

[TOOL]
34:She shut the lid, hooked the box back to her hip, and kept moving.

[ASSISTANT]
bash -lc cat > /mnt/data/N01_Chapter_10_The_Shard_LOCKFIX_v4.md <<'EOF'

The rope lanes tightened. The crowd moved.

Chloen moved with them. But her eyes stayed on the stairs where Mobel had stood, where the copper smears caught the light and didn't let go.

Chapter 10 — The Shard

bash -lc cat > /mnt/data/N01_Chapter_11_The_Sequence_LOCKFIX_v2.md <<'EOF'
Chapter 11 — The Sequence
Tiffani watched the tremor in Jhace’s hands like she would watch a bridge seam widen under load.
Not with pity. With measurement.

He stood in the rope lane where the wind pulled hardest, directing a brace team through a collapsed archway. His wedge bar moved in steady arcs—drive, pull, seat—but every time his hand came off the handle to adjust his grip, it shook. Fine, rapid tremors that started at the fingertips and climbed the forearm like a signal losing its way.

Tiffani didn’t write it down yet. She waited. Counted breaths. Counted pull cycles. Counted when the shaking worsened and when it eased.

When the pull phase hit, his hands steadied.
When the slack came, they shook harder.

Correlation, not causation. Not yet.

She moved closer, keeping her distance, letting the crowd flow around her like water around a stone. Her slate stayed closed. Her pencil stayed still. She recorded in her head:
Subject J. T4 baseline. Exacerbates on slack phase. Compensates during pull.

A child’s voice cut through the wind. “Jhace!”
Chloen emerged from behind a broken wall, face smudged, eyes wide. She held up a canteen. “Water’s here.”

Jhace’s hand jerked once as he took it—just a flicker before he forced stillness. He drank without spilling. Gave the canteen back. “Thanks.” His voice was flat. Controlled.

Chloen nodded and stepped back, but her eyes stayed on his hands. She’d seen them shake longer than Tiffani had.

Tiffani noted it: Observer C. Recognizes pattern. Does not name it.

A pressure pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted in slow sheets. Coppergilt gathered along Tiffani’s slate edge, lifting in tiny arcs toward Jhace’s hands before settling again.

Jhace’s hands steadied.

The slack came. Air rushed back in. Sound returned with it—bells, voices, the scrape of stone on stone.

Jhace’s tremor spiked. His fingers curled into fists at his sides. His jaw tightened like something was trying to climb out of his throat.

Tiffani moved forward then, not to him, but to a nearby brace captain marking cracks with chalk. She opened her slate at the edge of his sightline without looking at him.

Slack spikes tremor. Pull dampens. Work steadies. Attention worsens.

The captain’s chalk stopped mid-stroke. “What’s that mean?” he asked, voice low.

Tiffani didn’t answer. She closed the slate. Walked away.

Jhace watched her go. His hands were still shaking.

***

The upper terrace had become a counting place.
Not by choice. By physics.
The rope lanes converged there, funneled by the mountain’s shape and the city’s broken grid. People moved through the lanes like water finding channels—some with wax tabs on their wrists, some without. The tabbed moved faster. The untabbed hesitated at every checkpoint.

Tiffani stood at a high point where the lanes met, slate open, pencil ready. Not counting people. Counting patterns.

She watched where shoulders tensed when the pull hit. Where hands went to throats when breath failed. Where coppergilt gathered on metal buckles and slate edges. Where the doubled hum made stone vibrate like a tensioned cable taking sudden load.

She wrote:
Breath failure correlates to pull phase intensity.
Coppergilt mobilizes on pull; settles and clings on slack—strongest on brass/iron.
Tremor patterns cluster where attention increases.

A Crown recorder stood nearby, brass emblem bright on her cloak, slate held tight to her ribs. She counted people. Tiffani counted symptoms.

The recorder glanced at Tiffani’s slate. “What’s that?”
“Measurements,” Tiffani said.
“What kind?”
“Baseline shifts.”
The recorder’s eyes narrowed. “For who?”
Tiffani did not answer. She closed her slate.

***

Jhace found her at the edge of the sorting area, away from the crowd, where the rope lanes thinned into single-file paths. He carried his wedge bar like it was part of his arm. His hands were steady on the handle.

“You were watching my hands,” he said. No accusation. Statement.

Tiffani didn’t look at him. She watched the dust lift on the pull phase. “I watch everything.”

“Not everything,” he said. “Just the shaking.”

She met his eyes then. “It’s data.”

“Whose data?”
“Yours. Mine. The planet’s. It doesn’t belong to anyone.”

Jhace’s grip tightened on the wedge bar. His knuckles whitened. The tremor started again at his fingertips. “It’s in my hands,” he said. “It belongs to me.”

Tiffani’s knee ached. She shifted her weight. Ignored it. “When you work, it lessens,” she said. “When people watch, it worsens. When the slack comes, it spikes.”

Jhace stared at her. “You counted that.”
“Yes.”

“Why?”
“Because it’s the shape of the cost,” she said. “If we don’t know the cost, we can’t measure the hold.”

Jhace’s jaw worked. “You think I don’t know the cost?”
“I think you feel it,” Tiffani said. “I think I can measure it.”

A pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted. Coppergilt rose off Tiffani’s slate in tiny arcs and settled again on the slack.

Jhace’s hands steadied on the wedge bar.

Tiffani wrote one line on her slate, small and crisp:
Sequence confirmed: tremor ↔ phase ↔ attention.

She closed the slate.
Jhace didn’t move. “What does that mean?”

“It means,” Tiffani said, “that the next time you hold something, I’ll know how much it costs before you do.”

She walked away before he could answer.
His hands started shaking again as soon as she turned her back.
She didn’t look.
She didn’t need to.
She already knew the sequence.
The bells kept ringing. The doubled hum kept vibrating underfoot.
And Tiffani moved through the lanes, slate under her arm, counting the cost of being alive in a world that had learned to break.
EOF

[TOOL]
Chapter 10 — The Shard
Chloen followed the rope lanes through the terrace streets, keeping her eyes on the knots where they were tied to stone cleats. The ropes moved in the wind pulls, tightening on the inhale phase, going slack on the exhale. Like breathing made visible.

She'd stayed with Tiffani and Jhace as long as she could, but when the worklines broke into smaller groups and the Crown runners started collecting names, she'd slipped away. Not running. Just moving. Like water finding cracks.

Her pocket held the slip of slate with the numbers Tiffani had given her—3-7-2, terrace marks, lane markers. She touched it every few steps, felt the graphite smear against her thumb. Paper wouldn't have survived the slab-fall day. Slate would.

She passed a brace team propping a leaning wall with scavenged timber. A man with cracked knuckles drove wedges while his partner called out pressure shifts. Their rhythm was precise: pull, drive, check. Pull, drive, check. The doubled hum underfoot seemed to guide them as much as hinder.

Ahead, where three rope lanes converged, a new post had been set up. Not wood. Not stone. A metal pole driven deep, strung with lanterns and bells. A Crown emblem hung from the crossbar.

Crowds thickened there. People stood in rows, not talking much. Some with wax tabs on their wrists. Some without. The ones without stood farther back, shoulders tight.

Chloen slowed. Watched.

A woman with a small, sharp face and a brass emblem on her cloak moved down the line, checking wrists, making marks on a slate. She didn't speak. Just pointed. Tabbed or untabbed. The untabbed got a nod toward the back rows.

Chloen's fingers tightened on her pocket. The numbers felt like a lifeline and a lie at once.

Then she saw Mobel.

He wasn't in line. He stood beside the post, talking low to the Crown woman. Not arguing. Not pleading. Just talking. His hands were clean. His clothes were travel-stained but not torn. He carried a small pack, worn smooth at the straps.

The Crown woman shook her head once. Final. She pointed to the back rows.

Mobel didn't move. He just stood there, weight on his heels, waiting. His face didn't change. But his shoulders did—subtle shift, like a brace settling under load.

Chloen edged closer, keeping behind a man with a broken arm. She didn't want to be seen watching. She just wanted to see what happened when someone refused to break.

A runner approached the post, breathless. He said something to the Crown woman, low and urgent. She glanced at Mobel, then back at the runner. Her mouth tightened.

Mobel didn't smile. But something in his posture relaxed. Like a weight lifted that nobody else could see.

The runner turned to the crowd. "Corlexi of Infrastructure requests Mobel of Te'Oga. Assembly at Basin Stadium. Live briefing. Now."

The Crown woman's head snapped toward Mobel. Her eyes narrowed. "You didn't say—" she started.

"I didn't need to," Mobel said. His voice was quiet but clear. "The request stands."

The woman made a sharp gesture toward the front rows. "Clear path," she barked at her assistants. "This one moves through."
The stadium bells began to ring. Three short. One long. Two short.

The rope lanes tightened. The crowd moved.

Chloen moved with them. But her eyes stayed on the stairs where Mobel had stood, where the copper smears caught the light and didn't let go.

bash -lc cat > /mnt/data/N01_Chapter_12_The_Question_LOCKFIX_v2.md <<'EOF'
Chapter 12 — The Question
Chloen stayed in the rope-lane shadows where the dust was thickest.
Not hiding. Waiting for the counting to finish with her.

The upper terrace had become a sorting ground.
Wax-tabbed moved first. Untabbed waited.

Chloen’s wrist was bare. Her sleeve covered the graphite smear where Tiffani’s numbers lived. She touched it every few breaths to make sure it was still there—like the mark could evaporate if she stopped believing in it.

A brace team worked the edge of the sorting area, propping a leaning wall with scavenged timber. Jhace directed them, wedge bar moving in steady arcs. His hands were steady on the handle. Off the handle, they shook—fine tremors running up his forearms like water finding cracks.

A tabbed runner approached with a slate.

Chloen watched the runner’s eyes flick to Jhace’s fingers before he asked his name.

T4, Chloen thought. That’s what Tiffani would call it.
Not fear. Not weakness. A pattern. A cost with a schedule.

The runner moved on. Jhace’s shaking got worse the moment attention left him, like his nerves had been waiting for permission to fail.

A hand touched Chloen’s shoulder. Light. Professional. Not gentle.

“Child,” a voice said. “You’re in the wrong lane.”

Chloen turned.

Bram stood there. Older. Dust on his sleeves. A sash with muted brass threads across his chest—not Crown gold, not Infrastructure silver. Something in between. His eyes were tired but sharp, and they didn’t settle on her face first. They checked boots, hands, shoulders. Load capacity, not sympathy.

“I’m waiting for the water ration,” Chloen said. Flat. Not small.

Bram’s gaze flicked to her cuff. To the graphite smear showing. “Mark three?” he asked.

Chloen didn’t answer. She didn’t need to. He’d already seen it.

Bram made a sound like he was about to say something helpful, then his mouth got ahead of his brain. “Wrong lane. Unless you’re—” he paused, eyes narrowing like he was rewinding himself— “Unless you’re trying to win an award for Most Patient Bare-Wrist in a Disaster.” He blinked once. “Bad award. No prize. Don’t recommend.”

Chloen stared at him.

Bram exhaled through his nose. “Water ration moved. Two terraces down. Rope lane three.” He didn’t step aside yet. Just held the space.

Above them, the Rift hung in the Kindling-light—black in blue, stars where there shouldn’t be stars. The doubled hum ran through the stone.

“You saw the man at the stadium stairs,” Bram said.

Chloen’s throat tightened. She kept her eyes on his sash threads instead of his face. “I saw people.”

“Not just people.” Bram’s voice stayed low, even. “One man. Walking slow. Stopping on the pull. Hand to his chest.”

Chloen’s fingers curled into her palms. Grit stung where it found cracks.

“He left copper smears on the stone,” Bram went on. “Like the world’s been handling him without gloves.”

Chloen didn’t move. Didn’t speak.

A pull phase hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted in slow sheets.

Bram’s eyes tracked nothing obvious—just the way bodies tightened when breath failed. His hand went to his own sternum for one breath. Just one. Then dropped.

“I know what it tastes like,” he said. “Not every mouth floods. But some do.”

Chloen swallowed hard. Metal taste surged anyway, like the world enjoyed proving people wrong.

Bram’s mouth twitched, like a joke tried to escape and he hated himself for wanting to let it. “Breathe shallow on pull. Full on slack.” He lifted two fingers, then caught himself. “That’s the whole sermon. Don’t make me repeat it. I charge per repetition.” His eyes flicked to her, then to the lanes. “Kidding. Mostly.”

The slack came. Air rushed back in. Sound returned with it—bells, voices, chalk scraping stone.

Bram stepped aside. “Rope lane three,” he said again. “Tell the runner Bram sent you.”

Chloen didn’t move. “Why?”

Bram’s gaze went to Jhace, still directing the brace team. Jhace’s hands shook harder now that he wasn’t actively driving the wedge. “Because the ones who hold break first,” Bram said. “The ones who count break next.” He paused, and his brain did that stutter-hop again—almost a joke, almost a shrug, almost a truth. “The ones who watch…” He swallowed. “They’re last to break. Or first to remember what breaking looks like.”

A runner shoved past carrying a water yoke. Behind him, a dock worker—broad shoulders, forearms roped with strain—dragged a crate across the stone like he was trying to out-muscle the planet.

Chloen saw Tiffani across the lane for half a breath, giving a sharp hand signal to redirect a crowd away from a crack. Her posture was straight. Her slate stayed closed. Her knee hitched once and she pretended it didn’t.

Then her gaze snagged—just a fraction—on the dock worker’s hands.

Not admiration. Not softness. Something hungry and inconvenient that looked like anger until you watched closely.

Tiffani blinked, cut her eyes away, and went back to directing bodies like nothing had happened.

Chloen didn’t understand it. She only understood the shape: Tiffani’s control wasn’t absence of wanting. It was wanting that got strangled quiet.

Bram noticed too. His eyes flicked once in her direction, then back to Chloen like he’d caught himself thinking something that wasn’t useful. “Anyway,” he said, too fast, as if speed could erase the detour, “you’re not unmarked yet. Keep it that way.”

A child’s cry rose from the sorting area—frustration, not fear. A boy with a broken wrist had been told to wait in the back rows. His mother argued with a brace captain pointing at chalk lines on the ground like they were laws.

Chloen watched the mother’s shoulders tighten. Watched the captain’s hands gesture at the lines like the stone itself had spoken.

“The question isn’t whether you saw him,” Bram said softly. “The question is what you do with what you saw.”

Chloen looked at her hands. At the graphite smear hidden under her sleeve. At the coppergilt catching in the creases like it wanted ownership.

Jhace’s voice cut across the terrace. Sharp. “Not that angle. Reset the wedge.”

His hands were steady on the bar. Off the bar, they shook like leaves.

“Do you break things, little watcher?” Bram asked. His mouth did the stutter again—almost a joke, almost not. “Or do you… I don’t know—help keep them from breaking worse?”

Chloen didn’t answer. She put her hand in her pocket and touched the numbers again. 3-7-2.

Bram didn’t wait. He moved toward the argument.

He didn’t speak to the captain first. Didn’t speak to the mother. He just knelt, boot heel scraping chalk, and redrew the boundary into a narrow path around the boy’s broken wrist.

“Path’s for people,” Bram said to no one in particular. Then, like his brain couldn’t stop itself: “Not just stones. Stones don’t complain enough. If stones started yelling, we’d be out of work.” He blinked. “That’s also a bad joke. Ignore it.”

The mother pulled her son through the gap without thanking him. The captain didn’t protest. Bram stood, brushed dust from his knees, and looked back at Chloen.

“Rope lane three,” he repeated. “Before they change the marks again.”

Chloen moved.

Not toward rope lane three.

Toward Jhace.

Bram didn’t stop her. He just watched her choose and didn’t flinch.

At the work site, Jhace leaned on his wedge bar, breathing shallow. His hands shook badly. Teeth buzzing. Someone handed him a canteen; he spilled half before getting it to his mouth.

Chloen stopped three steps away.

Jhace saw her. His eyes flicked to her sleeve. To the graphite smear. “Bram send you?” he asked, voice rough.

Chloen nodded. “He knows about the numbers.”

Jhace’s grip tightened on the wedge bar. The shaking eased a fraction—like the body heard a threat and decided to behave for one more breath.

“Bram knows about most things that matter,” Jhace said. “He just doesn’t talk about the ones that don’t.”

Chloen watched his hands. Watched the tremor climb when he wasn’t working. “He asked me a question,” she said.

Jhace’s jaw worked. “What question?”

“What I do with what I see.”

Jhace stared at her for three breaths—pull phase building, slack incoming, his throat tightening like it knew the schedule.

Then he handed her the canteen. “Hold this,” he said. “Steady.”

Chloen wrapped both hands around it. Held.

Jhace’s fingers stopped shaking for one thin slice of time, like the tremor got confused by having nothing to perform with.

Above them, the Rift stayed open. The doubled hum stayed underfoot.

And Chloen held the canteen steady while Jhace’s hands learned—briefly—how to be still again.
EOF

114 /mnt/data/N01_Chapter_11_The_Sequence_LOCKFIX_v2.md
Chapter 11 — The Sequence
Tiffani watched the tremor in Jhace’s hands like she would watch a bridge seam widen under load.
Not with pity. With measurement.

He stood in the rope lane where the wind pulled hardest, directing a brace team through a collapsed archway. His wedge bar moved in steady arcs—drive, pull, seat—but every time his hand came off the handle to adjust his grip, it shook. Fine, rapid tremors that started at the fingertips and climbed the forearm like a signal losing its way.
She didn’t look.
She didn’t need to.
She already knew the sequence.
The bells kept ringing. The doubled hum kept vibrating underfoot.
And Tiffani moved through the lanes, slate under her arm, counting the cost of being alive in a world that had learned to break.

bash -lc wc -l /mnt/data/N01_Chapter_12_The_Question_LOCKFIX_v2.md; head -n 6 /mnt/data/N01_Chapter_12_The_Question_LOCKFIX_v2.md; tail -n 6 /mnt/data/N01_Chapter_12_The_Question_LOCKFIX_v2.md

bash -lc cat > /mnt/data/N01_Chapter_13_The_Bowl_LOCKFIX_v2.md <<'EOF'
Chapter 13 — The Bowl

The bowl sat on the stone shelf in the under-terrace kitchen, half-full of water that never quite settled.

It wasn’t a special bowl. Chipped rim. Clay fired unevenly. The kind of vessel that survived collapse because nobody tried to save it. It held water. That was enough.

Every few breaths, the doubled hum climbed up through the floor. The water shivered—not ripples, not waves, just a fine trembling that made the surface look fractured even when nothing touched it.

A child reached for the bowl. Fingers dipped in, came out wet. Droplets fell back and made the surface jitter harder for a breath.

The child tried again and missed the center. Water kissed the rim and ran down the side in a thin line. The child didn’t seem to notice. They wiped the wet on their sleeve and went back to watching the surface.

Across the room, an older woman worked a makeshift stove. She moved when the air was full enough to trust. When it thinned, she slowed without thinking, one hand flattening at her chest as if it could pin breath in place.

A spoon tapped the pot rim once. Then again. A small, sharp sound, out of rhythm with her intent.

She didn’t curse. She didn’t pause. She just kept stirring, and when broth slopped over the lip and hissed on the stove stone, she wiped it with her apron like it was normal mess.

A pull phase came through. Air thinned. Dust lifted from the floor in lazy spirals. Along the pot handle and the stove’s iron bracket, bright grit lifted a hair, then settled again as if it preferred metal.

The woman’s hand stayed at her chest until the slack returned.

Air rushed back in. Distant bells threaded through the ceiling. The woman ladled broth into smaller bowls with steady, practiced motions. When she set the ladle down, it rolled a finger’s width before she caught it against the pot without looking.

The child abandoned the water bowl and came to the stove, palms out for heat.

“Careful,” the woman said. “Hot.”

She placed a small bowl into the child’s hands. The child held it with both palms and didn’t spill a drop.

The woman poured into a chipped cup for herself. A thin sheet of broth ran over her knuckles. She didn’t flinch. She just set the cup down and wiped her hand on her apron, eyes never leaving the stove as if attention alone kept things from sliding.

In the next room, a man with a brace captain’s sash sat at a table with a slate open. His pencil made small, precise marks. When he stopped, the pencil hovered an instant above the slate as if his fingers had to renegotiate stillness before letting go.

He closed the slate and stood. On the next pull he caught the table edge—not dramatic, not a fall, just a correction made fast enough to pretend it hadn’t happened. He walked out without finishing his broth.

The child watched him go. “Why does it keep slipping?” they asked.

The woman didn’t look up. “Some bodies keep the bill closer,” she said. “Some don’t.”

The child stared at the water bowl again. At the surface that never smoothed. “Will it stop?”

The woman’s hand returned to her chest as the air leaned thin again. “Not soon,” she said. “So you hold something that matters. And you hold it steady.”

She handed the child another bowl, fuller this time. “Don’t spill.”

The child wrapped both hands around it and carried it without slosh.

High above, in the Basin Stadium where the briefing would be held next Kindling, the stone kept the same wrong vibration. Grit slept in mortar seams until the pull came, then lifted and settled again.

Waiting.
EOF

bash -lc cat > /mnt/data/N01_Chapter_14_The_Briefing_LOCKFIX_v1.md <<'EOF'
Chapter 14 — The Briefing

The Basin Stadium wasn't built for silence.
It was carved to hold voices—thousands of them, layered, competing, rising to the stone ceiling and bouncing back as one roar.
Today it held stillness.
A measured, brittle kind of quiet.

Tiffani stood at the back of the upper tier where the stone steps were widest. Her slate was closed. Her chronometer box hung at her hip, unused. She didn't need to count ticks. She could feel the doubled hum through the sole of her boot, vibrating up through bone to the old ache in her knee.

Below her, the stadium floor had been transformed.
Tables formed a U-shape around a raised central platform. Crown officials sat behind the tables—Infrastructure brass at the center, Crown Security flanking them, registrars at the edges with stacks of blank slates. Their cloaks were clean. Their sashes were bright. Their hands rested on wood that hadn't been dusted since the Piercing.

Corlexi of Infrastructure sat at the head table. Older than Tiffani remembered. Sharper. His face was a map of decisions that had cost someone else something. He didn't look at the crowd. He looked at the data-slates before him.

Jhace stood near the front, off to one side, where he could see both the platform and the exit ramp. His wedge bar lay across his knees like it was part of the bench. His hands were steady on the handle. Off the handle, they shook—fine tremors running up his forearms when the breath between pulls stretched too long.

Tiffani didn't write it down. She'd already written it. She just watched the timing.

A man with Mobel's limp sat at Corlexi's right hand. The shard was not visible. But the copper smears on his shirt were. They caught the Kindling-light like stains that refused to wash out.

Corlexi cleared his throat. The sound cut through the stadium's hush like a blade.

"We have three problems," he said. His voice wasn't loud. It didn't need to be. The stadium shape carried it. "The Exit Face. The Rift. And the cost of holding them back."

He paused. Let the words sink in. Let the doubled hum fill the silence.

"Infrastructure has confirmed the Exit Face continues to rise. The Rift persists. Pressure differentials remain. Coppergilt concentrations increase. These are not opinions. These are measurements."

Tiffani's pencil hand twitched. She kept it still.

Corlexi tapped a slate on the table. "This data comes from observatory logs. From field counters. From Crown Infrastructure. It is the shape of our new baseline."

He looked up then. Not at the crowd. At Jhace.

"One pattern has emerged consistently across all reports," Corlexi said. "Those who perform holds—slab suspension, water redirection, structural stabilization—show a specific physiological response."

Jhace's grip tightened on his wedge bar. The shaking lessened.

Corlexi continued. "Tremor. Copper surge. Sternum pressure. Breath restriction. These symptoms escalate with use. They do not decay."

Tiffani's jaw tightened. She hadn't written "escalate." She'd written "vary." She'd written "correlate." She hadn't written "do not decay."

"Onset begins immediately after first use," Corlexi said. "Progression is variable but inevitable. Subject J, for example—"

Jhace's head snapped up.

"—shows T4 baseline tremor. Visible. Persistent. Worsens under observation or during slack phase. Improves during physical exertion or pull phase. The cost is measurable. The cost is cumulative."

Tiffani's knee ached. She shifted her weight. Ignored it.

Corlexi's gaze swept the stadium. "This isn't fear. This is physics. The body has limits. We break those limits at our peril."

A registrar stood. "What's the restriction?" he asked.

"Active holds require authorization," Corlexi said. "No holds outside designated zones. No holds without prior registration. No holds without monitoring."

Murmurs ran through the crowd. Not agreement. Not rebellion. The sound of people calculating what this would cost them.

Jhace stood. Slowly. Deliberately. His wedge bar stayed on the bench. His hands were out where everyone could see them. Shaking.

"I held a slab," he said. His voice was flat. Controlled. "Saved a child. Where was the authorization then?"

Corlexi didn't flinch. "After-action assessment shows the hold was unsustainable. The tremor spiked post-release. Copper levels spiked. Breath failed. You collapsed. The cost nearly killed you. It will kill you next time."

Jhace's hands stilled. Not from control. From anger. "I know the cost," he said.

"Do you?" Corlexi asked. "Or do you just feel it?"

Tiffani moved then. Down the stone steps, quiet, deliberate. She stopped three paces from the platform. Her slate stayed closed.

"Permission to speak," she said.

Corlexi's eyes narrowed. "Tiffani of the Glass-house. Your logs are cited in this briefing. Speak."

Tiffani opened her slate. Not to the page Corlexi had quoted. To her raw data. "Subject J's tremor does not spike post-release," she said. "It spikes on slack phase. It lessens during pull phase. It lessens during physical exertion. It worsens under observation."

She turned the slate so the platform could see. "Sequence confirmed: tremor ↔ phase ↔ attention. Not decay. Pattern."

Corlexi's mouth tightened. "Pattern or decay, the cost exists."

"Cost is not permission," Tiffani said. "Measurement is not control."

A security officer shifted. His hand went to his belt. Not his weapon. His slate.

Corlexi leaned forward. "Your logs show the progression," he said softly. "T1 after first hold. T2 intermittently. T3 frequent. T4 baseline. What comes after T4, Tiffani?"

Tiffani didn't look at Jhace. She looked at her slate. At the data. "T5," she said. "Constant tremor. Operational ceiling."

"And after that?" Corlexi asked.

Tiffani closed her slate. "I don't have that data yet."

Corlexi nodded once. "Then we restrict until we do."

He turned back to the crowd. "Effective immediately, all holds require authorization. Infractions will be recorded. Repeated infractions will result in confinement for assessment."

Jhace's hands started shaking again. Worse than before. Not from slack phase. From rage. He reached for his wedge bar.

Tiffani stepped forward. "Jhace," she said. Just his name.

He stopped. His fingers hovered over the handle.

"Data first," she said. "Reaction after."

Jhace stared at her. His eyes were hard. His throat worked like he was swallowing copper. "Your data got used to make this," he said.

"Yes," Tiffani said.

He held her gaze for three breaths. Then he turned and walked out, shoulders stiff, hands curled into fists at his sides.

Corlexi watched him go. "The first rule of survival," he said to the crowd, "is knowing your limits before the world enforces them for you."

Tiffani didn't move. She didn't close her slate.

Corlexi's eyes met hers. "Thank you for your measurements, Tiffani," he said. "They saved lives today."

Tiffani didn't smile. She didn't nod. She just closed her slate and walked back up the steps, past the rows of people who were now calculating their own limits.

At the top tier, she stopped. Looked down at the platform. At the clean tables. At the bright sashes. At the slates full of her data, twisted into control.

She opened her slate one last time. Wrote one line at the bottom of the page.

*Measurement is not permission.*

Then she closed it again.

Below her, the briefing continued. Voices rose and fell. Decisions were made. Limits were drawn.

Tiffani stood at the edge of the stadium, slate under her arm, and counted the cost of being the one who measured the breaking.

The doubled hum kept vibrating underfoot.
The copper smears on Mobel's shirt glittered in the Kindling-light.
And Jhace's hands kept shaking long after he was gone.
EOF

The child wrapped both hands around it and carried it without slosh.

High above, in the Basin Stadium where the briefing would be held next Kindling, the stone kept the same wrong vibration. Grit slept in mortar seams until the pull came, then lifted and settled again.

Waiting.

20:Then she saw Mobel.
26:Mobel didn't move. He just stood there, weight on his heels, waiting. His face didn't change. But his shoulders did—subtle shift, like a brace settling under load.
30:A runner approached the post, breathless. He said something to the Crown woman, low and urgent. She glanced at Mobel, then back at the runner. Her mouth tightened.
32:Mobel didn't smile. But something in his posture relaxed. Like a weight lifted that nobody else could see.
34:The runner turned to the crowd. "Corlexi of Infrastructure requests Mobel of Te'Oga. Assembly at Basin Stadium. Live briefing. Now."
36:The Crown woman's head snapped toward Mobel. Her eyes narrowed. "You didn't say—" she started.
38:"I didn't need to," Mobel said. His voice was quiet but clear. "The request stands."
42:People shifted. Made space. Mobel walked through the gap without looking at anyone. He passed within three steps of Chloen.
50:Mobel walked with a slight limp. Not old injury. New. As if his body had learned a different rhythm and hadn't quite settled into it. His steps matched the slack phase of the pulls. In. Out. In. Out.
54:Mobel stopped. Looked at the horse. Looked at the runner.
58:Mobel didn't take the reins. "I don't ride," he said.
62:"So is the ground," Mobel replied. "I'll walk."
66:Mobel inclined his head once. "Two spans."
70:Chloen stayed in the shadow of a half-collapsed arch. Watched Mobel walk.
78:Mobel stopped walking. Stood perfectly still. His hand pressed flat against his chest. His eyes closed for three breaths. When they opened, he looked different. Calmer. Sharper.
84:She didn't understand what she was seeing. But she understood this: Mobel carried something no one else had. Something that made the Crown woman step back. Something that made a runner wait. Something that lived under his ribs and answered to the doubled hum.
90:Mobel reached the stadium steps and paused. His hand pressed against his chest again. This time longer. When he lowered it, his fingers left smears on his shirt. Not dust. Not sweat.
110:Chloen looked back once at the stadium steps. Mobel was gone. Only the copper smears remained on the stone where he'd stood.
122:Chloen moved with them. But her eyes stayed on the stairs where Mobel had stood, where the copper smears caught the light and didn't let go.
34:The runner turned to the crowd. "Corlexi of Infrastructure requests Mobel of Te'Oga. Assembly at Basin Stadium. Live briefing. Now."

Corlexi is now **he/him** everywhere (no she/her references remain).

[USER]
# Chapter 15 — The Omission

Tiffani’s slate sat open on the glass-house workbench. Not clean. Not organized.  
Raw data from the last thirty pulls covered the surface—numbers, sketches, timestamps logged by breath-count, not tick-count. Her pencil rested beside it. Unsharpened. Heavy.  

The chronometer box stayed closed.  
If time was broken, measuring its fragments seemed like arranging debris after a blast.  

She’d been up here since the briefing ended. Since the crowd dispersed. Since Jhace walked out with his hands curled into fists, shaking not from slack phase but from the cost of being measured wrong.  

Corlexi had thanked her.  
“Your measurements saved lives.”  
He’d meant, *Your measurements became our authority*.  

Tiffani had closed her slate. Walked away.  
But she’d come back here to open it again. To the pages where she’d written truth.  

Now she needed to unwrite part of it.  

***  

The glass-house held its breath.  
Dust motes hung in the Kindling-light like frozen stars. The iron ribs vibrated with the doubled hum, a low thrum that had become the new silence.  

Tiffani’s knee ached.  
Not the old ache. The new one. The one that remembered salt under nails and rough hands and the relief of being pulled out of her own head for a while.  

She cut the thought off.  
There was no room for that here. Not now.  

She flipped to the page where she’d recorded Jhace’s pattern.  
*Subject J. T4 baseline. Exacerbates on slack phase. Compensates during pull. Improves with physical exertion. Worsens under observation.*  

Below it, she’d written the raw observations from the stadium:  
*08:42 (approx). Tremor spiked on slack phase. J did not collapse. Continued movement.*  
*08:47. Observed water-carrying task. Tremor reduced to baseline during load bearing.*  
*08:59. At edge of stadium, tremor visible but steady enough to grip stair rail.*  

Truth.  
But not the whole truth.  

The whole truth was what she’d noted in tiny letters at the page edge, then crossed out:  
*Pattern suggests adaptation. Not decay. Not inevitable progression to T5.*  

She’d crossed it out an hour ago.  
Now she needed to erase it completely.  

***  

A knock at the stairwell door.  
Sharp. Official. Not Jhace’s heavy thud. Not Chloen’s hesitant tap.  

Tiffani didn’t move.  
Let them wait. Let them feel the hum in the stone steps.  

The knock came again.  
“Tiffani. Infrastructure.”  

Corlexi’s voice. Not a request.  

Tiffani closed her slate. Not over the crossed-out line. Over the clean version.  
She opened the chronometer box. Let the ticks fill the silence for three breaths.  
Then she went to the door.  

Corlexi stood there alone. No sash. No slate. Just the dust of the stadium on his boots. His eyes went past her to the glass-house interior. To the theodolite. To the slates. To the workbench where her crossed-out truth lay hidden.  

“We need your logs,” he said. “All of them. From the first pressure drop.”  

Tiffani didn’t step aside. “What for?”  

“Standardization,” Corlexi said. “We’re building a unified protocol. Your data is foundational.”  

“My data is observational,” Tiffani said. “Not prescriptive.”  

Corlexi’s gaze went to her hands. Not shaking. Steady. Like his.  
“The city is broken in new ways,” he said. “We need to fix it in new ways. Even if the fix costs something.”  

Tiffani thought of Jhace’s hands. Of the wedge bar steady on his knees while his fingers trembled off the handle.  
“Cost isn’t the same as breakage,” she said.  

Corlexi didn’t argue. He just waited. A stone in the current.  

Tiffani stepped aside.  

***  

Corlexi moved through the glass-house like he was measuring its angles.  
He stopped at the workbench. Looked at the slate she’d left open.  
“The stadium observations,” he said.  

Tiffani nodded.  

He turned the slate to read her clean version. Nodded once. “Accurate.”  

Then he reached past it to the chronometer box. Picked it up. Weighed it in his hand. “You stopped logging time.”  

“I stopped trusting it,” Tiffani said.  

Corlexi set the box down. Looked at her. “The shard is unstable. Mobel barely contained it during transit. If it fractures, the resonance could destabilize entire terraces. We need to know who can hold it. Who can hold the cost.”  

Tiffani’s throat tightened. “You mean Jhace.”  

“We mean the pattern,” Corlexi said. “Your pattern. It’s the only one we have. The only one with measurements.”  

He opened her slate to the page where she’d recorded Jhace’s data. Flipped backward through pages—Exit Face measurements, Rift persistence, pressure pulse cycles. Stopped at the first mention of the slab hold.  

“You noticed progression before anyone else,” he said. “T1, T2, T3, T4. You named it. You tracked it. That’s why we need everything. Every page. Every breath.”  

Tiffani watched his fingers on her slate. Clean nails. No grit. No copper smears.  
“What happens when you find T5?” she asked.  

Corlexi’s eyes met hers. “We prepare for it.”  

“You restrict it.”  

“It’s not restriction if it saves lives.”  

Tiffani looked at the crossed-out line on her hidden page. *Pattern suggests adaptation.*  
That truth would kill their protocol. It would make Jhace’s hands a variable, not a limit.  
Corlexi would use it to demand more holds, not fewer.  
Measurement would become requirement.  

Her pencil lay beside the slate. Unsharpened. Heavy.  

Corlexi closed the slate. “We’ll send runners. All your logs. Every page. By the end of this stretch.”  

He turned to go. Stopped at the door. “You did good work, Tiffani.”  

Not *I* trust you.  
*You did good work.*  
Like a foreman praising a tool.  

Tiffani didn’t answer.  

***  

After he left, she went back to the workbench.  
Opened to the page with the crossed-out line.  
Took her pencil. Not to erase. To overwrite.  

She wrote one clean line where the truth had been:  
*Pattern confirms progression. No adaptation observed.*  

Then she flipped to a new page.  
Wrote:  
*M5: Shard resonance containment. Protocol test.*  
*Basis: Subject J’s progression pattern.*  

Below it, she left space. Space for data she wouldn’t collect. Space for measurements she would pretend not to see.  

She closed the slate.  
Wiped her hands on her apron until the coppergilt grit was gone. Until they were just hands again.  

The chronometer ticked.  
Steady. Indifferent.  

Tiffani picked it up. Opened it.  
Listened to the ticks fill the doubled hum.  
One. Two. Three.  

Then she closed it again.  

Outside, the bells began to ring.  
Three short. One long. Two short.  
The count had started.  

Tiffani went to the glass panes. Looked down at the city. At the rope lanes. At the spaces between the chalk lines.  

Somewhere out there, Jhace’s hands were shaking.  
Somewhere out there, a shard was waiting to be held.  
Somewhere out there, a truth was missing from a page.  

She hummed once. Thin. Sharp.  
Then cut it off before it could become a song.  

She opened her slate one last time.  
Wrote at the very bottom, small and crisp:  
*Omission is not forgiveness.*  

Then she closed it.  
And waited for the runners to come.

[ASSISTANT]
## Chapter 15 — The Omission (LOCKFIX v2)

Tiffani’s slate sat open on the glass-house workbench. Not clean. Not organized.  
Raw data from the last thirty pull cycles covered the surface—numbers, sketches, interval marks logged by breath-count, not tick-count. Her pencil rested beside it. Unsharpened. Heavy.

The chronometer box stayed closed.  
If time was broken, measuring its fragments felt like arranging debris after a blast.

She’d been up here since the briefing ended. Since the crowd dispersed. Since Jhace walked out with his hands curled into fists, shaking not from slack phase but from the cost of being measured wrong.

Corlexi had thanked her.  
“Your measurements saved lives.”  
He’d meant, *Your measurements became our authority*.

Tiffani had closed her slate. Walked away.  
But she’d come back here to open it again. To the pages where she’d written truth.

Now she needed to unwrite part of it.

***

The glass-house held its breath.  
Dust motes hung in the Kindling-light like frozen stars. The iron ribs vibrated with the doubled hum, a low thrum that had become the new silence.

Tiffani’s knee ached.  
Not the old ache. The newer one—the kind that remembered salt under nails, rough hands, and the relief of being pulled out of her own head for a while.

She cut the thought off.  
There was no room for that here. Not now.

She flipped to the page where she’d recorded Jhace’s pattern.  
*Subject J. T4 baseline. Exacerbates on slack phase. Compensates during pull. Improves with physical exertion. Worsens under observation.*

Below it, she’d written raw observations from the stadium:  
*08:42 (approx). Tremor spiked on slack phase. J did not collapse. Continued movement.*  
*08:47. Observed water-carrying task. Tremor reduced to baseline during load bearing.*  
*08:59. At edge of stadium, tremor visible but steady enough to grip stair rail.*

Truth.  
But not the whole truth.

The whole truth was what she’d noted in tiny letters at the page edge, then crossed out:  
*Pattern suggests adaptation. Not decay. Not inevitable progression to T5.*

She’d crossed it out an hour ago.  
Now she needed it gone.

***

A knock at the stairwell door.  
Sharp. Official. Not Jhace’s heavy thud. Not Chloen’s hesitant tap.

Tiffani didn’t move.  
Let them wait. Let them feel the hum in the stone steps.

The knock came again.  
“Tiffani. Infrastructure.”

Corlexi’s voice. Not a request.

Tiffani closed her slate. Not over the crossed-out line. Over the clean version.  
She opened the chronometer box. Let the ticks fill the silence for three breaths.  
Then she went to the door.

Corlexi stood there alone. No sash. No slate. Just stadium dust on his boots. His eyes went past her to the glass-house interior—to the theodolite, the stacked slates, the workbench where her crossed-out truth lay hidden under a cleaner page.

“We need your logs,” he said. “All of them. From the first pressure drop.”

Tiffani didn’t step aside. “What for?”

“Standardization,” Corlexi said. “We’re building a unified protocol. Your data is foundational.”

“My data is observational,” Tiffani said. “Not prescriptive.”

Corlexi’s gaze went to her hands. Not shaking. Steady. Like his.  
“The city is broken in new ways,” he said. “We need to fix it in new ways. Even if the fix costs something.”

Tiffani thought of Jhace’s hands. Of the wedge bar steady on his knees while his fingers trembled off the handle.  
“Cost isn’t the same as breakage,” she said.

Corlexi didn’t argue. He just waited. A stone in the current.

Tiffani stepped aside.

***

Corlexi moved through the glass-house like he was measuring its angles.  
He stopped at the workbench. Looked at the slate she’d left open.

“The stadium observations,” he said.

Tiffani nodded.

He turned the slate to read her clean version. Nodded once. “Accurate.”

Then he reached past it to the chronometer box. Picked it up. Weighed it in his hand. “You stopped logging time.”

“I stopped trusting it,” Tiffani said.

Corlexi set the box down. Looked at her. “The shard is unstable. Mobel barely contained it during transit. If it fractures, the resonance could destabilize entire terraces. We need to know who can hold it. Who can hold the cost.”

Tiffani’s throat tightened. “You mean Jhace.”

“We mean the pattern,” Corlexi said. “Your pattern. It’s the only one we have. The only one with measurements.”

He opened her slate to the page where she’d recorded Jhace’s data. Flipped backward through pages—Exit Face measurements, Rift persistence, pressure pulse cycles. Stopped at the first mention of the slab hold.

“You noticed progression before anyone else,” he said. “T1, T2, T3, T4. You named it. You tracked it. That’s why we need everything. Every page. Every breath.”

Tiffani watched his fingers on her slate. Clean nails. No grit. No copper smears.  
“What happens when you find T5?” she asked.

Corlexi’s eyes met hers. “We prepare for it.”

“You restrict it.”

“It’s not restriction if it saves lives.”

Tiffani looked at the crossed-out line on her hidden page. *Pattern suggests adaptation.*  
That truth would kill their protocol. It would make Jhace’s hands a variable, not a limit.  
Corlexi would use it to demand more holds, not fewer.  
Measurement would become requirement.

Her pencil lay beside the slate. Unsharpened. Heavy.

Corlexi closed the slate. “We’ll send runners. All your logs. Every page. Before the next bell sequence changes.”

He turned to go. Stopped at the door. “You did good work, Tiffani.”

Not *I trust you.*  
*You did good work.*  
Like a foreman praising a tool.

Tiffani didn’t answer.

***

After he left, she went back to the workbench.  
Opened to the page with the crossed-out line.  
Took her pencil. Not to erase. To overwrite.

She wrote one clean line where the truth had been:  
*Pattern confirms progression. No adaptation observed.*

Then she flipped to a new page.  
Wrote:  
*M5: Shard resonance containment. Protocol test.*  
*Basis: Subject J’s progression pattern.*

Below it, she left space. Space for data she wouldn’t collect. Space for measurements she would pretend not to see.

She closed the slate.  
Wiped her hands on her apron until the coppergilt grit was gone. Until they were just hands again.

The chronometer ticked.  
Steady. Indifferent.

Tiffani picked it up. Opened it.  
Listened to the ticks cut through the doubled hum.  
One. Two. Three.

Then she closed it again.

Outside, the bells began to ring.  
Three short. One long. Two short.  
The count had started.

Tiffani went to the glass panes. Looked down at the city. At the rope lanes. At the spaces between chalk lines.

Somewhere out there, Jhace’s hands were shaking.  
Somewhere out there, a shard was waiting to be held.  
Somewhere out there, a truth was missing from a page.

A thin, sharp sound slipped out of her throat. She stopped it immediately.

She opened her slate one last time.  
Wrote at the very bottom, small and crisp:  
*Omission is not forgiveness.*

Then she closed it.  
And waited for the runners to come.

### Changes made
- Removed musical phrasing at the end (“song” and the lead-in), replaced with a non-musical physical reflex.
- Replaced “timestamps” with “interval marks” to keep her log language consistent with breath-count framing.
- Replaced “by the end of this stretch” with “Before the next bell sequence changes” to keep urgency concrete and in-world.

He’d meant, *Your measurements became our authority*.  

Tiffani had closed her slate. Walked away.  
But she’d come back here to open it again. To the pages where she’d written truth.  

Now she needed to unwrite part of it.

She cut the thought off.  
There was no room for that here. Not now.

*Subject J. T4 baseline. Exacerbates on slack phase. Compensates during pull. Improves with physical exertion. Worsens under observation.*  

Below it, she’d written the raw observations from the stadium:  
*08:42 (approx). Tremor spiked on slack phase. J did not collapse. Continued movement.*  
*08:47. Observed water-carrying task. Tremor reduced to baseline during load bearing.*  
*08:59. At edge of stadium, tremor visible but steady enough to grip stair rail.*  

Truth.  
But not the whole truth.  

The whole truth was what she’d noted in tiny letters at the page edge, then crossed out:  
*Pattern suggests adaptation. Not decay. Not inevitable progression to T5.*  

She’d crossed it out an hour ago.  
Now she needed to erase it completely.  

***  

A knock at the stairwell door.  
Sharp. Official. Not Jhace’s heavy thud. Not Chloen’s hesitant tap.  

Tiffani didn’t move.  
Let them wait. Let them feel the hum in the stone steps.  

The knock came again.  
“Tiffani. Infrastructure.”  

Corlexi’s voice. Not a request.  

Tiffani closed her slate. Not over the crossed-out line. Over the clean version.  
She opened the chronometer box. Let the ticks fill the silence for three breaths.  
Then she went to the door.

Corlexi’s gaze went to her hands. Not shaking. Steady. Like his.

Tiffani thought of Jhace’s hands. Of the wedge bar steady on his knees while his fingers trembled off the handle.

Corlexi didn’t argue. He just waited. A stone in the current.  

Tiffani stepped aside.

Corlexi moved through the glass-house like he was measuring its angles.  
He stopped at the workbench. Looked at the slate she’d left open.
