Tiffani nodded.  

He turned the slate to read her clean version. Nodded once. “Accurate.”  

Then he reached past it to the chronometer box. Picked it up. Weighed it in his hand. “You stopped logging time.”  

“I stopped trusting it,” Tiffani said.  

Corlexi set the box down. Looked at her. “The shard is unstable. Mobel barely contained it during transit. If it fractures, the resonance could destabilize entire terraces. We need to know who can hold it. Who can hold the cost.”  

Tiffani’s throat tightened. “You mean Jhace.”  

“We mean the pattern,” Corlexi said. “Your pattern. It’s the only one we have. The only one with measurements.”  

He opened her slate to the page where she’d recorded Jhace’s data. Flipped backward through pages—Exit Face measurements, Rift persistence, pressure pulse cycles. Stopped at the first mention of the slab hold.  

“You noticed progression before anyone else,” he said. “T1, T2, T3, T4. You named it. You tracked it. That’s why we need everything. Every page. Every breath.”  

Tiffani watched his fingers on her slate. Clean nails. No grit. No copper smears.  
“What happens when you find T5?” she asked.  

Corlexi’s eyes met hers. “We prepare for it.”  

“You restrict it.”  

“It’s not restriction if it saves lives.”  

Tiffani looked at the crossed-out line on her hidden page. *Pattern suggests adaptation.*  
That truth would kill their protocol. It would make Jhace’s hands a variable, not a limit.  
Corlexi would use it to demand more holds, not fewer.  
Measurement would become requirement.  

Her pencil lay beside the slate. Unsharpened. Heavy.  

Corlexi closed the slate. “We’ll send runners. All your logs. Every page. By the end of this stretch.”  

He turned to go. Stopped at the door. “You did good work, Tiffani.”  

Not *I* trust you.  
*You did good work.*  
Like a foreman praising a tool.  

Tiffani didn’t answer.  

***  

After he left, she went back to the workbench.  
Opened to the page with the crossed-out line.  
Took her pencil. Not to erase. To overwrite.  

She wrote one clean line where the truth had been:  
*Pattern confirms progression. No adaptation observed.*  

Then she flipped to a new page.  
Wrote:  
*M5: Shard resonance containment. Protocol test.*  
*Basis: Subject J’s progression pattern.*  

Below it, she left space. Space for data she wouldn’t collect. Space for measurements she would pretend not to see.  

She closed the slate.  
Wiped her hands on her apron until the coppergilt grit was gone. Until they were just hands again.  

The chronometer ticked.  
Steady. Indifferent.  

Tiffani picked it up. Opened it.  
Listened to the ticks fill the doubled hum.  
One. Two. Three.  

Then she closed it again.  

Outside, the bells began to ring.  
Three short. One long. Two short.  
The count had started.  

Tiffani went to the glass panes. Looked down at the city. At the rope lanes. At the spaces between the chalk lines.  

Somewhere out there, Jhace’s hands were shaking.  
Somewhere out there, a shard was waiting to be held.  
Somewhere out there, a truth was missing from a page.  

She hummed once. Thin. Sharp.  
Then cut it off before it could become a song.  

She opened her slate one last time.  
Wrote at the very bottom, small and crisp:  
*Omission is not forgiveness.*  

Then she closed it.  
And waited for the runners to come.
"""

# Write Chapter 15 file
ch15_path = base / "N01_Chapter_15_The_Omission_LOCKFIX_v1.md"
ch15_path.write_text(chapter15_text)

# Create Chapter 11-13 lockfix files from provided latest text (as in user's message)
chapter11_text = """# Chapter 11 — The Sequence (LOCKFIX pass)

Tiffani watched the tremor in Jhace’s hands like she would watch a bridge seam widen under load.
Not with pity. With measurement.

He stood in the rope lane where the wind pulled hardest, directing a brace team through a collapsed archway. His wedge bar moved in steady arcs—drive, pull, seat—but every time his hand came off the handle to adjust his grip, it shook. Fine, rapid tremors that started at the fingertips and climbed the forearm like a signal losing its way.

Tiffani didn’t write it down yet. She waited. Counted breaths. Counted pull cycles. Counted when the shaking worsened and when it eased.

When the pull phase hit, his hands steadied.
When the slack came, they shook harder.

Correlation, not causation. Not yet.

She moved closer, keeping her distance, letting the crowd flow around her like water around a stone. Her slate stayed closed. Her pencil stayed still. She recorded in her head:
Subject J. T4 baseline. Exacerbates on slack phase. Compensates during pull.

A child’s voice cut through the wind. “Jhace!”
Chloen emerged from behind a broken wall, face smudged, eyes wide. She held up a canteen. “Water’s here.”

Jhace’s hand jerked once as he took it—just a flicker before he forced stillness. He drank without spilling. Gave the canteen back. “Thanks.” His voice was flat. Controlled.

Chloen nodded and stepped back, but her eyes stayed on his hands. She’d seen them shake longer than Tiffani had.

Tiffani noted it: Observer C. Recognizes pattern. Does not name it.

A pressure pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted in slow sheets. Coppergilt gathered along Tiffani’s slate edge, lifting in tiny arcs toward Jhace’s hands before settling again.

Jhace’s hands steadied.

The slack came. Air rushed back in. Sound returned with it—bells, voices, the scrape of stone on stone.

Jhace’s tremor spiked. His fingers curled into fists at his sides. His jaw tightened like something was trying to climb out of his throat.

Tiffani moved forward then, not to him, but to a nearby brace captain marking cracks with chalk. She opened her slate at the edge of his sightline without looking at him.

Slack spikes tremor. Pull dampens. Work steadies. Attention worsens.

The captain’s chalk stopped mid-stroke. “What’s that mean?” he asked, voice low.

Tiffani didn’t answer. She closed the slate. Walked away.

Jhace watched her go. His hands were still shaking.

The upper terrace had become a counting place.
Not by choice. By physics.
The rope lanes converged there, funneled by the mountain’s shape and the city’s broken grid. People moved through the lanes like water finding channels—some with wax tabs on their wrists, some without. The tabbed moved faster. The untabbed hesitated at every checkpoint.

Tiffani stood at a high point where the lanes met, slate open, pencil ready. Not counting people. Counting patterns.

She watched where shoulders tensed when the pull hit. Where hands went to throats when breath failed. Where coppergilt gathered on metal buckles and slate edges. Where the doubled hum made stone vibrate like a tensioned cable taking sudden load.

She wrote:
Breath failure correlates to pull phase intensity.
Coppergilt mobilizes on pull; settles and clings on slack—strongest on brass/iron.
Tremor patterns cluster where attention increases.

A Crown recorder stood nearby, brass emblem bright on her cloak, slate held tight to her ribs. She counted people. Tiffani counted symptoms.

The recorder glanced at Tiffani’s slate. “What’s that?”
“Measurements,” Tiffani said.
“What kind?”
“Baseline shifts.”
The recorder’s eyes narrowed. “For who?”
Tiffani did not answer. She closed her slate.

Jhace found her at the edge of the sorting area, away from the crowd, where the rope lanes thinned into single-file paths. He carried his wedge bar like it was part of his arm. His hands were steady on the handle.

“You were watching my hands,” he said. No accusation. Statement.

Tiffani didn’t look at him. She watched the dust lift on the pull phase. “I watch everything.”

“Not everything,” he said. “Just the shaking.”

She met his eyes then. “It’s data.”

“Whose data?”
“Yours. Mine. The planet’s. It doesn’t belong to anyone.”

Jhace’s grip tightened on the wedge bar. His knuckles whitened. The tremor started again at his fingertips. “It’s in my hands,” he said. “It belongs to me.”

Tiffani’s knee ached. She shifted her weight. Ignored it. “When you work, it lessens,” she said. “When people watch, it worsens. When the slack comes, it spikes.”

Jhace stared at her. “You counted that.”
“Yes.”

“Why?”
“Because it’s the shape of the cost,” she said. “If we don’t know the cost, we can’t measure the hold.”

Jhace’s jaw worked. “You think I don’t know the cost?”
“I think you feel it,” Tiffani said. “I think I can measure it.”

A pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted. Coppergilt rose off Tiffani’s slate in tiny arcs and settled again on the slack.

Jhace’s hands steadied on the wedge bar.

Tiffani wrote one line on her slate, small and crisp:
Sequence confirmed: tremor ↔ phase ↔ attention.

She closed the slate.
Jhace didn’t move. “What does that mean?”
“It means,” Tiffani said, “that the next time you hold something, I’ll know how much it costs before you do.”

She walked away before he could answer.
His hands started shaking again as soon as she turned her back.
She didn’t look.
She didn’t need to.
She already knew the sequence.
The bells kept ringing. The doubled hum kept vibrating underfoot.
And Tiffani moved through the lanes, slate under her arm, counting the cost of being alive in a world that had learned to break.
"""

chapter12_text = """# Chapter 12 — The Question (revised)

Chloen stayed in the rope-lane shadows where the dust was thickest.
Not hiding. Waiting for the counting to finish with her.

The upper terrace had become a sorting ground.
Wax-tabbed moved first. Untabbed waited.

Chloen’s wrist was bare. Her sleeve covered the graphite smear where Tiffani’s numbers lived. She touched it every few breaths to make sure it was still there—like the mark could evaporate if she stopped believing in it.

A brace team worked the edge of the sorting area, propping a leaning wall with scavenged timber. Jhace directed them, wedge bar moving in steady arcs. His hands were steady on the handle. Off the handle, they shook—fine tremors running up his forearms like water finding cracks.

A tabbed runner approached with a slate.

Chloen watched the runner’s eyes flick to Jhace’s fingers before he asked his name.

*T4,* Chloen thought. That’s what Tiffani would call it.
Not fear. Not weakness. A pattern. A cost with a schedule.

The runner moved on. Jhace’s shaking got worse the moment attention left him, like his nerves had been waiting for permission to fail.

A hand touched Chloen’s shoulder. Light. Professional. Not gentle.

“Child,” a voice said. “You’re in the wrong lane.”

Chloen turned.

Bram stood there. Older. Dust on his sleeves. A sash with muted brass threads across his chest—not Crown gold, not Infrastructure silver. Something in between. His eyes were tired but sharp, and they didn’t settle on her face first. They checked boots, hands, shoulders. Load capacity, not sympathy.

“I’m waiting for the water ration,” Chloen said. Flat. Not small.

Bram’s gaze flicked to her cuff. To the graphite smear showing. “Mark three?” he asked.

Chloen didn’t answer. She didn’t need to. He’d already seen it.

Bram made a sound like he was about to say something helpful, then his mouth got ahead of his brain. “Wrong lane. Unless you’re—” he paused, eyes narrowing like he was rewinding himself— “Unless you’re trying to win an award for *Most Patient Bare-Wrist in a Disaster.*” He blinked once. “Bad award. No prize. Don’t recommend.”

Chloen stared at him.

Bram exhaled through his nose. “Water ration moved. Two terraces down. Rope lane three.” He didn’t step aside yet. Just held the space.

Above them, the Rift hung in the Kindling-light—black in blue, stars where there shouldn’t be stars. The doubled hum ran through the stone.

“You saw the man at the stadium stairs,” Bram said.

Chloen’s throat tightened. She kept her eyes on his sash threads instead of his face. “I saw people.”

“Not just people.” Bram’s voice stayed low, even. “One man. Walking slow. Stopping on the pull. Hand to his chest.”

Chloen’s fingers curled into her palms. Grit stung where it found cracks.

“He left copper smears on the stone,” Bram went on. “Like the world’s been handling him without gloves.”

Chloen didn’t move. Didn’t speak.

A pull phase hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted in slow sheets.

Bram’s eyes tracked nothing obvious—just the way bodies tightened when breath failed. His hand went to his own sternum for one breath. Just one. Then dropped.

“I know what it tastes like,” he said. “Not every mouth floods. But some do.”

Chloen swallowed hard. Metal taste surged anyway, like the world enjoyed proving people wrong.

Bram’s mouth twitched, like a joke tried to escape and he hated himself for wanting to let it. “Breathe shallow on pull. Full on slack.” He lifted two fingers, then caught himself. “That’s the whole sermon. Don’t make me repeat it. I charge per repetition.” His eyes flicked to her, then to the lanes. “Kidding. Mostly.”

The slack came. Air rushed back in. Sound returned with it—bells, voices, chalk scraping stone.

Bram stepped aside. “Rope lane three,” he said again. “Tell the runner Bram sent you.”

Chloen didn’t move. “Why?”

Bram’s gaze went to Jhace, still directing the brace team. Jhace’s hands shook harder now that he wasn’t actively driving the wedge. “Because the ones who hold break first,” Bram said. “The ones who count break next.” He paused, and his brain did that stutter-hop again—almost a joke, almost a shrug, almost a truth. “The ones who watch…” He swallowed. “They’re last to break. Or first to remember what breaking looks like.”

A runner shoved past carrying a water yoke. Behind him, a dock worker—broad shoulders, forearms roped with strain—dragged a crate across the stone like he was trying to out-muscle the planet.

Chloen saw Tiffani across the lane for half a breath, giving a sharp hand signal to redirect a crowd away from a crack. Her posture was straight. Her slate stayed closed. Her knee hitched once and she pretended it didn’t.

Then her gaze snagged—just a fraction—on the dock worker’s hands.

Not admiration. Not softness. Something hungry and inconvenient that looked like anger until you watched closely.

Tiffani blinked, cut her eyes away, and went back to directing bodies like nothing had happened.

Chloen didn’t understand it. She only understood the shape: Tiffani’s control wasn’t absence of wanting. It was wanting that got strangled quiet.

Bram noticed too. His eyes flicked once in her direction, then back to Chloen like he’d caught himself thinking something that wasn’t useful. “Anyway,” he said, too fast, as if speed could erase the detour, “you’re not unmarked *yet.* Keep it that way.”

A child’s cry rose from the sorting area—frustration, not fear. A boy with a broken wrist had been told to wait in the back rows. His mother argued with a brace captain pointing at chalk lines on the ground like they were laws.

Chloen watched the mother’s shoulders tighten. Watched the captain’s hands gesture at the lines like the stone itself had spoken.

“The question isn’t whether you saw him,” Bram said softly. “The question is what you do with what you saw.”

Chloen looked at her hands. At the graphite smear hidden under her sleeve. At the coppergilt catching in the creases like it wanted ownership.

Jhace’s voice cut across the terrace. Sharp. “Not that angle. Reset the wedge.”

His hands were steady on the bar. Off the bar, they shook like leaves.

“Do you break things, little watcher?” Bram asked. His mouth did the stutter again—almost a joke, almost not. “Or do you… I don’t know—help keep them from breaking worse?”

Chloen didn’t answer. She put her hand in her pocket and touched the numbers again. **3-7-2.**

Bram didn’t wait. He moved toward the argument.

He didn’t speak to the captain first. Didn’t speak to the mother. He just knelt, boot heel scraping chalk, and redrew the boundary into a narrow path around the boy’s broken wrist.

“Path’s for people,” Bram said to no one in particular. Then, like his brain couldn’t stop itself: “Not just stones. Stones don’t complain enough. If stones started yelling, we’d be out of work.” He blinked. “That’s also a bad joke. Ignore it.”

The mother pulled her son through the gap without thanking him. The captain didn’t protest. Bram stood, brushed dust from his knees, and looked back at Chloen.

“Rope lane three,” he repeated. “Before they change the marks again.”

Chloen moved.

Not toward rope lane three.

Toward Jhace.

Bram didn’t stop her. He just watched her choose and didn’t flinch.

At the work site, Jhace leaned on his wedge bar, breathing shallow. His hands shook badly. Teeth buzzing. Someone handed him a canteen; he spilled half before getting it to his mouth.

Chloen stopped three steps away.

Jhace saw her. His eyes flicked to her sleeve. To the graphite smear. “Bram send you?” he asked, voice rough.

Chloen nodded. “He knows about the numbers.”

Jhace’s grip tightened on the wedge bar. The shaking eased a fraction—like the body heard a threat and decided to behave for one more breath.

“Bram knows about most things that matter,” Jhace said. “He just doesn’t talk about the ones that don’t.”

Chloen watched his hands. Watched the tremor climb when he wasn’t working. “He asked me a question,” she said.

Jhace’s jaw worked. “What question?”

“What I do with what I see.”

Jhace stared at her for three breaths—pull phase building, slack incoming, his throat tightening like it knew the schedule.

Then he handed her the canteen. “Hold this,” he said. “Steady.”

Chloen wrapped both hands around it. Held.

Jhace’s fingers stopped shaking for one thin slice of time, like the tremor got confused by having nothing to perform with.

Above them, the Rift stayed open. The doubled hum stayed underfoot.

And Chloen held the canteen steady while Jhace’s hands learned—briefly—how to be still again.
"""

chapter13_text = """# Chapter 13 — The Bowl (LOCKFIX)

The bowl sat on the stone shelf in the under-terrace kitchen, half-full of water that never quite settled.

It wasn’t a special bowl. Chipped rim. Clay fired unevenly. The kind of vessel that survived collapse because nobody tried to save it. It held water. That was enough.

Every few breaths, the doubled hum climbed up through the floor. The water shivered—not ripples, not waves, just a fine trembling that made the surface look fractured even when nothing touched it.

A child reached for the bowl. Fingers dipped in, came out wet. Droplets fell back and made the surface jitter harder for a breath.

The child tried again and missed the center. Water kissed the rim and ran down the side in a thin line. The child didn’t seem to notice. They wiped the wet on their sleeve and went back to watching the surface.

Across the room, an older woman worked a makeshift stove. She moved when the air was full enough to trust. When it thinned, she slowed without thinking, one hand flattening at her chest as if it could pin breath in place.

A spoon tapped the pot rim once. Then again. A small, sharp sound, out of rhythm with her intent.

She didn’t curse. She didn’t pause. She just kept stirring, and when broth slopped over the lip and hissed on the stove stone, she wiped it with her apron like it was normal mess.

A pull phase came through. Air thinned. Dust lifted from the floor in lazy spirals. Along the pot handle and the stove’s iron bracket, bright grit lifted a hair, then settled again as if it preferred metal.

The woman’s hand stayed at her chest until the slack returned.

Air rushed back in. Distant bells threaded through the ceiling. The woman ladled broth into smaller bowls with steady, practiced motions. When she set the ladle down, it rolled a finger’s width before she caught it against the pot without looking.

The child abandoned the water bowl and came to the stove, palms out for heat.

“Careful,” the woman said. “Hot.”

She placed a small bowl into the child’s hands. The child held it with both palms and didn’t spill a drop.

The woman poured into a chipped cup for herself. A thin sheet of broth ran over her knuckles. She didn’t flinch. She just set the cup down and wiped her hand on her apron, eyes never leaving the stove as if attention alone kept things from sliding.

In the next room, a man with a brace captain’s sash sat at a table with a slate open. His pencil made small, precise marks. When he stopped, the pencil hovered an instant above the slate as if his fingers had to renegotiate stillness before letting go.

He closed the slate and stood. On the next pull he caught the table edge—not dramatic, not a fall, just a correction made fast enough to pretend it hadn’t happened. He walked out without finishing his broth.

The child watched him go. “Why does it keep slipping?” they asked.

The woman didn’t look up. “Some bodies keep the bill closer,” she said. “Some don’t.”

The child stared at the water bowl again. At the surface that never smoothed. “Will it stop?”

The woman’s hand returned to her chest as the air leaned thin again. “Not soon,” she said. “So you hold something that matters. And you hold it steady.”

She handed the child another bowl, fuller this time. “Don’t spill.”

The child wrapped both hands around it and carried it without slosh.

High above, in the Basin Stadium where the briefing would be held next Kindling, the stone kept the same wrong vibration. Grit slept in mortar seams until the pull came, then lifted and settled again.

Waiting.
"""

# Build a new zip containing handover + latest lockfix chapters 2-15 where available.
# Determine latest versions by picking highest _vN if multiple exist.
import re

def latest_version_file(prefix_pattern):
    # prefix_pattern is regex that matches base name excluding _v
    candidates=[f for f in base.iterdir() if f.is_file() and re.match(prefix_pattern, f.name)]
    if not candidates:
        return None
    def vnum(name):
        m=re.search(r"_v(\d+)\.md$", name)
        return int(m.group(1)) if m else -1
    candidates.sort(key=lambda p:(vnum(p.name), p.name))
    return candidates[-1]

handover = base / "N01_COMPLETE_HANDOVER.md"
chapter_patterns = {
    2: r"N01_Chapter_2_Glass-House_Observatory_LOCKFIX_v\d+\.md$",
    3: r"N01_Chapter_3_Ground-Level_Continuation_LOCKFIX_v\d+\.md$",
    4: r"N01_Chapter_4_Firebreak_LOCKFIX_v\d+\.md$",
    5: r"N01_Chapter_5_The_Child_Under_the_Slab_LOCKFIX_v\d+\.md$",
    6: r"N01_Chapter_6_Parapet_Tsunami_Turn_LOCKFIX_v\d+\.md$",
    7: r"N01_Chapter_7_Upper_Terrace_Sorting_Meeting_LOCKFIX_v\d+\.md$",
    8: r"N01_Chapter_8_Names_LOCKFIX_v\d+\.md$",
    9: r"N01_Chapter_9_The_Gap_LOCKFIX_v\d+\.md$",
    10: r"N01_Chapter_10_The_Shard_LOCKFIX_v\d+\.md$",
    11: r"N01_Chapter_11_The_Sequence_LOCKFIX_v\d+\.md$",
    12: r"N01_Chapter_12_The_Question_LOCKFIX_v\d+\.md$",
    13: r"N01_Chapter_13_The_Bowl_LOCKFIX_v\d+\.md$",
    14: r"N01_Chapter_14_The_Briefing_LOCKFIX_v\d+\.md$",
    15: r"N01_Chapter_15_The_Omission_LOCKFIX_v\d+\.md$",
}

selected=[handover] if handover.exists() else []
missing=[]
for ch,pat in chapter_patterns.items():
    p=latest_version_file(pat)
    if p is None:
        missing.append(ch)
    else:
        selected.append(p)

zip_path = base / "N01_handover_plus_ch2_to_ch15_lockfix_v1.zip"
with zipfile.ZipFile(zip_path, "w", compression=zipfile.ZIP_DEFLATED) as z:
    for p in selected:
        z.write(p, arcname=p.name)

(ch15_path.exists(), zip_path.exists(), missing, [p.name for p in selected[:5]], len(selected))

Chapter 1 — Underface Forge

Jhace set the blade down on the anvil block and didn't look at the edge. That wasn't the part that failed in the hands. The handle was where people lied to themselves. 

The forge bay sat in the Underface—stone ribs, pipework stitched overhead, damp air that never fully warmed even with a fire burning. Bellows breath hissed. Hammer ring echoed. Quench water sizzled. The grid's low thrum ran through the walls. 

He kept his work at the left bench where the light was steady and the drafts didn't steal heat off steel. 

The blade itself was already done—ground true, spine straight, bevels even. A working knife, not a show piece. The tang ran full-length, squared shoulders clean where it met the ricasso. That meant the handle had no excuses. 

He chose a block of dark hardwood from the bin—close-grained, oily, heavy for its size. Not brittle. Not pretty, just stubborn. He checked it with his thumbnail, then with a quick tap against the bench. The vibration stayed dull. No hidden splits. Good. 

He cut it into two scales with a saw that needed oil yesterday. He cleaned the cuts with a plane until both faces lay flat against a straightedge. Any high spot became a future crack. Any gap became movement. Movement became failure. 

He set the tang on the first scale, traced it with a scribe, and marked three pin positions where the steel had enough meat. He clamped the scales together, drilled through both at once so the holes matched. Slow speed, steady pressure—he let the bit cut. Forcing it heated the wood, burned the hole oval. 

Guard came next. 

Most people treated a guard like jewelry. Jhace treated it like a washer that could save a hand. 

He pulled a strip of mild steel off the rack, measured, cut it, and squared the edges. Then he heated the guard plate to a dull red and drove it onto the tang shoulders with a wood mallet. The heat gave it just enough give. The tang burned its own fit, high spots confessing with smoke. He pulled it back off, filed the bright marks, and did it again. 

Drive. Pull. File. 

On the third cycle the guard seated with a solid stop—no bounce, no gap, no twist. Jhace let out a breath he hadn't known he was holding. 

While the resin cured he went back to the guard and chamfered the edges. Not for beauty—sharp edges found skin. He rounded the corners until they wouldn't tear a glove. 

The grid thrummed under his feet. Normal. Familiar. The quarter's steady thrum: pipe-rattle, brace-resonance, the constant vibration that told you the city was still held up. 

He knew that vibration so well he heard problems in it without thinking. 

Mid-hammer, the thrum stuttered. A ghost-ring laid on top that didn't belong. His teeth prickled. His sternum felt crowded. 

He stopped the hammer in the air and listened with his hands on the bench. 

The pipe-rattle was still there, but something deeper was moving through it. Slow. Heavy. Sustained. Not a quake that rattled loose stone—this was the stone no longer holding the same way under load. 

A vibration shuddered through the anvil block. The forge tools clicked against each other without being touched. Dust lifted from the floor in a fine drift. 

Jhace's first thought was structural. A brace slip. A terrace shift. A pipe main failing somewhere it shouldn't. 

Then the bell rang. 

It wasn't the forge bell or the shift bell. The emergency bell, from above, from the streets. 

One strike. Uneven. 

Another. A different direction. 

He moved before deciding. Chalk down, slate left open on the bench, hands already reaching for his kit by habit—wedge bar, line, quick clamps, a coil of cord, collar keys. Moving. Then thinking. 

The ghost-ring thickened. 

He hooked the kit strap over his shoulder, scooped the keys, and went for the ladder shaft. 

More bells started. 

Different streets. Same urgency. 

The Underface corridor was tight, stone on both sides, pipework overhead sweating. People were already moving, faces pale in the Kindling light, some running, some walking too carefully like they'd forgotten how to trust the floor. The air tasted dusty. With every step he could feel the vibration climb from the soles of his boots up through his legs. 

He took the ladder two steps at a time. The rungs chattered under his hands. 

Halfway up, the pressure changed. It wasn't wind. It was absence. Like the air had been thinned with a knife. 

His ears popped hard. His chest tightened as if someone had set a strap around his ribs and pulled. 

He tried to inhale and got less than he asked for. 

Below, somewhere in the Underface, a torch flame bent sideways. Smoke that should have risen got pulled flat. 

Jhace forced a breath through the thinning air. Stopping didn't help. 

He came up through a service hatch behind a row of terraces into light. Blue kindling-light sky. People on the street. Shop signs. Dust in the air. 

And a deep, wrong thrum running through all of it, stuttering everything. 

The street had gone still. Tools lay where they'd dropped. A cart sat half-hitched, mule wide-eyed, nostrils flared, fighting the harness. Faces were turned in one direction. 

Jhace stepped out into the street and followed their gaze. 

On the plain beyond Saint Merro, something had risen. 

At first his mind tried to name it small. Wall. Cliff. A terrace collapse out beyond the far districts. Then the scale hit and the name fell apart. 

It wasn't a wall. It was the edge of the world being forced up. 

Banded strata—light over dark, dark over light—turned vertical. The thing curved away left and right until it vanished into its own distance. Its base was lost in a brown-gray cloud that boiled without flame. 

Above it, the sky was wounded. 

A hole—no, a cut. A clean absence where blue stopped and black began. Stars inside it in full day. Hard points of light like they'd been drilled through the air. 

The edge shimmered, not like heat. The air couldn't decide where to exist. 

People made sounds behind him, but the air was thin enough that sound didn't carry right. 

Jhace stood with his kit strap biting his shoulder and tried to make his eyes tell him something usable. 

The light was wrong—too sharp, like the sun had shifted on its axle without asking. 

Something buried down there just tore free. That was the shape of it—a thing that had forced its passage up from below, breaching the world's skin. 

The thrum deepened. His teeth buzzed. Somewhere in the city, shutters rattled against their frames. 

Then—movement. 

Something flashed above the rising face, so fast his brain lagged behind his eyes. A shape crossing the open wound in the sky. Not a bird. Not a machine. Too big. Too fast. 

It turned as it moved. Rotating, like a spindle between invisible fingers. Veins of light ran along it in pulses—copper's dull red, gold's warm gleam, and colors he couldn't name because they weren't colors he'd ever seen in metal. 

No fire. No trail of burn. Just the violent fact of it. 

Behind it—following—debris. 

A smear of dark fragments and bright sparks that did not behave like falling. Some pieces continued after it, pulled along. Some lagged and began to arc back, caught between up and down. 

The air pulled again. Harder. 

Jhace's breath got taken, not by wind but by pressure. His throat opened and nothing came in for a beat. People clutched at their chests. Someone dropped to their knees and gagged without sound. 

Smoke that had been rising from a chimney across the street got yanked down into the stone mouth it came from. 

Then the pull shifted—not gone, just redirected. The wind stopped being a direction and became a demand. Everything loose went toward that horizon where the wound sat above the new stone face. 

Paper tore out of hands. Dust lifted in sheets. A woman's scarf whipped free and streamed away until it vanished into the gray. 

Far out near the rising face, water hung in the air. 

Too much of it. Too high. Too steady. A suspended mass that had no business staying up. 

For a few breaths it held. The thrum underfoot made it quiver in place. 

Then the world collected its bill. 

Water fell. 

Not a clean pour. It broke into heavy slaps and mist and sheets that flashed in the kindling-light as they came down. The sound reached the city late, a rolling crash that made the shutters jump and old mortar spit dust. 

The street around Jhace swayed. Not like a quake wave—like the stone itself had softened and shifted under the load of being itself. 

A building nearby answered the vibration with a groan. 

Jhace's eyes snapped left. 

The terrace edge above them—stone façade—had been weakened by the wrong thrum. Mortar that should have been solid had behaved like paste for a moment and set again in the wrong shape. Now it was giving up. 

A slab tore loose. A big one. Old stone, thick as a man is tall, coming down toward the street where bodies still weren't moving fast enough. 

Jhace moved before he thought. 

He shoved two people back by the shoulders. "Move—!" 

His arm came up, palm out, the stupid reflex of anyone who has ever watched a load shift and felt their body try to argue with gravity. 

And something in his chest answered. 

Heat behind the sternum. A pressure like a hand closing from the inside. His mouth went slick with metal, thick as oil on the tongue. 

The slab stopped. 

It hung in mid-fall like the world had stalled. 

Dust froze with it. Pebbles drifted instead of dropping. A hammer that had been knocked off a windowsill floated for a heartbeat before clattering down sideways, like gravity was returning in pieces. 

Jhace couldn't move. 

Not because he chose not to. Because the thing inside his ribs had tied itself to that stone and locked. He felt it like a line under tension—exact, punishing. The more his body tried to pull away, the more it tightened, teaching him without words that resistance had a cost. 

The street noise broke into fragments: boots, coughing, a name shouted and swallowed, the wet animal sound of panic trying to become speech in thin air. 

People ran under the suspended slab. 

Jhace couldn't turn his head to track them. He only felt the stone through the line—micro-shifts, strain, the constant insistence of gravity pressing down and being denied. 

His vision narrowed. The air wouldn't pull in. The thrum filled what was left. 

A small body stumbled in the dust under the slab—child-small, white with grit, hair stuck to her cheeks. 

She fell, hands scrabbling on stone that had turned slick with fine powder. 

Somebody reached for her and missed. 

Jhace's jaw clenched without permission. His throat tightened like the world had reached in and gripped his windpipe. 

The line in his chest screamed tighter, and the slab held. 

For one more breath. For one more step. 

The child cleared the shadow. 

The instant she did, something inside Jhace slipped—not a release he chose, a release like a knot letting go when the load is gone. 

The slab dropped. 

It hit the street with a crack that shook the quarter. Stone shattered. Dust punched up in a wave. The shock threw Jhace backward. His kit strap snapped. Tools scattered into the gutter—wedge bar, line, cord. Useless in the dust. He hit hard on his shoulder and the world flashed white at the edges. 

He lay there with no air, throat open, nothing coming. 

Then his lungs finally pulled something in—thin, dusty, metallic. 

He rolled onto an elbow. His hands were steady for exactly one breath. 

Then they started shaking. 

Around him people were screaming now, full voice, as if the air had remembered how to carry sound. Someone dragged the child away from the rubble. Someone else heaved, spitting dark flecks into the gutter and sobbed like they'd been struck. 

Jhace stared at his own trembling fingers like they belonged to someone else. 

The thrum under the street didn't stop. It held, stuttered, wrong, as if the planet had been struck and would never settle back into one clean vibration again. 

He forced himself upright. 

The horizon still held that impossible vertical face. The wound in the sky above it still showed stars in day. And high above, the debris smear had thinned, some of it vanishing after the spindle, some of it arcing away into a band that did not fall—caught, circling. 

Jhace didn't have words for ring or orbit. He only knew: pieces weren't coming down. Pieces were staying up. 

Bells kept ringing. Somewhere farther into the quarter a façade gave up and fell with a crash that made people flinch hard. 

Jhace wiped his mouth with the back of his wrist and felt the grit of metal on his skin too. 

The gutter held rainwater. It did not move. 

He looked at the street—at the warped doors, the slumped corners of buildings that had softened and re-set wrong, at the dust that clung to everything—and he did the only thing his body knew how to do when the world started failing. 

He went toward the sound of the next bell.

# Read chapter 1 content
with open(ch1_path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    ch1_text = f.read()

# Create new zip: copy everything from src_zip, then add chapter 1 under standardized name
arcname_ch1 = "N01_Chapter_1_Underface_Forge_LOCKFIX_v1.md"

with zipfile.ZipFile(src_zip, "r") as zin, zipfile.ZipFile(out_zip, "w", compression=zipfile.ZIP_DEFLATED) as zout:
    # Copy existing entries
    for info in zin.infolist():
        data = zin.read(info.filename)
        zout.writestr(info, data)
    # Add Chapter 1
    zout.writestr(arcname_ch1, ch1_text)

# Verify contents
with zipfile.ZipFile(out_zip, "r") as z:
    names = z.namelist()

# Chapter 16 — The Easy Answer

Chloen stood at the rope lane checkpoint, watching the hands.

Not the hands holding slates or marking chalk lines. The hands that shook.

Jhace's hands.

He stood across from her near the water ration station, directing people to fill canteens. His wedge bar leaned against the stone wall. When he wasn't gripping it, his fingers trembled—fine, constant movements running up his forearms like water finding cracks in stone.

People watched him. Not openly. From the corners of their eyes. The way you watch a crack in a wall that hasn't fallen yet.

A woman with a broken wrist approached him. "Which line?" she asked.

Jhace pointed. His hand steadied as it moved. "That one. Fill slow. Don't spill."

The woman's eyes flicked to his fingers. Then back to his face. "They say it's catching," she said quietly. "The shaking."

Jhace's grip tightened on the air. His jaw worked once. "Fill your canteen."

She moved on, but she glanced back twice.

Chloen knew what people were saying. She'd heard it in the rope lanes, in ration lines, in the spaces between chalk marks.

*Weakness.*
*Contagion.*
*A mark from the sky.*

None of it was true. But truth didn't matter when bodies were breaking.

***

The checkpoint guard called Chloen's number. "Mark three. Next."

She stepped forward, sleeve pulled down to hide Tiffani's graphite numbers. The guard checked her wrist. Bare. His eyes went to her sleeve.

"Mark three," she said. Flat. Not small.

The guard's gaze dropped to her hands. Not shaking. Steady. "You sure?" he asked.

Chloen didn't answer. She just held out her hand for the ration token.

The guard hesitated. His hand hovered near his slate. Chloen saw the calculation in his eyes—bare wrist versus steady hands. Which mattered more when the world was coming apart?

A pressure pull hit.
Air thinned.
Dust lifted in slow sheets from the stone.
Coppergilt gathered along the metal token tray, lifting in tiny arcs before settling again.

Chloen's ears popped. Her breath caught. She didn't move. Didn't flinch. Just stood there, hands steady at her sides, until the slack came and air rushed back in.

The guard's shoulders tensed on the pull. His own hand shook slightly on the token tray—not from the cost, from the thinning air. From fear.

He handed her the token. Didn't look at her sleeve again.

***

Chloen found Jhace near the broken archway where the brace team worked. He leaned on his wedge bar, breathing shallow. His hands were steady on the handle. Off the handle, they shook.

"You're being watched," Chloen said.

Jhace didn't look at her. "I know."

"Not just by the Crown. By everyone." She pointed to a knot of people near the water station. "They think it's catching. The shaking."

Jhace's grip tightened on the wedge bar. The trembling lessened. "Let them think it."

"But it's not true," Chloen said. "It's not catching. It's—"

"—the cost," Jhace finished. "I know what it is."

A runner approached with a slate. Young. Nervous. His sash was too bright for the dust. He didn't look at Jhace's hands. He looked at the slate.

"Corlexi requests your presence," he said. "Basin Stadium. Immediately."

Jhace didn't move. "What for?"

"Shard containment protocol," the runner said. He finally looked up. His eyes went straight to Jhace's fingers. "They need someone who can hold the weight."

Jhace's hands stilled. Not from control. From anger. "You mean someone whose hands already shake."

The runner flinched. "I'm just delivering the request."

Jhace stared at him for three breaths. Then he nodded once. "Tell them I'll be there."

The runner fled.

Chloen watched Jhace's hands start shaking again as soon as the runner was gone. Worse than before.

"Don't go," she said.

Jhace looked at her. "They'll come get me if I don't."

"Let them," Chloen said. "Let them see what it costs."

Jhace's mouth tightened. He shifted his weight, and Chloen saw the strain in his shoulders—the way he held himself still against the doubled hum underfoot.

"You think I haven't thought about running?" he asked. His voice was low. "I have. Every breath."

"Then run," Chloen said.

"And do what?" Jhace asked. "Hide in the gaps? Watch from the shadows? That's your skill, not mine." He looked at his hands. "I hold things. That's what I do. Even when it breaks me."

Chloen didn't know what to say to that. She just stood there, watching his fingers tremble.

A woman's voice cut through the crowd. "There he is. That's him."

Chloen turned. Three women stood nearby, pointing at Jhace. Their faces were tight. Afraid.

"He held the slab," one whispered. "He saved the child."

"The same child who's been coughing up copper since," another said. "My sister's boy. He was fine until he stood near that shaking man."

Chloen's throat tightened. She wanted to shout. To tell them it wasn't true. But shouting wouldn't fix broken wrists or fill canteens.

Jhace didn't look at them. He just picked up his wedge bar and started walking toward the stadium steps.

Chloen followed.

***

At the stadium threshold, a new post had been set up. Metal pole. Lanterns. Bells. A Crown emblem bright against dust.

Two guards stood there, slates in hand. They watched Jhace approach. Their eyes went straight to his hands.

"Name and purpose," one guard said.

"Jhace Torrins," he said. "Shard containment."

The guard's pencil hand twitched. He made a mark on his slate. "Observed tremor," he wrote. "Baseline T4. Visible. Persistent."

Jhace didn't argue. He just stood there, hands out where everyone could see them shaking.

The guard looked up. "You understand the risk?"

Jhace met his eyes. "I understand the cost."

The guard nodded once. Stepped aside.

Jhace walked through the gap. Chloen followed.

Inside the stadium, the doubled hum vibrated through the stone underfoot. Mobel stood near the central platform, the shard wrapped in cloth at his feet. Copper smears covered his shirt.

Corlexi waited beside him. His eyes went to Jhace's hands.

"T4 baseline," he said. Not a question.

Jhace didn't answer. He just stood there, hands steady on his wedge bar, fingers trembling off the handle.

Corlexi looked at Chloen. "Why is the child here?"

"She follows things," Jhace said. "Like coppergilt follows metal."

Corlexi didn't smile. He just nodded to a guard. "Mark her. Rope lane three. Water rationing."

The guard approached Chloen with chalk.

Jhace moved then. Fast. His hand shot out and caught the guard's wrist before the chalk could touch her skin.

The guard froze. Everyone froze.

Jhace's hand shook badly where it gripped the guard's wrist. His breath shortened. His teeth buzzed.

"Don't mark her," Jhace said. His voice was rough. "She's not the one who shakes."

The guard pulled back. Corlexi's eyes narrowed.

Jhace lowered his hand. It trembled violently. He clenched it into a fist to stop it.

Corlexi watched him for three breaths. Then he turned to Chloen. "Go," he said. "Before I change my mind."

A woman's voice came from the stairwell—not loud, not challenging. Just present, like stone settling after a pull.

"Or she stays."

Everyone turned.

She stood at the top of the stadium steps where the doubled hum vibrated strongest through the stone. Not tall. Not armed. Her hands were empty. Her cloak was travel-stained but whole, no tears at the seams. She carried a small satchel slung across her body, worn smooth at the strap.

Salee.

Chloen knew her name before she heard it. Everyone did. The woman who moved between districts when bridges broke. The one who brought water to quarantined lanes. The one who could sit between two crews that wouldn't share air and somehow get them passing tools hand-to-hand by sunset.

Salee walked down three steps and stopped. Her eyes went to Jhace's clenched fist. To the tremor running up his forearm. To the copper smears on his knuckles.

She didn't look at Corlexi. She looked at the cracked stone between them—a hairline fracture running from the threshold post toward Jhace's boots, widened by the latest pull phase.

Salee knelt. Not dramatically. Just knelt. She placed her palm flat over the crack.

Nothing happened for three breaths.

Then the stone sighed.

Not a sound. A shift. The edges of the fracture drew together like lips closing. The dust in the seam settled inward. The crack sealed without mortar, without pressure, without anything Chloen could name except that it was wrong and it was whole again.

Salee stood. Her hand came away clean. But her left eye twitched once—just a flicker—and a thin line of copper smears appeared at her temple, as if something had transferred.

Corlexi's mouth tightened. "That's not authorized."

Salee finally looked at him. "It wasn't a hold. It was a mend."

"Same cost."

"No," Salee said. "Different debt."

She stepped between Jhace and the guard. Looked at Chloen. "The child stays with him. Or I walk back up these steps and every cracked seam in this stadium becomes a failure point on the next pull."

Corlexi's eyes narrowed. He knew she wasn't threatening. She was stating physics.

Salee turned to Jhace. Her voice dropped, just for him. "The shard won't break you. But holding its fracture will. There's another way."

Jhace stared at her. At the sealed crack in the stone. At the copper line at her temple that hadn't been there before.

"What way?" he asked.

Salee's eye twitched again. "I carry the break so you don't have to."

Corlexi made a sharp gesture. "Two subjects. One protocol. The shard containment proceeds."

Salee didn't argue. She just stood there, a quiet wall between Jhace and the stadium interior. Between Chloen and the chalk mark. Between the system and what it couldn't yet name.

Jhace looked at Chloen. At Salee. At his own trembling hands.

He didn't move toward the shard.

He didn't move away.

He just stood there, and for the first time since the sky tore, the choice wasn't his alone to carry.

***

Chloen watched Salee's back. Watched the copper line at her temple darken as the pull phase built. Watched her shoulders stay level while Jhace's tremor climbed his arms.

The easy answer was still out there—*contagion, weakness, mark from the sky*—but now there was a harder truth standing in the stadium doorway. A woman who mended cracks and carried breaks. A woman whose eye twitched when stone remembered how to be whole.

Corlexi's runners approached with fresh slates. They didn't write on them yet. They just stood, waiting for the system to catch up to what it was seeing.

Salee didn't look at them. She looked at Jhace's hands.

"Your hands hold weight," she said quietly. "Mine hold breaks. Different tools. Same world."

Jhace's fingers stilled for one breath. Not from control. From being seen.

Then the pull hit.

Air thinned. Dust lifted. Coppergilt rose off metal edges in tiny arcs.

Salee's eye twitched again. The copper line at her temple spread like a bruise.

Jhace's hands started shaking.

But he didn't move toward the shard.

He didn't move away.

He just stood there, and the stadium held its breath around them—three bodies between a system that wanted to count and a world that kept breaking.

The bells began to ring. Three short. One long. Two short.

Chloen kept her hands steady at her sides.

Her throat tasted of copper.

And somewhere between Jhace's shaking hands and Salee's mending palm, the easy answer broke open.

[ASSISTANT]
chapter16_text = """# Chapter 16 — The Easy Answer (LOCKFIX)

Chloen stood at the rope-lane checkpoint and watched the hands that shook.

Jhace worked the water station a short way down the line, directing bodies through the troughs and canteen fills. His wedge bar rested against the wall within reach. When he gripped it, his fingers obeyed. When he released it, the tremor came back—fine, constant, as if the signal in his bones couldn’t find an idle position.

People watched him without turning their heads. The way they watched a hairline seam in a parapet: nothing happening, everything possible.

A woman with a wrapped wrist stepped up to him. “Which line?” she asked.

Jhace pointed. His hand steadied for the motion. “That one. Fill slow. Keep the mouth of the canteen low.”

Her eyes flicked to his fingertips. “They’re saying it spreads,” she said, quiet enough to pretend it wasn’t an accusation.

Jhace didn’t lift his voice. “Fill your canteen.”

She moved on. She looked back anyway.

Chloen had heard the lane talk too. People reached for simple words because simple words made fear feel manageable: weakness, contagion, sky-mark. They weren’t measuring anything. They were trying to put a fence around the unknown.

***

The guard called, “Mark three. Forward.”

Chloen stepped up with her sleeve pulled down, hiding the graphite smear and the slate slip tucked against her skin. The guard checked her bare wrist and then studied her hands.

They held steady.

“You sure?” he asked, eyes on her cuff like it might confess.

Chloen held her palm out for the ration token. “Mark three.”

A pull phase rolled in. Air leaned thin. Dust lifted from the stone in a slow peel. Brass on the token tray pricked with bright grit that rose a hair and settled again.

The guard’s throat worked. His fingers shook on the tray. His fear looked a lot like sickness if you didn’t know how to watch.

Chloen didn’t move until the slack returned and the air came full again. Then the token landed in her hand.

The guard didn’t ask a second time.

Across the lane, Tiffani moved between stations with her slate closed, giving short hand signals—redirect, wait, breathe—like she’d been doing this for years instead of breaths. A dock worker shouldered past with a water yoke. For a fraction of time, Tiffani’s gaze caught on him—forearms, grip, the kind of strength that didn’t negotiate. Something in her face tightened and went flat again. She took a measured breath and kept walking.

Chloen filed it away the way she filed everything now: as proof that control was an action, not an absence.

***

She found Jhace by the broken arch where a brace team had set a timber prop. He leaned on the wedge bar and breathed shallow through the next pull, eyes fixed on the work like staring could keep the stone in place.

“You’re being watched,” Chloen said.

Jhace’s mouth moved once, like he’d bite down on copper if it would help. “I know.”

“By everyone,” she said. “They’re calling it catching.”

Jhace’s grip tightened. The tremor eased to a quieter line. “Let them talk.”

A runner pushed through the lane with a slate clutched to his chest. Young. Clean sash. Nervous posture. He spoke without looking up.

“Corlexi requests your presence. Basin Stadium. Immediate.”

Jhace didn’t move. “For what.”

“Shard protocol,” the runner said, and only then did his eyes lift—straight to Jhace’s hands. “They need someone who can hold the weight.”

Jhace’s fingers went still for a beat, the way metal stills right before it snaps. “Someone who already shows the bill.”

The runner swallowed. “I’m delivering the request.”

Jhace nodded once. “Tell him I’m coming.”

The runner left fast, as if speed could erase what he’d witnessed.

Chloen stepped in closer. “Don’t go.”

Jhace looked down at her like she was a brace he didn’t want to test. “They’ll drag me if I don’t.”

“Let them,” Chloen said. “Let them see what it costs.”

Jhace stared past her shoulder at the rope lanes, the chalk boundaries, the people who wanted an answer they could hold in one hand. “I’ve thought about running,” he said. “Every slack phase.”

“Then do it.”

“And do what,” he said, quiet. “Hide in gaps. Watch from edges. That’s your work.” His eyes dropped to his own fingers. “Mine is holding.”

Chloen didn’t have a counter for that. She followed when he started walking.

Behind them, three women near the trough watched Jhace go. One leaned toward the other, voice sharp.

“That’s him.”

“He held the slab,” another said.

“My sister’s boy keeps coughing metal,” the third added. “He stood near him in line.”

Chloen felt heat in her throat and kept it down. Arguing didn’t refill canteens. Arguing didn’t rebuild terraces.

Jhace didn’t turn. He just kept moving.

***

At the stadium threshold, the checkpoint had been rebuilt into something permanent: metal post, lanterns, bells, Crown emblem bright against dust.

Two guards waited with slates. Their eyes dropped to Jhace’s hands before they asked his name.

“Jhace Torrins,” he said. “Shard containment.”

One guard wrote while saying the words out loud, as if naming the tremor made it controllable. “Baseline T4. Visible.”

Jhace held his hands where they could be seen and let them shake.

“You understand the risk,” the guard said.

Jhace met his eyes. “I understand the cost.”

They stepped aside.

Chloen stayed at Jhace’s shoulder as they entered the stadium bowl. The doubled hum pressed through the stone seats like a second foundation.

Mobel stood near the central platform with a cloth-wrapped bundle at his feet. Copper smeared his shirt in thumbprints and streaks, as if the shard had been handled with bare intention.

Corlexi waited beside him, posture clean, attention sharp. His gaze went to Jhace’s hands. “T4,” he said, like a ledger entry.

Then he looked at Chloen. “Why is the child here.”

“She sees,” Jhace said. “She remembers.”

Corlexi lifted two fingers. A guard moved with chalk.

Jhace moved first.

His hand closed on the guard’s wrist before the chalk touched Chloen. His grip shook violently. Breath shortened. Teeth buzzed.

“Don’t mark her,” Jhace said. “She’s not your resource.”

The guard froze. The stadium held still around the contact.

Jhace released him. His hand kept shaking, and he clenched it until his knuckles went pale.

Corlexi watched the tremor the way he watched fracture lines: interest without tenderness. “Leave,” he said to Chloen. “Before I revise the instruction.”

A voice from the upper steps cut through the moment, calm as a brace set true.

“She stays.”

Heads turned.

A woman stood on the stair run where the vibration felt strongest. Empty hands. Travel-stained cloak. Satchel strap worn smooth across her chest. She didn’t flare authority. She carried it the way some people carried water: quiet, practiced, essential.

Salee.

Chloen had heard the name in rope lanes and under-terrace kitchens. A mover. A fixer. Someone who went where bridges failed and still arrived.

Salee came down three steps and stopped. Her eyes went to the hairline crack running from the threshold post toward Jhace’s boots, widened by the last pull.

She knelt and placed her palm flat over the seam.

Three breaths passed. Nothing dramatic.

Then the stone shifted under her hand. The fracture drew together, edges nesting like they’d always been one piece. Dust sank into the sealed line. The crack disappeared.

Salee stood. Her hand stayed clean. Her left eye twitched once, small and involuntary. A thin copper line appeared at her temple, like a bruise drawn in metal.

Corlexi’s mouth tightened. “Unauthorized.”

Salee looked at the repaired seam instead of his face. “Mended.”

“Same bill,” Corlexi said.

“Different debt,” Salee replied.

She stepped between Chloen and the chalk, then turned to Jhace. Her voice dropped to a range meant for one set of ears. “The shard won’t break you. Holding its fracture will. Let me carry that.”

Jhace stared at the sealed seam, then at the copper at her temple.

“What way,” he asked.

Salee’s eye twitched again. “The way where you stop being the only option.”

Corlexi’s hand sliced the air in a clean directive. “Two subjects. One protocol. Proceed.”

Salee didn’t argue. She stood like a brace post, placed where failure wanted to start.

Jhace looked from Chloen to Salee to his own hands.

For the first time since the sky tore, the choice stopped sitting only on his bones.

***

Chloen watched Salee’s back. Watched the copper line at her temple deepen as the next pull gathered. Watched her shoulders stay level while Jhace’s tremor climbed his forearms.

Runners approached with fresh slates, ready to turn whatever they saw into an easy answer.

Salee didn’t look at them. She looked at Jhace.

“Your hands hold weight,” she said. “Mine holds breaks. Same work. Different tool.”

Jhace’s fingers stilled for one breath—not from control, from being seen in the right way.

Then the pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted. Bright grit rose from metal edges and settled again.

Salee’s eye twitched. Copper spread at her temple like heat under skin.

Jhace’s hands started shaking.

He didn’t step toward the shard.

He didn’t step away.

He stood there, and the stadium held its breath around three bodies while the easy answer started to fail.
"""
# write to file
out_path = pathlib.Path("/mnt/data/N01_Chapter_16_The_Easy_Answer_LOCKFIX_v1.md")
out_path.write_text(chapter16_text, encoding="utf-8")
out_path.as_posix(), len(chapter16_text.splitlines())

out_path.write_text("test", encoding="utf-8")
out_path.exists(), out_path.stat().st_size

[ASSISTANT]
import zipfile, pathlib, shutil, os
old_zip = pathlib.Path("/mnt/data/N01_handover_plus_ch1_to_ch15_lockfix_v1.zip")
new_zip = pathlib.Path("/mnt/data/N01_handover_plus_ch1_to_ch16_lockfix_v1.zip")
chapter16_file = pathlib.Path("/mnt/data/N01_Chapter_16_The_Easy_Answer_LOCKFIX_v1.md")

# create new zip
with zipfile.ZipFile(old_zip, 'r') as zin, zipfile.ZipFile(new_zip, 'w', compression=zipfile.ZIP_DEFLATED) as zout:
    # copy all from old
    for item in zin.infolist():
        data = zin.read(item.filename)
        zout.writestr(item, data)
    # add chapter 16 file
    zout.write(chapter16_file, arcname=chapter16_file.name)

new_zip.exists(), new_zip.stat().st_size

[ASSISTANT]
Updated Chapter 16 with the “no more Not X / Not Y” cadence stripped out, repetition tightened, motifs varied, and a small Tiffani urge-seed threaded in during a pull/breath moment.

## Chapter 16 — The Easy Answer (LOCKFIX)

# Chapter 16 — The Easy Answer (LOCKFIX)

Chloen stood at the rope-lane checkpoint and watched the hands that shook.

Jhace worked the water station a short way down the line, directing bodies through the troughs and canteen fills. His wedge bar rested against the wall within reach. When he gripped it, his fingers obeyed. When he released it, the tremor came back—fine, constant, as if the signal in his bones couldn’t find an idle position.

People watched him without turning their heads. The way they watched a hairline seam in a parapet: nothing happening, everything possible.

A woman with a wrapped wrist stepped up to him. “Which line?” she asked.

Jhace pointed. His hand steadied for the motion. “That one. Fill slow. Keep the mouth of the canteen low.”

Her eyes flicked to his fingertips. “They’re saying it spreads,” she said, quiet enough to pretend it wasn’t an accusation.

Jhace didn’t lift his voice. “Fill your canteen.”

She moved on. She looked back anyway.

Chloen had heard the lane talk too. People reached for simple words because simple words made fear feel manageable: weakness, contagion, sky-mark. They weren’t measuring anything. They were trying to put a fence around the unknown.

***

The guard called, “Mark three. Forward.”

Chloen stepped up with her sleeve pulled down, hiding the graphite smear and the slate slip tucked against her skin. The guard checked her bare wrist and then studied her hands.

They held steady.

“You sure?” he asked, eyes on her cuff like it might confess.

Chloen held her palm out for the ration token. “Mark three.”

A pull phase rolled in. Air leaned thin. Dust lifted from the stone in a slow peel. Brass on the token tray pricked with bright grit that rose a hair and settled again.

The guard’s throat worked. His fingers shook on the tray. His fear looked a lot like sickness if you didn’t know how to watch.

Chloen didn’t move until the slack returned and the air came full again. Then the token landed in her hand.

The guard didn’t ask a second time.

Across the lane, Tiffani moved between stations with her slate closed, giving short hand signals—redirect, wait, breathe—like she’d been doing this for years instead of breaths. A dock worker shouldered past with a water yoke. For a fraction of time, Tiffani’s gaze caught on him—forearms, grip, the kind of strength that didn’t negotiate. Something in her face tightened and went flat again. She took a measured breath and kept walking.

Chloen filed it away the way she filed everything now: as proof that control was an action, not an absence.

***

She found Jhace by the broken arch where a brace team had set a timber prop. He leaned on the wedge bar and breathed shallow through the next pull, eyes fixed on the work like staring could keep the stone in place.

“You’re being watched,” Chloen said.

Jhace’s mouth moved once, like he’d bite down on copper if it would help. “I know.”

“By everyone,” she said. “They’re calling it catching.”

Jhace’s grip tightened. The tremor eased to a quieter line. “Let them talk.”

A runner pushed through the lane with a slate clutched to his chest. Young. Clean sash. Nervous posture. He spoke without looking up.

“Corlexi requests your presence. Basin Stadium. Immediate.”

Jhace didn’t move. “For what.”

“Shard protocol,” the runner said, and only then did his eyes lift—straight to Jhace’s hands. “They need someone who can hold the weight.”

Jhace’s fingers went still for a beat, the way metal stills right before it snaps. “Someone who already shows the bill.”

The runner swallowed. “I’m delivering the request.”

Jhace nodded once. “Tell him I’m coming.”

The runner left fast, as if speed could erase what he’d witnessed.

Chloen stepped in closer. “Don’t go.”

Jhace looked down at her like she was a brace he didn’t want to test. “They’ll drag me if I don’t.”

“Let them,” Chloen said. “Let them see what it costs.”

Jhace stared past her shoulder at the rope lanes, the chalk boundaries, the people who wanted an answer they could hold in one hand. “I’ve thought about running,” he said. “Every slack phase.”

“Then do it.”

“And do what,” he said, quiet. “Hide in gaps. Watch from edges. That’s your work.” His eyes dropped to his own fingers. “Mine is holding.”

Chloen didn’t have a counter for that. She followed when he started walking.

Behind them, three women near the trough watched Jhace go. One leaned toward the other, voice sharp.

“That’s him.”

“He held the slab,” another said.

“My sister’s boy keeps coughing metal,” the third added. “He stood near him in line.”

Chloen felt heat in her throat and kept it down. Arguing didn’t refill canteens. Arguing didn’t rebuild terraces.

Jhace didn’t turn. He just kept moving.

***

At the stadium threshold, the checkpoint had been rebuilt into something permanent: metal post, lanterns, bells, Crown emblem bright against dust.

Two guards waited with slates. Their eyes dropped to Jhace’s hands before they asked his name.

“Jhace Torrins,” he said. “Shard containment.”

One guard wrote while saying the words out loud, as if naming the tremor made it controllable. “Baseline T4. Visible.”

Jhace held his hands where they could be seen and let them shake.

“You understand the risk,” the guard said.

Jhace met his eyes. “I understand the cost.”

They stepped aside.

Chloen stayed at Jhace’s shoulder as they entered the stadium bowl. The doubled hum pressed through the stone seats like a second foundation.

Mobel stood near the central platform with a cloth-wrapped bundle at his feet. Copper smeared his shirt in thumbprints and streaks, as if the shard had been handled with bare intention.

Corlexi waited beside him, posture clean, attention sharp. His gaze went to Jhace’s hands. “T4,” he said, like a ledger entry.

Then he looked at Chloen. “Why is the child here.”

“She sees,” Jhace said. “She remembers.”

Corlexi lifted two fingers. A guard moved with chalk.

Jhace moved first.

His hand closed on the guard’s wrist before the chalk touched Chloen. His grip shook violently. Breath shortened. Teeth buzzed.

“Don’t mark her,” Jhace said. “She’s not your resource.”

The guard froze. The stadium held still around the contact.

Jhace released him. His hand kept shaking, and he clenched it until his knuckles went pale.

Corlexi watched the tremor the way he watched fracture lines: interest without tenderness. “Leave,” he said to Chloen. “Before I revise the instruction.”

A voice from the upper steps cut through the moment, calm as a brace set true.

“She stays.”

Heads turned.

A woman stood on the stair run where the vibration felt strongest. Empty hands. Travel-stained cloak. Satchel strap worn smooth across her chest. She didn’t flare authority. She carried it the way some people carried water: quiet, practiced, essential.

Salee.

Chloen had heard the name in rope lanes and under-terrace kitchens. A mover. A fixer. Someone who went where bridges failed and still arrived.

Salee came down three steps and stopped. Her eyes went to the hairline crack running from the threshold post toward Jhace’s boots, widened by the last pull.

She knelt and placed her palm flat over the seam.

Three breaths passed. Nothing dramatic.

Then the stone shifted under her hand. The fracture drew together, edges nesting like they’d always been one piece. Dust sank into the sealed line. The crack disappeared.

Salee stood. Her hand stayed clean. Her left eye twitched once, small and involuntary. A thin copper line appeared at her temple, like a bruise drawn in metal.

Corlexi’s mouth tightened. “Unauthorized.”

Salee looked at the repaired seam instead of his face. “Mended.”

“Same bill,” Corlexi said.

“Different debt,” Salee replied.

She stepped between Chloen and the chalk, then turned to Jhace. Her voice dropped to a range meant for one set of ears. “The shard won’t break you. Holding its fracture will. Let me carry that.”

Jhace stared at the sealed seam, then at the copper at her temple.

“What way,” he asked.

Salee’s eye twitched again. “The way where you stop being the only option.”

Corlexi’s hand sliced the air in a clean directive. “Two subjects. One protocol. Proceed.”

Salee didn’t argue. She stood like a brace post, placed where failure wanted to start.

Jhace looked from Chloen to Salee to his own hands.

For the first time since the sky tore, the choice stopped sitting only on his bones.

***

Chloen watched Salee’s back. Watched the copper line at her temple deepen as the next pull gathered. Watched her shoulders stay level while Jhace’s tremor climbed his forearms.

Runners approached with fresh slates, ready to turn whatever they saw into an easy answer.

Salee didn’t look at them. She looked at Jhace.

“Your hands hold weight,” she said. “Mine holds breaks. Same work. Different tool.”

Jhace’s fingers stilled for one breath—not from control, from being seen in the right way.

Then the pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted. Bright grit rose from metal edges and settled again.

Salee’s eye twitched. Copper spread at her temple like heat under skin.

Jhace’s hands started shaking.

He didn’t step toward the shard.

He didn’t step away.

He stood there, and the stadium held its breath around three bodies while the easy answer started to fail.

---

# Chapter 17 — Deployment

Tiffani stood at the glass-house threshold and watched the deployment form below.

Not with hope. With measurement.

The rope lanes had been cleared into a single corridor leading from the stadium steps to the shard containment site—a flat stone platform where Mobel waited with the cloth-wrapped bundle at his feet. Copper smears covered his shirt in streaks that hadn't been there yesterday.

Jhace stood at the corridor's mouth with his wedge bar across his knees. His hands were steady on the handle. Off the handle, they shook—not intermittently anymore, not tied to slack phase or observation. Constant. A fine vibration running up his forearms like a wire under tension.

T5.

Tiffani's pencil hand twitched. She didn't write it down yet. She waited for confirmation.

Salee stood beside him.

Not touching. Not speaking. Just standing close enough that their shoulders shared the same air. Her travel-stained cloak hung straight. Her satchel strap was worn smooth at the shoulder. She watched Jhace's hands the way a brace captain watches a leaning wall—not with fear, with assessment.

Tiffani's knee ached. Not the old ache. The newer one—the kind that remembered salt under nails and rough hands and the relief of being pulled out of her own head for a while.

She cut the thought off.

A runner approached Jhace with a slate. Young. Nervous. His eyes went straight to the tremor in Jhace's fingers.

"Corlexi's directive," the runner said. "Shard fracture containment. You hold the edges. She mends the center."

Jhace didn't look at Salee. He just nodded once.

Salee's gaze shifted to the runner. "Protocol?"

"Three breaths per mend cycle," the runner said. "No more. The resonance builds."

Salee nodded. Her left eye twitched once—small, involuntary. A copper line had appeared at her temple overnight, thin as a hair. It hadn't been there yesterday.

Tiffani's pencil moved on her slate without her willing it.

*Subject S. Copper transfer observed. Left temple. Post-mend manifestation.*

She stopped writing. Crossed out the line.

Not data. Interference.

Below, Jhace stood and hefted his wedge bar. His hands shook constantly now—no respite on pull phase, no steadying through work. The tremor had become his baseline. His new normal.

T5 confirmed.

Tiffani wrote it cleanly on a fresh slate page.

*Subject J. T5 baseline. Constant tremor. Operational ceiling.*

She underlined *ceiling* twice.

Salee moved beside Jhace as they walked toward the platform. Not leading. Not following. Matching his pace exactly, as if they'd practiced the rhythm. Her shoulder brushed his arm once when the pull phase hit and the ground shifted underfoot. She didn't pull away. He didn't flinch.

Tiffani's breath caught. Thin. Sharp.

She forced it out on the slack phase. In. Out.

Her knee pulsed. She shifted her weight and ignored it.

At the platform, Mobel unwrapped the shard.

It wasn't stone. Not metal. Something in between—a sliver of the spindle's body, long and sharp with vein-lines of copper and gold pulsing beneath its surface. It lay on the cloth like a bone that refused to stay buried.

The air around it shimmered. Not heat haze. A lensing effect—light bending around the shard's edges as if space itself couldn't decide where to exist.

Jhace knelt. His hands hovered over the shard's edges. The tremor in his fingers intensified—fine vibrations climbing his wrists, his forearms, visible now in the set of his shoulders.

He didn't touch it yet.

Salee knelt opposite him. Her hands stayed empty. Her eyes went to the hairline fractures running through the shard's body—thin splits where the material had begun to separate.

"The edges first," she said. Quiet. Certain.

Jhace's hands descended. His palms flattened against the shard's outer edges. Not gripping. Not pressing. Just holding—like a brace holding a failing seam.

The moment his skin made contact, his whole body locked.

Not collapse. Not failure. A stillness so absolute it looked like death. His breath stopped. His eyes went wide. His mouth opened but no sound came out.

Copper flooded the air around him—a visible mist rising from his skin, catching the Kindling-light in hard flashes.

Salee's hands moved then. Not toward the shard. Toward the fractures running through its center. She placed her palms flat over the splits.

The stone platform beneath them sighed.

Not a sound. A shift. The fractures in the shard drew together—edges nesting like they'd always been one piece. The copper-gold veins pulsed once, bright, then settled into a steady rhythm.

Salee's left eye twitched. The copper line at her temple spread—a thin branch reaching toward her hairline. Her breath shortened. Her shoulders tensed.

But the shard held together.

Jhace's hands remained locked on the edges. His body stayed rigid. His eyes stayed wide. But the copper mist around him lessened. The stillness became something else—not death, endurance.

Three breaths passed.

Salee lifted her hands. The fractures stayed sealed.

Jhace's body unlocked. He sagged forward, catching himself on his wedge bar. His hands kept shaking—constant, unrelenting—but he stayed upright.

Salee didn't touch him. She just watched his breathing until it found a rhythm again.

Then she looked up.

Not at Corlexi. Not at the runners with their slates.

At the glass-house.

At Tiffani.

Her gaze held for three breaths. Not challenging. Not friendly. Acknowledging.

Tiffani's pencil hand twitched again. She didn't write.

Salee turned back to the shard. Placed her hands over the next fracture set.

Jhace's hands returned to the edges. Locked again. Copper mist rose. The platform sighed. The fractures sealed.

Three breaths.

Unlock.

Sag.

Recover.

Repeat.

Tiffani watched the cycle. Counted the breaths. Measured the copper concentration in the air. Documented the tremor amplitude in Jhace's shoulders.

Her slate filled with numbers.

Her throat tightened with something that wasn't data.

Salee's copper line spread with every mend—branches reaching across her temple like cracks in porcelain. Her eye twitched more frequently. Her breathing grew shallow even on the slack phase.

But she didn't stop.

And Jhace didn't fall.

They worked like a single system—his hands holding the weight, her hands carrying the break. Not talking. Not touching beyond what the work required. Just moving through the cycles like they'd always known the rhythm.

Tiffani's knee ached. Her breath caught on the pull phase. Her pencil hovered over a fresh slate page.

She wrote one line. Small. Crisp.

*Subject J. T5 sustained with external support.*

She didn't write what she saw next.

Salee's hand brushing Jhace's wrist between cycles—just a flicker of contact, barely there, but his tremor lessened for one breath.

She didn't write the way Salee's shoulders stayed level while his shook.

She didn't write the copper line spreading across Salee's temple like a map of what she was carrying.

She didn't write the heat in her own throat when Salee looked up at the glass-house again.

*Interference*, she told herself. *Observer bias contaminating measurement.*

She closed the slate.

The deployment continued below. Jhace holding edges. Salee mending fractures. Copper mist rising. Copper lines spreading. The shard staying whole.

Tiffani stood at the glass-house threshold and counted the cost of being the one who measured while others carried.

Her knee pulsed. She ignored it.

Her breath caught. She forced it out.

Her pencil hand twitched. She stilled it.

She hummed once—thin, sharp—the moment between observe and classify.

Then she cut it off before it could become anything else.

Below, the bells began to ring. Three short. One long. Two short.

The count continued.

Tiffani opened her slate one last time.

Wrote at the bottom, small and crisp:

*Deployment ceiling reached. No further escalation observed.*

She didn't write what she felt.

She didn't write what she wanted.

She just closed the slate and watched the two bodies below hold the world together while her own stayed perfectly, clinically still.
