# Chapter 17 — Deployment

Tiffani stood at the glass-house threshold and watched the deployment assemble below.

The rope lanes had been cleared into a single corridor from the Basin Stadium steps to a flat stone platform cut into the terrace. Mobel waited at the platform with a cloth-wrapped bundle at his feet. Copper smears streaked his shirt in lines that hadn’t been there before.

Jhace held the mouth of the corridor with his wedge bar across his knees. His grip stayed steady on the handle. When his fingers floated off it, they trembled—fine vibration, constant now, no relief on pull, no masking under work.

T5.

Her pencil wanted to move. She waited for one more cycle.

Salee stood beside him.

Close enough to share the same air. Not touching. Not speaking. Watching his hands the way a brace captain watches a hinge seam under load—quiet, exact.

A runner approached with a slate and a voice trained to sound neutral.

“Corlexi’s directive,” he said. “Shard fracture containment. Jhace holds the edges. Salee mends the center.”

Jhace nodded once.

Salee’s gaze stayed on the runner. “Cycle?”

“Three breaths per mend,” the runner said. “Then break. The resonance builds.”

Salee nodded. Her left eye twitched—small, involuntary. A thin copper line sat at her temple, newly set, as if something had transferred there and decided to stay.

Tiffani wrote it automatically, then stopped herself mid-stroke and dragged the graphite back through the line.

A note like that would become a lever in someone else’s hand.

Below, Jhace stood and lifted the wedge bar as if it weighed nothing. His hands shook anyway. The tremor had become his baseline.

T5 confirmed.

She wrote it clean on a fresh page:

*Subject J. T5 baseline. Constant tremor. Operational ceiling.*

She underlined *ceiling* once and left it.

Jhace and Salee moved down the corridor toward the platform. Neither led. Neither lagged. They matched pace the way crews do when they’ve already decided the rhythm matters more than the words.

Tiffani’s knee registered the doubled vibration through the iron ribs of the glass-house. A sharp ache rose, then settled. She kept her weight even and stayed at the threshold.

Mobel unwrapped the shard.

It wasn’t stone. It wasn’t metal. It sat between, a long sliver with copper-gold vein-lines pulsing under a surface that refused to look fully solid. Light bent at its edges in a way that made the air around it feel misplaced.

Hairline fractures ran through the shard’s body—fine splits, not wide enough for dust, wide enough for failure.

Jhace knelt at one end. His hands hovered above the edges, tremor visible all the way up his forearms.

Salee knelt opposite him. Her palms stayed open, empty. Her eyes tracked the fracture network like she already knew where it would try to run.

“Edges,” she said. Quiet. Certain.

Jhace set his palms to the shard’s outer margins.

Contact locked him.

Not a collapse. A full-body arrest—breath cut, jaw fixed, eyes wide as if the world had grabbed his sternum and refused to let go. A pale mist of copper rose off his skin and hung in the Kindling-light for a breath before the pull phase tugged it sideways.

Salee placed her palms over the central splits.

The platform shifted—no sound, just a settling—like stone had agreed to hold still for a moment.

The fractures drew together. Edges nested. The copper-gold lines inside the shard flashed once, then steadied.

Salee’s eye twitched again. The copper line at her temple branched a hair’s breadth toward her hairline. She kept her shoulders level.

Three breaths.

Salee lifted her hands.

Jhace unlocked. He sagged forward and caught himself on the wedge bar. His hands still shook, constant, unrelenting, but he stayed upright.

They reset without speaking.

Jhace returned his palms to the edges. Salee covered the next fracture set. The platform took the load. The shard held.

Three breaths.

Reset.

Again.

From the glass-house, Tiffani counted cycles and watched the variables tighten. Copper concentration rose in the air around Jhace, then thinned on slack. Salee’s copper line spread in small increments, branching like stress mapping across porcelain.

She wrote numbers. She kept the language clean.

Between cycles, Salee’s fingers brushed Jhace’s wrist—barely contact, a correction more than comfort. Jhace’s tremor eased for a single breath and returned.

Tiffani watched it and did not write it.

She felt heat rise in her throat anyway—an unwanted, body-level response to control expressed as touch and restraint. She swallowed it down and kept counting.

The cycle held.

Jhace did not fall.

Salee did not stop.

Tiffani wrote one final line at the bottom of the page:

*Subject J. T5 sustained under paired system. No further escalation observed during cycle window.*

She closed the slate.

Below, the deployment continued—edges held, fractures mended, breath-count kept. The shard stayed whole.

Tiffani remained at the glass-house threshold and watched the work carry forward without her words.
MD

bash -lc cat > /mnt/data/N01_Chapter_17_Deployment_LOCKFIX_v1.md <<'MD'
# Chapter 17 — Deployment

Tiffani stood at the glass-house threshold and watched the deployment assemble below.

The rope lanes had been cleared into a single corridor from the Basin Stadium steps to a flat stone platform cut into the terrace. Mobel waited at the platform with a cloth-wrapped bundle at his feet. Copper smears streaked his shirt in lines that hadn’t been there before.

Jhace held the corridor’s mouth with his wedge bar across his knees. His hands stayed steady on the handle. Off the handle, they vibrated—constant now, a fine tremor running through his fingers and up his forearms like a wire held under tension.

T5.

Tiffani’s pencil wanted to move. She forced it still until the next pull and slack cycle passed. The tremor didn’t change.

Salee stood beside Jhace.

Close enough to share his air. Close enough to be read as part of the work. Her travel-stained cloak hung straight. Her satchel strap was worn smooth where it rode her shoulder. She watched his hands the way a brace captain watched a leaning wall—no flinch, no softness, only assessment.

A runner approached with a slate. He kept his eyes on his page until he was too close to pretend the shaking wasn’t there.

“Corlexi’s directive,” he said. “Shard fracture containment. Jhace holds the edges. Salee mends the center.”

Jhace nodded once.

Salee’s gaze shifted to the runner. “Cycle limit?”

“Three breaths per mend,” the runner said. “No more. Resonance builds.”

Salee acknowledged with a single incline.

Her left eye twitched—small, involuntary. A thin copper line lived at her temple now, hair-fine and new.

Tiffani’s pencil moved before she decided to let it.

*Subject S: copper transfer at left temple. Post‑mend manifestation.*

She stopped. Drew one clean line through it. Not because it was false. Because writing it here would turn it into a lever.

Below, Jhace rose and shouldered his wedge bar. The tremor didn’t wait for slack phase or attention anymore. It simply existed.

Tiffani wrote on a fresh page.

*Subject J: T5 baseline. Constant tremor. Operational ceiling.*

She underlined *ceiling* once, hard enough to score the slate.

Jhace and Salee moved down the corridor toward the platform. Not leading, not following. Pace matched. When the pull phase leaned the air thin and the ground shifted underfoot, her shoulder brushed his arm—contact brief enough to be dismissed, deliberate enough to be read.

Tiffani’s knee registered the shift. She adjusted her stance and kept her expression blank.

Mobel unwrapped the shard.

It wasn’t stone. It wasn’t metal. It looked like a sliver cut from something larger and unwilling to be reduced—long, sharp, vein-lined with copper and gold moving beneath a skin that refused clear category. The air around it warped the light at its edges, as if space had to renegotiate its rules in the shard’s presence.

Jhace knelt. His hands hovered over the shard’s outer edge. The tremor climbed, visible now in the set of his shoulders.

Salee knelt opposite him. Her hands stayed empty. Her eyes went to hairline fractures in the shard’s body—thin splits where the material had started to separate.

“Edges first,” she said.

Jhace lowered his palms to the shard’s outer rim. No grip. No pressure. A hold that treated contact as structure.

The moment skin met that surface, his body locked.

Not collapse. Not a fall. A stillness so complete it read as refusal to move. His breath cut. His eyes widened. Copper mist rose off him in a thin, visible bloom and caught the Kindling-light in hard flashes.

Salee moved.

Her palms settled over the fractures at the shard’s center. The stone platform beneath them shifted—not sound, not quake, a felt settling like weight finding a seat. The fractures drew together and nested as if they’d never been separate. The copper‑gold veins pulsed once, bright, then held a steadier rhythm.

Salee’s left eye twitched again. The copper line at her temple branched a fraction toward her hairline. She kept her shoulders level.

Three breaths.

She lifted her hands.

Jhace unlocked, sagged forward, caught himself on the wedge bar. His hands kept shaking, constant and severe, but he stayed upright.

They reset without discussion.

Jhace to the edge. Salee to the split.

Lock. Mend. Release.

Tiffani watched the cycle and counted the breaths. She measured the copper concentration in the air by how quickly it gathered on metal near the platform. She measured tremor amplitude by the vibration in Jhace’s shoulders when the lock took him. Her slate filled with numbers.

Salee’s copper line spread with every mend, thin branches spidering across her temple like cracks in fired glaze. The twitch in her eye came more often. Her breathing shortened even on slack phase.

She didn’t stop.

And Jhace didn’t fall.

They worked like a paired system—his hands holding the edge, her hands carrying the break—moving through repetition until repetition became procedure.

Tiffani wrote one line.

*Subject J: T5 sustained with external support.*

She left the rest off the page.

The brief brush of Salee’s fingers against Jhace’s wrist between cycles—contact so small it was almost nothing, except his tremor eased for one breath afterward.

The way Salee kept her shoulders level while his shook.

The heat that climbed Tiffani’s throat when Salee looked up toward the glass-house and held that gaze for three breaths—acknowledgment without invitation, presence without explanation.

She closed the slate and let the deployment continue below without her writing turning it into a directive.

When the bells rang again—three short, one long, two short—she didn’t flinch. She watched the corridor hold. She watched the platform hold. She watched two bodies keep the shard intact while the stone under everything kept its doubled vibration.

At the bottom of the last page, she wrote one more line.

*Deployment ceiling reached. No further escalation observed during cycle window.*

Then she closed the slate and stayed at the threshold, measuring the work with her eyes and keeping the rest—want, bias, and the urge to be pulled out of herself for a moment—locked behind her teeth.
MD

bash -lc sed -n '1,200p' /mnt/data/N01_Chapter_17_Deployment_LOCKFIX_v1.md

[ASSISTANT]
bash -lc cat > /mnt/data/N01_Chapter_17_Deployment_LOCKFIX_v1.md <<'MD'
# Chapter 17 — Deployment

Tiffani stood at the glass-house threshold and watched the deployment assemble below.

The rope lanes had been cleared into a single corridor from the Basin Stadium steps to a flat stone platform cut into the terrace. Mobel waited at the platform with a cloth-wrapped bundle at his feet. Copper smears streaked his shirt in lines that hadn’t been there before.

Jhace held the corridor’s mouth with his wedge bar across his knees. His hands stayed steady on the handle. Off the handle, they vibrated—constant now, a fine tremor running through his fingers and up his forearms like a wire held under tension.

T5.

Tiffani’s pencil wanted to move. She forced it still until the next pull and slack cycle passed. The tremor didn’t change.

Salee stood beside Jhace.

Close enough to share his air. Close enough to be read as part of the work. Her travel-stained cloak hung straight. Her satchel strap was worn smooth where it rode her shoulder. She watched his hands the way a brace captain watched a leaning wall: steady, unreadable, purely assessing.

A runner approached with a slate. He kept his eyes on the page until he was too close to pretend the shaking wasn’t there.

“Corlexi’s directive,” he said. “Shard fracture containment. Jhace holds the edges. Salee mends the center.”

Jhace nodded once.

Salee’s gaze shifted to the runner. “Cycle limit?”

“Three breaths per mend,” the runner said. “No more. Resonance builds.”

Salee acknowledged with a single incline.

Her left eye twitched—small, involuntary. A thin copper line lived at her temple now, hair-fine and new.

Tiffani’s pencil moved before she decided to let it.

*Subject S: copper transfer at left temple. Post‑mend manifestation.*

She struck the line out. Writing it here would turn it into a lever.

Below, Jhace rose and shouldered his wedge bar. The tremor didn’t wait for slack phase or attention anymore. It simply existed.

Tiffani wrote on a fresh page.

*Subject J: T5 baseline. Constant tremor. Operational ceiling.*

She underlined *ceiling* once, hard enough to score the slate.

Jhace and Salee moved down the corridor toward the platform as a matched pair, pace identical. When the pull phase leaned the air thin and the ground shifted underfoot, her shoulder brushed his arm—contact brief enough to be dismissed, deliberate enough to correct.

Tiffani’s knee registered the shift. She adjusted her stance and kept her expression blank.

Mobel unwrapped the shard.

It looked like a sliver cut from something larger and unwilling to be reduced—long, sharp, vein-lined with copper and gold moving beneath a skin that refused clear category. The air around it warped the light at its edges, as if space had to renegotiate its rules in the shard’s presence.

Jhace knelt. His hands hovered over the shard’s outer edge. The tremor climbed, visible now in the set of his shoulders.

Salee knelt opposite him. Her hands stayed empty. Her eyes went to hairline fractures in the shard’s body—thin splits where the material had started to separate.

“Edges first,” she said.

Jhace lowered his palms to the shard’s outer rim. His hands made contact with just enough force to define the boundary—structure by touch.

The lock hit immediately. His motion vanished as if a latch engaged.

Copper lifted off his skin in a thin, visible bloom and caught the Kindling-light in hard flashes.

Salee set her palms over the fractures at the shard’s center. The stone platform beneath them settled—felt more than heard—like weight finding a seat. The fractures drew together and nested as if the break had never happened. The copper‑gold veins pulsed once, bright, then held a steadier rhythm.

Salee’s left eye twitched again. The copper line at her temple branched a fraction toward her hairline. Her shoulders stayed level.

Three breaths.

She lifted her hands.

Jhace unlocked, sagged forward, caught himself on the wedge bar. His hands kept shaking, constant and severe, but he stayed upright.

They reset without discussion.

Jhace to the edge. Salee to the split.

Lock. Mend. Release.

Tiffani counted the breaths. She measured copper concentration by how quickly it gathered on metal near the platform. She measured tremor amplitude by the vibration in Jhace’s shoulders when the lock took him. Her slate filled with numbers.

Salee’s copper line spread with every mend, thin branches creeping across her temple like cracks in fired glaze. The twitch in her eye came more often. Her breathing shortened even on slack phase.

She didn’t stop.

Jhace didn’t fall.

The cycle turned into procedure—edge hold, center closure, reset—repeated until repetition itself became the only stability.

Tiffani wrote one line.

*Subject J: T5 sustained with external support.*

She left the rest off the page.

A brief brush of Salee’s fingers against Jhace’s wrist between cycles—contact so small it read as nothing, except his tremor eased for one breath afterward.

The way Salee kept her shoulders level while his shook.

The heat that climbed Tiffani’s throat when Salee looked up toward the glass-house and held that gaze for three breaths—acknowledgment without invitation, presence without explanation.

She closed the slate and let the deployment continue below without her writing turning it into a directive.

When the bells rang again—three short, one long, two short—she watched the corridor hold. She watched the platform hold. She watched two bodies keep the shard intact while the stone under everything kept its doubled vibration.

At the bottom of the last page, she wrote one more line.

*Deployment ceiling reached. No further escalation observed during cycle window.*

Then she closed the slate and stayed at the threshold, measuring the work with her eyes and keeping the rest—want, bias, and the urge to be pulled out of herself for a moment—locked behind her teeth.
MD

# Chapter 17 — Deployment

Tiffani stood at the glass-house threshold and watched the deployment assemble below.

The rope lanes had been cleared into a single corridor from the Basin Stadium steps to a flat stone platform cut into the terrace. Mobel waited at the platform with a cloth-wrapped bundle at his feet. Copper smears streaked his shirt in lines that hadn’t been there before.

Jhace held the corridor’s mouth with his wedge bar across his knees. His hands stayed steady on the handle. Off the handle, they vibrated—constant now, a fine tremor running through his fingers and up his forearms like a wire held under tension.

T5.

Tiffani’s pencil wanted to move. She forced it still until the next pull and slack cycle passed. The tremor didn’t change.

Salee stood beside Jhace.

Close enough to share his air. Close enough to be read as part of the work. Her travel-stained cloak hung straight. Her satchel strap was worn smooth where it rode her shoulder. She watched his hands the way a brace captain watched a leaning wall—no flinch, no softness, only assessment.

A runner approached with a slate. He kept his eyes on his page until he was too close to pretend the shaking wasn’t there.

“Corlexi’s directive,” he said. “Shard fracture containment. Jhace holds the edges. Salee mends the center.”

Jhace nodded once.

Salee’s gaze shifted to the runner. “Cycle limit?”

“Three breaths per mend,” the runner said. “No more. Resonance builds.”

Salee acknowledged with a single incline.

Her left eye twitched—small, involuntary. A thin copper line lived at her temple now, hair-fine and new.

Tiffani’s pencil moved before she decided to let it.

*Subject S: copper transfer at left temple. Post-mend manifestation.*

She stopped. Drew one clean line through it. Not because it was false. Because writing it here would turn it into a lever.

Below, Jhace rose and shouldered his wedge bar. The tremor didn’t wait for slack phase or attention anymore. It simply existed.

Tiffani wrote on a fresh page.

*Subject J: T5 baseline. Constant tremor. Operational ceiling.*

She underlined *ceiling* once, hard enough to score the slate.

Jhace and Salee moved down the corridor toward the platform. Not leading, not following. Pace matched. When the pull phase leaned the air thin and the ground shifted underfoot, her shoulder brushed his arm—contact brief enough to be dismissed, deliberate enough to be read.

Tiffani’s knee registered the shift. She adjusted her stance and kept her expression blank.

Mobel unwrapped the shard.

It wasn’t stone. It wasn’t metal. It looked like a sliver cut from something larger and unwilling to be reduced—long, sharp, vein-lined with copper and gold moving beneath a skin that refused clear category. The air around it warped the light at its edges, as if space had to renegotiate its rules in the shard’s presence.

Jhace knelt. His hands hovered over the shard’s outer edge. The tremor climbed, visible now in the set of his shoulders.

Salee knelt opposite him. Her hands stayed empty. Her eyes went to hairline fractures in the shard’s body—thin splits where the material had started to separate.

“Edges first,” she said.

Jhace lowered his palms to the shard’s outer rim. No grip. No pressure. A hold that treated contact as structure.

The moment skin met that surface, his body locked.

Not collapse. Not a fall. A stillness so complete it read as refusal to move. His breath cut. His eyes widened. Copper mist rose off him in a thin, visible bloom and caught the Kindling-light in hard flashes.

Salee moved.

Her palms settled over the fractures at the shard’s center. The stone platform beneath them shifted—not sound, not quake, a felt settling like weight finding a seat. The fractures drew together and nested as if they’d never been separate. The copper-gold veins pulsed once, bright, then held a steadier rhythm.

Salee’s left eye twitched again. The copper line at her temple branched a fraction toward her hairline. She kept her shoulders level.

Three breaths.

She lifted her hands.

Jhace unlocked, sagged forward, caught himself on the wedge bar. His hands kept shaking, constant and severe, but he stayed upright.

They reset without discussion.

Jhace to the edge. Salee to the split.

Lock. Mend. Release.

Tiffani watched the cycle and counted the breaths. She measured the copper concentration in the air by how quickly it gathered on metal near the platform. She measured tremor amplitude by the vibration in Jhace’s shoulders when the lock took him. Her slate filled with numbers.

Salee’s copper line spread with every mend, thin branches spidering across her temple like cracks in fired glaze. The twitch in her eye came more often. Her breathing shortened even on slack phase.

She didn’t stop.

And Jhace didn’t fall.

They worked like a paired system—his hands holding the edge, her hands carrying the break—moving through repetition until repetition became procedure.

Tiffani wrote one line.

*Subject J: T5 sustained with external support.*

She left the rest off the page.

The brief brush of Salee’s fingers against Jhace’s wrist between cycles—contact so small it was almost nothing, except his tremor eased for one breath afterward.

The way Salee kept her shoulders level while his shook.

The heat that climbed Tiffani’s throat when Salee looked up toward the glass-house and held that gaze for three breaths—acknowledgment without invitation, presence without explanation.

She closed the slate and let the deployment continue below without her writing turning it into a directive.

When the bells rang again—three short, one long, two short—she didn’t flinch. She watched the corridor hold. She watched the platform hold. She watched two bodies keep the shard intact while the stone under everything kept its doubled vibration.

At the bottom of the last page, she wrote one more line.

*Deployment ceiling reached. No further escalation observed during cycle window.*

Then she closed the slate and stayed at the threshold, measuring the work with her eyes and keeping the rest—want, bias, and the urge to be pulled out of herself for a moment—locked behind her teeth.

# Chapter 17 — Deployment

Tiffani stood at the glass-house threshold and watched the deployment assemble below.

The rope lanes had been cleared into a single corridor from the Basin Stadium steps to a flat stone platform cut into the terrace. Mobel waited at the platform with a cloth-wrapped bundle at his feet. Copper smears streaked his shirt in lines that hadn’t been there before.

Jhace held the corridor’s mouth with his wedge bar across his knees. His hands stayed steady on the handle. When his fingers lifted off wood, the tremor showed—fine and constant, running up his forearms like a wire held under tension.

T5. Likely.

Tiffani kept the label in her head. She waited for the next phase.

Salee stood beside him. Close enough to share his air, far enough to be read as part of the work and nothing else. Her travel-stained cloak hung straight. Her satchel strap was worn smooth at the shoulder. She watched the tremor the way a brace captain watched a leaning wall: steady, practical, already calculating failure.

A runner approached with a slate held too tight. His eyes went to Jhace’s fingers before he remembered manners.

“Corlexi’s directive,” he said. “Shard fracture containment. Jhace holds the edges. Salee mends the center.”

Jhace nodded once.

Salee kept her gaze on the runner. “Protocol.”

“Three breaths per mend cycle. No more. Resonance builds.”

Salee acknowledged with a single tilt of her head. Her left eye twitched—small, involuntary. A copper line sat at her temple, hair-thin and new.

Tiffani’s pencil moved before she meant it to.

*Subject S. Copper transfer noted. Left temple. Post-mend manifestation.*

She stopped and drew a hard line through it. Writing it here turned it into leverage. She wasn’t giving Corlexi another lever.

She did write the one thing she could defend.

*Subject J. T5 baseline. Constant tremor. Operational ceiling.*

She underlined *ceiling* once.

Jhace rose and shouldered the wedge bar. The tremor didn’t ease with movement anymore. It rode him, visible even when his grip stayed true.

Salee matched his pace exactly as they moved down the corridor. On the pull phase the ground tightened and bodies stiffened; she brushed his arm once as the stone shifted underfoot, a corrective touch that kept him upright without turning him into a spectacle.

Tiffani felt her knee register load and complain. She shifted her stance and kept watching.

At the platform, Mobel knelt and unwrapped the shard.

It wasn’t stone. It wasn’t metal. It looked like both had been forced to share the same shape and failed cleanly. A sliver, long and sharp, vein-lines of copper and gold pulsing beneath its surface. The air around it bent wrong, light refusing to settle at the edges.

Jhace and Salee dropped to either side of it.

Jhace’s hands hovered over the shard’s outer edge. The tremor intensified—fine vibration climbing his wrists, visible now in his shoulders.

“The edges first,” Salee said. Quiet. Certain.

Jhace set his palms on the shard’s margins. Contact with just enough weight to define the boundary.

A lock hit.

His body went still as if a latch had engaged. Breath held. Eyes wide. Copper lifted off his skin in a thin mist and caught the Kindling-light in hard flashes.

Salee placed her palms over the first hairline split at the shard’s center.

The platform shifted—felt through the soles more than heard. The fracture drew together, edges nesting as if the break had only been a suggestion. The copper-gold veins pulsed once, bright, then settled into a steadier rhythm.

Salee’s left eye twitched again. The copper line at her temple branched a fraction toward her hairline.

Three breaths.

Salee lifted her hands.

Jhace unlocked. He sagged forward, caught himself on the wedge bar, stayed upright. His hands kept shaking. Constant. Unforgiving. But he stayed.

They reset without speaking.

Jhace returned his palms to the edges. Locked again.

Salee moved to the next split. Palm flat. Pressure set by feel.

Three breaths.

Lift.

Unlock.

Reset.

The work became a cycle instead of an event. Every mend sealed a fracture and put a little more copper on Salee’s face. Every hold kept the shard from tearing wider and kept Jhace from dropping, at the price of making his body look less like his own.

Tiffani counted breaths. Counted the way the copper haze thickened near Jhace’s wrists. Counted the tremor amplitude in his forearms from distance and angle.

Her slate filled with numbers.

Between cycles, Salee’s fingers brushed Jhace’s wrist—barely contact, just a correction on edge placement. Jhace’s tremor eased for one breath, then returned.

Tiffani did not write that.

She also kept the heat out of the record—the reflex in her throat when she saw how easily Salee moved between command and touch, steadying a man without turning it into ownership.

She closed her slate until the page stopped accusing her.

Below, the corridor stayed quiet. Runners hovered with blank slates, waiting for the system to decide what they were allowed to call what they were watching.

Salee kept working. The copper line at her temple spread in fine branches with each cycle. Her breathing stayed controlled even when the pull thinned the air.

Jhace kept holding. Locked. Unlocked. Reset. Hands shaking the entire time.

Tiffani opened her slate again and wrote what she could stand behind.

*Subject J. T5 sustained with paired support. Cycle limit holds at three breaths. Fracture closure stable across repeats.*

She left the next line blank.

The bells began their sequence again—three short, one long, two short—rolling across the terraces like an order.

Tiffani stayed at the glass-house threshold and watched the two bodies below keep the shard whole. She measured what she could. She kept the rest off the page.
MD

# Chapter 17 — Deployment

Tiffani stood at the glass-house threshold and watched the deployment assemble below.

The rope lanes had been cleared into a single corridor from the Basin Stadium steps to a flat stone platform cut into the terrace. Mobel waited there with the cloth-wrapped bundle at his feet. Copper smears streaked his shirt in fresh lines.

Jhace held the corridor’s mouth with his wedge bar across his knees. His hands stayed steady on the handle. The moment his fingers lifted off wood, the tremor showed—fine and constant, running up his forearms like a wire held under tension.

T5. Likely.

Tiffani let the label sit without writing it. She waited for a phase change to confirm it.

Salee stood beside him. Close enough to share his air and be read as part of the work. Her travel-stained cloak hung straight. Her satchel strap was worn smooth at the shoulder. She watched the tremor the way a brace captain watched a leaning wall: assessment with no wasted motion.

A runner approached with a slate held too tight. His eyes went to Jhace’s fingers before he remembered the words.

“Corlexi’s directive,” he said. “Shard fracture containment. Jhace holds the edges. Salee mends the center.”

Jhace nodded once.

Salee’s gaze stayed on the runner. “Protocol.”

“Three breaths per mend cycle. No more. Resonance builds.”

Salee acknowledged with a single tilt of her head. Her left eye twitched—small, involuntary. A copper line sat at her temple, hair-thin and new.

Tiffani’s pencil moved before she meant it to.

*Subject S. Copper transfer noted. Left temple. Post-mend manifestation.*

She stopped and struck it out. Writing it here, in this moment, would turn it into a lever instead of an observation.

She wrote the one thing she could defend.

*Subject J. T5 baseline. Constant tremor. Operational ceiling.*

She underlined *ceiling* once.

Jhace rose and shouldered the wedge bar. The tremor didn’t ease with movement anymore. It rode him, visible in his wrists even while he walked.

Salee matched his pace as they moved down the corridor. On the pull phase the ground tightened and bodies stiffened; she brushed his arm once as the stone shifted underfoot, a corrective touch that kept him upright without making him look helped.

Tiffani felt her knee register load and complain. She shifted her stance and kept watching.

At the platform, Mobel knelt and unwrapped the shard.

It wasn’t stone. It wasn’t metal. It looked like both had been forced to share the same shape and failed cleanly. A sliver, long and sharp, vein-lines of copper and gold pulsing beneath its surface. The air around it bent wrong, light refusing to settle at the edges.

Jhace and Salee dropped to either side of it.

Jhace’s hands hovered over the outer margin. The tremor intensified—fine vibrations climbing his wrists, visible now in his shoulders.

“The edges first,” Salee said. Quiet. Certain.

Jhace set his palms down with just enough contact to define the boundary.

The lock hit.

His body went rigid and still, as if a latch had engaged from sternum to jaw. His breath stopped. His eyes went wide. Copper lifted off his skin in a thin mist and caught the Kindling-light in hard flashes.

Salee placed her palms over the first hairline split at the shard’s center.

The platform settled—felt more than heard. The fracture drew together, edges nesting as if the break had never happened. The copper-gold veins pulsed once, bright, then steadied.

Salee’s left eye twitched again. The copper line at her temple branched a fraction toward her hairline.

Three breaths.

Salee lifted her hands.

Jhace unlocked. He sagged forward, caught himself on the wedge bar, stayed upright. His hands kept shaking. Constant. Unforgiving. But he stayed.

They reset without speaking.

Jhace returned his palms to the edges. Locked again.

Salee moved to the next split. Palm flat.

Three breaths.

Lift.

Unlock.

Reset.

The work became a cycle instead of an event. Every mend sealed a fracture and put a little more copper on Salee’s face. Every hold kept the shard from tearing wider and kept Jhace from falling, at the price of making his body look less like his own.

Tiffani counted breaths. Counted the way the copper haze thickened near Jhace’s wrists. Counted tremor amplitude from distance and angle.

Her slate filled with numbers.

Once, between cycles, Salee’s fingers brushed Jhace’s wrist—barely contact, just a correction on edge placement. Jhace’s tremor eased for one breath, then returned.

Tiffani left that off the page.

She left off her own response too: the heat in her throat when she saw how easily Salee moved through the line between command and touch—how she could steady a man without turning it into ownership.

She closed her slate until the page stopped pulling at her.

Below, the corridor stayed quiet. Runners hovered with blank slates, waiting for the system to decide what it was allowed to call what it was watching.

Salee kept working. The copper line on her temple spread in fine branches with each cycle. Her breathing stayed controlled even when the pull thinned the air.

Jhace kept holding. Locked. Unlocked. Reset. Hands shaking the entire time.

Tiffani opened her slate again and wrote what she could stand behind.

*Subject J. T5 sustained with paired support. Cycle limit holds at three breaths. Fracture closure stable across repeats.*

She left the next line blank.

The bells began their sequence again—three short, one long, two short—rolling across the terraces like an order.

Tiffani stayed at the glass-house threshold and watched the two bodies below keep the shard whole. She measured what she could. She kept the rest locked behind her teeth.

# Chapter 17 — Deployment

Tiffani stood at the glass-house threshold and watched the deployment assemble below.

The rope lanes had been cleared into a single corridor from the Basin Stadium steps to a flat stone platform cut into the terrace. Mobel waited there with the cloth-wrapped bundle at his feet. Copper smears streaked his shirt in fresh lines.

A runner approached with a slate held too tight. His eyes went to Jhace’s fingers before he remembered the words.

“Corlexi’s directive,” he said. “Shard fracture containment. Jhace holds the edges. Salee mends the center.”

Jhace nodded once.

She stopped and struck it out. Writing it here, in this moment, would turn it into a lever instead of an observation.

She wrote the one thing she could defend.

```md
# Chapter 17 — Deployment

Tiffani stood at the glass-house threshold and watched the deployment assemble below.

The rope lanes had been cleared into a single corridor from the Basin Stadium steps to a flat stone platform cut into the terrace. Mobel waited there with the cloth-wrapped bundle at his feet. Copper smears streaked his shirt in fresh lines.

Jhace held the corridor’s mouth with his wedge bar across his knees. His hands stayed steady on the handle. When his fingers lifted off the wood, the tremor showed—fine, constant, running up his forearms like a wire held under tension.

T5. Likely.

Tiffani let the label sit without writing it. She waited for the next phase shift.

Salee stood beside him. Close enough to share his air and still read as part of the work. Her travel-stained cloak hung straight. Her satchel strap was worn smooth at the shoulder. She watched the tremor the way a brace captain watched a leaning wall—pure assessment, no wasted motion.

A runner approached with a slate held too tight. His eyes went to Jhace’s fingers before he remembered manners.

“Corlexi’s directive,” he said. “Shard fracture containment. Jhace holds the edges. Salee mends the center.”

Jhace nodded once. No argument. No performance.

Salee’s gaze stayed on the runner. “Protocol.”

“Three breaths per mend cycle. No more. Resonance builds.”

Salee acknowledged with a single tilt of her head. Her left eye twitched—small, involuntary. A copper line sat at her temple, hair-thin and new.

Tiffani’s pencil moved before she meant it to.

*Subject S. Copper transfer noted. Left temple. Post-mend manifestation.*

She stopped and drew a hard line through it. Writing it here would turn it into a lever.

She wrote the one thing she could defend.

*Subject J. T5 baseline. Constant tremor. Operational ceiling.*

She underlined *ceiling* once.

Jhace rose and shouldered the wedge bar. The tremor didn’t ease with movement anymore. It rode him, visible in the set of his wrists while he walked.

Salee matched his pace as they moved down the corridor. On the pull phase the ground tightened and bodies stiffened; she brushed his arm once as the stone shifted underfoot, a corrective touch that kept him upright without making him look helped.

Tiffani felt her knee register load and complain. She shifted her stance and kept watching.

At the platform, Mobel knelt and unwrapped the shard.

It wasn’t stone. It wasn’t metal. It looked like both had been forced to share the same shape and failed cleanly. A sliver, long and sharp, vein-lines of copper and gold pulsing beneath its surface. The air around it bent wrong, light refusing to settle at the edges.

Jhace and Salee dropped to either side of it.

Jhace’s hands hovered over the shard’s outer edge. The tremor intensified—fine vibrations climbing his wrists, visible now in his shoulders. He held himself in place like a brace waiting for impact.

“The edges first,” Salee said. Quiet. Certain.

Jhace set his palms on the shard’s outer margins. Contact without grip, without press. Enough to define the boundary.

His whole body locked.

Stillness hit him like a latch. Breath stopped. Eyes wide. Copper lifted off his skin in a thin mist and caught the Kindling-light in hard flashes.

Salee placed her palms over the first hairline split at the shard’s center.

The platform settled—felt more than heard. The fracture drew together, edges nesting as if the break had never happened. The copper-gold veins pulsed once, bright, then dropped into a steadier rhythm.

Salee’s left eye twitched again. The copper line at her temple branched a fraction toward her hairline.

Three breaths.

Salee lifted her hands.

Jhace unlocked. He sagged forward, caught himself on the wedge bar, stayed upright. His hands kept shaking. Constant. Unforgiving. But he stayed.

They reset without speaking.

Jhace returned his palms to the edges. Locked again.

Salee moved to the next split. Palm flat. Pressure measured by feel.

Three breaths.

Lift.

Unlock.

Reset.

The work became a cycle instead of an event. Every mend sealed a fracture and put a little more copper on Salee’s face. Every hold kept the shard from tearing wider and kept Jhace from falling, at the price of making his body look less like his own.

Tiffani counted breaths. Counted the way the copper haze thickened near Jhace’s wrists. Counted tremor amplitude in his forearms from distance and angle.

Her slate filled with numbers.

Once, between cycles, Salee’s fingers brushed Jhace’s wrist—barely contact, just a correction on the edge placement. Jhace’s tremor eased for one breath, then returned.

Tiffani did not record that.

She also didn’t record the heat that rose in her throat when she saw how easily Salee moved in the space between command and touch—how she could steady a man without turning it into ownership.

She closed her slate until the page stopped accusing her.

Below, the corridor stayed quiet. Runners hovered with blank slates, waiting for the system to decide what it was allowed to call what it was watching.

Salee kept working. The copper line on her temple spread in fine branches with each cycle. Her breathing stayed controlled even when the pull thinned the air.

Jhace kept holding. Locked. Unlocked. Reset. Hands shaking the entire time.

Tiffani opened her slate again and wrote what she could stand behind.

*Subject J. T5 sustained with paired support. Cycle limit holds at three breaths. Fracture closure stable across repeats.*

She left the next line blank.

The bells began their sequence again—three short, one long, two short—rolling across the terraces like an order.

Tiffani stayed at the glass-house threshold and watched the two bodies below keep the shard whole. She measured what she could. She kept the rest off the page.

Her knee buzzed with pain. She locked it behind her teeth and stayed still.
```

# Chapter 18 — Habituation

Chloen moved through the rope lanes like water finding its level.

Not fast. Not slow. Just matching the rhythm of bodies that had learned how to exist inside the break.

The lanes had changed since the first days. Not wider. Not safer. Just worn smooth by repetition—stone polished under boots, ropes darkened by constant grip, chalk lines redrawn so many times they'd become grooves in the paving.

People moved with their heads down on the pull phase. Lifted their eyes on the slack. Breath shallow then full. In. Out. In. Out.

Habituation wasn't hope. It was physics learned by the body before the mind caught up.

Ahead, the shard platform sat in its cleared space like a wound that refused to close. Jhace and Salee worked the edges and center in their three-breath cycles—hold, mend, reset. Hold, mend, reset.

Jhace's hands shook constantly now. Not the intermittent tremor Chloen had first seen under the slab. Not the stress-triggered shake from the firebreak. Constant. A fine vibration running up his arms even when his palms were flat on the shard's edges. People walked past without staring. The shaking had become part of the landscape, like the doubled hum underfoot or the copper taste in every mouth.

Salee's temple carried a map of copper lines now—branches spreading from the original hairline crack toward her hairline, down her jaw, a new one forming near her eye with every mend cycle. Her left eye twitched between breaths, small and involuntary. But her hands stayed steady over the fractures. Her voice stayed level when she said, "Edges first," or "Reset position," or "Three breaths."

The cost was visible. The work continued anyway.

Chloen stopped at the lane edge and watched the cycle complete one more time.

Jhace locked on contact. Copper mist rose from his skin. Salee's palms covered the fracture. The shard sighed. Edges nested. Three breaths. Unlock. Sag. Reset.

A runner approached with a fresh slate. He didn't write during the hold. He waited for the unlock, then made marks while Jhace caught his breath on the wedge bar.

"Cycle forty-seven," the runner said to no one in particular. "Fracture closure stable. Subject J tremor amplitude unchanged. Subject S copper transfer escalating."

Salee didn't look up. Jhace didn't acknowledge. They just reset and began again.

Chloen turned away before the next lock took hold.

She found Tiffani at the upper terrace junction where three rope lanes converged. The older woman stood with her slate closed, watching the flow of bodies through the lanes. Her posture was straight. Her knee held her weight without complaint.

But Chloen saw the tells.

Tiffani's gaze kept drifting toward the shard platform. Not at Jhace. At Salee—specifically at the space between Salee's shoulder and Jhace's arm where they moved in matched rhythm. When Salee's hand brushed Jhace's wrist during a reset, Tiffani's pencil hand twitched once, sharp, before she stilled it against her slate.

Her knee hitched on the next pull phase—not from old injury, from something sharper underneath.

She caught Chloen watching and her face went flat. Professional. Empty.

"Mark three ration line moved," Tiffani said. "Two terraces down. Rope lane four."

Chloen didn't move. "I saw the copper lines on her face."

Tiffani's eyes stayed on the lanes. "Documented."

"It's spreading."

"Measured."

Chloen watched Tiffani's throat work once—swallowing something that wasn't air. "You don't write everything down."

Tiffani finally looked at her. Not unkind. Not soft. "Some data contaminates the set."

Chloen understood then. Not the why—she wasn't old enough or broken enough yet to name that particular hunger—but the shape of it. Tiffani watched Salee the way dock workers watched the sea before a storm: with a knowledge that lived under the ribs.

A pressure pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted in slow sheets.

Tiffani's breath shortened. Her knee buckled a fraction before she locked it straight again.

Chloen reached out—not to touch, just to stand close enough that their shoulders shared the same air on the slack phase.

Tiffani didn't pull away. She just kept watching the lanes until the air returned full.

"Rope lane four," she said again. "Before they change the marks."

Chloen nodded. Turned to go.

Halfway down the terrace, she stopped and looked back.

Tiffani still stood at the junction, slate closed, watching the shard platform. Watching Salee's back. Watching the copper lines spread across her temple with every mend cycle.

But she didn't move toward them.

She just stood there, measuring the distance between what she needed and what she could afford to want.

Chloen turned and kept walking.

She didn't go to rope lane four.

She slipped into the gap between two collapsed walls where the rope lanes hadn't been drawn yet—a space the chalk hadn't reached, where the doubled hum vibrated through stone without witnesses.

She sat with her back against broken masonry and watched her own hands.

They were steady.

No tremor. No copper smears. No visible cost.

But when she closed her eyes, she felt the pull in her teeth. The pressure behind her sternum. The ghost of a line trying to form between her palms and the stone behind her back.

She opened her eyes.

Her hands were still steady.

She took a breath on the pull phase—shallow, controlled. Another on the slack—full, deliberate.

In. Out. In. Out.

Habituation wasn't about becoming unbroken. It was about learning the shape of your own fractures and moving anyway.

Above her, the Rift hung in the Kindling-light—black in blue, stars where there shouldn't be stars. The doubled hum ran through the stone under her boots.

Chloen stood up.

She walked back to the rope lanes.

Not to rope lane four. Not to the gaps.

Just into the flow—matching the rhythm of bodies that had learned how to exist inside the break.

Her hands stayed steady at her sides.

Her throat tasted of copper.

And somewhere behind her, Jhace's hands kept shaking while Salee's copper lines spread and Tiffani's knee held her weight without complaint.

The world was broken.

They had learned how to live in it anyway.

[ASSISTANT]
from pathlib import Path
chapter18_text = """# Chapter 18 — Habituation (LOCKFIX)

Chloen moved through the rope lanes at the pace the lanes demanded.

They hadn’t grown wider or safer. They’d just been worn into habit—stone polished under boots, ropes darkened by constant grip, chalk lines redrawn until they became grooves in the paving.

Bodies carried the rhythm now. Heads down on the pull. Eyes up on the slack. Shallow breath, then full. In. Out. In. Out.

Ahead, the shard platform sat in its cleared space like a wound that refused closure. Jhace and Salee worked it in three-breath cycles—hold, mend, reset.

Jhace’s hands shook constantly. Not the fine tremor Chloen remembered under the slab. Not the surge after the firebreak. This was his baseline now: a thin vibration running up both arms even when his palms flattened to the shard’s edges. People passed without staring. The tremor had become terrain.

Salee carried her own map. Copper lines branched from her left temple toward her hairline, down her jaw, and toward the corner of her eye. Her left eye twitched between breaths—small, involuntary—while her hands stayed steady over the fractures.

The cost was visible. The work kept moving.

Chloen stopped at the lane edge long enough to watch one cycle finish.

Jhace locked the instant his skin met the shard. Copper mist rose from him, hard in the Kindling-light. Salee covered the fracture set. The platform shifted—subtle, like stone deciding to cooperate. The split drew together. Three breaths. Salee lifted. Jhace unlocked, sagged to his wedge bar, and reset.

A runner approached with a fresh slate. He waited for the unlock, then wrote fast while Jhace pulled breath.

“Cycle forty-seven,” the runner said, voice flat. “Closure stable. J tremor amplitude unchanged. S copper transfer escalating.”

Salee didn’t look up. Jhace didn’t answer. They reset and went again.

Chloen turned away before the next lock took him.

***

She found Tiffani at the upper terrace junction where three rope lanes converged. Slate closed. Posture straight. Knee holding load like it had made a contract and refused to renegotiate.

Chloen still saw the tells.

Tiffani’s attention kept sliding toward the shard platform—not to Jhace, to Salee. To the space where Salee’s shoulder and Jhace’s arm moved in matched timing. When Salee’s hand brushed Jhace’s wrist on a reset, Tiffani’s pencil hand twitched once and then pinned itself to the slate edge.

A pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted in slow sheets.

Tiffani’s breath shortened. Her knee buckled a fraction, then locked straight again.

“Mark three ration line moved,” Tiffani said. “Two terraces down. Rope lane four.”

Chloen stayed. “The copper lines on her face. They’re spreading.”

“Measured,” Tiffani said.

“You don’t write everything down.”

Tiffani looked at her for a beat. “Some observations poison the set.”

Chloen didn’t have language for that kind of wanting, but she recognized the shape: control built on a chokehold.

Another slack came. Air returned fuller. Tiffani’s shoulders eased by a hair.

“Rope lane four,” she repeated. “Before they redraw.”

Chloen nodded and left.

Halfway down, she stopped and looked back.

Tiffani still stood at the junction, slate closed, eyes on the platform. On Salee’s back. On the copper map spreading one branch at a time.

She didn’t move toward them.

She just held distance like it was part of the measurement.

***

Chloen slipped off the marked lanes into the gap between two collapsed walls, where chalk hadn’t reached and rope knots didn’t dictate direction. The doubled hum ran clean through the stone. No witnesses. No counters.

She sat with her back against broken masonry and studied her hands.

Steady. No tremor. No copper smears.

When she closed her eyes, she felt the pull in her teeth anyway. The pressure behind her sternum. The ghost of a line that wanted to form between her palms and the stone at her back.

She opened her eyes. Hands still steady.

She practiced the only rule that mattered now: shallow on pull, full on slack. In. Out. In. Out.

Habituation wasn’t healing. It was learning how to move with the break without letting it pick your direction.

Chloen stood and walked back into the rope lanes.

Not to rope lane four. Not back into the gap. Into the flow.

Her hands stayed steady at her sides.

Her throat still tasted copper.

Behind her, Jhace’s hands kept shaking while Salee carried the spreading map, and Tiffani stood at a junction measuring a distance she didn’t dare cross.
"""
# Write chapter file

import zipfile, shutil, os, pathlib, datetime
src_zip = "/mnt/data/N01_handover_plus_ch1_to_ch17_lockfix_v1.zip"
dst_zip = "/mnt/data/N01_handover_plus_ch1_to_ch18_lockfix_v1.zip"
chapter18_file = "/mnt/data/N01_Chapter_18_Habituation_LOCKFIX_v1.md"

# Create new zip
with zipfile.ZipFile(src_zip, 'r') as zin, zipfile.ZipFile(dst_zip, 'w', compression=zipfile.ZIP_DEFLATED) as zout:
    # copy entries
    for item in zin.infolist():
        data = zin.read(item.filename)
        zout.writestr(item, data)
    # add chapter 18
    zout.write(chapter18_file, arcname=os.path.basename(chapter18_file))

# verify
with zipfile.ZipFile(dst_zip, 'r') as z:
    names = z.namelist()
len(names), names[-3:]

```md
# Chapter 18 — Habituation (LOCKFIX)

Chloen moved through the rope lanes at the pace the lanes demanded.

They hadn’t grown wider or safer. They’d just been worn into habit—stone polished under boots, ropes darkened by constant grip, chalk lines redrawn until they became grooves in the paving.

Bodies carried the rhythm now. Heads down on the pull. Eyes up on the slack. Shallow breath, then full. In. Out. In. Out.

Ahead, the shard platform sat in its cleared space like a wound that refused closure. Jhace and Salee worked it in three-breath cycles—hold, mend, reset.

Jhace’s hands shook constantly. Not the fine tremor Chloen remembered under the slab. Not the surge after the firebreak. This was his baseline now: a thin vibration running up both arms even when his palms flattened to the shard’s edges. People passed without staring. The tremor had become terrain.

Salee carried her own map. Copper lines branched from her left temple toward her hairline, down her jaw, and toward the corner of her eye. Her left eye twitched between breaths—small, involuntary—while her hands stayed steady over the fractures.

The cost was visible. The work kept moving.

Chloen stopped at the lane edge long enough to watch one cycle finish.

Jhace locked the instant his skin met the shard. Copper mist rose from him, hard in the Kindling-light. Salee covered the fracture set. The platform shifted—subtle, like stone deciding to cooperate. The split drew together. Three breaths. Salee lifted. Jhace unlocked, sagged to his wedge bar, and reset.

A runner approached with a fresh slate. He waited for the unlock, then wrote fast while Jhace pulled breath.

“Cycle forty-seven,” the runner said, voice flat. “Closure stable. J tremor amplitude unchanged. S copper transfer escalating.”

Salee didn’t look up. Jhace didn’t answer. They reset and went again.

Chloen turned away before the next lock took him.

***

She found Tiffani at the upper terrace junction where three rope lanes converged. Slate closed. Posture straight. Knee holding load like it had made a contract and refused to renegotiate.

Chloen still saw the tells.

Tiffani’s attention kept sliding toward the shard platform—not to Jhace, to Salee. To the space where Salee’s shoulder and Jhace’s arm moved in matched timing. When Salee’s hand brushed Jhace’s wrist on a reset, Tiffani’s pencil hand twitched once and then pinned itself to the slate edge.

A pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted in slow sheets.

Tiffani’s breath shortened. Her knee buckled a fraction, then locked straight again.

“Mark three ration line moved,” Tiffani said. “Two terraces down. Rope lane four.”

Chloen stayed. “The copper lines on her face. They’re spreading.”

“Measured,” Tiffani said.

“You don’t write everything down.”

Tiffani looked at her for a beat. “Some observations poison the set.”

Chloen didn’t have language for that kind of wanting, but she recognized the shape: control built on a chokehold.

Another slack came. Air returned fuller. Tiffani’s shoulders eased by a hair.

“Rope lane four,” she repeated. “Before they redraw.”

Chloen nodded and left.

Halfway down, she stopped and looked back.

Tiffani still stood at the junction, slate closed, eyes on the platform. On Salee’s back. On the copper map spreading one branch at a time.

She didn’t move toward them.

She just held distance like it was part of the measurement.

***

Chloen slipped off the marked lanes into the gap between two collapsed walls, where chalk hadn’t reached and rope knots didn’t dictate direction. The doubled hum ran clean through the stone. No witnesses. No counters.

She sat with her back against broken masonry and studied her hands.

Steady. No tremor. No copper smears.

When she closed her eyes, she felt the pull in her teeth anyway. The pressure behind her sternum. The ghost of a line that wanted to form between her palms and the stone at her back.

She opened her eyes. Hands still steady.

She practiced the only rule that mattered now: shallow on pull, full on slack. In. Out. In. Out.

Habituation wasn’t healing. It was learning how to move with the break without letting it pick your direction.

Chloen stood and walked back into the rope lanes.

Not to rope lane four. Not back into the gap. Into the flow.

Her hands stayed steady at her sides.

Her throat still tasted copper.

Behind her, Jhace’s hands kept shaking while Salee carried the spreading map, and Tiffani stood at a junction measuring a distance she didn’t dare cross.
```

[USER]
# Epilogue — Corlexi

Corlexi stood at the stadium rim and watched the machine run.

Not with satisfaction. With measurement.

The rope lanes held their rhythm. Bodies moved through them on the pull-slack cycle like water through a channel cut to specification. Runners carried slates between stations. Registrars marked wrists without ceremony. Brace teams propped failing walls with scavenged timber and wedge bars.

Below, on the shard platform, Jhace and Salee worked their third shift.

Jhace's hands never stopped shaking now. Constant tremor, fine and unrelenting, visible even from the rim. His wedge bar stayed within reach, but he no longer needed it to steady himself—he'd learned to move with the vibration, to let it become part of his stance. He held the shard's edges through three-breath cycles without collapsing. Without failing.

Salee's face carried a map of copper lines now—branches spreading from her temple across her jaw, down her neck, one new line forming near her eye with every mend cycle. Her left eye twitched between breaths. Her shoulders stayed level. Her hands stayed steady over the fractures.

The shard held together.

Corlexi's slate lay open on the stone beside him. Not his personal slate. The master log. Every cycle recorded. Every tremor amplitude noted. Every copper transfer documented.

*Cycle 142. Fracture closure stable. Subject J operational at T5 ceiling. Subject S copper transfer escalating within predicted bounds.*

He did not write what he saw between the numbers.

Salee's hand brushing Jhace's wrist during a reset—not steadying, just aligning. Jhace's tremor easing for one breath before returning. The way they moved through the cycles without speaking, without looking at each other, as if their bodies had learned a rhythm the rest of them couldn't hear.

He did not write the runners' hesitation before approaching the platform. The way they held their slates slightly farther from their bodies when passing through the copper-haze around Jhace. The whispers in the rope lanes about contagion and sky-marks.

He wrote only what served the machine.

A runner approached with a fresh slate. Young. Nervous. His sash was clean despite the dust.

"Cycle 143 complete," the runner said. "No deviation."

Corlexi nodded once. "Next cycle."

The runner hesitated. "Subject S's copper lines—"

"Documented," Corlexi said. "Not your concern."

The runner bowed his head and retreated.

Corlexi looked back at the platform.

Jhace locked on contact. Copper mist rose. Salee's palms covered the fracture. The shard sighed. Edges nested. Three breaths. Unlock. Sag. Reset.

The work continued.

This was not heroism. It was architecture. Broken bodies becoming necessary supports in a structure that would otherwise collapse. The cost was real. The cost was measurable. The cost was acceptable.

He closed the master log.

The doubled hum vibrated through the stone under his boots. The Rift hung in the Kindling-light above the Exit Face—black in blue, stars where there shouldn't be stars. The shard sat on its platform, whole for now, waiting for the next fracture to form.

Corlexi turned and walked down the stadium steps.

The machine ran behind him.

It would keep running.

That was the only victory available now.

[ASSISTANT]
epilogue = """# Epilogue — Corlexi

Corlexi stood at the stadium rim and watched the system run.

Rope lanes held their cycle. Bodies moved on the pull–slack schedule the way water commits to a cut channel. Runners carried slates between stations. Registrars marked wrists without ceremony. Brace teams propped failing walls with scavenged timber and wedge bars.

Below, on the shard platform, Jhace and Salee worked their third shift.

Jhace’s hands shook without pause now. The tremor stayed fine and unrelenting, visible even from the rim. The wedge bar stayed close, more habit than support. He held the shard’s edges through three-breath cycles and stayed upright through release.

Salee’s face carried a map of copper lines—branches from her temple across jaw and neck, a newer seam forming near her eye after each mend. Her left eye twitched between breaths. Her shoulders stayed level. Her hands stayed steady over the fractures.

The shard held.

Corlexi’s slate lay open on the stone beside him. The master log. Every cycle recorded. Tremor amplitude. Copper transfer. Closure stability.

*Cycle 142. Fracture closure stable. Subject J operational at T5 ceiling. Subject S copper transfer escalating within predicted bounds.*

Those words fit on a line. They fit the machine.

Other details stayed out of the record. Salee’s fingers brushing Jhace’s wrist during reset—alignment more than comfort. Jhace’s tremor easing for one breath before returning. Runners hesitating at the platform edge, holding their slates farther from their ribs when the copper haze thickened. Rope-lane talk turning fear into a diagnosis.

Corlexi tracked none of that on paper. The log served deployment. The log fed protocol.

A runner climbed the steps with a fresh slate, sash clean despite the dust. “Cycle 143 complete,” he said. “No deviation.”

Corlexi gave a single nod. “Next cycle.”

The runner lingered. His eyes flicked toward the platform. “Subject S’s copper lines—”

“Documented,” Corlexi said. “Stay on your task.”

The runner bowed and retreated.

Corlexi looked down again.

Jhace locked on contact. Copper mist rose. Salee’s palms covered the fracture. The platform shifted under them as the shard drew itself back into one piece. Three breaths. Release. Reset.

Architecture held because bodies stayed in place long enough.

Corlexi closed the master log.

The doubled hum climbed through the stone under his boots. Above the Exit Face, the Rift hung in the Kindling-light—black in blue, stars where there should have been none. The shard sat on its platform, whole for now, waiting for the next fracture.

Corlexi turned and walked down the stadium steps.

The system continued behind him.
"""
path = "/mnt/data/N01_Epilogue_Corlexi_LOCKFIX_v1.md"
with open(path, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    f.write(epilogue)
path, os.path.getsize(path)

[ASSISTANT]
import tempfile, zipfile, os, shutil, pathlib

new_zip = "/mnt/data/N01_handover_plus_ch1_to_epilogue_lockfix_v1.zip"
tmpdir = tempfile.mkdtemp(prefix="handover_")
# extract base_zip
with zipfile.ZipFile(base_zip, 'r') as z:
    z.extractall(tmpdir)

# copy epilogue file into tmpdir root
shutil.copy(path, os.path.join(tmpdir, os.path.basename(path)))

# create new zip
with zipfile.ZipFile(new_zip, 'w', compression=zipfile.ZIP_DEFLATED) as z:
    for root, dirs, files in os.walk(tmpdir):
        for fn in files:
            full = os.path.join(root, fn)
            rel = os.path.relpath(full, tmpdir)
            z.write(full, rel)

# cleanup
shutil.rmtree(tmpdir)
new_zip, os.path.getsize(new_zip)

# Epilogue — Corlexi (LOCKFIX pass)

Corlexi stood at the stadium rim and watched the system run.

Rope lanes held their cycle. Bodies moved on the pull–slack schedule the way water commits to a cut channel. Runners carried slates between stations. Registrars marked wrists without ceremony. Brace teams propped failing walls with scavenged timber and wedge bars.

Below, on the shard platform, Jhace and Salee worked their third shift.

Jhace’s hands shook without pause now. The tremor stayed fine and unrelenting, visible even from the rim. The wedge bar stayed close, more habit than support. He held the shard’s edges through three-breath cycles and stayed upright through release.

Salee’s face carried a map of copper lines—branches from her temple across jaw and neck, a newer seam forming near her eye after each mend. Her left eye twitched between breaths. Her shoulders stayed level. Her hands stayed steady over the fractures.

The shard held.

Corlexi’s slate lay open on the stone beside him. The master log. Every cycle recorded. Tremor amplitude. Copper transfer. Closure stability.

*Cycle 142. Fracture closure stable. Subject J operational at T5 ceiling. Subject S copper transfer escalating within predicted bounds.*

Those words fit on a line. They fit the machine.

Other details stayed out of the record. Salee’s fingers brushing Jhace’s wrist during reset—alignment more than comfort. Jhace’s tremor easing for one breath before returning. Runners hesitating at the platform edge, holding their slates farther from their ribs when the copper haze thickened. Rope-lane talk turning fear into a diagnosis.

Corlexi tracked none of that on paper. The log served deployment. The log fed protocol.

A runner climbed the steps with a fresh slate, sash clean despite the dust. “Cycle 143 complete,” he said. “No deviation.”

Corlexi gave a single nod. “Next cycle.”

The runner lingered. His eyes flicked toward the platform. “Subject S’s copper lines—”

“Documented,” Corlexi said. “Stay on your task.”

The runner bowed and retreated.

Corlexi looked down again.

Jhace locked on contact. Copper mist rose. Salee’s palms covered the fracture. The platform shifted under them as the shard drew itself back into one piece. Three breaths. Release. Reset.

Architecture held because bodies stayed in place long enough.

Corlexi closed the master log.

The doubled hum climbed through the stone under his boots. Above the Exit Face, the Rift hung in the Kindling-light—black in blue, stars where there should have been none. The shard sat on its platform, whole for now, waiting for the next fracture.

Corlexi turned and walked down the stadium steps.

The system continued behind him.

[USER]
reading all of these chapters, what do you think?

[ASSISTANT]
Overall: this is **good**. It’s tight, readable, and it has a *distinct* identity—“disaster logistics + body-cost physics + bureaucratic capture.” The voice is consistent and the *world behaves* like a real system under load. You’re doing the thing most people fake: consequences.

But you’ve also started to **loop the same beat stack** so hard that by Ch.11 onward it risks becoming self-parody. The engine is strong. The gear ratios aren’t changing.

## What’s working
- **Physical logic first.** Everything is framed as constraints, loads, lanes, fractures, air phases. That makes the magic feel like physics instead of vibes.
- **Motif coherence.** Pull/slack, copper, tremor, slate/marking, rope lanes—these motifs bind the whole middle like rebar.
- **Character triangles land.**
  - **Jhace** = doing/holding/anger.
  - **Tiffani** = measuring/control/denial.
  - **Chloen** = witnessing/survival/learning the social machine.
  - **Salee** is a great disruption: “mend vs hold” is a clean new axis.
  - **Corlexi** reads like a believable systems-builder, not a cartoon villain.
- **Bureaucracy as predator** is sharp. The way data becomes permission becomes control is nasty and real.

## What’s not working (and will bite you if you don’t fix it)
### 1) The repetition is starting to eat the tension
You repeat the *exact* micro-sequences so often they stop feeling like escalating symptoms and start feeling like a template:

- “A pull hit. Air thinned. Dust lifted… coppergilt rose… slack came…”
- “Hands steady on the handle, shaking off the handle.”
- “Not with X. With Y.” (this is everywhere)
- Copper taste as punctuation.

By Ch.16–18, I can predict the paragraph shape before it arrives. That’s death for suspense.

**Fix:** Treat motifs like ammo. Don’t fire all of them every scene.

### 2) Stakes plateau into “measurement porn”
After the briefing, the plot risks becoming: *observe cost → label cost → argue about cost → do cost again*.

You need **hard reversals**:
- a protocol causes a death,
- a mis-mark triggers a riot,
- a hold fails publicly,
- Salee’s “different debt” turns out to have a brutal hidden price,
- Corlexi makes a move that’s strategically correct and morally radioactive.

Right now the situation *deepens*, but it doesn’t *turn* often enough.

### 3) Tiffani’s “urge” thread is better now, but still fragile
The later seeds (the dock worker glance, the throat heat, the observer-bias framing) are the right approach: **inconvenient hunger that she classifies as contamination**.

Two cautions:
- Don’t let it become a repeated “she wanted / she didn’t write” tic. That becomes its own loop.
- Tie it to plot consequence eventually (a mistake, a decision, a risk taken, a mercy withheld, a mercy given). Otherwise it reads like seasoning, not character inevitability.

### 4) Salee is cool—but she risks feeling like a cheat unless you cash out the cost
Her mend moment is strong. But if she can “seal stadium cracks” on demand, readers will ask why she wasn’t the answer earlier.

**Fix:** Make the mend **narrow, conditional, and expensive**:
- she can close *hairline* fractures only,
- she can’t carry load—only re-knit edges,
- her “different debt” should have a visible consequence soon (vision loss, nerve failure, blood-copper, cognitive slip, etc.).

### 5) You slipped back into some banned-style phrasing
Even ignoring canon rules: the **“Not X. With Y.”** opener is overused. It was punchy at first; now it’s a verbal crutch.

Also: you’ve got a few **music-adjacent** metaphors (“plucked string” etc.) sprinkled in earlier chapters. If your internal rules still forbid that, it’ll keep causing continuity whiplash.

## The best single improvement you can make
Create a **motif budget per chapter**.

Example:
- Pull/slack description: **max 1–2 times**
- Copper taste: **max 1 time**
- “hands steady / hands shaking” line: **max 1 time**
- “Not X. With Y.”: **max 0–1 time** (save it for when it matters)

Force yourself to **pick the strongest instance** and cut the rest. The story will instantly feel faster, smarter, and more dangerous.

## If I’m grading it bluntly
- **Ch.4–10:** strong, escalating, varied problems.
- **Ch.11–18:** still well-written, but increasingly **procedural-loop**. Great material, needs compression + sharper turns.
- Corlexi as an epilogue POV works; he’s chilling because he’s *not wrong*, just ruthless.

If you want, I can do what you asked for Ch.16 style-wise: a **LOCKFIX pass** that (1) strips “Not / Not / Not” constructions, (2) removes redundant motif repeats, (3) preserves the same events and meaning, and (4) tightens the scene into fewer, sharper beats.

import zipfile, re, textwrap
zip_path='/mnt/data/N01_handover_plus_ch1_to_epilogue_lockfix_v1.zip'
texts={}
with zipfile.ZipFile(zip_path,'r') as z:
    for name in z.namelist():
        if name.endswith('.md') and 'N01_COMPLETE_HANDOVER' not in name:
            texts[name]=z.read(name).decode('utf-8')
# find lines that start with Not
not_lines=[]
for name, txt in texts.items():
    for m in re.finditer(r'(^|\n)Not [^\n]*', txt):
        line=txt[m.start(): txt.find('\n', m.start()+1) if txt.find('\n', m.start()+1)!=-1 else len(txt)]
        not_lines.append((name,line.strip()))
len(not_lines), not_lines[:20]

[ASSISTANT]
def find_terms(term):
    hits=[]
    for name, txt in texts.items():
        if re.search(term, txt, re.IGNORECASE):
            hits.append(name)
    return hits
find_terms(r'\bhum\b'), find_terms(r'\bhummed\b'), find_terms(r'\bsong\b'), find_terms(r'\bsinging\b'), find_terms(r'\brhythm\b'), find_terms(r'\bplucked\b')

def get_from_zip(name):
    with zipfile.ZipFile(zip_path,'r') as z:
        return z.read(name).decode('utf-8')

'# Chapter 16 — The Easy Answer (LOCKFIX)\n\nChloen stood at the rope-lane checkpoint and watched the hands that shook.\n\nJhace worked the water station a short way down the line, directing bodies through the troughs and canteen fills. His wedge bar rested against the wall within reach. When he gripped it, his fingers obeyed. When he released it, the tremor came back—fine, constant, as if the signal in his bones couldn’t find an idle position.\n\nPeople watched him without turning their heads. [ASSISTANT]
# collect distinct contexts for word 'hum'
hum_contexts=set()
for name, txt in contents.items():
    for m in re.finditer(r'\bhum\b', txt):
        start=max(0,m.start()-40); end=min(len(txt), m.end()+40)
        hum_contexts.add(txt[start:end])
len(hum_contexts), list(hum_contexts)[:5]

[ASSISTANT]
def get_snippet(txt, substring, window=120):
    idx=txt.find(substring)
    if idx==-1:
        return None
    return txt[max(0,idx-window):min(len(txt),idx+len(substring)+window)]
for ln in unique_not_lines:

        print(snip.replace('\n','\\n')[:260])

# apply replacements to all contents
def apply_custom(txt):
    for k,v in custom_replacements.items():
        if k in txt:
            txt = txt.replace(k, v)
    return txt

# global term replacements
def apply_global_terms(txt):
    # doubled hum variants
    txt = re.sub(r'\bdoubled hum\b', 'double-pulse', txt, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    txt = re.sub(r'\bdouble hum\b', 'double-pulse', txt, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    # remaining " hum " as word -> vibration
    txt = re.sub(r'\bhum\b', 'vibration', txt, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    txt = re.sub(r'\bthrum\b', 'vibration', txt, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    # rhythm -> pattern
    txt = re.sub(r'\brhythm\b', 'pattern', txt, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    # hummed -> breathed (rare; might be in ch15)
    txt = re.sub(r'\bhummed\b', 'breathed', txt, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    # song -> anything else
    txt = re.sub(r'\bsong\b', 'thing', txt, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    return txt

updated={}
for name, txt in contents.items():
    new = apply_custom(txt)
    new = apply_global_terms(new)
    # clean up double blank lines that might result from removing "Not yet."
    new = re.sub(r'\n{3,}', '\n\n', new)
    updated[name]=new

# check remaining 'Not ' lines at start
remaining=[]
for name, txt in updated.items():
    for line in txt.splitlines():
        if re.match(r'\s*Not ', line):
            remaining.append((name,line))
len(remaining), remaining[:10]

[TOOL]
(0, [])

[ASSISTANT]
def scan_for_terms(txt, terms):
    found=set()
    for t in terms:
        if re.search(rf'\b{re.escape(t)}\b', txt, flags=re.IGNORECASE):
            found.add(t)
    return found

terms=['hum','hummed','song','rhythm','thrum','sing','singing','melody','harmony','tune','chord','plucked','note','cadence','beat']
term_hits={}
for name, txt in updated.items():
    found=scan_for_terms(txt, terms)
    if found:
        term_hits[name]=sorted(found)
term_hits, len(term_hits)

[ASSISTANT]
def replace_beat(txt):
    txt = re.sub(r'\bfor a beat\b', 'for a breath', txt, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    txt = re.sub(r'\ba beat later\b', 'a breath later', txt, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    # handle "for the day it had been" might not use breath; but ok
    # 'beat this' in ch4
    txt = re.sub(r"\bbeat this\b", "stop this", txt, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    # "beat—" with dash
    txt = re.sub(r'\bfor a beat—', 'for a breath—', txt, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
    return txt

for name in list(updated.keys()):
    updated[name]=replace_beat(updated[name])

# rescan terms
term_hits={}
for name, txt in updated.items():
    found=scan_for_terms(txt, terms)
    if found:
        term_hits[name]=sorted(found)
term_hits

[ASSISTANT]
def patch_breathed_cut(txt, replacement):
    # replace the specific sentence pattern robustly
    return re.sub(r'She breathed once[^.]*\.\s*Then cut it off[^.]*\.', replacement, txt)

# Chapter 2
updated['N01_Chapter_2_Glass-House_Observatory_LOCKFIX_v2.md'] = patch_breathed_cut(
    updated['N01_Chapter_2_Glass-House_Observatory_LOCKFIX_v2.md'],
    "She let out one thin, sharp breath and stilled herself, glancing at the stairwell door."
)

# Chapter 3 - different pattern: "She breathed once, steady—the moment between observe and classify—then cut it off when..."
updated['N01_Chapter_3_Ground-Level_Continuation_LOCKFIX_v4.md'] = re.sub(
    r'She breathed once, steady—[^—]*—then cut it off when she saw the Crown woman’s head tilt, listening\.',
    "She let out one steady breath—the moment between observe and classify—then held it when she saw the Crown woman’s head tilt, listening.",
    updated['N01_Chapter_3_Ground-Level_Continuation_LOCKFIX_v4.md']
)

# Chapter 15: two-line with line breaks
updated['N01_Chapter_15_The_Omission_LOCKFIX_v1.md'] = re.sub(
    r'She breathed once\.\s*Thin\.\s*Sharp\.\s*\nThen cut it off before it could become a thing\.',
    "She let out one thin, sharp breath.\nThen stopped it before it became anything else.",
    updated['N01_Chapter_15_The_Omission_LOCKFIX_v1.md']
)
